topics.title: Tractive Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tractive Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tractive Resistance** is defined as the total opposing force that a vehicle must overcome through its driving wheels (tractive effort) in order to maintain motion or accelerate under given operating conditions. It is the cumulative sum of all resistive forces acting opposite to the direction of vehicle travel along the longitudinal axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Tractive resistance is the net resistive force against the motion of a vehicle, comprising **rolling resistance**, **aerodynamic drag**, **grade resistance**, and **inertia (acceleration) resistance**. The tractive effort generated at the driving wheels must always exceed the tractive resistance for the vehicle to accelerate; it must equal the resistance for constant-speed cruise.

Mathematically, the equilibrium of forces on a vehicle moving on a road of inclination $\theta$ is expressed as:

$$
F_t \;=\; F_r \;+\; F_a \;+\; F_g \;+\; F_i
$$

Where each term is defined and quantified in subsequent sections.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Pushing a Loaded Cart:**
> Imagine you are pushing a heavy iron-wheeled cart up a sloped, windy street.
> - The **weight on your shoulders** trying to roll the wheel = *Rolling Resistance* ($F_r$).
> - The **wind blowing in your face** = *Aerodynamic Drag* ($F_a$).
> - The **slope pulling the cart backward** = *Grade Resistance* ($F_g$).
> - The **extra push to speed up** rather than just keep pace = *Inertia Resistance* ($F_i$).
> The total push (Tractive Force) you must apply is the sum of all four. If your push is greater than this sum, you accelerate; if equal, you cruise; if less, you slow down.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

For all tractive resistance computations, the following standard values are used (highlighted in bold in formulas throughout these notes):

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of air at STP | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Typical rolling resistance coefficient (car tire) | $\mu_r$ | **0.010 – 0.015** |
| Typical rolling resistance coefficient (truck tire) | $\mu_r$ | **0.006 – 0.012** |
| Typical drag coefficient (passenger car) | $C_d$ | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| Typical frontal area (passenger car) | $A$ | **1.8 – 2.5 m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Components of Tractive Resistance

### A. Rolling Resistance ($F_r$)

Rolling resistance arises due to the non-rigid contact between the tire and road surface. It is governed by the **hysteresis energy loss** in the tire carcass as it deforms and recovers during rotation.

**Key Drivers:**
- Tire construction material (rubber compound, ply material)
- Inflation pressure (lower pressure = higher deformation = higher $F_r$)
- Road surface texture and material
- Vehicle speed (slight increase with speed for radial tires)
- Vertical load on the tire

**KTU High-Yield Equation:**

$$
F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot W \;=\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> On level ground ($\theta = 0$), $\cos\theta = 1$, simplifying to $F_r = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$.

### B. Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$)

Aerodynamic drag is the resistance due to the vehicle displacing and shearing air molecules as it moves. It is **proportional to the square of velocity**, making it the dominant resistance at high speeds.

**Key Drivers:**
- Vehicle's frontal cross-sectional area $A$
- Drag coefficient $C_d$ (shape-dependent)
- Air density $\rho$ (decreases with altitude)
- Velocity squared $V^2$

**KTU High-Yield Equation:**

$$
F_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossover Insight:** Aerodynamic drag overtakes rolling resistance as the dominant resistance typically above **80–100 km/h** for passenger cars.

### C. Grade Resistance ($F_g$)

Grade resistance is the component of the vehicle's weight acting along the inclined road surface, directed opposite to motion when climbing.

**Key Drivers:**
- Mass of the vehicle $m$
- Gravitational acceleration $g$
- Road inclination angle $\theta$ (or grade percentage $G$)

**KTU High-Yield Equations:**

$$
F_g \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

For small angles (typical road grades $< 20\%$), using the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$:

$$
F_g \;\approx\; m \cdot g \cdot \tan\theta \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \tfrac{G}{100}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Grade Percentage Rule:** A road grade of "5%" means a rise of 5 m per 100 m horizontal — i.e., $\tan\theta = 0.05$.

### D. Inertia / Acceleration Resistance ($F_i$)

Inertia resistance is the force required to accelerate the vehicle's mass **and** the rotational inertia of rotating components (wheels, driveshaft, engine, transmission gears).

**Key Drivers:**
- Equivalent vehicle mass $m \cdot \delta$
- Desired linear acceleration $a$

**KTU High-Yield Equation:**

$$
F_i \;=\; m \cdot \delta \cdot a
$$

Where $\delta$ is the **mass factor** (rotational inertia correction):

$$
\delta \;=\; 1 \;+\; \dfrac{I_w}{m R^2} \;+\; \dfrac{I_e \cdot i_g^2 \cdot i_0^2 \cdot \eta_t}{m R^2}
$$

For typical passenger cars, $\delta \approx \mathbf{1.03 - 1.08}$. For heavy trucks, $\delta$ can be **1.10 – 1.30**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** This is the single most important table for solving any tractive resistance problem in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the formula, the dependency, and the unit for each row.

| # | Resistance Type | Symbol | Formula | Dominant Regime | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r$ | $\mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Low–medium speed | N |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_a$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2$ | High speed ($V^2$ dependent) | N |
| 3 | Grade Resistance | $F_g$ | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \approx m g G/100$ | Climbing operation | N |
| 4 | Inertia Resistance | $F_i$ | $m \cdot \delta \cdot a$ | Acceleration phases | N |
| 5 | **Total Tractive Resistance** | $F_{tr}$ | $F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$ | Always | N |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application | Use of Tractive Resistance Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Powertrain Sizing** | Determines required engine torque and gear ratios |
| **Battery EV Range Estimation** | $F_{tr} \cdot V = \text{Power required}$, integrated over drive cycle |
| **Fuel Economy Certification (WLTP/ARAI)** | Resistance forces integrated across standard drive cycle |
| **Hill-Start Assist Systems** | Grade resistance dictates hold-force at standstill |
| **Heavy Haul Truck Design** | Grade + inertia dominate tractive demand |
| **Brake System Design** | Peak deceleration force must overcome $F_i$ and $F_g$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rolling Resistance from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** A tire under vertical load $W$ deforms asymmetrically. The normal pressure distribution shifts forward of the wheel center, creating an offset that produces a moment opposing rotation.

Let the asymmetric pressure distribution result in a net horizontal force $F_r$ acting at the road contact, with a moment arm approximately equal to the tire radius $r$.

**Step 1:** Apply moment equilibrium about the wheel center. The applied driving torque $T$ must overcome the resistance torque created by $F_r$ at the contact patch:

$$
T \;=\; F_r \cdot r
$$

**Step 2:** Empirical observation (Racetrack tests, Magic Formula) establishes a linear relationship between $F_r$ and vertical load $W$ for a given tire, road, and speed condition. Define the rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r$:

$$
\mu_r \;\triangleq\; \dfrac{F_r}{W}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $W = m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ (component of weight normal to inclined road):

$$
F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot W \;=\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4:** Simplification for level ground ($\theta = 0$, $\cos 0 = 1$):

$$
F_r \;\approx\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because $F_r \propto \cos\theta$ and $F_g \propto \sin\theta$, on very steep grades the **grade resistance dominates** and rolling resistance becomes almost negligible. Conversely, on level highway cruise, $F_r$ is the largest non-aerodynamic term.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aerodynamic Drag from Fluid Mechanics

**Starting Premise:** A moving body in a fluid creates a pressure distribution and a viscous shear stress field on its surface. The net longitudinal component of the resulting force is the drag.

**Step 1:** Define the **dynamic pressure** of the airflow at vehicle speed $V$:

$$
q \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V^2
$$

**Step 2:** The integrated longitudinal force on the vehicle body, projected on the frontal area $A$, is proportional to $q$ and $A$. The proportionality constant is the dimensionless drag coefficient $C_d$, which captures the **shape factor** (streamlining):

$$
F_a \;=\; C_d \cdot A \cdot q
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $q$:

$$
F_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2
$$

**Step 4:** Verify dimensional consistency. In SI units: $[kg/m^3] \cdot [m^2] \cdot [m^2/s^2] = [kg \cdot m / s^2] = N$ ✓

**Step 5:** Velocity dependence — at $V = 2V_0$, drag becomes $4 \times F_a$; at $V = 3V_0$, drag becomes $9 \times F_a$. This **quadratic growth** is the fundamental reason EV manufacturers obsess over drag coefficient ($C_d$) reductions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grade Resistance from Free-Body Diagram

**Starting Premise:** A vehicle of mass $m$ on a road inclined at angle $\theta$ has its weight vector $W = m \cdot g$ acting vertically downward. We resolve this into components parallel and perpendicular to the road surface.

**Step 1:** Resolve weight $W$ along the inclined road:

- Component along slope (opposing motion on climb): $W \cdot \sin\theta$
- Component normal to slope: $W \cdot \cos\theta$

**Step 2:** The force component along the slope that the vehicle must overcome to climb is precisely the grade resistance:

$$
F_g \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to grade percentage form. By definition, grade percentage $G$ is the ratio of vertical rise to horizontal run, expressed as a percentage. Hence:

$$
\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{G}{100}
$$

**Step 4:** For typical road inclinations ($\theta < 12°$, i.e., $G < 20\%$), the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$ introduces less than 2% error. Therefore:

$$
F_g \;\approx\; m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{G}{100}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical sanity check. A $1500 \text{ kg}$ car on a 10% grade:

$$
F_g \;\approx\; 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.10 \;\approx\; 1471.5 \text{ N}
$$

This is roughly equivalent to the rolling resistance of the same car on flat ground — a useful benchmark.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inertia Resistance with Rotational Mass Correction

**Starting Premise:** Newton's second law for translation alone gives $F = m \cdot a$. However, when accelerating, the engine must also supply torque to spin up the wheels, driveline, and engine internals. This additional kinetic energy demand is captured as a "rotational mass" correction.

**Step 1:** Write total kinetic energy of the vehicle (translation + rotation of wheels and engine components):

$$
KE_{total} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,V^2 \;+\; \sum \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_i \,\omega_i^2
$$

**Step 2:** For wheels of moment of inertia $I_w$ and radius $R$, the rotational velocity is $\omega_w = V / R$:

$$
KE_{wheels} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_w \left(\dfrac{V}{R}\right)^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{I_w}{R^2}\right) V^2
$$

**Step 3:** Define the **effective mass** (including rotational inertia) such that $KE_{total} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{eff} V^2$:

$$
m_{eff} \;=\; m \;+\; \dfrac{I_w}{R^2} \;+\; \text{(engine, driveline terms scaled by gear ratio}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 4:** Express as a dimensionless mass factor $\delta$:

$$
\delta \;=\; 1 \;+\; \dfrac{I_w}{m R^2} \;+\; \dfrac{I_e \cdot i_g^2 \cdot i_0^2 \cdot \eta_t}{m R^2}
$$

Where $i_g$ = transmission gear ratio, $i_0$ = final drive ratio, $\eta_t$ = drivetrain efficiency.

**Step 5:** Final inertia resistance:

$$
F_i \;=\; m \cdot \delta \cdot a
$$

## 3.5 Master Working Problem — KTU Numerical Pattern

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car of mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ is traveling at $V = 90 \text{ km/h}$ up a road grade of $G = 5\%$. The frontal area is $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $C_d = 0.35$, $\mu_r = 0.012$, and the mass factor $\delta = 1.06$. Calculate the total tractive resistance and the required tractive power.

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- $V = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- $G = 5\% \Rightarrow \tan\theta = 0.05 \Rightarrow \sin\theta \approx 0.0499, \cos\theta \approx 0.9988$
- $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $C_d = 0.35$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu_r = 0.012$, $\delta = 1.06$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step (a) — Rolling Resistance:**

$$
F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_r \;=\; 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9988
$$

$$
F_r \;\approx\; 176.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (b) — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$
F_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2
$$

$$
F_a \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2
$$

$$
F_a \;\approx\; 294.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (c) — Grade Resistance:**

$$
F_g \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \;\approx\; m \cdot g \cdot \tfrac{G}{100}
$$

$$
F_g \;=\; 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \;=\; 735.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (d) — Inertia Resistance** (constant speed, $a = 0$):

$$
F_i \;=\; m \cdot \delta \cdot a \;=\; 0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (e) — Total Tractive Resistance:**

$$
F_{tr} \;=\; F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i
$$

$$
F_{tr} \;\approx\; 176.4 + 294.7 + 735.75 + 0
$$

$$
F_{tr} \;\approx\; 1206.85 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 1.21 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step (f) — Required Tractive Power:**

$$
P_{tr} \;=\; F_{tr} \cdot V
$$

$$
P_{tr} \;=\; 1206.85 \times 25 \;\approx\; 30171 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 30.17 \text{ kW}
$$

**Observations & Insights:**
1. Grade resistance contributes $\approx 61\%$ of total — it is the **dominant term** in this climbing cruise condition.
2. Aerodynamic drag is only $\approx 24\%$ — this is typical at moderate speed.
3. Rolling resistance is $\approx 15\%$.
4. Power required is $\approx 30 \text{ kW}$ — easily handled by a small passenger car engine ($\sim 80 \text{ kW}$ typical).

## 3.6 Dominant Resistance Regime Table (KTU Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TIP:** The single most common Part B question is: *"Under what conditions does each resistance dominate?"* Memorize this table.

| Speed Range | Dominant Resistance | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $0 - 10$ km/h | Inertia ($F_i$) | Starting from rest; $a$ is significant |
| $10 - 60$ km/h | Rolling ($F_r$) | Linear in speed, drag still small |
| $60 - 100$ km/h | $F_r \approx F_a$ | Crossover region |
| $> 100$ km/h | Aerodynamic ($F_a$) | $V^2$ dominance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Climbing Vehicle (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional force-flow architecture** of all forces acting on a vehicle accelerating up a slope. This is the conceptual architecture model used in vehicle dynamics textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["VEHICLE MASS m"]:::mainNode
    nodeB["WEIGHT VECTOR W = m·g"]:::force
    nodeC["NORMAL TO ROAD W·cosθ"]:::subforce
    nodeD["ALONG ROAD W·sinθ"]:::subforce
    nodeE["GRADE RESISTANCE Fg"]:::resist
    nodeF["ROLLING RESISTANCE Fr"]:::resist
    nodeG["AERO DRAG Fa"]:::resist
    nodeH["INERTIA RESISTANCE Fi"]:::resist
    nodeI["TOTAL TRACTIVE RESISTANCE Ftr"]:::sum
    nodeJ["REQUIRED TRACTIVE FORCE Ft"]:::req
    nodeK["POWER DEMAND P = Ft·V"]:::power

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK

    classDef mainNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef force fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef subforce fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef resist fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sum fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef req fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef power fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Resistance Component Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["VEHICLE PARAMETERS m, A, Cd"]:::input
    inp2["OPERATING V, θ, a"]:::input
    inp3["TIRE/ROAD μr"]:::input
    inp4["ROTATIONAL δ"]:::input

    blk1["ROLLING MODEL Fr = μr·m·g·cosθ"]:::calc
    blk2["AERO MODEL Fa = 0.5·ρ·Cd·A·V²"]:::calc
    blk3["GRADE MODEL Fg = m·g·sinθ"]:::calc
    blk4["INERTIA MODEL Fi = m·δ·a"]:::calc

    out1["Ftr = Fr + Fa + Fg + Fi"]:::sum
    out2["POWER P = Ftr·V"]:::power
    out3["DECISION ACCEL OR DECEL OR CRUISE"]:::decision

    inp1 --> blk1
    inp1 --> blk3
    inp1 --> blk4
    inp2 --> blk2
    inp2 --> blk3
    inp2 --> blk4
    inp3 --> blk1
    inp4 --> blk4

    blk1 --> out1
    blk2 --> out1
    blk3 --> out1
    blk4 --> out1

    out1 --> out2
    out1 --> out3

    classDef input fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sum fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef power fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Velocity vs Resistance — Conceptual Plot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START LOW SPEED"]:::stage
    nodeB["INERTIA DOMINATES Fi >> Fr, Fa, Fg"]:::stage
    nodeC["MID SPEED 10 TO 60 km per h"]:::stage
    nodeD["ROLLING DOMINATES Fr > Fa, Fg, Fi"]:::stage
    nodeE["HIGH SPEED ABOVE 100 km per h"]:::stage
    nodeF["AERO DOMINATES Fa >> Fr, Fg, Fi"]:::stage
    nodeG["CONCLUSION SPEED DOMINATES WHICH RESISTANCE WINS"]:::concl

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    classDef stage fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef concl fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define tractive resistance. List its four major components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Tractive resistance is the total opposing force that a vehicle must overcome at the driving wheels to maintain or increase its speed. Its four major components are:

1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_r$)** — due to tire deformation and road surface interaction.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$)** — due to air resistance acting on the vehicle body.
3. **Grade Resistance ($F_g$)** — the component of weight along an inclined road.
4. **Inertia Resistance ($F_i$)** — required to overcome vehicle mass and rotational inertia during acceleration.

**Equation:**

$$
F_t \;=\; F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i
$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is aerodynamic drag considered dominant at high vehicle speeds? Justify with an expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Aerodynamic drag is given by:

$$
F_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2
$$

The drag force is proportional to the **square of velocity**. Hence, doubling the speed quadruples the drag. Since rolling resistance is approximately linear in speed and inertia resistance is zero during cruise, $F_a$ grows far more rapidly and dominates the resistance budget above $80\text{–}100$ km/h for typical passenger cars. This $V^2$ dependence also explains why EV manufacturers aggressively pursue low $C_d$ values to extend range.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q. A (a) Derive the expression for aerodynamic drag force acting on a vehicle. Mention the factors that influence it. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**
When a vehicle moves through air, it experiences a longitudinal force opposing motion due to the pressure distribution and viscous shear at its surface.

**Step 1:** The dynamic pressure generated by air at relative velocity $V$ is:

$$
q \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V^2
$$

**Step 2:** The net longitudinal drag force is proportional to $q$ and the frontal area $A$:

$$
F_a \;\propto\; q \cdot A
$$

**Step 3:** The proportionality constant is the dimensionless drag coefficient $C_d$, which depends on the body's shape:

$$
F_a \;=\; C_d \cdot A \cdot q
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $q$:

$$
F_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^2
$$

**Influencing Factors:**
- Air density $\rho$ (affected by altitude, temperature)
- Drag coefficient $C_d$ (shape, surface smoothness, appendages)
- Frontal cross-sectional area $A$
- Vehicle velocity $V$ (square dependence)

---

**Q. A (b) A truck of mass 8000 kg climbs a 4% grade at 60 km/h. Compute the total tractive resistance. Given: $C_d = 0.6$, $A = 7 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_r = 0.008$. Assume constant speed. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $m = 8000 \text{ kg}$, $V = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$
- $G = 4\% \Rightarrow \tan\theta \approx 0.04, \sin\theta \approx 0.04, \cos\theta \approx 0.9992$
- $A = 7 \text{ m}^2$, $C_d = 0.6$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_r = 0.008$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$
F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \;=\; 0.008 \times 8000 \times 9.81 \times 0.9992
$$

$$
F_r \;\approx\; 627.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$
F_a \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 7 \times (16.67)^2
$$

$$
F_a \;\approx\; 714.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Grade Resistance:**

$$
F_g \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \;\approx\; 8000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \;=\; 3139.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Inertia Resistance:**

Since the truck moves at constant speed, $a = 0$:

$$
F_i \;=\; 0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Tractive Resistance:**

$$
F_{tr} \;=\; 627.5 + 714.4 + 3139.2 + 0
$$

$$
F_{tr} \;\approx\; 4481.1 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 4.48 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. B (a) Explain the term rolling resistance. Discuss the factors affecting it and derive its expression. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Rolling resistance is the force resisting the forward motion of a tire as it rolls on a road surface. It arises primarily due to **hysteresis energy loss** in the tire carcass during deformation and recovery.

**Factors Affecting Rolling Resistance:**
- Tire material and construction (radial vs bias, rubber compound)
- Tire inflation pressure (lower pressure increases $F_r$)
- Road surface texture and material
- Vehicle speed (mild increase with speed)
- Wheel load (linear in $W$)
- Temperature of the tire

**Derivation:**
From the asymmetric pressure distribution at the contact patch, the resultant horizontal force $F_r$ acts at a small moment arm. Moment equilibrium about the wheel center gives:

$$
F_r \cdot r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot W \cdot r \quad\Rightarrow\quad F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot W
$$

For an inclined road with $W = m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$:

$$
F_r \;=\; \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

**KTU Board Note:** Many students forget the $\cos\theta$ factor on inclines. Marks are lost in the slope-inclined sub-case.

---

**Q. B (b) A car of mass 1200 kg accelerates from rest at $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ on a level road. Compute the tractive force and power required after 8 seconds. Given: $\mu_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $\delta = 1.05$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $m = 1200 \text{ kg}$, $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$, level road ($\theta = 0$)
- $\mu_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $\delta = 1.05$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Velocity after 8 s:**

$$
V \;=\; u + a \cdot t \;=\; 0 + 2 \times 8 \;=\; 16 \text{ m/s} \quad(\approx 57.6 \text{ km/h})
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance:**

$$
F_r \;=\; 0.015 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times 1 \;=\; 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$
F_a \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times (16)^2
$$

$$
F_a \;\approx\; 100.35 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Grade Resistance:**

$$
F_g \;=\; 0 \text{ N} \quad (\text{level road})
$$

**Step 5 — Inertia Resistance:**

$$
F_i \;=\; m \cdot \delta \cdot a \;=\; 1200 \times 1.05 \times 2 \;=\; 2520 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Tractive Force:**

$$
F_{tr} \;=\; 176.58 + 100.35 + 0 + 2520 \;\approx\; 2796.93 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Tractive Power:**

$$
P \;=\; F_{tr} \cdot V \;=\; 2796.93 \times 16 \;\approx\; 44.75 \text{ kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in $F_r$** on inclined surfaces — costs 1 mark in part-(a) derivations.
> 2. **Using $V$ in km/h directly** in the drag equation — must convert to m/s. KTU examiners specifically check this unit-conversion.
> 3. **Omitting $\delta$ (mass factor)** when inertia resistance is asked — it is **not** simply $F_i = ma$; the rotational mass correction is mandatory.
> 4. **Mixing up $G$ and $\theta$** — students often substitute $G$ (in percent) directly into $\sin\theta$, giving wrong values. Use $\sin\theta \approx G/100$ (small-angle) only.
> 5. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU 2024 evaluation key explicitly deducts half-a-mark for missing SI units in numerical problems.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Card — Pin This Section**

- **Definition:** Tractive resistance is the cumulative opposing force on a vehicle — $F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$.
- **Rolling Resistance:** $F_r = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ — linear in load, dominant at low-medium speed.
- **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$ — quadratic in $V$, dominant above $\sim$80 km/h.
- **Grade Resistance:** $F_g = m g \sin\theta \approx m g (G/100)$ — present only on slopes; zero on level ground.
- **Inertia Resistance:** $F_i = m \cdot \delta \cdot a$ — $\delta \approx 1.03$–$1.30$ depending on drivetrain.
- **Tractive Power:** $P = F_{tr} \cdot V$ (Watts) when $V$ is in m/s.
- **Standard Values:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Regime Dominance:** Inertia (start) → Rolling (cruise low) → Aero (highway) → Grade (climb).
- **Equilibrium Rule:** $F_t > F_{tr}$ → accelerate, $F_t = F_{tr}$ → cruise, $F_t < F_{tr}$ → decelerate.
- **Unit Discipline:** Always N for force, W for power, m/s for velocity in the drag equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tractive Effort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tractive Effort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of vehicle dynamics and KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, **Tractive Effort (TE)** is defined as the **net longitudinal propulsive force** generated at the tire–road contact patch of the driving wheels, which is responsible for overcoming the various vehicle resistive forces and accelerating the vehicle mass. It is essentially the force transmitted from the powertrain through the driven wheels to the road surface.

Mathematically, tractive effort is the force exerted tangentially at the wheel–ground interface, directed opposite to the motion of the vehicle's resistance forces. It is fundamentally derived from the **engine torque**, scaled by the **transmission gear ratios**, reduced by **drivetrain losses**, and divided by the **effective rolling radius** of the driving wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tractive Effort is a foundational topic of Module 1 because it directly governs vehicle acceleration capability, grade climbing ability, and top-speed performance. Every subsequent module (braking, cornering, ride) ultimately refers back to this force balance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy, loaded shopping cart uphill. Your hands grip the handle, your legs apply force through your body to the wheels, and the wheels push backwards on the floor while the cart moves forward. The **force the wheels deliver to the floor** (and by Newton's Third Law, receive in the opposite direction) is conceptually identical to **Tractive Effort**.

In a car:
- The **engine** is your legs (the source of torque).
- The **gearbox + differential** is the lever that multiplies your effort.
- The **tires** are your hands (the contact patch with the ground).
- The **road** is the floor.

The force the tires push backward against the road is Tractive Effort. The road, by friction, pushes the car forward. If the tires slip (like shoes on wet ice), the engine can spin very fast but the cart barely moves — that means the *available* TE exceeds the *usable* TE limited by tire-road adhesion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Tractive Effort = "Push force at the wheels." Bigger gear ratio → bigger TE. Smaller wheel radius → bigger TE. More losses in the drivetrain → smaller TE.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Tractive Effort | $F_t$ or $TE$ | **N** (Newtons) or **kgf** |
| Engine Torque | $T_e$ | **N·m** |
| Overall Gear Ratio | $G$ | dimensionless |
| Transmission Efficiency | $\eta_t$ | dimensionless (0 < η ≤ 1) |
| Effective Rolling Radius | $r$ | **m** |
| Coefficient of Adhesion | $\mu$ | dimensionless |
| Vehicle Mass | $m$ | **kg** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU textbook problems, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ is used unless explicitly stated otherwise. Some numerical problems may use $g = 10 \, m/s^2$ for simplicity.

---

### 1.4 Vehicle Force Balance — The Governing Equation

The vehicle in straight-line motion must satisfy Newton's Second Law applied longitudinally:

$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a + m \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Tractive Effort at the driving wheels
- $F_r$ = Rolling Resistance Force
- $F_g$ = Grade (Hill Climb) Resistance Force
- $F_a$ = Aerodynamic Drag Force
- $m$ = Vehicle mass (or $m_{eff}$ including rotational inertia)
- $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ = Longitudinal acceleration

This equation is the **master equation** for longitudinal vehicle dynamics, and Tractive Effort is the **left-hand side driver** of the entire problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation of the Tractive Effort Equation

The tractive effort originates from the engine and travels through a sequence of mechanical elements before arriving at the road. Let us walk through the chain of mechanical advantage:

1. **Engine Torque Generation:** The internal combustion engine produces a torque $T_e$ at the crankshaft. This torque is a function of engine speed and throttle position (load).

2. **Transmission Multiplication:** The gearbox provides a ratio $i_g$, and the final drive (differential) provides a ratio $i_0$. The overall gear ratio is:
   $$G = i_g \times i_0$$

3. **Torque at the Driving Wheels (Ideal):** Without losses, the torque at each driving wheel would be:
   $$T_{w,ideal} = T_e \times G$$

4. **Drivetrain Losses:** Real drivetrains have friction losses in gears, bearings, and the differential. These are lumped into a transmission efficiency $\eta_t$. The actual torque at the wheels becomes:
   $$T_w = T_e \times G \times \eta_t$$

5. **Conversion of Wheel Torque to Tractive Force:** The wheel torque $T_w$ acts about the wheel center. At the tire-road contact patch, the lever arm is the effective rolling radius $r$. Therefore, the tangential force at the contact patch is:
   $$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{T_e \times G \times \eta_t}{r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Tractive Effort Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$\boxed{F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}}$$

---

### 2.2 Maximum Tractive Effort vs. Available Tractive Effort

A critical distinction in KTU problems is between two kinds of tractive effort:

- **Available Tractive Effort** ($F_{t,available}$): The force the powertrain *can deliver* based on engine torque, gear ratio, and efficiency. This is what we computed above.

- **Maximum Tractive Effort** ($F_{t,max}$): The force the tire-road friction *can sustain* before slipping occurs. This is adhesion-limited:
  $$F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot W_d$$
  Where $\mu$ is the coefficient of adhesion (longitudinal) and $W_d$ is the dynamic load on the driven wheels.

The actual tractive effort is the **minimum** of these two:
$$F_{t,actual} = \min(F_{t,available},\ F_{t,max})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** If the engine produces enormous torque in 1st gear (large $G$), $F_{t,available}$ can exceed $F_{t,max}$. The tire slips, the wheel spins, and the vehicle cannot accelerate further. This is why race cars have wide, sticky tires — to raise $F_{t,max}$.

---

### 2.3 Typical Values of Coefficient of Adhesion $\mu$

| Surface Condition | Dry μ | Wet μ | Icy μ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concrete / Asphalt | 0.85 – 1.0 | 0.50 – 0.70 | 0.10 – 0.25 |
| Gravel | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.40 – 0.55 | 0.10 |
| Snow | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.05 – 0.10 |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving Module 1 problems on Tractive Effort. Memorize these equations and their variable meanings.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ | Tractive Effort from engine torque | N |
| 2 | $G = i_g \times i_0$ | Overall gear ratio | — |
| 3 | $T_w = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t$ | Torque at the driving wheel | N·m |
| 4 | $F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot W_d$ | Maximum tractive effort (adhesion limit) | N |
| 5 | $F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a + m\,\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Longitudinal force balance (master equation) | N |
| 6 | $F_r = f_r \cdot mg\cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 7 | $F_g = mg\sin\theta$ | Grade resistance force | N |
| 8 | $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 9 | $m_{eff} = m\left(1 + \dfrac{I_w}{mr^2}\right)$ | Effective mass including wheel rotational inertia | kg |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$ | Vehicle speed from engine rpm | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** All absolute-value expressions in formulas are written using the open-style $\vert x \vert$ notation to keep the markdown table pipe character `|` separate from mathematical bars. This avoids table-parsing errors.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tractive effort concept is indispensable in:

- **Vehicle Performance Design:** Determining 0–100 km/h acceleration time, maximum grade (gradient in %) a vehicle can climb at constant speed, and top speed in each gear.
- **Railway Engineering:** Electric locomotives are designed so that their tractive effort-speed characteristic (a hyperbolic curve $F_t \cdot V = \text{const}$) matches the train's resistance curve for efficient operation.
- **Agricultural Tractors:** Heavy loads at low speeds demand maximum tractive effort — hence the use of large diameter rear wheels and dual-clutch systems.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** A single-speed transmission is feasible because electric motors deliver peak torque at zero rpm, providing enormous low-speed tractive effort naturally.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tractive Effort from Engine Torque

We begin with the engine producing a torque $T_e$. This torque is multiplied through the powertrain.

**Step 1: Torque transmission through the gearbox.**

The gearbox has a gear ratio $i_g$, which means the input torque is multiplied by this factor (neglecting losses for now). Output torque from gearbox:
$$T_{gb,out} = T_e \cdot i_g$$

**Step 2: Torque through the final drive (differential).**

The differential (final drive) has a ratio $i_0$. After this stage:
$$T_{fd,out} = T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 = T_e \cdot G$$

Where $G = i_g \cdot i_0$.

**Step 3: Accounting for transmission efficiency.**

The drivetrain is not 100% efficient. A fraction $\eta_t$ of the ideal torque reaches the wheels. Thus:
$$T_w = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\eta_t$ is multiplied (not divided) because it represents the *fraction of input power that reaches the output*. Since power = torque × angular velocity, and angular velocity is reduced by $G$, the torque is reduced by $\eta_t$ directly when input/output power conservation is applied.

**Step 4: Convert wheel torque to tangential force at the road.**

A wheel of effective rolling radius $r$ with applied torque $T_w$ generates a tangential force at the contact patch equal to the torque divided by the radius:
$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r}$$

**Step 5: Combine.**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:
$$\boxed{F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Grade Climbing Ability

Consider a vehicle climbing a slope of angle $\theta$ at constant velocity ($dV/dt = 0$). The master equation reduces to:
$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a$$

For low speeds (negligible aerodynamic drag) on a hard surface, $F_a \approx 0$. Also, $\cos\theta \approx 1$ for small angles.

Thus:
$$F_t \approx f_r \cdot mg + mg\sin\theta$$

Solving for $\sin\theta$:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_t}{mg} - f_r$$

Converting angle to **gradient percentage** ($G\% = \tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$ for small angles):
$$\boxed{G_{\%} = 100 \times \left(\frac{F_t}{mg} - f_r\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution)

> **Problem:** A passenger car has the following specifications:
> - Engine torque (max): $T_e = 200 \, N \cdot m$
> - 1st gear ratio: $i_{g1} = 3.5$
> - Final drive ratio: $i_0 = 3.7$
> - Transmission efficiency: $\eta_t = 0.9$
> - Effective wheel radius: $r = 0.32 \, m$
> - Vehicle mass: $m = 1500 \, kg$
> - Coefficient of rolling resistance: $f_r = 0.015$
> - Road gradient: $10\%$
>
> **Find:**
> (a) The maximum tractive effort available in 1st gear.
> (b) Whether the car can climb the 10% gradient at constant speed (neglect aerodynamic drag).

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum Tractive Effort in 1st Gear**

**Step 1:** Calculate the overall gear ratio.
$$G = i_{g1} \times i_0 = 3.5 \times 3.7 = 12.95$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the wheel torque.
$$T_w = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t = 200 \times 12.95 \times 0.9$$
$$T_w = 200 \times 11.655 = 2331 \, N \cdot m$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the tractive effort.
$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{2331}{0.32}$$
$$F_t = 7284.375 \, N$$

**(b) Can the car climb the 10% gradient?**

**Step 1:** Express gradient as an angle. For 10% gradient, $\tan\theta = 0.10$, so:
$$\theta = \arctan(0.10) = 5.7106^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.
$$\sin\theta = \sin(5.7106^\circ) \approx 0.0995$$
$$\cos\theta = \cos(5.7106^\circ) \approx 0.9950$$

**Step 3:** Calculate rolling resistance force.
$$F_r = f_r \cdot mg\cos\theta = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9950$$
$$F_r = 0.015 \times 14638.07 = 219.57 \, N$$

**Step 4:** Calculate grade resistance force.
$$F_g = mg\sin\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995$$
$$F_g = 1464.04 \, N$$

**Step 5:** Calculate total resistance.
$$F_{res} = F_r + F_g = 219.57 + 1464.04 = 1683.61 \, N$$

**Step 6:** Compare tractive effort with total resistance.
$$F_t = 7284.375 \, N \quad \gg \quad F_{res} = 1683.61 \, N$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the car can comfortably climb the 10% gradient in 1st gear. The surplus tractive force is:
$$F_{surplus} = 7284.375 - 1683.61 = 5600.77 \, N$$

This surplus will accelerate the vehicle up the slope (if not held at constant speed).

**Maximum achievable grade (theoretical):**
$$\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{F_t}{mg} - f_r = \frac{7284.375}{1500 \times 9.81} - 0.015$$
$$\sin\theta_{max} = 0.4950 - 0.015 = 0.4800$$
$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.4800) \approx 28.7^\circ$$
$$G_{\%,max} = \tan(28.7^\circ) \approx 0.5470 = 54.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world check:** Such steep theoretical grades are limited by **tire-pavement adhesion** and **tip-over angle**, not by available tractive effort. The actual achievable grade is governed by $\mu \geq \tan\theta$, requiring $\mu \geq 0.55$ for $\theta \approx 28.7°$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Tractive Force Generation Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the flow of mechanical energy from fuel combustion to road force. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, not a physical drawing, but accurately captures the signal-flow logic of the powertrain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL COMBUSTION] --> B[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT]
    B --> C[ENGINE TORQUE Te]
    C --> D[CLUTCH COUPLING]
    D --> E[GEARBOX RATIO ig]
    E --> F[FINAL DRIVE RATIO i0]
    F --> G[DRIVETRAIN EFFICIENCY FACTOR]
    G --> H[WHEEL TORQUE Tw]
    H --> I[EFFECTIVE ROLLING RADIUS r]
    I --> J[TRACTIVE EFFORT Ft]
    J --> K[VEHICLE LONGITUDINAL FORCE BALANCE]
    K --> L[ROLLING RESISTANCE Fr]
    K --> M[GRADE RESISTANCE Fg]
    K --> N[AERO DRAG Fa]
    K --> O[INERTIAL FORCE ma]
    L --> P[VEHICLE MOTION]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Actual vs. Maximum Tractive Effort

This diagram captures the logical decision-making process when the actual tractive effort is selected by the minimum of the available and the adhesion-limited values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Compute Available TE] --> B[Ft_avail = Te x G x nt / r]
    C[Compute Maximum TE from adhesion] --> D[Ft_max = mu x Wd]
    B --> E{Compare Ft_avail vs Ft_max}
    D --> E
    E -->|Ft_avail < Ft_max| F[Actual TE = Ft_avail]
    E -->|Ft_avail >= Ft_max| G[Actual TE = Ft_max]
    F --> H[VEHICLE ACCELERATES PER MASTERS EQUATION]
    G --> I[TIRE SLIPS WHEEL SPIN]
    I --> J[NO FURTHER ACCELERATION]
    H --> K[END]
    J --> K
```

---

### 4.3 Force Diagram at the Driven Wheel — Free Body Representation

The following ASCII schematic shows a driven wheel with all longitudinal forces acting at the contact patch. Use this when reasoning about force balance.

```
              W (Vertical Load on wheel)
                    |
                    |
            ________V________
           /                  \
          /     WHEEL  r       \
         |        O             |  ----->  Ft  (Tractive effort, forward)
         |       /|\            |  <-----  Fr  (Rolling resistance, backward)
          \   T (Wheel torque) /
           \__________________/
                    |
                    |
                ROAD CONTACT PATCH
                
   Net longitudinal force on vehicle = Ft - Fr - Fg - Fa = m*a
```

**Interpretation:**
- $T$ is the wheel torque applied by the half-shaft.
- $F_t$ is the forward tractive effort (reaction from road on tire).
- $F_r$ is the backward rolling resistance at the tire-road interface.
- $W$ is the normal reaction from the road.
- The net force on the vehicle body is the algebraic sum of $F_t$, $F_r$, $F_g$, and $F_a$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Tractive Effort. State its mathematical expression in terms of engine torque.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Tractive Effort is the net longitudinal propulsive force available at the tire-road contact patch of the driving wheels of a vehicle. It is the force responsible for overcoming resistive forces (rolling, grade, aerodynamic) and for accelerating the vehicle. **(1.5 Marks)**

Mathematically:
$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$

where $T_e$ is engine torque, $G = i_g \cdot i_0$ is the overall gear ratio, $\eta_t$ is the transmission efficiency, and $r$ is the effective wheel rolling radius. **(1.5 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q2. Distinguish between "Available Tractive Effort" and "Maximum Tractive Effort." Why is this distinction important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Available Tractive Effort** is the force the powertrain can deliver at the wheel-road interface based on engine torque, gear ratio, and drivetrain efficiency. **(1 Mark)**
- **Maximum Tractive Effort** is the upper limit imposed by the tire-road friction (adhesion), given by $F_{t,max} = \mu W_d$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of adhesion and $W_d$ is the dynamic wheel load. **(1 Mark)**
- The actual tractive effort is the **minimum** of the two. If available TE exceeds maximum TE, the tire slips, wasting engine power and producing no acceleration. This distinction is important because it determines the actual acceleration and traction limits of a vehicle. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the tractive effort of a vehicle in terms of engine torque, gear ratios, transmission efficiency, and wheel radius. State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1800 kg** has an engine that produces a maximum torque of **250 N·m**. The first gear ratio is **3.2**, the final drive ratio is **4.1**, and the transmission efficiency is **88%**. The wheel radius is **0.33 m**. The coefficient of adhesion on dry asphalt is **0.85**, and 60% of the vehicle weight is on the driven wheels. Determine:
- (i) The available tractive effort in 1st gear.
- (ii) The maximum tractive effort limited by adhesion.
- (iii) The maximum grade the vehicle can climb at constant speed, assuming rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.018$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The engine produces a torque $T_e$ at the crankshaft. **(1 Mark)**

This torque is multiplied by the gearbox ratio $i_g$, giving output torque $T_{gb} = T_e \cdot i_g$. **(1 Mark)**

The gearbox output passes through the final drive with ratio $i_0$, giving $T_{fd} = T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 = T_e \cdot G$, where $G$ is the overall gear ratio. **(1 Mark)**

Accounting for transmission efficiency $\eta_t$ (which represents power losses in gears, bearings, and differential), the actual torque at the driving wheels is:
$$T_w = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

The wheel torque $T_w$ acts at a lever arm of the effective rolling radius $r$. The tangential force at the tire-road contact patch is:
$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**Meanings:**
- $T_e$: Engine torque (N·m)
- $G = i_g \cdot i_0$: Overall gear ratio (dimensionless)
- $\eta_t$: Transmission efficiency (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $r$: Effective rolling radius of the tire (m) **(1 Mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1800 \, kg$
- $T_e = 250 \, N \cdot m$
- $i_{g1} = 3.2$
- $i_0 = 4.1$
- $\eta_t = 0.88$
- $r = 0.33 \, m$
- $\mu = 0.85$
- Weight distribution on driven wheels: 60%
- $f_r = 0.018$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Part (i): Available Tractive Effort (2 Marks)**

Overall gear ratio:
$$G = i_{g1} \times i_0 = 3.2 \times 4.1 = 13.12$$

Wheel torque:
$$T_w = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t = 250 \times 13.12 \times 0.88 = 2886.4 \, N \cdot m$$

Available tractive effort:
$$F_{t,avail} = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{2886.4}{0.33} = 8746.67 \, N$$

---

**Part (ii): Maximum Tractive Effort (2 Marks)**

Dynamic load on driven wheels:
$$W_d = 0.60 \times mg = 0.60 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 10594.8 \, N$$

Maximum tractive effort:
$$F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot W_d = 0.85 \times 10594.8 = 9005.58 \, N$$

---

**Part (iii): Maximum Grade (3 Marks)**

Since $F_{t,avail} = 8746.67 \, N < F_{t,max} = 9005.58 \, N$, the powertrain is the limiting factor, so we use $F_t = 8746.67 \, N$. **(0.5 Mark)**

At constant speed on a slope of angle $\theta$, force balance gives:
$$F_t = f_r \cdot mg\cos\theta + mg\sin\theta$$

Dividing through by $mg$:
$$\frac{F_t}{mg} = f_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta$$

$$\frac{8746.67}{1800 \times 9.81} = 0.018\cos\theta + \sin\theta$$

$$0.4952 = 0.018\cos\theta + \sin\theta$$

Solving numerically (using $\theta \approx 28°$ as initial guess):
- $\sin(28°) \approx 0.4695$
- $\cos(28°) \approx 0.8829$
- RHS = $0.018 \times 0.8829 + 0.4695 = 0.0159 + 0.4695 = 0.4854$ (slightly low)

- $\sin(28.5°) \approx 0.4772$
- $\cos(28.5°) \approx 0.8788$
- RHS = $0.018 \times 0.8788 + 0.4772 = 0.0158 + 0.4772 = 0.4930$

- $\sin(28.7°) \approx 0.4800$
- $\cos(28.7°) \approx 0.8772$
- RHS = $0.018 \times 0.8772 + 0.4800 = 0.0158 + 0.4800 = 0.4958$ ✓ **(2 Marks)**

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} \approx 28.7^\circ, \quad G_{\%} \approx \tan(28.7^\circ) \approx 54.7\%}$$ **(0.5 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the longitudinal force balance acting on a vehicle moving up an incline. List and define all the resistive forces acting on the vehicle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A truck of mass **5000 kg** moves at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a level road. The tractive effort required to overcome resistances is found to be **3500 N**. The engine runs at **1800 rpm**, the gearbox ratio in this gear is **2.8**, the final drive ratio is **5.0**, and the overall efficiency is **85%**. Calculate:
- (i) The effective rolling radius of the tires.
- (ii) The engine torque being delivered.
- (iii) If the truck now ascends a **5% gradient** at the same speed, what additional tractive effort is required? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Longitudinal Force Balance Diagram and Resistive Forces (7 Marks):**

A vehicle climbing an incline of angle $\theta$ experiences the following longitudinal forces (refer to the force diagram in Section 4.3 for visual aid): **(2 Marks)**

1. **Tractive Effort ($F_t$):** Acts forward along the direction of motion at the driving wheel contact patch.

2. **Rolling Resistance Force ($F_r$):** Force opposing motion due to tire deformation and road surface friction. Expressed as $F_r = f_r \cdot mg\cos\theta$. **(1 Mark)**

3. **Grade Resistance Force ($F_g$):** Component of vehicle weight acting down the slope. Expressed as $F_g = mg\sin\theta$. **(1 Mark)**

4. **Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_a$):** Force due to air resistance. Expressed as $F_a = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$. **(1 Mark)**

5. **Inertial Force ($m \cdot a$):** Force required to accelerate the vehicle mass, applicable when speed is changing. **(1 Mark)**

The master equation is:
$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a + ma \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 5000 \, kg$
- $V = 60 \, km/h = 16.667 \, m/s$
- $F_t = 3500 \, N$ (level road)
- $N = 1800 \, rpm$
- $i_g = 2.8$
- $i_0 = 5.0$
- $\eta_t = 0.85$
- New gradient: 5%

**Part (i): Effective Rolling Radius (3 Marks)**

The vehicle speed in terms of engine rpm:
$$V = \frac{2\pi N r}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$$

Solving for $r$:
$$r = \frac{V \cdot 60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{2\pi N}$$

$$r = \frac{16.667 \times 60 \times 2.8 \times 5.0}{2\pi \times 1800}$$

$$r = \frac{14000.04}{11309.73}$$

$$r \approx 1.238 \, m \quad \textbf{(1 Mark for setup, 1 Mark for substitution, 1 Mark for final answer)}$$

---

**Part (ii): Engine Torque (2 Marks)**

From the tractive effort formula:
$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$

$$T_e = \frac{F_t \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{3500 \times 1.238}{14.0 \times 0.85}$$

$$T_e = \frac{4333}{11.9} \approx 364.12 \, N \cdot m$$

---

**Part (iii): Additional Tractive Effort for 5% Gradient (2 Marks)**

For a 5% gradient: $\tan\theta = 0.05$, so $\theta \approx 2.862°$.
- $\sin\theta \approx 0.0499$
- $\cos\theta \approx 0.9988$

Assuming rolling resistance coefficient remains similar and aerodynamic drag remains unchanged (since speed is the same), the **additional** grade resistance force is:
$$\Delta F_g = mg\sin\theta = 5000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0499 = 2447.6 \, N$$

$$\boxed{\Delta F_t \approx 2447.6 \, N}$$

(The actual total increase in tractive effort required is approximately 2448 N.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in Tractive Effort problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply** $i_g$ and $i_0$ to get the overall gear ratio $G$. Many students mistakenly use $i_g$ alone. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $\eta_t$ as a divisor** instead of a multiplier. The transmission efficiency reduces torque, so it multiplies the ideal torque. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Ignoring the difference between available and maximum tractive effort.** Students often compute only one and proceed. Always compute both and select the minimum. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Unit errors in $N$ vs $N \cdot m$:** Confusing torque units with force units. Always check that $r$ is in meters (not mm or cm). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not converting km/h to m/s** in speed-related calculations. KTU expects SI units consistently. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Ignoring weight transfer** when computing $W_d$. Always state whether the problem gives front/rear distribution or total weight.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tractive Effort**
> - **Definition:** $F_t = T_e \cdot G \cdot \eta_t / r$ — the propulsive force at the tire-road contact patch.
> - **Overall gear ratio:** $G = i_g \cdot i_0$. Always use the product, never one alone.
> - **Maximum TE (adhesion limit):** $F_{t,max} = \mu W_d$.
> - **Actual TE:** $\min(F_{t,available}, F_{t,max})$.
> - **Master equation:** $F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a + ma$.
> - **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = f_r mg\cos\theta$, with $f_r \approx 0.013$–$0.02$ for cars on asphalt.
> - **Grade resistance:** $F_g = mg\sin\theta$. For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G\%/100$.
> - **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_a = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$, with $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$.
> - **Speed–engine rpm relation:** $V = 2\pi N r / (60 \, i_g i_0)$. Convert km/h → m/s for SI consistency.
> - **Effective mass:** Use $m_{eff}$ (including rotational inertia of wheels) for highly accurate acceleration calculations.
> - **Real-world limitations:** Tire slip (adhesion), drivetrain losses, and rolling resistance all reduce usable tractive effort.
> - **Units reminder:** Torque in N·m, force in N, radius in m, speed in m/s, mass in kg.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Required for Propulsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Required for Propulsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Required for Propulsion** of a road vehicle is defined as the *rate at which mechanical work must be supplied at the wheel–road contact patch* to overcome the various resistances acting on a moving vehicle and to provide the desired acceleration. Mathematically, it is the scalar dot product of the **Tractive Force (F_t)** at the driving wheels and the **Instantaneous Vehicle Velocity (v)** along the direction of motion.

In KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, the total propulsion power requirement is the algebraic sum of the instantaneous powers dissipated against:
1. **Rolling Resistance** (P_rr)
2. **Aerodynamic Drag** (P_ad)
3. **Gradient (Climbing) Resistance** (P_gr)
4. **Inertial / Acceleration Resistance** (P_acc)

$$P_{propulsion} = F_t \cdot v = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{gr} + F_{acc}) \cdot v$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The propulsion power is conventionally expressed at the **wheels (P_wheel)**. To find the power demanded from the **engine (P_engine)**, one must divide P_wheel by the *overall drivetrain efficiency (η_d)* which accounts for losses in the transmission, differential, and final drive.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Commuter Cyclist"**
> Imagine you are cycling to your KTU college at **15 km/h** on a flat road with a mild headwind. You feel a constant, light push-back — that is the **rolling resistance + aerodynamic drag** demanding a steady "trickle" of leg power (this is why P_rr grows linearly with *v* and P_ad grows with *v³*).
> Now you hit an overbridge slope of **5 degrees**. Suddenly your legs burn — the **gradient resistance** demands additional power scaling with *m·g·sinθ·v*.
> Just as you crest the bridge, you sprint to beat a red signal. The explosive leg burst needed is the **acceleration power (m·a·v)**.
> Propulsion power analysis simply *quantifies* these four sensations as four exact equations on paper. The total power your legs must produce = the sum of all four. Your body (engine) supplies this power, but some energy is "wasted" warming up your joints (drivetrain loss), so the engine (heart/lungs) must actually generate *more* power than reaches the pedals.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Air density (sea level, 25°C) | **ρ** | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | **μ or f_r** | **0.008 – 0.015** | dimensionless |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient | **C_d** | **0.25 – 0.45** | dimensionless |
| Frontal projected area | **A** | **1.8 – 2.5** | m² |
| Overall drivetrain efficiency | **η_d** | **0.85 – 0.92** | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Resistive Forces — Logic Decomposition

A vehicle moving on a road experiences **four distinct resistive loads** that the engine must continuously overcome. Each one is derived from a different physical phenomenon, and hence has a *unique velocity dependence*.

### A. Rolling Resistance Force (F_rr)
- **Physical Origin:** Hysteresis loss in the tyre rubber as it deforms under load, plus friction at the wheel bearings and minor losses at the tyre–road interface.
- **Engineering Approximation:** For a pneumatic tyre on a hard, dry surface, the force is empirically linear in the normal load:
$$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$
- **Why does it depend on cosθ?** Because on an incline of angle θ, the *normal reaction* on the tyres reduces to *mg·cosθ*. Only the normal component causes tyre deformation.
- **Velocity dependence:** Almost linear with *v* (P_rr ∝ v). At highway speeds, advanced low-rolling-resistance tyres (μ ≈ 0.006) become critical for EV range.

### B. Aerodynamic Drag Force (F_ad)
- **Physical Origin:** Pressure differential (form drag) and viscous shear (skin friction) acting on the vehicle body as it pushes air aside.
- **Engineering Model:** The classical **1/2 ρ C_d A v²** formula derived from dimensional analysis of fluid momentum flux.
$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$$
- **Why v²?** Drag is a *momentum-flux* phenomenon: doubling speed means twice the air mass encountered per second *and* twice the velocity change imparted — hence v².
- **Critical Insight:** Because power = Force × velocity, **P_ad ∝ v³**. This is the single most important reason why highway fuel economy plummets above 80 km/h — *a mere 20% increase in speed nearly doubles the aerodynamic power demand*.

### C. Gradient (Climbing) Resistance Force (F_gr)
- **Physical Origin:** The component of gravitational weight vector acting *backwards* along an inclined road of slope angle θ.
$$F_{gr} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$
- **Engineering Reality:** Road gradients are usually expressed as **percentage grade** (e.g., 5% means tan θ = 0.05, so θ ≈ 2.86°). The sinθ vs tanθ distinction is critical in KTU numericals.
- **Velocity dependence:** Linear (P_gr ∝ v). For EVs and hybrids, *regenerative braking* can recover a portion of this energy on downhills.

### D. Acceleration (Inertial) Resistance Force (F_acc)
- **Physical Origin:** Newton's Second Law — force required to change the linear momentum of the vehicle mass, plus the rotational inertia of the engine, transmission and wheels (captured via *mass factor δ*).
$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a + I_{rot}\,eq$$
$$F_{acc} = m(1 + \varepsilon)\,\frac{dv}{dt} + \delta\,m\,\frac{dv}{dt} \equiv \delta\,m\,a$$
- **Where δ (mass factor):** Typically 1.05 – 1.15 for passenger cars. KTU problems often set δ = 1 for simplification.
- **Velocity dependence:** Linear (P_acc ∝ v). At zero velocity (launch), F_acc is maximum but power is zero. The peak power during acceleration occurs at mid-range RPM of the engine.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Matrix — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Unit | Velocity Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr}$ | $f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\theta$ | N | Constant w.r.t v |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_{ad}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$ | N | ∝ v² |
| 3 | Gradient Resistance Force | $F_{gr}$ | $m \, g \, \sin\theta$ | N | Constant w.r.t v |
| 4 | Acceleration Resistance | $F_{acc}$ | $\delta \, m \, a$ | N | Constant w.r.t v |
| 5 | **Total Tractive Force** | $F_t$ | $F_{rr}+F_{ad}+F_{gr}+F_{acc}$ | N | Composite |
| 6 | Power to Overcome F_rr | $P_{rr}$ | $f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\theta \, v$ | W (J/s) | ∝ v |
| 7 | Power to Overcome F_ad | $P_{ad}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^3$ | W | ∝ v³ |
| 8 | Power to Overcome F_gr | $P_{gr}$ | $m \, g \, \sin\theta \, v$ | W | ∝ v |
| 9 | Power to Overcome F_acc | $P_{acc}$ | $\delta \, m \, a \, v$ | W | ∝ v |
| 10 | **Total Wheel Power** | $P_{wheel}$ | $\sum P_i$ | W | Composite |
| 11 | Engine Power Output | $P_{engine}$ | $P_{wheel} \,/\, \eta_d$ | W | Composite |
| 12 | Total Resistance Force | $F_{res}$ | $F_t$ | N | Composite |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Estimation:** Total energy consumed per km = ∫ (F_t · v) dt = ∫ F_t ds. Knowing the v³ dependence of P_ad is why EVs achieve near-rated range only in city traffic, not highways.
- **Powertrain Sizing:** A manufacturer designing a 1500 kg sedan for a 200 km/h top speed must size the engine such that P_engine(v_max) ≥ P_total(v_max). This gives the *minimum engine rating*.
- **Fuel Economy Optimization:** Cruise control algorithms maintain the velocity at which **dP/dv** is minimum (the *sweet spot*), typically 50–70 km/h for most cars.
- **Gradeability:** Maximum climb angle θ_max occurs when *F_engine_max = F_rr + F_gr + F_ad*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Power Required to Overcome Aerodynamic Drag (P_ad)

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Newton's Momentum-Flux Argument**
Consider a control volume moving with the vehicle. The rate at which the vehicle imparts momentum to the air it displaces is the fundamental origin of pressure drag.

$$F_{ad} = \text{(mass flow rate)} \times \text{(velocity change)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Mass Flow Rate of Air Encountered**
The vehicle sweeps through a frontal area *A* at velocity *v*, displacing air of density *ρ*. The mass of air encountered per second is:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Change Imparted to the Air**
For a streamlined bluff body, the air is approximately brought from *v* to rest relative to the vehicle. The fraction of momentum transferred is captured by the drag coefficient *C_d*:

$$\Delta v_{eff} = C_d \cdot v$$

**Step 4 — Combine to Get Drag Force**

$$F_{ad} = \rho \, A \, v \cdot (C_d \, v) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2$$

> *Note:* The factor of ½ arises from the proper definition of *C_d*, which is referenced against the **dynamic pressure** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.

**Step 5 — Multiply by Velocity to Obtain Power**

$$\boxed{P_{ad} = F_{ad} \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^3}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Style)**
For a typical sedan: ρ = **1.225 kg/m³**, C_d = **0.32**, A = **2.2 m²**, v = **100 km/h = 27.78 m/s**.

$$P_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^3$$

$$P_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times 21432.5$$

$$P_{ad} = 9237.6 \text{ W} \approx 9.24 \text{ kW}$$

This single calculation proves that at highway speeds, *aerodynamic drag alone* demands nearly 10 kW continuously — roughly the output of a small motorcycle engine.

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Propulsion Power

**Step 1 — Tractive Effort Equation (Free Body Diagram Approach)**
Applying Newton's second law along the road direction:

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = \delta \, m \, a$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Required Tractive Force**

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{gr} + \delta \, m \, a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Four Component Forces**

$$F_t = f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2 + m \, g \, \sin\theta + \delta \, m \, a$$

**Step 4 — Multiply Both Sides by *v* to Get Power**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$$

$$P_{wheel} = f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\theta \, v + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^3 + m \, g \, \sin\theta \, v + \delta \, m \, a \, v$$

**Step 5 — Engine Power Including Drivetrain Losses**

$$P_{engine} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_d}$$

**Step 6 — Special Case Analysis (Frequently Asked in KTU)**

| Driving Scenario | Conditions | Simplified Power Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Constant speed, flat road | a = 0, θ = 0 | $P = f_r \, m \, g \, v + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^3$ |
| Constant speed, gradient θ | a = 0 | $P = (f_r m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta) v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ |
| Acceleration on flat road | θ = 0 | $P = f_r m g v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3 + \delta m a v$ |
| Quasi-steady cruise | a ≈ 0, θ = 0 | $P \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ (high v regime) |
| Quasi-steady creep | v ≈ 0, θ = 0 | $P \approx 0$ (rolling negligible at standstill) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU Model Problem)

**Problem:** A vehicle of mass **m = 1500 kg** with frontal area **A = 2.0 m²**, drag coefficient **C_d = 0.30**, and rolling resistance coefficient **f_r = 0.012** is cruising on a level road at **v = 90 km/h**. Take **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³**, **g = 9.81 m/s²**, **η_d = 0.90**.

**Step 1 — Convert Units**
$v = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25 \text{ m/s}$, $\theta = 0$, $a = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute Each Power Component**

$P_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0) \times 25$

$P_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 \times 25 = 4414.5 \text{ W}$

$P_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 \times (25)^3$

$P_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 \times 15625 = 5625 \text{ W}$

$P_{gr} = 0$ (level road), $P_{acc} = 0$ (cruise).

**Step 3 — Total Wheel Power**

$P_{wheel} = 4414.5 + 5625 + 0 + 0 = 10039.5 \text{ W} \approx 10.04 \text{ kW}$

**Step 4 — Engine Power Output**

$$P_{engine} = \frac{10039.5}{0.90} = 11155 \text{ W} \approx 11.16 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
At just 90 km/h on a flat road, the engine must deliver over **11 kW** to maintain speed. Observe how the aerodynamic component (5.6 kW) already exceeds rolling resistance (4.4 kW). At **v = 120 km/h**, the aerodynamic term alone would scale by $(120/90)^3 = 2.37$, jumping to 13.3 kW — a striking demonstration of the *cubic penalty*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Propulsion Power Flow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates how the chemical/electrical energy of the engine is converted, transmitted, dissipated, and finally delivered as tractive power at the wheel contact patch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FUEL TANK or BATTERY]:::source -->|Energy In| nodeB[ENGINE or MOTOR]
    nodeB -->|P_engine| nodeC[TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
    nodeC -->|P_trans| nodeD[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    nodeD -->|P_shaft| nodeE[DIFFERENTIAL and FINAL DRIVE]
    nodeE -->|P_wheel| nodeF[DRIVE WHEELS]
    nodeF -->|F_t at v| nodeG[ROAD CONTACT PATCH]
    nodeG -->|Used to overcome| nodeH[ROLLING RESISTANCE]
    nodeG -->|Used to overcome| nodeI[AERODYNAMIC DRAG]
    nodeG -->|Used to overcome| nodeJ[GRADIENT RESISTANCE]
    nodeG -->|Used to overcome| nodeK[ACCELERATION RESISTANCE]
    classDef source fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

## 4.2 Power Balance Sankey-Style Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[P_wheel at Contact Patch]:::power -->|P_rr fraction| nodeB[P_rr Rolling]
    nodeA -->|P_ad fraction| nodeC[P_ad Aerodynamic]
    nodeA -->|P_gr fraction| nodeD[P_gr Gradient]
    nodeA -->|P_acc fraction| nodeE[P_acc Inertial]
    nodeF[P_engine at Flywheel]:::power -->|Divided by eta_d| nodeA
    nodeF -->|1 minus eta_d fraction| nodeG[DRIVETRAIN LOSSES heat and noise]
    classDef power fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

## 4.3 Velocity-vs-Power Contribution Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[LOW SPEED CITY 0 to 30 km/h]:::low -->|Dominant| nodeB[Rolling P_rr dominates]
    nodeC[MEDIUM SPEED 30 to 80 km/h]:::med -->|Balanced| nodeD[P_rr approx P_ad crossover]
    nodeE[HIGH SPEED 80 to 200 km/h]:::high -->|Dominant| nodeF[Aerodynamic P_ad dominates]
    classDef low fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#52c41a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef med fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#faad14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef high fill:#fff1f0,stroke:#f5222d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

## 4.4 System-Level Schematic (Text-Style Block Diagram)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                VEHICLE PROPULSION POWER PIPELINE                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

   ┌────────────┐    η_d     ┌────────────┐     v (m/s)     ┌────────────┐
   │  P_engine  │ ────────► │  P_wheel   │ ──────────────► │   P_use    │
   │  (kW)      │  losses   │  (kW)      │   tractive      │   (kW)     │
   └────────────┘           └────────────┘   effort x v     └────────────┘
                                                                │
                                  ┌─────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┐
                                  │                             │                             │
                                  ▼                             ▼                             ▼
                           ┌─────────────┐              ┌─────────────┐              ┌─────────────┐
                           │ P_rr ∝ v    │              │ P_ad ∝ v³   │              │ P_gr ∝ v    │
                           │ (Tyre Hys.) │              │ (Air Drag)  │              │ (Gravity)   │
                           └─────────────┘              └─────────────┘              └─────────────┘
                                  │                             │                             │
                                  └─────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┘
                                                                │
                                                                ▼
                                                       ┌────────────────┐
                                                       │ P_acc ∝ v      │
                                                       │ (Inertia)      │
                                                       └────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Power required for propulsion" of a road vehicle. List the four major resistive forces that must be overcome.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Power required for propulsion is defined as the rate at which mechanical work must be supplied at the driving wheels to maintain a vehicle's motion against resistive forces and to provide the desired acceleration. Mathematically, it equals the dot product of the tractive force *F_t* at the wheel and the vehicle velocity *v*. (1.5 marks)

The four major resistive forces are:
1. Rolling resistance (tyre hysteresis)
2. Aerodynamic drag (air resistance)
3. Gradient resistance (gravity component on slope)
4. Acceleration resistance (inertial force from mass and rotational inertia) (1.5 marks)

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why does aerodynamic drag power demand vary as the cube of velocity, while rolling resistance power varies only linearly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Aerodynamic drag force itself varies as *v²* (from momentum-flux theory), and since power = force × velocity, the drag power demand scales as **v × v² = v³**. (1.5 marks)

Rolling resistance force is, to a first approximation, **independent of velocity** (it depends on normal load and tyre hysteresis), hence the power to overcome rolling resistance is P = F_rr × v, which scales **linearly with v**. (1.5 marks)

This cubic dependence is why aerodynamic losses become dominant at highway speeds, while rolling resistance dominates at low city speeds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Students answer either Question A OR Question B. Each carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**A car of mass 1400 kg has a frontal area of 2.0 m² and a drag coefficient of 0.32. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.014. The drivetrain efficiency is 0.88. Determine:**

**(a)** The total tractive force and engine power required to maintain a constant speed of **100 km/h** on a **level road**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the same car now climbs a **6% gradient** at the same constant speed, compute the additional power required and the new total engine power. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
m = 1400 kg, A = 2.0 m², C_d = 0.32, f_r = 0.014, η_d = 0.88, v = 100 km/h, ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Convert velocity:** v = 100 × 5/18 = **27.78 m/s**.

**Gradient angle:** tan θ = 0.06 ⇒ θ = tan⁻¹(0.06) = 3.434°. sin θ = 0.0599, cos θ = 0.9982.

---

**(a) Solution on Level Road (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force:**
$$F_{rr} = f_r \, m \, g = 0.014 \times 1400 \times 9.81 = 192.27 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag Force:**
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A \times v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times (27.78)^2$$
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times 771.6 = 296.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Gradient Force on level road:**
$$F_{gr} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Total Tractive Force:**
$$F_t = 192.27 + 296.3 + 0 = 488.57 \text{ N} \approx 488.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Wheel Power:**
$$P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 488.6 \times 27.78 = 13573.3 \text{ W} \approx 13.57 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Engine Power:**
$$P_{engine} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_d} = \frac{13573.3}{0.88} = 15424.2 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{15.42 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**(b) Solution on 6% Gradient (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force (note cos θ correction):**
$$F_{rr} = f_r \, m \, g \, \cos\theta = 0.014 \times 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.9982 = 191.91 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag Force (unchanged):**
$$F_{ad} = 296.3 \text{ N (same as before)}$$

**Step 3 — Gradient Force:**
$$F_{gr} = m \, g \, \sin\theta = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.0599 = 822.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — New Total Tractive Force:**
$$F_t = 191.91 + 296.3 + 822.7 = 1310.91 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — New Wheel Power:**
$$P_{wheel} = 1310.91 \times 27.78 = 36417.1 \text{ W} \approx 36.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — New Engine Power:**
$$P_{engine} = \frac{36417.1}{0.88} = 41383.1 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{41.38 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 7 — Additional Power Required:**
$$\Delta P = 41383.1 - 15424.2 = \mathbf{25958.9 \text{ W} \approx 25.96 \text{ kW}}$$

**Observation:** A modest 6% grade nearly **triples** the engine power demand. This is why hills drastically affect real-world fuel economy.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]

**A sports car of mass 1800 kg has a frontal area of 2.2 m², drag coefficient 0.28, and rolling resistance coefficient 0.010. The drivetrain efficiency is 0.90. Determine:**

**(a)** The engine power required for the car to **accelerate uniformly from 0 to 100 km/h in 10 seconds** on a **level road**. Assume the mass factor δ = 1.05 and neglect aerodynamic drag during the initial phase but include it for the final calculation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The maximum constant speed the car can sustain on a level road if its maximum engine power output is **150 kW**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
m = 1800 kg, A = 2.2 m², C_d = 0.28, f_r = 0.010, η_d = 0.90, g = 9.81 m/s², ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, δ = 1.05.

---

**(a) Acceleration Phase Power (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Average Acceleration:**
$$v_f = 100 \text{ km/h} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}, \quad a_{avg} = \frac{27.78 - 0}{10} = 2.778 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Force at Average Speed (≈ v/2 = 13.89 m/s):**
$$F_{rr} = f_r \, m \, g = 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic Drag at Final Speed v = 27.78 m/s:**
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 771.6 = 285.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration Force at Final Speed:**
$$F_{acc} = \delta \, m \, a = 1.05 \times 1800 \times 2.778 = 5249.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Total Force at v = 27.78 m/s:**
$$F_t = 176.58 + 285.1 + 5249.8 = 5711.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Wheel Power at v = 27.78 m/s:**
$$P_{wheel} = 5711.5 \times 27.78 = 158647 \text{ W} \approx 158.65 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Engine Power:**
$$P_{engine} = \frac{158647}{0.90} = \mathbf{176.27 \text{ kW}}$$

This is the *peak* engine power needed at the end of the 0–100 km/h sprint.

---

**(b) Maximum Constant Speed (7 marks):**

At maximum speed, acceleration = 0 and gradient = 0. Power equation reduces to:

$$P_{wheel} = f_r \, m \, g \, v + 0.5 \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^3$$

Using P_engine = 150 kW and η_d = 0.90:
$$P_{wheel} = 150 \times 0.90 = 135 \text{ kW} = 135000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute:**
$$135000 = 176.58 \cdot v + 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \cdot v^3$$
$$135000 = 176.58 \, v + 0.3696 \, v^3$$

**Step 2 — Solve the Cubic Equation by Iteration:**
At v = 65 m/s: P = 176.58 × 65 + 0.3696 × 65³ = 11478 + 101525 = 113003 W (too low)
At v = 70 m/s: P = 176.58 × 70 + 0.3696 × 70³ = 12361 + 126787 = 139148 W (too high)
