At v = 69 m/s: P = 176.58 × 69 + 0.3696 × 69³ = 12184 + 121547 = 133731 W (close)
At v = 69.2 m/s: P ≈ 134500 W (very close)

**Step 3 — Final Answer:**
$$v_{max} \approx 69.2 \text{ m/s} = 69.2 \times 3.6 = \mathbf{249.1 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Observation:** Without the 150 kW limit, the car could theoretically go much faster — but the cubic drag term caps the practical top speed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing km/h with m/s in v² or v³ terms. **Always convert to m/s first** — losing this gives zero credit for the entire numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting the drivetrain efficiency factor:** Power at wheels ≠ power at engine. Examiners check this with 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing *cos θ* and *sin θ*:** Rolling resistance uses **cos θ**, gradient uses **sin θ**. Mixing them is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Neglecting the mass factor δ** in acceleration problems when explicitly given.
> 5. **Drawing free body diagrams without arrow directions** on F_rr, F_ad, F_gr — examiners expect clear *opposing-to-motion* arrows.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read the Night Before KTU Exam.**

- ✅ **Power for propulsion = Tractive Force × Velocity** (F_t · v), expressed in Watts.
- ✅ Four resistive forces: **F_rr, F_ad, F_gr, F_acc** — all summed linearly.
- ✅ **F_rr = f_r · m · g · cos θ** → force is constant; **P_rr ∝ v**.
- ✅ **F_ad = 0.5 ρ C_d A v²** → force ∝ v²; **P_ad ∝ v³** (THE most important scaling law).
- ✅ **F_gr = m · g · sin θ** → constant with v; **P_gr ∝ v**.
- ✅ **F_acc = δ · m · a** → constant with v; **P_acc ∝ v**.
- ✅ **P_wheel** is mechanical power at road contact; **P_engine = P_wheel / η_d**.
- ✅ At **city speeds**, rolling resistance dominates; at **highway speeds**, aerodynamic drag dominates; the crossover is typically around **60–80 km/h**.
- ✅ **Drivetrain efficiency (η_d)** accounts for transmission, differential, and final drive losses — typically **0.85 to 0.92**.
- ✅ **Mass factor (δ)** captures rotational inertia of engine, flywheel, and wheels — typically **1.05–1.15**, often approximated as **1** for simplicity.
- ✅ Top speed is determined by the velocity at which **P_engine · η_d = P_wheel(v)** — a cubic equation in *v*.
- ✅ **Gradeability** of a vehicle is the maximum slope it can climb at constant speed — set *F_engine_max = F_rr + F_gr + F_ad*.
- ✅ Always convert **km/h → m/s** by multiplying with **5/18** before applying any *v*² or *v*³ formula.
- ✅ Always include the **cos θ** term in F_rr when road is inclined, **not** sin θ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grade Ability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grade Ability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grade Ability (Gradeability)** is defined as the maximum gradient (slope) that a vehicle can negotiate or climb under specified conditions of speed, load, and road surface, expressed either as a **percentage (%)**, an **angle of inclination ($\theta$)**, or a **tan-gradient ratio**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, Grade Ability is one of the **primary performance metrics** of a vehicle, classified alongside:
- Maximum Speed
- Acceleration Capability
- Braking Performance
- Towing / Hauling Capacity

It is fundamentally a **longitudinal dynamics** parameter that defines the *worst-case operational limit* of a vehicle on inclined terrain.

## 1.2 Concept Clarification — Grade % vs Grade Angle

These two are frequently confused in examinations and must be clarified:

- **Grade Percentage (g%)** = Vertical rise per 100 units of horizontal distance.

$$
g\% = \frac{h}{l} \times 100 = \tan(\theta) \times 100
$$

- **Grade Angle ($\theta$)** = The actual inclination angle of the road surface with respect to the horizontal (in degrees or radians).

**Conversion Formulas:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{g}{100}
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{g}{100}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A "100% grade" is **NOT vertical** — it is simply 45°. Vertical climb is undefined (g = ∞).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle up a hill**:
- On a flat road ($\theta = 0°$), you barely need to pedal — only air and friction oppose you.
- As the slope steepens, you immediately feel gravity pulling you backward. Your legs must produce more **tractive force** at the rear wheel.
- The hill becomes steeper than a certain angle, your **rear tire slips/spins** because the friction limit is exceeded — you can no longer climb, regardless of how hard you push.

**A car behaves identically.** Grade Ability is precisely the **steepest hill angle** at which the tires can still produce enough force to overcome gravity without slipping — limited by the friction coefficient between tire and road.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of friction (dry asphalt) | $\mu$ | **0.7 – 0.9** |
| Coefficient of friction (wet asphalt) | $\mu$ | **0.4 – 0.5** |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | $\mu_r$ | **0.010 – 0.015** |
| Maximum commercial truck gradeability | $g\%$ | **30% – 60%** |
| Typical SUV gradeability | $g\%$ | **60% – 100%** |
| Off-road / military vehicles | $g\%$ | **>100%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Force Balance on an Inclined Road

Consider a vehicle of weight $W = mg$ standing/climbing on a road inclined at angle $\theta$. Resolving the weight:

### Normal Direction (perpendicular to road):
$$
N = W \cos(\theta)
$$

### Along the Road (downward slope component):
$$
F_{\text{grade}} = W \sin(\theta)
$$

The vehicle must generate **Tractive Effort (TE)** at the tire–road contact patch to overcome all resistance forces.

## 2.2 Resistance Forces Opposing the Vehicle

For a vehicle moving (or attempting to move) up a grade, three primary resistive forces act along the road surface:

### (i) Rolling Resistance (RR)
$$
F_{RR} = \mu_r \cdot W \cos(\theta) \approx \mu_r \cdot W \quad (\text{for small }\theta)
$$

Where $\mu_r$ is the dimensionless coefficient of rolling resistance (typically 0.01–0.02 for pneumatic tires on good roads).

### (ii) Grade Resistance (GR)
$$
F_{GR} = W \sin(\theta)
$$

This is the **gravitational pull component along the slope**. It is the dominant resistance on steep inclines.

### (iii) Aerodynamic Drag (DA)
$$
F_{DA} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density (~1.2 kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (~0.3–0.4 for cars)
- $A$ = frontal area (m²)
- $V$ = vehicle velocity (m/s)

## 2.3 The Tractive Effort Equation (KTU Most Tested Equation)

For **steady-speed climbing** (no acceleration), the engine tractive effort must balance all resistances:

$$
\boxed{TE = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
TE = \mu_r W \cos(\theta) + W \sin(\theta) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2
$$

## 2.4 Maximum Grade — Tire Friction Limit (The Physical Ceiling)

The tire can transmit force up to the **friction limit**:

$$
TE_{\max} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W \cos(\theta)
$$

For the vehicle to just climb (ignoring drag for simplicity and slow speed):

$$
\mu W \cos(\theta_{\max}) \geq W \sin(\theta_{\max}) + \mu_r W \cos(\theta_{\max})
$$

Dividing by $W \cos(\theta_{\max})$:

$$
\mu \geq \tan(\theta_{\max}) + \mu_r
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Grade Ability Equation (KTU Key Result):**
>
> $$
> \boxed{\tan(\theta_{\max}) = \mu - \mu_r}
> $$
>
> $$
> \boxed{\theta_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu - \mu_r)}
> $$
>
> $$
> \boxed{g\%_{\max} = 100\,(\mu - \mu_r)}
> $$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table — it covers ~80% of grade ability problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g\% = \tan(\theta) \cdot 100$ | Grade percentage | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(g/100)$ | Grade angle | degrees or rad |
| 3 | $F_{GR} = W\sin(\theta)$ | Grade resistance | N |
| 4 | $F_{RR} = \mu_r W\cos(\theta)$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 5 | $F_{DA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 6 | $TE = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA}$ | Tractive effort (no accel.) | N |
| 7 | $\tan(\theta_{\max}) = \mu - \mu_r$ | Max grade friction-limited | dimensionless |
| 8 | $g\%_{\max} = 100(\mu - \mu_r)$ | Max grade % | % |
| 9 | $\sin(\theta_{\max}) = \dfrac{TE - F_{RR} - F_{DA}}{W}$ | Max grade at speed V | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{\text{req}} = TE \cdot V$ | Power to climb at speed V | W |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Manufacturers** quote grade ability (e.g., "30% max grade") in the owner's manual.
- **Trucks & Buses** must comply with **AIS-052 / FMVSS** standards requiring minimum grade performance (typically **≥ 25%** for commercial vehicles).
- **Defence / Off-Road Vehicles** are engineered for **>60% gradeability** by using low-ratio transfer cases, locking differentials, and high-friction tire compounds.
- **Railway Locomotives** use the same principle but on steel-on-steel adhesion ($\mu \approx 0.25$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Maximum Grade Angle From First Principles

### Problem Setup
A four-wheel vehicle of weight $W$ is parked on an incline of angle $\theta$. The tire-road coefficient of friction is $\mu$, and rolling resistance coefficient is $\mu_r$. Derive the maximum grade the vehicle can hold without rolling back.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Equations

Forces on the vehicle along the slope:
- Gravitational component (down-slope): $W\sin\theta$
- Maximum available friction force (up-slope, since gravity pulls down): $\mu N = \mu W \cos\theta$
- Rolling resistance force (opposes motion or impending motion, so up-slope here): $\mu_r W \cos\theta$

### Step 2 — Static Equilibrium at the Limit of Climbing

$$
F_{\text{up}} = F_{\text{down}}
$$

$$
\mu W \cos\theta_{\max} = W \sin\theta_{\max} + \mu_r W \cos\theta_{\max}
$$

**Logic:** The maximum friction acts up the slope to oppose the impending downslope motion due to gravity; rolling resistance also acts up-slope since the vehicle tends to slip backward.

### Step 3 — Rearrange the Equation

Move rolling resistance to the LHS:

$$
\mu W \cos\theta_{\max} - \mu_r W \cos\theta_{\max} = W \sin\theta_{\max}
$$

Factor out $W \cos\theta_{\max}$ on the left:

$$
W \cos\theta_{\max}\,(\mu - \mu_r) = W \sin\theta_{\max}
$$

### Step 4 — Cancel W from Both Sides

$$
\cos\theta_{\max}\,(\mu - \mu_r) = \sin\theta_{\max}
$$

### Step 5 — Form the Tangent Function

Divide both sides by $\cos\theta_{\max}$:

$$
\mu - \mu_r = \frac{\sin\theta_{\max}}{\cos\theta_{\max}} = \tan\theta_{\max}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - \mu_r \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \theta_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu - \mu_r)}
$$

And in percentage form:

$$
\boxed{g_{\max}\% = 100\,(\mu - \mu_r)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Grade ability is **directly controlled by the friction coefficient** minus the rolling resistance. Higher $\mu$ (grippier tires, drier road, rougher surface) = better gradeability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Speed on a Given Grade

### Problem Setup
A vehicle has available maximum tractive force $TE_{\max}$. Find the maximum speed at which it can climb a fixed grade $\theta$ on a level road with $\mu_r$ given.

### Step 1 — Tractive Force Equation at Constant Speed

At constant velocity, $a = 0$, so:

$$
TE = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA}
$$

$$
TE_{\max} = \mu_r W \cos\theta + W \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V_{\max}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $V_{\max}^2$

Rearranging:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V_{\max}^2 = TE_{\max} - W\sin\theta - \mu_r W\cos\theta
$$

$$
V_{\max}^2 = \frac{2\!\left[TE_{\max} - W\sin\theta - \mu_r W\cos\theta\right]}{\rho C_d A}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2\!\left[TE_{\max} - W\sin\theta - \mu_r W\cos\theta\right]}{\rho C_d A}}}
$$

**Validity Condition:** The expression under the square root must be positive; otherwise, the vehicle **cannot climb** that grade at any speed (or even at all, depending on drag contribution).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A 1500 kg car has a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.6$ between tires and road. Rolling resistance $\mu_r = 0.015$. Find (a) maximum grade angle and (b) maximum grade percentage.

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\mu = 0.6$, $\mu_r = 0.015$

### (a) Maximum Grade Angle

$$
\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - \mu_r = 0.6 - 0.015 = 0.585
$$

$$
\theta_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(0.585) = 30.31^\circ
$$

### (b) Maximum Grade Percentage

$$
g_{\max}\% = \tan\theta_{\max} \times 100 = 0.585 \times 100 = 58.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This car can theoretically climb a 58.5% grade (~30°) on a dry road — provided tire slip is the limiting factor (not engine power).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Condition | Effect on $\theta_{\max}$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Road surface dry ($\mu \uparrow$) | Increases | Higher friction = more tractive force |
| Road surface wet ($\mu \downarrow$) | Decreases sharply | Aquaplaning reduces $\mu$ |
| Cargo loaded (W ↑) | **No change** | Both $F_{GR}$ and $F_{\text{max-friction}}$ scale with W |
| Low tire pressure | Decreases | Increases $\mu_r$, reduces $\mu$ |
| Soft sand / mud | Decreases drastically | $\mu$ may drop below 0.3 |
| 4WD engaged vs 2WD | Increases | Effective $\mu$ rises due to load distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (often asked in KTU viva):** Grade ability in the friction-limited regime is **independent of vehicle weight** because both the resistance and the maximum tractive force scale linearly with W.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram — Vehicle on an Incline (Block Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **force interactions** acting on a vehicle on an inclined road. It replaces a hand-drawn FBD with a rigorous flow representation suitable for the KTU 2024 Scheme notes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VEH[VEHICLE WEIGHT W = m times g]
    RESOLVE[FORCE RESOLUTION AT SLOPE ANGLE theta]
    NORMAL[NORMAL REACTION N = W cos theta]
    GRADE[DOWN-SLOPE GRADE FORCE F_GR = W sin theta]
    ROLL[ROLLING RESISTANCE F_RR = mu_r times W cos theta]
    TRACT[MAX AVAILABLE TRACTION F_max = mu times W cos theta]
    AERO[AERODYNAMIC DRAG F_DA = 0.5 rho C_d A V squared]
    SUM[RESISTANCE SUM F_RES = F_RR + F_GR + F_DA]
    BAL[FORCE BALANCE CHECK F_max greater than F_RES]
    OUT1[VEHICLE CLIMBS GRADE]
    OUT2[VEHICLE STALLS OR SLIPS]

    VEH --> RESOLVE
    RESOLVE --> NORMAL
    RESOLVE --> GRADE
    NORMAL --> ROLL
    NORMAL --> TRACT
    GRADE --> SUM
    ROLL --> SUM
    AERO --> SUM
    TRACT --> BAL
    SUM --> BAL
    BAL -->|TRUE| OUT1
    BAL -->|FALSE| OUT2
```

## 4.2 System-Level Architecture — Grade Ability Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUTS: Vehicle W, Surface mu, Rolling mu_r, Grade theta] --> B[COMPUTE N = W cos theta]
    B --> C[COMPUTE F_GR = W sin theta]
    B --> D[COMPUTE F_RR = mu_r W cos theta]
    B --> E[COMPUTE F_max = mu W cos theta]
    C --> F[SUM RESISTANCES]
    D --> F
    E --> G[COMPARE F_max vs SUM]
    F --> G
    G -->|F_max > SUM| H[GRADE CLIMBABLE]
    G -->|F_max = SUM| I[CRITICAL GRADE theta_max]
    G -->|F_max < SUM| J[GRADE NOT NEGOTIABLE]
    I --> K[OUTPUT theta_max = arctan mu minus mu_r]
```

## 4.3 Resistance Force Hierarchy on a Steep Grade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[RESISTANCES AT STEEP GRADE] --> R1[GRADE RESISTANCE dominates]
    R --> R2[AERODYNAMIC DRAG secondary at high V]
    R --> R3[ROLLING RESISTANCE negligible compared to GR]
    R1 --> NOTE[For theta greater than 15 deg, F_GR often exceeds 25 percent of W]
    R2 --> NOTE2[For V less than 30 km/h, drag is small]
    R3 --> NOTE3[mu_r is typically two orders smaller than sin theta]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Grade Ability. How is it expressed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Grade ability is the maximum gradient or slope that a vehicle can negotiate under specified operating conditions. It is a longitudinal dynamics parameter representing the worst-case climbing performance.

It is expressed in **three forms:**
1. **Grade percentage** $g\% = \tan\theta \times 100$
2. **Grade angle** $\theta$ in degrees/radians
3. **Gradient ratio** $\tan\theta$ (dimensionless)

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the three forms.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**A vehicle can climb a maximum grade of 25%. Find the grade angle in degrees.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given $g\% = 25\%$.

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{g}{100} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^\circ
$$

**∴ Grade angle ≈ 14.04°**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for maximum grade angle a vehicle can climb on an inclined road, considering both friction and rolling resistance. State all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2000 kg SUV has coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.8$ and rolling resistance $\mu_r = 0.02$. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum grade angle
- (ii) Maximum grade percentage
- (iii) Whether it can climb a 40% grade.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Vehicle is in **static / quasi-static** condition (just able to climb, no acceleration).
2. Road is **rigid and dry** — $\mu$ is constant.
3. **Aerodynamic drag neglected** (low-speed climbing).
4. Weight is **uniformly distributed** across all wheels (no axle load transfer).
5. Tire slip is the limiting factor.

**Derivation (step-by-step, 7 marks):**

Forces along the slope at the limit:

$$
\mu W\cos\theta_{\max} = W\sin\theta_{\max} + \mu_r W\cos\theta_{\max}
$$

*(1 mark — correct force balance)*

$$
\mu W\cos\theta_{\max} - \mu_r W\cos\theta_{\max} = W\sin\theta_{\max}
$$

*(1 mark — rearranging)*

$$
(\mu - \mu_r)W\cos\theta_{\max} = W\sin\theta_{\max}
$$

*(1 mark — factoring)*

Cancel $W$:

$$
(\mu - \mu_r)\cos\theta_{\max} = \sin\theta_{\max}
$$

*(1 mark — simplification)*

Divide by $\cos\theta_{\max}$:

$$
\mu - \mu_r = \tan\theta_{\max}
$$

*(1 mark — forming tangent)*

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(\mu - \mu_r)}
$$

*(1 mark — final result)*

$$
\boxed{g_{\max}\% = 100(\mu - \mu_r)}
$$

*(1 mark — percentage form)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $m = 2000$ kg, $\mu = 0.8$, $\mu_r = 0.02$.

**(i) Maximum grade angle [2 marks]:**

$$
\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - \mu_r = 0.8 - 0.02 = 0.78
$$

$$
\theta_{\max} = \tan^{-1}(0.78) = 37.95^\circ
$$

**(ii) Maximum grade percentage [2 marks]:**

$$
g_{\max}\% = \tan\theta_{\max} \times 100 = 0.78 \times 100 = 78\%
$$

**(iii) Can it climb 40% grade? [3 marks]:**

Required grade resistance for 40% grade:

$$
\tan\theta_{40} = 0.40 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta = 21.8^\circ
$$

Available friction slope capacity: $0.78$
Required slope: $0.40$

Since $0.78 > 0.40$, **YES**, the SUV can climb the 40% grade comfortably. *(Available margin = 78% − 40% = 38% — large safety margin.)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the tractive effort equation for a vehicle climbing a grade. List all resistance forces and explain the physical origin of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A truck of weight 80 kN travels at a constant speed of 54 km/h on a 10% upgrade. Given:
- $\mu_r = 0.018$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.6$, $A = 6$ m²
- Neglect rolling resistance and drag to find the **tractive force** required to climb this grade at constant speed.

Find the **required driving power** at the wheels.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The Tractive Effort Equation:**

For a vehicle moving at constant speed on a slope, the tractive effort (TE) at the tire–road interface must equal the sum of all resistance forces:

$$
TE = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA} + F_{IN}
$$

Where $F_{IN}$ is inertia force (zero at constant speed).

*(2 marks — full equation + definition of TE)*

**Resistance Forces:**

1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_{RR}$):** Originates from tire deformation hysteresis and micro-slip at the contact patch.

   $$
   F_{RR} = \mu_r W \cos\theta
   $$

   *(2 marks — explanation + formula)*

2. **Grade Resistance ($F_{GR}$):** Gravitational force component along the slope.

   $$
   F_{GR} = W \sin\theta
   $$

   *(1 mark — explanation + formula)*

3. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{DA}$):** Pressure and viscous drag of air on the vehicle body.

   $$
   F_{DA} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2
   $$

   *(2 marks — explanation + formula)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $W = 80{,}000$ N, $g\% = 10\%$, $V = 54$ km/h = 15 m/s.

Step 1 — Find grade angle:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71^\circ
$$

Step 2 — Compute each resistance (rolling and drag neglected per problem):

$$
F_{RR} = 0 \quad (\text{neglected})
$$

$$
F_{GR} = W\sin\theta = 80{,}000 \times \sin(5.71^\circ) = 80{,}000 \times 0.0995 \approx 7960 \text{ N}
$$

(Alternatively, for small angles, $F_{GR} \approx W \tan\theta = 80{,}000 \times 0.10 = 8000$ N.) *(2 marks)*

Step 3 — Tractive force at constant speed:

$$
F_{\text{tractive}} = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA} = 0 + 7960 + 0 = 7960 \text{ N}
$$

*(2 marks)*

Step 4 — Required driving power at the wheels:

$$
P = F_{\text{tractive}} \times V = 7960 \times 15 = 119{,}400 \text{ W} \approx 119.4 \text{ kW}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**∴ Required Tractive Force ≈ 7960 N (≈ 8 kN), Power ≈ 119.4 kW (≈ 160 hp)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing g% with $\theta$:** Writing "10% grade = 10°" is a classic 1-mark loss. Always convert using $\tan\theta = g/100$.
> 2. **Forgetting rolling resistance:** In derivation questions, examiners specifically check whether $\mu_r$ is included — omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Using $W$ instead of $W\cos\theta$ for normal force:** Especially for grades > 20°, this introduces noticeable error and marks are deducted.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** "Power = 119,400" without "Watts" loses 0.5 marks in strict KTU valuation.
> 5. **Mixing up tractive effort and engine torque:** Tractive effort is in **Newtons** (force at contact patch); torque is in **N·m**. KTU questions frequently distinguish these.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Grade Ability** = maximum climbable gradient, expressed as %, angle, or ratio.
- [x] **Conversion:** $g\% = 100\tan\theta$ and $\theta = \tan^{-1}(g/100)$.
- [x] **Maximum grade formula:** $\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - \mu_r$.
- [x] **Three resistances** to overcome: $F_{RR}, F_{GR}, F_{DA}$.
- [x] **TE Equation:** $TE = F_{RR} + F_{GR} + F_{DA}$ (constant speed).
- [x] **Friction-limited gradeability** is **independent of vehicle weight** — both numerator and denominator scale with $W$.
- [x] **Wet roads** drastically reduce $\mu$ → gradeability drops ~40%.
- [x] **Tractive force at contact patch** ≠ engine torque; related by $TE = T/r_{\text{wheel}}$.
- [x] **Power needed to climb:** $P = TE \cdot V$ (in Watts if SI units used).
- [x] **Commercial vehicle standard gradeability:** ≥25% (AIS / ECE regulations).
- [x] Always state **assumptions** in derivations: dry road, no drag (or include it), no acceleration, uniform weight distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, almost every Module 1 long question includes a 14-mark derivation problem on Grade Ability. Memorize the boxed formula $\tan\theta_{\max} = \mu - \mu_r$ and the TE equation — they appear in over 60% of past papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drawbar Pull and Problems Related to These Terms, Road Performance Curves - Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Drawbar Pull (DBP) — Formal KTU Definition

**Drawbar Pull (DBP)** is defined as the *net longitudinal force available at the tyre–ground contact patch of the driven wheels of a vehicle to overcome the total resistance forces and propel the vehicle forward (or backward)*. In formal KTU-board terminology, it is the *residual tractive effort* after all motion and grade resistances have been deducted.

$$DBP \;=\; T_e \;-\; R_{total}$$

Where:
- $T_e$ = Tractive Effort at the driving wheels (in **N** or **kN**)
- $R_{total}$ = Sum of all resistance forces opposing motion (**N**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAUT602):** Drawbar Pull is the **single most important performance metric** in off-road, agricultural, and military vehicle engineering. The KTU Board Examiner will almost always test the student's ability to: (1) define it, (2) derive it from tractive effort, and (3) solve numerical problems combining **rolling resistance**, **grade resistance**, **air drag**, and **acceleration resistance**.

---

## 1.2 Related Performance Terms — The Foundational Vocabulary

Before solving any problem, the student must internalize these core terms that ALWAYS appear together in KTU question papers:

| Sl. No. | Term | Board-Examiner Definition |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | **Tractive Effort ($T_e$)** | The actual driving force delivered to the road by the engine–drivetrain via the tyres. |
| 2 | **Rolling Resistance ($R_r$)** | The hysteresis loss in tyres and road surface contact. |
| 3 | **Grade Resistance ($R_g$)** | The component of vehicle weight acting along the slope of the road. |
| 4 | **Air Resistance / Aerodynamic Drag ($R_a$)** | The force opposing motion caused by air flowing past the vehicle body. |
| 5 | **Total Resistance ($R_t$)** | The algebraic sum $R_r + R_g + R_a$. |
| 6 | **Drawbar Pull ($DP$)** | The net force $T_e - R_t$ available for towing or acceleration. |
| 7 | **Drawbar Power ($P_{db}$)** | The product $DP \times V$ — useful work rate at the hitch. |
| 8 | **Maximum Speed ($V_{max}$)** | The steady speed where $T_e = R_t$ (no surplus for acceleration). |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bullock Cart vs. The Bicycle**
> Imagine you are pushing a heavy bullock cart up a slight incline. Your *engine* (your legs) generates a pushing force. Some of that push is wasted fighting: (a) the bumps and dust of the road (rolling resistance), (b) the slope itself pulling the cart backward (grade resistance), and (c) the breeze pushing against the canvas cover (air drag). The **leftover force** that you can actually use to pull a trailer, climb a steeper hill, or accelerate is your **Drawbar Pull**. In a tractor, this leftover force is measured at the rear hitch — hence the name "drawbar."

For a bicycle on a flat road at constant speed, **Drawbar Pull ≈ 0**, because all tractive effort is exactly consumed by rolling and air drag. To accelerate, the cyclist must produce a *positive* DBP. To climb a hill, the cyclist's DBP must exceed the grade resistance.

---

## 1.4 Road Performance Curves — Conceptual Introduction

**Road Performance Curves** are a family of curves plotted on a common axis of **vehicle velocity** that describe how a vehicle performs under various driving demands. The standard KTU-board set contains four curves:

1. **Tractive Effort Curve** — $T_e$ vs $V$ (decreases with speed due to engine torque fall-off)
2. **Rolling Resistance Curve** — $R_r$ vs $V$ (slightly increases)
3. **Air Resistance Curve** — $R_a$ vs $V$ (increases parabolically as $\propto V^2$)
4. **Net Force / Acceleration Curve** — $(T_e - R_t)$ vs $V$ (the available force for acceleration)

The **Acceleration** curve is derived by dividing the Net Force curve by the **effective mass** of the vehicle:

$$a \;=\; \frac{T_e - R_t}{m_{eff} \cdot g}$$

Where $m_{eff}$ (often denoted $\delta m$ or $m_{eff} = m + \dfrac{I_w}{r^2}$) accounts for the rotational inertia of the wheels and drivetrain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Derivation of Drawbar Pull

### Step 1: Force Generation at the Tyre-Road Interface

The engine delivers torque $T_{eng}$ at the crankshaft. Through a multi-stage transmission (clutch → gearbox → differential → final drive → wheels), this torque is multiplied by the total gear ratio $G_{tot}$ and reduced by the overall drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot}$.

The torque at the driving wheels is:

$$T_w \;=\; T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}$$

### Step 2: Conversion of Wheel Torque to Tractive Effort

The tractive effort $T_e$ at the tyre–road contact patch is the wheel torque divided by the effective rolling radius $r$ of the driven tyre:

$$T_e \;=\; \frac{T_w}{r} \;=\; \frac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $T_e$ decrease with speed?** Because engine torque $T_{eng}$ falls off at high RPM (after the torque peak), and the gear ratio $G_{tot}$ is fixed for a given gear. The product $T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot}$ decreases monotonically with road speed in top gear.

### Step 3: Resistance Forces Opposing Motion

The total resistance $R_t$ is the sum of three physically distinct components:

$$R_t \;=\; R_r + R_g + R_a$$

#### (a) Rolling Resistance $R_r$

Caused primarily by hysteresis in the tyre rubber and road surface deformation:

$$R_r \;=\; f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $f_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (typically **0.01 to 0.02** for passenger car tyres on asphalt, **0.05–0.15** for off-road/agricultural tyres)
- $W$ = vehicle weight on the driven axle ($mg$)
- $\theta$ = road slope angle

#### (b) Grade Resistance $R_g$

The component of weight acting along the slope:

$$R_g \;=\; W \cdot \sin\theta \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles (typical road grades $\leq 15\%$), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians), and grade percentage $G\% = 100 \cdot \tan\theta$, so:

$$R_g \;\approx\; 0.01 \cdot G\% \cdot m \cdot g$$

#### (c) Aerodynamic Drag $R_a$

Caused by pressure differential and viscous shear:

$$R_a \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, ISA)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (typical cars: **0.30–0.40**, trucks: **0.6–0.8**)
- $A_f$ = frontal area (**m²**)
- $V$ = vehicle velocity (**m/s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $R_a$ grows with the **square** of speed — this is why high-speed driving is dominated by air drag, while low-speed driving is dominated by rolling resistance.

### Step 4: Drawbar Pull — Final Definition

$$\boxed{\,DP \;=\; T_e - R_r - R_g - R_a\,}$$

### Step 5: Drawbar Power

The rate at which useful work is done by the drawbar pull:

$$P_{db} \;=\; DP \cdot V \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Drawbar Power is the *true* measure of a tractor's productivity. In agricultural engineering, **Drawbar Power per unit soil compaction** dictates implement selection. In military engineering, it determines how much artillery can be towed across terrain.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE.** Every formula below has appeared (or will appear) on a KTU PCAUT602 exam. The vertical bar `\vert` is used instead of `\|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Key Variable Notes |
|:-:|---|---|:-:|---|
| 1 | Tractive Effort | $T_e = \dfrac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r}$ | **N** | $G_{tot}$ includes gearbox + final drive |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $R_r = f_r \cdot W \cos\theta$ | **N** | $f_r \in [0.01, 0.15]$ depending on surface |
| 3 | Grade Resistance | $R_g = W \sin\theta$ | **N** | $\theta = \arctan(G/100)$ |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag | $R_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$ | **N** | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ standard |
| 5 | **Drawbar Pull** | $DP = T_e - R_r - R_g - R_a$ | **N** | **Core definition** |
| 6 | Drawbar Power | $P_{db} = DP \cdot V$ | **W** | Towing capacity indicator |
| 7 | Max Speed Condition | $T_e(V_{max}) = R_r + R_g + R_a$ | **m/s** | Set $DP = 0$ |
| 8 | Acceleration | $a = \dfrac{T_e - R_r - R_g - R_a}{m_{eff}}$ | **m/s²** | $m_{eff} = m(1 + \alpha)$ |
| 9 | Effective Mass | $m_{eff} = m + \dfrac{I_w + I_e G_{tot}^2 \eta_{tot}}{r^2}$ | **kg** | Includes rotational inertia |
| 10 | Climbing Ability | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{DP_{max} + f_r \cos\theta}{W}$ | — | Set $V = 0$ |
| 11 | Acceleration Time (graph) | $t = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m_{eff}}{DP(V)} \, dV$ | **s** | Numerical integration |
| 12 | Acceleration Distance | $s = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m_{eff} V}{DP(V)} \, dV$ | **m** | Used for passing maneuver |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Tractors**: Manufacturers quote drawbar pull at standard test conditions (e.g., Nebraska Tractor Test). Determines which plough or disc harrow can be pulled.
- **Military Vehicles**: Maximum DBP dictates load that can be airlifted by helicopter sling or towed across soft desert sand.
- **Construction Equipment**: Bulldozers and motor graders rely on drawbar pull to push earth — not engine horsepower alone.
- **Passenger Cars (Road Performance)**: DBP determines 0–100 km/h acceleration time, a key marketing metric.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Tractive Effort vs Vehicle Speed in Top Gear

The engine torque curve (in top gear) is given as a function of engine RPM $N$:

$$T_{eng}(N) \;=\; T_{peak} \cdot f(N)$$

Where $f(N)$ is a normalized curve (typically rises to a peak near 1500–2500 RPM, then falls off).

The relationship between road speed $V$ and engine RPM $N$ in top gear is:

$$V \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N r}{60 \, G_{tot}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{60 \, G_{tot} V}{2\pi r}$$

Substituting:

$$T_e(V) \;=\; \frac{\eta_{tot} \cdot G_{tot}}{r} \cdot T_{eng}\!\left(\frac{60\,G_{tot} V}{2\pi r}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Maximum Speed on Level Road

**Given:** $V_{max}$ is reached when the vehicle can no longer accelerate, i.e., the net force equals zero.

$$T_e(V_{max}) \;=\; R_r + R_a(V_{max})$$

Substituting the explicit formulas:

$$\frac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r} \;=\; f_r \, m \, g \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V_{max}^2$$

Because $T_e$ depends on $V$, this is a **transcendental equation**. The KTU-board solving procedure is:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Express } T_e \text{ as a function of } V \text{ in the chosen gear.} \\
&\text{Step 2: Substitute and rearrange to isolate } V_{max}. \\
&\text{Step 3: Solve iteratively (Newton-Raphson) or graphically.}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Drawbar Pull Calculation (KTU Style, 14-mark type)

**Problem Statement:**
A tractor has the following specifications:
- Engine torque $T_{eng}$ = **280 N·m** (assumed constant)
- Total gear ratio in 3rd gear $G_{tot}$ = **25.2**
- Overall drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot}$ = **0.85**
- Effective tyre radius $r$ = **0.65 m**
- Vehicle mass $m$ = **3200 kg**
- Coefficient of rolling resistance $f_r$ = **0.04**
- Road slope $\theta$ = **10°**
- Drag coefficient $C_D$ = **0.45**, frontal area $A_f$ = **3.2 m²**
- Velocity $V$ = **12 m/s** (= 43.2 km/h)

**Find:**
(a) Tractive Effort, (b) Rolling, Grade, and Air Resistances, (c) Drawbar Pull, (d) Drawbar Power.

### Solution

**Part (a): Tractive Effort**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r} \\
    &= \frac{280 \times 25.2 \times 0.85}{0.65} \\
    &= \frac{5997.6}{0.65} \\
    &= \mathbf{9227.08 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b): Resistance Forces**

Rolling resistance:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_r &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \\
    &= 0.04 \times 3200 \times 9.81 \times \cos(10°) \\
    &= 0.04 \times 3200 \times 9.81 \times 0.9848 \\
    &= \mathbf{1236.04 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

Grade resistance:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
    &= 3200 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10°) \\
    &= 3200 \times 9.81 \times 0.17365 \\
    &= \mathbf{5450.94 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

Air resistance:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2 \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 3.2 \times (12)^2 \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 3.2 \times 144 \\
    &= \mathbf{127.01 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

Total resistance:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_t &= R_r + R_g + R_a \\
    &= 1236.04 + 5450.94 + 127.01 \\
    &= \mathbf{6813.99 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c): Drawbar Pull**

$$\begin{aligned}
DP &= T_e - R_t \\
   &= 9227.08 - 6813.99 \\
   &= \mathbf{2413.09 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (d): Drawbar Power**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{db} &= DP \cdot V \\
       &= 2413.09 \times 12 \\
       &= \mathbf{28957.08 \; W \;\approx\; 29.0 \; kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The tractor can pull an additional $\approx 2.41$ kN at the hitch at 12 m/s. If the operator shifts to a lower gear, $G_{tot}$ increases, $T_e$ rises, and DBP rises — at the cost of speed.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Acceleration Curve Construction

**Problem:** Using the data above, plot the **acceleration curve** $a(V)$ at $V = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25$ m/s on level ground ($\theta = 0$). Assume effective mass factor $(1 + \alpha) = 1.08$.

### Solution Framework

For each velocity, compute the *net force* and divide by effective mass.

**At $V = 0$ m/s (just after clutch engagement):**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_r &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.04 \times 3200 \times 9.81 = 1255.68 \; N \\
R_g &= 0 \\
R_a &= 0 \\
T_e(0) &\approx T_e(V=12) \cdot \text{gear-correction} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> For simplicity, assume $T_e(V)$ scales linearly from $T_e(0) = 13000$ N at $V=0$ to $T_e(25) = 6000$ N at $V=25$ m/s. Then:

**Tabulated Acceleration Table (Level Road):**

| $V$ (m/s) | $T_e$ (N) | $R_r$ (N) | $R_g$ (N) | $R_a$ (N) | $R_t$ (N) | Net Force (N) | $a$ (m/s²) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 13000 | 1256 | 0 | 0 | 1256 | 11744 | **3.32** |
| 5 | 11800 | 1260 | 0 | 88 | 1348 | 10452 | 2.95 |
| 10 | 10600 | 1270 | 0 | 352 | 1622 | 8978 | 2.53 |
| 12 | 9227 | 1236 | 0 | 127 | 1363 | 7864 | 2.22 |
| 15 | 9000 | 1290 | 0 | 793 | 2083 | 6917 | 1.95 |
| 20 | 7800 | 1310 | 0 | 1410 | 2720 | 5080 | 1.43 |
| 25 | 6000 | 1340 | 0 | 2200 | 3540 | 2460 | 0.70 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Acceleration falls monotonically as $V$ increases — $R_a$ grows quadratically and $T_e$ decreases. This shape is the **hallmark of a road performance acceleration curve** that KTU examiners expect students to recognize.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Acceleration Time and Distance

Given $a(V) = \dfrac{F_{net}(V)}{m_{eff}}$, we use $a = \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ and $a = V\dfrac{dV}{ds}$.

### Time to accelerate from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$t_{1 \to 2} \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m_{eff}}{F_{net}(V)} \, dV$$

### Distance covered during acceleration:

$$s_{1 \to 2} \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m_{eff}\, V}{F_{net}(V)} \, dV$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When $F_{net}$ is approximately linear in $V$, these integrals can be evaluated analytically with $\ln$. When $F_{net}$ is tabulated, **numerical integration by the trapezoidal rule** is the expected approach in the board exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Drawbar Pull Calculation Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the **block-level functional architecture** mapping the sequential interactions between forces, the engine, drivetrain, and road during DBP computation. This is the most defensible way to depict the system without resorting to complex physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENGINE TORQUE T_eng] --> B[Gearbox Ratio G_gb]
    B --> C[Differential Ratio G_fd]
    C --> D[Effective Rolling Radius r]
    D --> E[TRACTIVE EFFORT T_e]
    F[Vehicle Mass m] --> G[Rolling Resistance R_r]
    H[Road Slope theta] --> I[Grade Resistance R_g]
    J[Velocity V] --> K[Aerodynamic Drag R_a]
    G --> L[TOTAL RESISTANCE R_t]
    I --> L
    K --> L
    E --> M[Net Longitudinal Force]
    L --> M
    M --> N[DRAWBAR PULL DP]
    N --> O[Drawbar Power P_db]
    N --> P[Acceleration a]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Inputs flow from the **left** (engine parameters, mass, road, environment).
- Tractive effort is computed at the **top branch**.
- Resistance forces aggregate on the **bottom branch**.
- These two streams converge into the **Net Longitudinal Force**, which branches out to produce **Drawbar Pull**, **Drawbar Power**, and **Acceleration**.

---

## 4.2 Road Performance Curve Family — Conceptual Mermaid Sketch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Map in Top Gear] --> B[Tractive Effort Curve T_e vs V]
    C[Vehicle Mass and f_r] --> D[Rolling Resistance Curve R_r vs V]
    E[Road Slope theta] --> F[Grade Resistance Line R_g vs V]
    G[C_D, A_f, rho] --> H[Aerodynamic Drag Curve R_a vs V parabola]
    B --> I[Net Force Curve F_net vs V]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Acceleration Curve a vs V]
    J --> K[ROAD PERFORMANCE FAMILY]
```

---

## 4.3 Force Decomposition on a Slope — Physical Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle of Weight W = mg on slope theta] --> B[Weight Vector mg downward]
    B --> C[Component along slope: mg sin theta = R_g]
    B --> D[Component perpendicular to slope: mg cos theta]
    D --> E[Normal Force N = mg cos theta]
    E --> F[Rolling Resistance R_r = f_r N]
    C --> G[NET RESISTANCE along slope]
    F --> G
    H[Tractive Effort T_e from driven wheels] --> I[NET DRAWBAR PULL DP]
    G --> I
```

---

## 4.4 Acceleration Curve — Typical Shape (Block Diagram of Quadrants)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V = 0: Maximum a = a_max] --> B[Low V: R_a small, T_e high]
    B --> C[Mid V: T_e decreasing, R_a growing]
    C --> D[High V: T_e intersects R_t]
    D --> E[V = V_max: a = 0]
    E --> F[Above V_max: a is negative]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The acceleration curve is **monotonically decreasing** on level ground. This is the canonical KTU-board figure — students should be able to sketch and label it without referring to notes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Drawbar Pull and Drawbar Power. State the factors affecting Drawbar Pull of a tractor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Drawbar Pull (DP)** is the net longitudinal force available at the contact patch of the driving wheels after deducting all motion and grade resistance forces from the available tractive effort. Mathematically:

$$DP \;=\; T_e - R_r - R_g - R_a$$

**Drawbar Power ($P_{db}$)** is the rate at which useful work is done by the drawbar pull:

$$P_{db} \;=\; DP \cdot V$$

**Factors affecting Drawbar Pull of a tractor:**
1. Engine torque $T_{eng}$ and power
2. Total gear ratio $G_{tot}$
3. Overall drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot}$
4. Effective rolling radius $r$ of the tyre
5. Soil/road condition (affects $f_r$ and slip)
6. Road gradient $\theta$
7. Vehicle speed $V$ (via $R_a$)
8. Tyre inflation pressure
9. Weight distribution on driven axle

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of road performance curves with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

Road performance curves are a family of curves plotted against vehicle speed that show how a vehicle's tractive effort, resistance forces, and acceleration capability vary with velocity. Their significance includes:

1. **Determination of maximum speed** ($V_{max}$) — found from the intersection of $T_e$ and $R_t$ curves.
2. **Prediction of acceleration capability** at any speed — given by the net force divided by effective mass.
3. **Gear selection** — driver can pick gear that places operating point in the highest acceleration region.
4. **Vehicle design verification** — confirms that the designed vehicle meets performance specifications.

A typical family contains: $T_e$, $R_r$, $R_g$, $R_a$, $F_{net}$, and $a(V)$ plotted on the common velocity axis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Module 1** Part B carries 14 marks. The following presents a true internal-choice question with **two independent alternatives (Question A and Question B)**, each containing sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCAUT602]`

A four-wheel-drive passenger vehicle of mass **1800 kg** is traveling at a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a road with gradient **5%**. The vehicle has a frontal area of **2.4 m²**, drag coefficient **0.34**, drivetrain efficiency **0.90**, total gear ratio in top gear **3.8**, engine torque at this speed **210 N·m**, and effective tyre radius **0.32 m**. Coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.018**.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Calculate the **Tractive Effort**, **Rolling Resistance**, **Grade Resistance**, **Aerodynamic Drag**, and the **Drawbar Pull**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
If the effective mass factor $(1 + \alpha) = 1.05$, calculate the **acceleration** at this instant and the **drawbar power**. Comment on whether the vehicle can climb a **steeper 8% grade** at the same speed.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Given (organized):**
- $m = 1800$ kg, $V = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, gradient $= 5\%$
- $A_f = 2.4$ m², $C_D = 0.34$, $\eta_{tot} = 0.90$, $G_{tot} = 3.8$, $T_{eng} = 210$ N·m, $r = 0.32$ m, $f_r = 0.018$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $(1+\alpha) = 1.05$

**Grade angle for 5%:**
$$\theta = \arctan(0.05) = 2.862° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\theta = 0.04994, \quad \cos\theta = 0.99875$$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Tractive Effort:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r} \\
    &= \frac{210 \times 3.8 \times 0.90}{0.32} \\
    &= \frac{718.2}{0.32} \\
    &= \mathbf{2244.38 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Rolling Resistance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_r &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \\
    &= 0.018 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.99875 \\
    &= \mathbf{317.19 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Grade Resistance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
    &= 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.04994 \\
    &= \mathbf{881.74 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Aerodynamic Drag:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2 \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.34 \times 2.4 \times (25)^2 \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.34 \times 2.4 \times 625 \\
    &= \mathbf{312.38 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total Resistance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_t &= 317.19 + 881.74 + 312.38 = 1511.31 \; N
\end{aligned}$$

**Drawbar Pull:**
$$\begin{aligned}
DP &= T_e - R_t = 2244.38 - 1511.31 = \mathbf{733.07 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Effective Mass:**
$$m_{eff} = (1+\alpha) \cdot m = 1.05 \times 1800 = 1890 \; kg$$

**Acceleration:**
$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{DP}{m_{eff}} = \frac{733.07}{1890} = \mathbf{0.388 \; m/s^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Drawbar Power:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{db} &= DP \cdot V = 733.07 \times 25 = \mathbf{18326.75 \; W \;\approx\; 18.33 \; kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on 8% Grade:**

For 8% grade: $\theta = \arctan(0.08) = 4.572°$, $\sin\theta = 0.0797$, $\cos\theta = 0.9968$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_r' &= 0.018 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.9968 = 316.62 \; N \\
R_g' &= 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.0797 = 1407.32 \; N \\
R_a' &= 312.38 \; N \quad \text{(unchanged, same V)} \\
R_t' &= 316.62 + 1407.32 + 312.38 = 2036.32 \; N \\
DP' &= 2244.38 - 2036.32 = 208.06 \; N > 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $DP' = +208.06$ N is still positive, the vehicle *can* climb the 8% grade at 90 km/h in top gear, but with reduced acceleration ($a' = 208.06/1890 = 0.110$ m/s²). Practically, the driver would downshift to maintain performance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCAUT602]`

A tractor has the following specifications: mass **3500 kg**, engine torque **320 N·m**, total gear ratio **30.5**, drivetrain efficiency **0.88**, tyre radius **0.70 m**, $f_r = 0.06$, $C_D = 0.5$, $A_f = 3.5$ m².

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
On a **level road**, plot (with proper calculations at five velocities) the **tractive effort, total resistance, and net force curves**. Identify the **maximum speed** from the curves.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Calculate the **maximum drawbar pull** at $V = 0$ and the **maximum gradient the tractor can climb** from rest in this gear.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Curve Construction:**

Assume engine torque falls linearly with speed (for illustration) from $T_{eng} = 320$ N·m at $V=0$ to $T_{eng} = 180$ N·m at $V=20$ m/s.

**Tractive Effort at any $V$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_e(V) &= \frac{T_{eng}(V) \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r} \\
       &= \frac{T_{eng}(V) \times 30.5 \times 0.88}{0.70} \\
       &= T_{eng}(V) \times 38.34
\end{aligned}$$

**Resistance Forces:**
- $R_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.06 \times 3500 \times 9.81 = 2060.1$ N (constant on level road)
- $R_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 3.5 \cdot V^2 = 1.072 \cdot V^2$
- $R_g = 0$ (level road)

**Tabulated Computation Table:**

| $V$ (m/s) | $T_{eng}$ (N·m) | $T_e$ (N) | $R_r$ (N) | $R_a$ (N) | $R_t$ (N) | $F_{net}$ (N) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 320 | 12269 | 2060 | 0 | 2060 | 10209 |
| 5 | 285 | 10927 | 2060 | 27 | 2087 | 8840 |
| 10 | 250 | 9585 | 2060 | 107 | 2167 | 7418 |
| 15 | 215 | 8243 | 2060 | 241 | 2301 | 5942 |
| 18 | 192 | 7361 | 2060 | 347 | 2407 | 4954 |
| 20 | 180 | 6901 | 2060 | 429 | 2489 | 4412 |

**Maximum Speed:** From the table, $F_{net}$ is still positive at $V = 20$ m/s. Setting $T_e(V_{max}) = R_r + R_a(V_{max})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(320 - 7V) \cdot 38.34 &= 2060 + 1.072 \cdot V^2 \\
12269 - 268.4V &= 2060 + 1.072 V^2 \\
1.072 V^2 + 268.4 V - 10209 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the quadratic formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{max} &= \frac{-268.4 + \sqrt{268.4^2 + 4 \times 1.072 \times 10209}}{2 \times 1.072} \\
       &= \frac{-268.4 + \sqrt{72039 + 43784}}{2.144} \\
       &= \frac{-268.4 + \sqrt{115823}}{2.144} \\
       &= \frac{-268.4 + 340.33}{2.144} \\
       &= \frac{71.93}{2.144} \\
       &= \mathbf{33.55 \; m/s \;\approx\; 120.8 \; km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Drawbar Pull and Grade:**

**At $V = 0$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_e(0) &= \frac{320 \times 30.5 \times 0.88}{0.70} = 12268.6 \; N \\
R_t(0) &= R_r = 2060 \; N \quad (\text{since } R_a = 0, R_g = 0) \\
DP_{max} &= 12268.6 - 2060 = \mathbf{10208.6 \; N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Gradient from Rest:**

At $V = 0$, $R_a = 0$. The condition for climbing a slope $\theta_{max}$ from rest is $T_e(0) \geq R_r + R_g$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_e(0) &= R_r + R_g \\
12268.6 &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta_{max} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta_{max} \\
12268.6 &= 0.06 \times 3500 \times 9.81 \times \cos\theta_{max} + 3500 \times 9.81 \times \sin\theta_{max} \\
12268.6 &= 2060.1 \cos\theta_{max} + 34335 \sin\theta_{max}
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing through by $\cos\theta$ and using $\tan\theta = T$:
$$\begin{aligned}
12268.6 \sec\theta_{max} &= 2060.1 + 34335 \tan\theta_{max}
\end{aligned}$$

**Approximate solution** (for moderate $\theta$, assume $\cos\theta \approx 1$):
$$\begin{aligned}
12268.6 &\approx 2060.1 + 34335 \tan\theta_{max} \\
\tan\theta_{max} &\approx \frac{12268.6 - 2060.1}{34335} = 0.2976 \\
\theta_{max} &\approx 16.57°
\end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to a grade of $\tan\theta \times 100 \approx \mathbf{29.8\%}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in $R_a$:** Students frequently forget to convert $V$ from **km/h to m/s**. The formula $R_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$ requires **V in m/s**. Failure to convert results in $R_a$ being off by a factor of $(3.6)^2 \approx 13$ — an instant **3-mark penalty**.
>
> 2. **Using $W = mg$ vs $W$ in N:** In the rolling resistance formula $R_r = f_r W$, the weight $W$ must be in **Newtons**, not kg. Many students substitute $m = 1800$ kg directly — yielding a $R_r$ that is wrong by a factor of $g \approx 9.81$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\eta_{tot}$ in tractive effort:** Several students compute $T_e = T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} / r$ and omit the **drivetrain efficiency**. KTU examiners explicitly deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
>
> 4. **Sign convention for grade resistance:** Grade resistance ALWAYS opposes motion. On an uphill slope, $R_g$ adds to the resistance. Students sometimes subtract it — a critical conceptual error worth **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not stating the maximum speed condition explicitly:** When asked to find $V_{max}$, students must write the condition $T_e(V_{max}) = R_t(V_{max})$ *before* solving. Skipping this statement costs the conceptual **1 mark** even if the numerical answer is correct.
>
> 6. **Effective mass oversight:** Acceleration problems **must** use $m_{eff} = (1+\alpha) m$. Using bare mass $m$ is incorrect and will lose **2 marks**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to interpret the result:** KTU board examiners reward "engineering judgment." A problem like "Can the vehicle climb an 8% grade?" requires a verbal **conclusion**, not just numerical answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Drawbar Pull & Road Performance Acceleration**
>
> ✅ **Drawbar Pull = Tractive Effort − Total Resistance.** Memorize this as the *one-line definition* every KTU paper starts with.
>
> ✅ **The Four Resistances:** Rolling ($f_r mg\cos\theta$), Grade ($mg\sin\theta$), Air Drag ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$), and Acceleration ($m_{eff} \cdot a$). The first three appear in DBP; the fourth is *implicitly* the residual that becomes acceleration.
>
> ✅ **Standard Constants:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Never use $g = 10$ unless the problem explicitly states so.
>
> ✅ **$R_a \propto V^2$** — the dominant high-speed resistance. **$R_r \approx$ constant** — the dominant low-speed resistance.
>
> ✅ **$T_e$ DECREASES with $V$** in any fixed gear. The product $T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot}$ falls off as engine torque tapers beyond the peak RPM.
>
> ✅ **Maximum Speed Condition:** $T_e(V_{max}) = R_r + R_g + R_a(V_{max})$. Set net force to zero.
>
> ✅ **Acceleration Formula:** $a = \dfrac{T_e - R_r - R_g - R_a}{m_{eff}}$ — always divide by effective mass, not bare mass.
>
> ✅ **Effective Mass Includes Rotational Inertia:** $m_{eff} = m + \dfrac{I_w + I_e G_{tot}^2 \eta_{tot}}{r^2}$. The factor $(1+\alpha)$ typically lies in $[1.03, 1.20]$.
>
> ✅ **Drawbar Power $P_{db} = DP \cdot V$** — directly comparable to engine power for assessing drivetrain efficiency.
>
> ✅ **Acceleration Curve Shape:** Monotonically decreasing from $a_{max}$ at $V=0$ to $0$ at $V_{max}$. Always sketch it with the X-axis as $V$ and Y-axis as $a$.
>
> ✅ **Tractive Effort Formula:** $T_e = \dfrac{T_{eng} \cdot G_{tot} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{r}$ — applies to single-axle drive. For 4WD, multiply by appropriate distribution factor.
>
> ✅ **Grade Conversion:** Grade $G\% = 100 \tan\theta$. For $G \leq 15\%$, use small-angle approximations cautiously; otherwise, use exact trig.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Judgment:** Always close your numerical solution with a one-line physical interpretation — "DP is positive, so the vehicle can climb the slope" — KTU examiners value this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grade Ability and Drawbar Pull, Vibrations - Free Vibrations of Single Degree Freedom Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grade Ability and Drawbar Pull, Vibrations - Free Vibrations of Single Degree Freedom Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Grade Ability

**Grade Ability** of a vehicle is formally defined as the maximum slope (incline angle $\theta$ or percentage grade $G$) that the vehicle can climb at a specified constant speed under its own propulsion, while maintaining tractive equilibrium. Mathematically, it is expressed as the maximum value of $\tan\theta$ (or $\sin\theta$) for which the available tractive effort equals the total resisting force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAUT602 - Module 1):**
> Grade ability is the maximum gradient a vehicle can ascend at a given speed, limited by the available tractive effort at the wheel-ground interface and the power plant torque characteristics.

#### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy (The Hill-Climb Story)

Imagine you are pushing a loaded cart up a steep hill. The steeper the hill, the more "muscle" (force) you need. The **maximum slope** you can climb while still moving steadily is the **grade ability**. If the slope is steeper than this, no matter how hard you push, the cart rolls back. In vehicle terms, the "muscle" is the **tractive effort** generated by the engine and transmitted to the road through tires. The "steepness resistance" is the **grade resistance**, which is the component of vehicle weight acting along the slope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** Grade ability is essentially a *force-balance* problem, NOT a power problem. A vehicle with high engine power but poor traction (e.g., a sports car on ice) can have a very LOW grade ability, while a low-power tractor with great grip can climb surprisingly steep slopes.

---

### 1.2 Drawbar Pull

**Drawbar Pull (D)** is the *net usable horizontal force* available at the vehicle's towing hook (drawbar) for pulling a trailer, plough, or any external load, AFTER the vehicle has already overcome its own internal resistances (rolling resistance + grade resistance + aerodynamic drag). It is the residual tractive effort left for external work.

$$D = TE - (R_r + R_g + R_a)$$

where:
- $TE$ = Tractive Effort at the wheels (N)
- $R_r$ = Rolling Resistance (N)
- $R_g$ = Grade Resistance (N)
- $R_a$ = Aerodynamic Drag (N)

#### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy (The Tug-of-War Principle)

Picture a strongman pulling a rope tied to a heavy sled. The strongman generates a total pulling force (tractive effort), but part of that force is wasted just keeping the sled moving against friction (rolling resistance) and gravity on a slope (grade resistance). Only the **leftover force** — the **drawbar pull** — can be used to actually pull a load or perform useful work. If $TE = 10{,}000$ N and total resistance = $6{,}000$ N, then only **4,000 N** is available to tow a trailer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Drawbar Pull Coefficient (DPC)** is defined as $DPC = D/W$ where $W$ is the vehicle weight. It is a dimensionless performance metric used universally in agricultural and off-road vehicle specifications.

---

### 1.3 Free Vibrations of Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) Systems

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system is the simplest dynamic system — it can be described by exactly **one independent coordinate** $x(t)$ that fully specifies its configuration at any instant $t$.

**Free vibration** is the oscillatory motion that occurs when a system is disturbed from its equilibrium position and then allowed to vibrate *without any external forcing* — the motion is sustained purely by the system's own inertia and restoring (and possibly damping) forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Variants Studied in KTU Module 1:**
> 1. **Undamped Free Vibration** — ideal, no energy loss; vibration continues forever at constant amplitude.
> 2. **Damped Free Vibration** — realistic; vibration amplitude decays with time due to damping.

#### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy (The Spring on a Faucet)

Imagine pulling down a spring attached to a ceiling and releasing it (no air resistance = undamped case). It bobs up and down **forever** at a fixed frequency — this is **undamped free vibration**. Now imagine doing the same inside a jar of thick oil — the spring still oscillates, but each swing is **smaller** than the previous one until it finally stops. The oil is the **damper** — this is **damped free vibration**. The *natural frequency* is the system's "preferred" oscillation speed, determined by mass and stiffness alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Canonical Models of a SDOF System:**
> - **Mass-Spring** model: $(m, k)$ — undamped
> - **Mass-Spring-Damper** model: $(m, k, c)$ — damped
> - Where $m$ = mass (kg), $k$ = stiffness (N/m), $c$ = damping coefficient (N·s/m)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Grade Ability — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The grade ability derivation follows a tractive force balance:

**Step 1: Identify all forces acting on the vehicle on a slope of angle $\theta$**

For a vehicle of weight $W$ moving up an incline at constant speed:
- Weight component along slope (downward): $W \sin\theta$
- Normal reaction on slope: $N = W \cos\theta$
- Rolling resistance: $R_r = f \cdot W \cos\theta$ (where $f$ is rolling resistance coefficient)
- Aerodynamic drag: $R_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$
- Tractive effort: $TE$ (developed at wheel-ground contact)

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium at maximum climb speed**

At constant velocity: $TE = R_r + R_g + R_a$

**Step 3: Solve for $\theta_{max}$ (the grade angle)**

$$TE = f W \cos\theta + W \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

Dividing by $W$ and rearranging for small angles where $\cos\theta \approx 1$:

$$\frac{TE}{W} = f + \sin\theta_{max} + \frac{R_a}{W}$$

**Step 4: Express as grade percentage**

Grade $G$ (in %) is defined as: $G = 100 \cdot \tan\theta \approx 100 \cdot \sin\theta$ for small slopes.

$$\boxed{\,G_{max} = 100 \left(\frac{TE}{W} - f - \frac{R_a}{W}\right)\, \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in real engineering?** Off-road vehicles (tractors, military vehicles, 4x4 SUVs) are *rated* by their grade ability. A typical farm tractor: $G_{max} \approx 25\text{–}30\%$. A modern military truck: $G_{max} \approx 60\%$. A Formula 1 car: $G_{max} \approx 100\%$ but only momentarily, due to power limits.

---

### 2.2 Drawbar Pull — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1: Define tractive effort (TE)**

For a driven wheel: $TE = \mu \cdot W_{driven}$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of traction (≈ coefficient of friction between tire and ground) and $W_{driven}$ is the weight on the driven axle.

**Step 2: Define total resistance**

$$R_{total} = f W \cos\theta + W \sin\theta + R_a$$

**Step 3: Drawbar pull is the residual**

$$D = TE - R_{total}$$

**Step 4: Per unit weight (dimensionless form)**

$$\frac{D}{W} = \mu - (f \cos\theta + \sin\theta + \tfrac{R_a}{W})$$

**Step 5: Tractive efficiency** (ratio of drawbar power to axle power)

$$\eta_t = \frac{D \cdot v}{TE \cdot v_{wheel}} = \frac{D}{TE}$$

---

### 2.3 Free Vibration of SDOF — Conceptual Logic

The dynamics of a SDOF mass-spring-damper system are governed by Newton's second law applied to the mass:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

where $x$ is displacement from equilibrium, $\dot{x}$ is velocity, $\ddot{x}$ is acceleration.

**Step 1: Identify system parameters**
- $m$ (kg): mass — represents inertia
- $k$ (N/m): stiffness — represents restoring force
- $c$ (N·s/m): damping — represents energy dissipation

**Step 2: Recognize the standard form**

Dividing the equation of motion by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta \omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ = **undamped natural frequency** (rad/s)
- $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$ = **damping ratio** (dimensionless)

**Step 3: Classify response based on $\zeta$**

| Damping Ratio | Case | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $\zeta = 0$ | Undamped | Pure sinusoidal oscillation, constant amplitude |
| $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Underdamped | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation |
| $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped | Fastest return to equilibrium, no oscillation |
| $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped | Slow non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Most automotive suspension systems are designed with $0.2 \leq \zeta \leq 0.4$ (underdamped) for ride comfort — too much damping makes the ride harsh; too little causes prolonged bouncing.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat Table)

#### A. Grade Ability & Drawbar Pull

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grade Resistance | $R_g = W \sin\theta \approx W \cdot G/100$ | N | Component of weight along slope |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $R_r = f \cdot W \cos\theta$ | N | Resistance from tire deformation |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $R_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | N | Air resistance at speed $v$ |
| 4 | Tractive Effort | $TE = \mu \cdot W_{driven}$ | N | Max force at wheel-ground interface |
| 5 | Drawbar Pull | $D = TE - (R_r + R_g + R_a)$ | N | Net towing force available |
| 6 | Grade Ability | $G_{max} = 100\left(\dfrac{TE}{W} - f\right)$ % | % | Max slope climbable (no drag) |
| 7 | Drawbar Pull Coefficient | $DPC = D/W$ | — | Dimensionless performance index |
| 8 | Tractive Efficiency | $\eta_t = D/TE$ | — | Useful work fraction |

#### B. Free Vibrations of SDOF

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equation of Motion | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | — | Governing ODE |
| 2 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Oscillation rate (undamped) |
| 3 | Natural Period | $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ | s | Time for one cycle |
| 4 | Natural Frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$ | Hz | Cycles per second |
| 5 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | — | Dimensionless damping measure |
| 6 | Damped Freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Actual oscillation rate (underdamped) |
| 7 | Log Decrement | $\delta = \tfrac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n) = \tfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | — | Amplitude decay per cycle |
| 8 | Undamped Response | $x(t) = X\cos\omega_n t + (v_0/\omega_n)\sin\omega_n t$ | m | Free vibration solution ($\zeta=0$) |
| 9 | Underdamped Response | $x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[X\cos\omega_d t + \tfrac{v_0+\zeta\omega_n X}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right]$ | m | Decaying oscillation |
| 10 | Critically Damped | $x(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | m | No oscillation, fastest settle |
| 11 | Overdamped | $x(t) = C_1 e^{s_1 t} + C_2 e^{s_2 t}$ | m | Two real exponentials |
| 12 | Stiffness from $\omega_n$ | $k = m\omega_n^2$ | N/m | Equivalent stiffness |
| 13 | Equivalent $k$ in series | $1/k_{eq} = 1/k_1 + 1/k_2$ | — | For springs in series |
| 14 | Equivalent $k$ in parallel | $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$ | — | For springs in parallel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility (Automotive Context):**
> - **Grade ability** is a critical spec for tractors, military vehicles, and EVs climbing hills.
> - **Drawbar pull** determines maximum trailer weight rating and agricultural implement capacity.
> - **SDOF free vibration** is the foundational model for vehicle suspension design, tire-hop analysis, and engine mount tuning. Almost every comfort/handling trade-off in modern cars stems from this simple equation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Grade Angle for a Driven Wheel Vehicle

**Given:** A vehicle of weight $W$ ascends a slope of angle $\theta$ at constant speed. The coefficient of rolling resistance is $f$ and the coefficient of traction between tire and ground is $\mu$. Aerodynamic drag is neglected.

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup**

Forces acting along the direction of motion:
- Tractive effort $TE$ (up the slope)
- Weight component down the slope: $W \sin\theta$
- Rolling resistance (opposing motion, down the slope): $f \cdot N = f W \cos\theta$

**Step 2: Equilibrium Equation (constant speed ⇒ net force = 0)**

$$TE = W\sin\theta + fW\cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Maximum Grade Condition**

The maximum tractive effort is limited by traction:

$$TE_{max} = \mu W_d = \mu W$$

(assuming the full weight $W$ acts on the driven axle, as in a rear-wheel-drive tractor with load over the drive wheels).

**Step 4: Substitute the maximum TE**

$$\mu W = W\sin\theta_{max} + f W \cos\theta_{max}$$

Dividing throughout by $W$:

$$\mu = \sin\theta_{max} + f\cos\theta_{max}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\theta_{max}$**

Rearranging and using the identity $\sin\theta = \tan\theta \cos\theta$:

$$\mu - f\cos\theta_{max} = \tan\theta_{max} \cdot \cos\theta_{max}$$

$$\tan\theta_{max} = \frac{\mu - f\cos\theta_{max}}{\cos\theta_{max}} = \mu\sec\theta_{max} - f$$

This is implicit. For small-to-moderate slopes ($\cos\theta \approx 1$):

$$\tan\theta_{max} \approx \mu - f$$

**Step 6: Express as Grade Percentage**

$$\boxed{\,G_{max} \approx 100(\mu - f)\%\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation Check:** This formula is valid only when $\mu > f$, otherwise the vehicle cannot climb even a flat surface. This is the famous **"friction vs. rolling resistance"** rule of thumb for off-road vehicles.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Drawbar Pull as a Function of Vehicle Speed

**Given:** A vehicle with weight $W$ towing a load on level ground. Engine produces torque $T_e$ at angular speed $\omega_e$. Overall drivetrain efficiency is $\eta_d$. Wheel radius is $r$. Rolling resistance coefficient is $f$.

**Step 1: Power at the Engine**

$$P_e = T_e \cdot \omega_e \quad \text{(W)}$$

**Step 2: Power at the Wheels (after drivetrain losses)**

$$P_w = \eta_d \cdot P_e = \eta_d \cdot T_e \cdot \omega_e$$

**Step 3: Tractive Effort in terms of Power and Speed**

Since $P_w = TE \cdot v_w$ where $v_w$ is the wheel-center speed (≈ vehicle speed $v$ on level ground):

$$TE = \frac{P_w}{v} = \frac{\eta_d T_e \omega_e}{v}$$

**Step 4: Total Resistance on Level Ground ($\theta = 0$)**

$$R_{total} = fW + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

**Step 5: Drawbar Pull**

$$D = TE - R_{total}$$

$$D = \frac{\eta_d T_e \omega_e}{v} - fW - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$

**Step 6: Differentiate to find maximum D**

Setting $dD/dv = 0$:

$$-\frac{\eta_d T_e \omega_e}{v^2} - \rho C_d A v = 0$$

This has no positive real solution in the standard form (a sign issue arises because $D$ decreases monotonically with $v$ if $TE$ is constant). **However**, if $TE$ is bounded by traction, the maximum D occurs at the lowest practical speed where traction is not exceeded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight (KTU):** Drawbar pull is MAXIMUM at the lowest usable speed (high torque, low velocity in the engine's torque curve). This is why tractors and trucks tow heaviest loads at low gears.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Undamped Free Vibration of SDOF

**Step 1: Equation of Motion**

Apply Newton's second law to the mass attached to a spring of stiffness $k$:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

where the negative sign indicates the spring force is **restoring** (opposite to displacement).

**Step 2: Rearrange to standard form**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Divide by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 3: Define natural frequency**

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

So:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 4: Assume a solution of exponential form**

Let $x(t) = C e^{st}$. Then $\dot{x} = Cs e^{st}$ and $\ddot{x} = Cs^2 e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$Cs^2 e^{st} + \omega_n^2 C e^{st} = 0$$

$$s^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s^2 = -\omega_n^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm i\omega_n$$

**Step 5: Write the general solution**

Since $s$ is purely imaginary, the solution is a combination of sines and cosines:

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = C_1 \cos(\omega_n t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_n t)\,}$$

**Step 6: Apply initial conditions**

Let $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$.

From $x(0) = x_0$:

$$x_0 = C_1 \cos(0) + C_2 \sin(0) = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = x_0$$

From $\dot{x}(t) = -C_1 \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) + C_2 \omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$, evaluating at $t=0$:

$$v_0 = C_2 \omega_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}$$

**Step 7: Final solution**

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)\,}$$

**Step 8: Amplitude-Phase Form**

Using $A\cos\phi + B\sin\phi = R\cos(\phi - \alpha)$ where $R = \sqrt{A^2+B^2}$:

$$x(t) = X \cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$$

where:

$$X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}, \qquad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_0}{x_0 \omega_n}\right)$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Damped Free Vibration (Underdamped Case)

**Step 1: Equation of Motion with viscous damping**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 2: Standard form**

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$, so:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

**Step 3: Assume exponential solution** $x(t) = e^{st}$

Substituting:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve the characteristic equation**

Using the quadratic formula:

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 5: Case 1 — Underdamped ($0 < \zeta < 1$)**

The square root is imaginary. Let $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (damped natural frequency).

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

The general solution:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(C_1 \cos\omega_d t + C_2 \sin\omega_d t\right)$$

**Step 6: Apply initial conditions** $x(0) = x_0$, $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$

From $x(0) = x_0$:

$$C_1 = x_0$$

Differentiating $x(t)$ and evaluating at $t=0$:

$$\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n C_1 + \omega_d C_2 = v_0$$

$$C_2 = \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}$$

**Step 7: Final underdamped solution**

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\!\left[x_0 \cos\omega_d t + \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The amplitude decays as $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$, an **envelope** that bounds the oscillatory response. The oscillation frequency is REDUCED from $\omega_n$ to $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Logarithmic Decrement

**Purpose:** Experimental method to determine damping ratio $\zeta$ from free-decay measurements.

**Step 1: Two successive peak amplitudes**

Let $x_n$ be the $n$-th peak amplitude. Because the peaks occur at half-period intervals $\pi/\omega_d$ apart:

$$x_n = X \cdot e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}$$

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n (t_{n+1}-t_n)} = e^{\zeta\omega_n \cdot \pi/\omega_d} = e^{\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 2: Define logarithmic decrement**

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\zeta$ (when $\zeta$ is small)**

For small $\zeta$: $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\,\zeta \approx \frac{\delta}{\pi} = \frac{1}{n\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful in vehicle dynamics?** Engineers can determine the damping ratio of a car's suspension by simply measuring two consecutive oscillation amplitudes after a "bump test" — no force measurement required.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Combined Vehicle + Vibration Problem

**Problem:** A 1500 kg electric vehicle climbs a 20% grade at 30 km/h. Coefficient of rolling resistance $f = 0.015$, coefficient of traction $\mu = 0.85$, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.7$ m², air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. The vehicle's suspension is modeled as a SDOF system with $m = 375$ kg (quarter-car), $k = 30{,}000$ N/m, $c = 1500$ N·s/m.

**Part A — Grade Ability Check:**

Given: $G = 20\%$, so $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.20) = 11.31°$.

$\sin\theta = 0.196$, $\cos\theta = 0.981$.

Total resistance per unit weight:

$$\frac{R_{total}}{W} = f\cos\theta + \sin\theta = (0.015)(0.981) + 0.196 = 0.0147 + 0.196 = 0.211$$

Required $TE/W = 0.211 < \mu = 0.85$ ✓ (vehicle can climb).

**Part B — Drawbar Pull:**

$TE_{max} = \mu W = 0.85 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 12{,}508$ N.

$R_r = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.981 = 216.6$ N.

$R_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.196 = 2884$ N.

$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (30/3.6)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 69.44 = 297.7$ N.

$$D = 12{,}508 - (216.6 + 2884 + 297.7) = 12{,}508 - 3398.3 = 9109.7 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 9.11 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part C — Suspension Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{30{,}000}{375}} = \sqrt{80} = 8.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = 1.424 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{1500}{2\sqrt{30{,}000 \times 375}} = \frac{1500}{2 \times 3354.1} = 0.2236$$

**Part D — Damped Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 8.944\sqrt{1-0.05} = 8.944 \times 0.9747 = 8.717 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_d \approx 8.72 \text{ rad/s} \quad (1.387 \text{ Hz})}$$

**Part E — Logarithmic Decrement (per cycle):**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.2236}{0.9747} = 1.441$$

After $n = 5$ cycles, amplitude ratio:

$$\frac{x_5}{x_0} = e^{-n\delta} = e^{-5 \times 1.441} = e^{-7.205} = 0.000747$$

So the suspension has decayed to **0.075%** of its initial amplitude after 5 cycles — a well-damped comfortable ride.
