## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Forces on a Vehicle Climbing a Grade

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vehicle on Slope of Angle Theta] --> B[Forces Acting on Vehicle]
    B --> C1[Tractive Effort TE - Up the Slope]
    B --> C2[Weight W - Vertically Down]
    B --> C3[Normal Reaction N - Perpendicular to Slope]
    B --> C4[Aerodynamic Drag Ra - Opposing Motion]
    C2 --> D1[Component along slope: W sin theta DOWN]
    C2 --> D2[Component normal to slope: W cos theta]
    D2 --> E1[Rolling Resistance: Rr = f W cos theta]
    C1 --> F[Force Balance: TE = Rr + Rg + Ra]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    C4 --> F
    F --> G{TE > Total Resistance?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Vehicle Climbs - Grade Ability OK]
    G -->|No| I[Vehicle Stalls - Fails Grade Test]
    F --> J[Drawbar Pull D = TE - Total Resistance]
    J --> K[Available for Towing External Load]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: SDOF Mass-Spring-Damper System Components

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Wall Fixed Support] -->|Stiffness k| B[Mass m - kg]
    B -->|Damping c| A
    A2[Dashpot c] -.-> B
    S1[Spring k] --- B
    B --> M[Displacement x from Equilibrium]
    M --> EOM[Equation of Motion]
    EOM --> ODE1[m x double dot + c x dot + k x = 0]
    ODE1 --> SOL[Classify by zeta]
    SOL --> CASE1[zeta = 0 Undamped Pure Sine]
    SOL --> CASE2[zeta less than 1 Underdamped Decaying Sine]
    SOL --> CASE3[zeta = 1 Critically Damped]
    SOL --> CASE4[zeta greater than 1 Overdamped]
    CASE1 --> OUT1[Constant Amplitude Forever]
    CASE2 --> OUT2[Amplitude Envelope e power minus zeta omega n t]
    CASE3 --> OUT3[Fastest Non-Oscillatory Return]
    CASE4 --> OUT4[Slow Exponential Return]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Free Vibration Response Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[SDOF System Disturbed from Equilibrium] --> IC[Initial Conditions: x0 and v0]
    IC --> EOM[Governing ODE: m xddot + c xdot + kx = 0]
    EOM --> DAMPCHECK{Damping Ratio zeta}
    DAMPCHECK -->|zeta = 0| UNDAMPED[Undamped Solution]
    DAMPCHECK -->|0 less than zeta less than 1| UNDER[Underdamped Solution]
    DAMPCHECK -->|zeta = 1| CRIT[Critically Damped Solution]
    DAMPCHECK -->|zeta greater than 1| OVER[Overdamped Solution]
    UNDAMPED --> UOUT[x t equals X cos omega n t minus phi]
    UNDER --> UOOUT[x t equals envelope times harmonic]
    CRIT --> COUT[x t equals C1 plus C2 t times e power minus omega n t]
    OVER --> OOUT[x t equals C1 e power s1 t plus C2 e power s2 t]
    UOOUT --> LOGDEC[Log Decrement delta equals 2 pi zeta over sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    LOGDEC --> EXPFIND[Experimental zeta determination]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Vehicle Performance — Grade Ability vs. Drawbar Pull Trade-off

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Engine Produces Power P] --> T[Available Torque Te]
    T --> TRANS[Transmission Ratio G]
    TRANS --> W[Tractive Effort at Wheels TE equals mu W]
    W --> R[Resistances: Rr plus Rg plus Ra]
    R --> D[Drawbar Pull D equals TE minus R]
    D --> EXT[Pulls External Load]
    W --> GA[Grade Ability Gmax equals TE over W minus f]
    GA --> GOUT[Vehicle can climb maximum slope]
    note1[Higher Gear: Higher Speed Lower TE Lower Grade Ability] -.-> T
    note2[Lower Gear: Lower Speed Higher TE Higher Grade Ability and D] -.-> T
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Grade Ability" of a vehicle. List the parameters that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

Grade ability is defined as the maximum slope (expressed as a percentage $G$ or angle $\theta$) that a vehicle can ascend at a specified constant speed under its own propulsion, while maintaining equilibrium between the available tractive effort and the total resisting forces.

$$G_{max} = 100\left(\frac{TE}{W} - f - \frac{R_a}{W}\right)\%$$

**Parameters affecting grade ability:**
1. Tractive effort $TE$ at the wheel-road interface
2. Vehicle weight $W$
3. Rolling resistance coefficient $f$
4. Aerodynamic drag $R_a$ (speed-dependent)
5. Coefficient of traction $\mu$ (governs maximum TE)
6. Slope angle $\theta$

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Tip: Always state BOTH the physical definition AND the formula to score full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meant by "Drawbar Pull" of a vehicle? How is it different from Tractive Effort?**

**Model Answer:**

**Drawbar Pull (D)** is the net usable horizontal force available at the vehicle's drawbar for towing an external load, after the vehicle's own resistances (rolling, grade, aerodynamic) have been overcome.

$$D = TE - (R_r + R_g + R_a)$$

**Difference from Tractive Effort:**

| Aspect | Tractive Effort (TE) | Drawbar Pull (D) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Total force at wheel-ground contact | Net force available at towing hook |
| Includes internal resistances? | Yes (full driving force) | No (already deducted) |
| Available for external work? | No | Yes |
| Typical magnitude | Higher | Lower (TE minus losses) |
| Symbol | $TE$ | $D$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** A tractor of weight $W = 25$ kN ascends a slope of 18% at a constant speed. The coefficient of rolling resistance is $f = 0.04$ and the coefficient of traction is $\mu = 0.7$. Determine:
(i) The tractive effort required.
(ii) The drawbar pull available.
(iii) Whether the tractor can climb a 25% slope under the same conditions (neglect aerodynamic drag).

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum grade ability of a vehicle and explain how it varies with the coefficient of traction and rolling resistance.

---

**Model Solution to Q3(a):**

**Given:**
- $W = 25$ kN = 25,000 N
- Grade = 18% ⇒ $\tan\theta = 0.18$ ⇒ $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.18) = 10.20°$
- $\sin\theta = 0.1771$, $\cos\theta = 0.9842$
- $f = 0.04$, $\mu = 0.7$

**(i) Tractive Effort Required**

For constant speed up the slope, the force balance is:

$$TE = W\sin\theta + fW\cos\theta$$

$$TE = 25{,}000 \times 0.1771 + 0.04 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.9842$$

$$TE = 4427.5 + 984.2 = 5411.7 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{TE \approx 5.41 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Drawbar Pull**

Maximum available tractive effort (limited by traction):

$$TE_{max} = \mu W = 0.7 \times 25{,}000 = 17{,}500 \text{ N}$$

Assuming no aerodynamic drag, drawbar pull is the surplus tractive effort:

$$D = TE_{max} - TE = 17{,}500 - 5411.7$$

$$\boxed{D = 12{,}088.3 \text{ N} \approx 12.09 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) Check for 25% Slope**

For 25% grade: $\tan\theta = 0.25$ ⇒ $\theta = 14.04°$, $\sin\theta = 0.2425$, $\cos\theta = 0.9701$.

Required TE:

$$TE_{req} = W\sin\theta + fW\cos\theta = 25{,}000 \times 0.2425 + 0.04 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.9701$$

$$TE_{req} = 6062.5 + 970.1 = 7032.6 \text{ N}$$

Maximum available TE = $17{,}500$ N.

Since $TE_{req} = 7032.6 \text{ N} < 17{,}500 \text{ N} = TE_{max}$:

**Yes, the tractor CAN climb the 25% slope.** ✓

Maximum theoretical grade (from $TE_{max}/W = \mu$):

$$G_{max} = 100(\mu - f) = 100(0.7 - 0.04) = 66\%$$

So 25% is well within the capability.

---

**Model Solution to Q3(b):**

**Derivation of Maximum Grade Ability:**

Consider a vehicle of weight $W$ moving up an incline of angle $\theta$ at constant speed. The forces acting along the slope are:
- Tractive effort $TE$ (up the slope)
- Weight component down the slope: $W\sin\theta$
- Rolling resistance: $fW\cos\theta$

Equilibrium at maximum grade:

$$TE_{max} = W\sin\theta_{max} + fW\cos\theta_{max}$$

The maximum tractive effort is limited by tire-road friction:

$$TE_{max} = \mu W$$

Substituting:

$$\mu W = W\sin\theta_{max} + fW\cos\theta_{max}$$

Dividing by $W$:

$$\mu = \sin\theta_{max} + f\cos\theta_{max}$$

Solving for $\theta_{max}$:

$$\mu - f\cos\theta_{max} = \sin\theta_{max}$$

Squaring and using $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:

$$(\mu - f\cos\theta_{max})^2 = 1 - \cos^2\theta_{max}$$

After expansion and simplification (or directly using small-angle approximation $\cos\theta \approx 1$):

$$\boxed{\,G_{max} \approx 100(\mu - f)\%\,}$$

**Variation with Parameters:**

- $G_{max}$ **increases linearly** with $\mu$ (coefficient of traction): better tire-road grip → steeper climb.
- $G_{max}$ **decreases linearly** with $f$ (rolling resistance): smoother surface / lower $f$ → steeper climb.
- $G_{max}$ is **independent of vehicle weight** (the formula cancels $W$), as long as all wheels are driven.
- For partial drive (e.g., 4x2 with rear-wheel drive), only the driven axle weight contributes to TE, so the grade ability depends on load distribution.

---

**ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the equation of motion of a damped single degree of freedom (SDOF) free vibration system. Define natural frequency, damped natural frequency, and damping ratio.

**(b)** A SDOF system has mass $m = 50$ kg, stiffness $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 200$ N·s/m. The system is given an initial displacement of $x_0 = 0.05$ m and released from rest. Determine:
(i) The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damped natural frequency $\omega_d$.
(ii) The damping ratio $\zeta$.
(iii) The displacement response $x(t)$.
(iv) The logarithmic decrement $\delta$.
(v) The number of cycles required for the amplitude to decay to 10% of its initial value.

---

**Model Solution to Q4(a):**

**Sketch Description (Mass-Spring-Damper System):**

A mass $m$ is connected to a fixed wall via a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$. The displacement $x$ is measured from the static equilibrium position.

**Derivation of Equation of Motion:**

Applying Newton's second law to the mass in the direction of $x$:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

The negative signs indicate both the spring force and the damping force oppose the motion (or velocity). Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

Standard form: $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$, where:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(undamped natural frequency, rad/s)}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} \quad \text{(damping ratio, dimensionless)}$$

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \quad \text{(damped natural frequency, rad/s)}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q4(b):**

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, $c = 200$ N·s/m, $x_0 = 0.05$ m, $v_0 = 0$.

**(i) Natural frequencies:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 20 \text{ rad/s} \quad (f_n = 3.183 \text{ Hz})}$$

Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{200}{2\sqrt{20{,}000 \times 50}} = \frac{200}{2 \times 1000} = 0.1$$

Since $\zeta = 0.1 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1-0.01} = 20\sqrt{0.99} = 20 \times 0.99499 = 19.90 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_d = 19.90 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.1}$$

**(iii) Displacement Response:**

Since $v_0 = 0$:

$$C_1 = x_0 = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d} = \frac{0 + 0.1 \times 20 \times 0.05}{19.90} = \frac{0.1}{19.90} = 0.005025$$

Therefore:

$$x(t) = e^{-2t}\left[0.05\cos(19.90t) + 0.005025\sin(19.90t)\right]$$

In amplitude-phase form:

$$X = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (0.005025)^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 2.526 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.05025 \text{ m}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.005025}{0.05}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.1005) = 0.1002 \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{x(t) = 0.05025 \cdot e^{-2t} \cos(19.90t - 0.1002) \text{ m}}$$

**(iv) Logarithmic Decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.1}{0.99499} = 0.6313$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 0.6313}$$

**(v) Number of cycles to decay to 10%:**

We require $\dfrac{x_n}{x_0} = 0.10$:

$$0.10 = e^{-n\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{-\ln(0.10)}{\delta} = \frac{\ln(10)}{0.6313} = \frac{2.3026}{0.6313}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 3.65 \text{ cycles}}$$

So approximately 3.65 oscillation cycles (about 4 full cycles) are required for the amplitude to drop to 10% of the initial value.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in EOM:** Forgetting the negative sign in $m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}$ — the spring force is *restoring*, not driving.
> 2. **Forgetting initial conditions:** Many students write the general solution but forget to evaluate $C_1, C_2$ using $x(0)$ and $\dot{x}(0)$. **Full marks require the complete displacement expression.**
> 3. **Confusing $\omega_n$ and $\omega_d$:** Damped natural frequency is ALWAYS less than undamped natural frequency (for $\zeta < 1$). For $\zeta \ll 1$, $\omega_d \approx \omega_n$.
> 4. **Mixing up grade resistance:** $R_g = W\sin\theta$ — NOT $W\tan\theta$. Grade percentage $G$ uses $\tan\theta$ (in degrees), but force calculations MUST use $\sin\theta$.
> 5. **Forgetting the "minus f" in grade ability:** $G_{max} = 100(\mu - f)$ is the correct small-angle formula, not $G_{max} = 100\mu$. Many students miss the rolling resistance subtraction.
> 6. **Units confusion in Log Decrement:** $\delta$ is dimensionless — always take ratios of amplitudes (not absolute values) to compute it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

#### Grade Ability

- [ ] **Definition:** Maximum slope a vehicle can climb at constant speed.
- [ ] **Core formula:** $G_{max} = 100(\mu - f)\%$ (small-angle approximation, no drag).
- [ ] **Must be positive:** $\mu > f$ for the vehicle to climb anything.
- [ ] **Independent of weight** (if all wheels are driven and grip is uniform).
- [ ] Decreased by aerodynamic drag, climbing speed, and rolling resistance.
- [ ] Increased by better tire-road friction (higher $\mu$).

#### Drawbar Pull

- [ ] **Definition:** Residual force available at the drawbar for towing.
- [ ] **Core formula:** $D = TE - (R_r + R_g + R_a)$.
- [ ] Maximum at lowest practical speed (high torque, low velocity).
- [ ] Drawbar pull coefficient $DPC = D/W$ is dimensionless and used in tractor/vehicle specifications.
- [ ] Tractive efficiency $\eta_t = D/TE$ is the fraction of tractive effort converted to useful work.

#### Forces on Slope

- [ ] Grade resistance: $R_g = W\sin\theta$
- [ ] Rolling resistance: $R_r = fW\cos\theta$
- [ ] Normal reaction: $N = W\cos\theta$
- [ ] Grade percentage: $G\% = 100\tan\theta$

#### Free Vibration of SDOF — Undamped

- [ ] EOM: $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$
- [ ] Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (rad/s)
- [ ] Period: $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ (s)
- [ ] Frequency in Hz: $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$
- [ ] Solution: $x(t) = x_0\cos\omega_n t + (v_0/\omega_n)\sin\omega_n t$
- [ ] Amplitude: $X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$

#### Free Vibration of SDOF — Damped

- [ ] EOM: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$
- [ ] Standard form: $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$
- [ ] Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$
- [ ] Damped natural frequency: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- [ ] **Case $\zeta = 0$:** Undamped — pure sine, constant amplitude.
- [ ] **Case $0 < \zeta < 1$:** Underdamped — decaying sine, envelope $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$.
- [ ] **Case $\zeta = 1$:** Critically damped — fastest non-oscillatory return.
- [ ] **Case $\zeta > 1$:** Overdamped — slow non-oscillatory return.
- [ ] Underdamped solution: $x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}[x_0\cos\omega_d t + ((v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0)/\omega_d)\sin\omega_d t]$

#### Logarithmic Decrement (Experimental Damping Identification)

- [ ] Definition: $\delta = (1/n)\ln(x_0/x_n)$
- [ ] Formula: $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- [ ] Small-$\zeta$ approximation: $\zeta \approx \delta/\pi$
- [ ] Number of cycles to decay from $x_0$ to $x_n$: $n = (1/\delta)\ln(x_0/x_n)$

#### Automotive Connection (Bonus Memory Aid)

- [ ] **Suspension design:** $\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ for ride comfort (underdamped).
- [ ] **Quarter-car model:** Used to analyze bounce (heave) mode of a vehicle.
- [ ] **Tractor:** High drawbar pull, low speed → first gear, high torque.
- [ ] **Sports car:** Lower drawbar pull, high speed → top gear, low torque, high power.
- [ ] **Off-road vehicle (4×4):** Higher grade ability due to lockable differentials and off-road tires (higher $\mu$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Method, Undamped and Damped Free Vibrations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Method, Undamped and Damped Free Vibrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Energy Method (Rayleigh's Energy Method)

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rayleigh's Energy Method** is an analytical technique used to determine the **natural frequency** of a conservative vibrating system by equating the **maximum kinetic energy** stored in the moving masses to the **maximum potential energy** stored in the elastic elements (springs). It is based on the principle of **conservation of mechanical energy** in a frictionless (loss-less) system.

Mathematically, the governing equality is:

$$T_{max} = U_{max}$$

where $T_{max}$ is the maximum **kinetic energy (KE)** and $U_{max}$ is the maximum **potential energy (PE)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a child on a swing. At the highest point of the swing's arc, the child is momentarily still — all the energy is "potential" (height). At the lowest point of the arc, the child is moving fastest — all the energy is "kinetic" (motion). The total energy at both points is the same because no energy is lost (ignoring air drag). The Energy Method simply uses this elegant conservation principle to determine *how fast* the swing oscillates, without ever having to write down the messy differential equation of motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Energy Method useful in Vehicle Dynamics?**
> Real automotive systems (suspension, chassis, driveline) are highly complex with many degrees of freedom. Solving coupled differential equations for such systems is tedious. The Energy Method provides an approximate but highly accurate natural frequency estimate by considering only the dominant masses and stiffnesses — a critical tool for NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) engineers.

---

## 1.2 Undamped Free Vibration

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Undamped Free Vibration** is an oscillatory motion of a system that occurs in the **absence of any dissipative force** (no friction, no air resistance, no damping) after the system is displaced from its equilibrium position and released. The amplitude of oscillation remains **constant indefinitely**, governed solely by the system's stiffness $k$ and mass $m$.

The governing scalar relationship is the natural (angular) frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a perfectly elastic steel ball bearing dropped into a frictionless bowl. It would oscillate back and forth, reaching exactly the same height on every swing — forever. There is no energy loss, so the motion never decays. An undamped vibration is the *ideal* (and physically unrealizable) mathematical abstraction of free oscillation.

---

## 1.3 Damped Free Vibration

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Damped Free Vibration** is an oscillatory motion in which a **dissipative force** (typically viscous damping proportional to velocity) progressively removes mechanical energy from the system. The amplitude of oscillation decays exponentially with time. The rate of decay depends on the **damping ratio** $\zeta$, defined as the ratio of actual damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_c$.

The canonical classification of damping regimes:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

| Regime | Damping Ratio | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Decaying oscillation |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return |
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Sustained oscillation |

### Conceptual Analogy
Now imagine the same ball bearing in a bowl filled with thick oil (honey). It still moves when you push it, but the oscillation dies out quickly — the oil "absorbs" the motion's energy as heat. A shock absorber (damper) in your car suspension behaves exactly this way: it controls the oscillation of the wheel after hitting a bump so the ride settles quickly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units**: Mass $m$ in **kg**, Stiffness $k$ in **N/m**, Damping coefficient $c$ in **Ns/m**, Natural frequency $\omega_n$ in **rad/s**, Frequency $f_n$ in **Hz**, Time period $T$ in **seconds**, Damping ratio $\zeta$ is **dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Rayleigh's Energy Method — Step-by-Step Logic

### Why this method works (The "Why")
For any conservative system, **total mechanical energy** $E$ is constant. At the **equilibrium (mean) position**:
- Displacement = 0 → Potential Energy $U = 0$
- Velocity = maximum → Kinetic Energy $T = T_{max}$

At the **extreme position** (maximum amplitude):
- Displacement = maximum ($x_{max}$) → Potential Energy $U = U_{max}$
- Velocity = 0 → Kinetic Energy $T = 0$

Because $E$ is constant:
$$T_{max} + U_{min} = T_{min} + U_{max} \implies T_{max} = U_{max}$$

### How to apply it (The "How")
1. **Assume a reasonable mode shape** $x(t)$ that satisfies the geometric boundary conditions (e.g., $x(t) = X_{max} \sin(\omega_n t)$).
2. **Compute the maximum KE**: substitute $\dot{x}_{max} = \omega_n X_{max}$ into $\frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^2$.
3. **Compute the maximum PE**: substitute $x_{max}$ into $\frac{1}{2} k x^2$.
4. **Equate** $T_{max}$ to $U_{max}$ and **solve** for $\omega_n$.

### Why it's approximate
The assumed mode shape is rarely the *true* mode shape. By Rayleigh's theorem, however, the error in the natural frequency is of the **second order** relative to the error in the assumed shape. Hence even a "guessed" shape yields a very accurate frequency estimate — invaluable for complex multi-DOF automotive systems.

---

## 2.2 Undamped Free Vibration — Logical Framework

### Step 1: Equation of Motion (Newton's 2nd Law)
For a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, displaced by $x$ from equilibrium:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

### Step 2: Characteristic Equation
Assume solution $x(t) = Ce^{\lambda t}$:

$$m\lambda^2 + k = 0 \implies \lambda = \pm i\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \pm i\omega_n$$

### Step 3: General Solution
$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

Equivalently: $x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$, where $X = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

---

## 2.3 Damped Free Vibration — Logical Framework

### Step 1: Equation of Motion with Viscous Damping
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

### Step 2: Normalize by $m$ and substitute $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

### Step 3: Characteristic Equation
$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

### Step 4: Three Solution Branches

**Case A — Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** Define the damped natural frequency:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$
$$x(t) = Xe^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

**Case B — Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):**
$$x(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Case C — Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):**
$$x(t) = C_1 e^{s_1 t} + C_2 e^{s_2 t}, \quad s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

---

## 2.4 Logarithmic Decrement (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Decrement** $\delta$ is the **natural logarithm of the ratio of successive peak amplitudes** in an underdamped system. It is the standard experimental method to determine the damping ratio $\zeta$ from a free-decay vibration test (e.g., using an oscilloscope on a real vehicle suspension).

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For **lightly damped systems** ($\zeta \ll 1$): $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural Angular Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Time Period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | s |
| 4 | Critical Damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | Ns/m |
| 5 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Damping Ratio via Decay | $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Decay Envelope | $x_{env}(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ | m |
| 10 | Energy Method Identity | $T_{max} = U_{max}$ | scalar, J |
| 11 | Equivalent Stiffness (Springs in Series) | $1/k_{eq} = \sum 1/k_i$ | N/m |
| 12 | Equivalent Stiffness (Springs in Parallel) | $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$ | N/m |
| 13 | Equivalent Mass (Springs in Parallel) | $1/m_{eq} = \sum 1/m_i$ | kg |
| 14 | Equivalent Mass (Springs in Series) | $m_{eq} = \sum m_i$ | kg |
| 15 | Rayleigh's Quotient | $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{\int_0^L EI(x)\left(\dfrac{d^2 Y}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx}{\int_0^L \rho A(x) Y^2 dx}$ | applies to distributed systems (e.g., drive shafts, leaf springs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In modern vehicle NVH testing, the logarithmic decrement is computed from accelerometer data by detecting successive peaks of a free-decay response (e.g., "ring-down" test on an instrumented suspension corner). The empirical $\zeta$ is then matched to the design target ($\zeta \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ for passenger car suspension ride comfort).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Method for a Single-DOF Spring–Mass System

### Given
A mass $m$ is suspended from a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The mass is displaced from equilibrium and released to oscillate freely (no damping).

### Step 1: Assume a Harmonic Motion Mode
We propose a sinusoidal displacement satisfying the boundary condition of returning to the original position:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t)$$

The velocity is obtained by differentiating with respect to time:

$$\dot{x}(t) = X \omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$$

### Step 2: Identify the Maximum Values
- Maximum displacement: $x_{max} = X$ (when $\sin(\omega_n t) = 1$)
- Maximum velocity: $\dot{x}_{max} = X\omega_n$ (when $\cos(\omega_n t) = 1$)

### Step 3: Compute Maximum Kinetic Energy

$$T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m\left(X\omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2 \cos^2(\omega_n t)$$

Maximum KE (when $\cos^2 = 1$):

$$T_{max} = \frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2$$

### Step 4: Compute Maximum Potential Energy
PE stored in the spring at maximum displacement $X$:

$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2}k X^2$$

### Step 5: Apply Conservation of Energy

$$T_{max} = U_{max}$$

$$\frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2 = \frac{1}{2}k X^2$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Natural Frequency
The amplitude $X^2$ cancels from both sides (proving that the result is **independent of amplitude**):

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

This is identical to the result obtained via the differential equation — confirming the **equivalence** of the energy approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equation of Motion of an Undamped System (D'Alembert's Principle)

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram
- Spring force (restoring): $F_s = -kx$ (acts opposite to displacement $x$)
- Inertia force (D'Alembert): $F_i = -m\ddot{x}$ (acts opposite to acceleration)

### Step 2: Apply D'Alembert's Principle (Equilibrium of dynamic forces)

$$F_i + F_s = 0$$

$$-m\ddot{x} - kx = 0$$

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + kx = 0}$$

### Step 3: Form the Characteristic Equation
Substituting the trial solution $x(t) = Ce^{\lambda t}$ yields:

$$mC\lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + kCe^{\lambda t} = 0$$

$$m\lambda^2 + k = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve the Characteristic Equation

$$\lambda^2 = -\frac{k}{m} = -\omega_n^2 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \pm i\omega_n$$

### Step 5: Write the General Solution
The roots are purely imaginary, giving an oscillatory solution:

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{+i\omega_n t} + C_2 e^{-i\omega_n t}$$

Applying Euler's identity $e^{\pm i\theta} = \cos\theta \pm i\sin\theta$:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants determined by initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:

$$A = x_0, \qquad B = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}$$

### Step 6: Compact Single-Trigonometric Form

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where:

$$\boxed{X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2}, \qquad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x_0 \omega_n}{v_0}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equation of Motion of a Damped Free Vibration System

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram
- Inertia force: $-m\ddot{x}$
- Damping force: $-c\dot{x}$ (viscous, opposes velocity)
- Spring force: $-kx$

### Step 2: Newton's Second Law

$$m\ddot{x} = -c\dot{x} - kx$$

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0}$$

### Step 3: Normalize by Mass
Divide throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

Define two shorthand constants:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}, \qquad 2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{c}{m}$$

The equation becomes the canonical second-order form:

$$\boxed{\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0}$$

### Step 4: Characteristic Equation
Substituting $x = Ce^{st}$:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

### Step 5: Apply the Quadratic Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2} \\
s &= -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

The sign of the radicand determines the nature of the response.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Solution: Underdamped Case ($\zeta < 1$)

### Step 1: Define the Damped Natural Frequency
The radicand $\zeta^2 - 1$ is **negative**, so the square root is **imaginary**:

$$\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} = i\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

Define:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

The two roots become **complex conjugates**:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

### Step 2: Write the General Solution

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{(-\zeta\omega_n + i\omega_d)t} + C_2 e^{(-\zeta\omega_n - i\omega_d)t}$$

Factor out the exponential decay envelope:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[C_1 e^{i\omega_d t} + C_2 e^{-i\omega_d t}\right]$$

### Step 3: Apply Euler's Identity

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[(C_1+C_2)\cos(\omega_d t) + i(C_1-C_2)\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

For real motion, redefine constants as $A = C_1 + C_2$ and $B = i(C_1 - C_2)$:

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]}$$

### Step 4: Equivalent Single-Trigonometric Form

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

---

## 3.5 Detailed Derivation: Logarithmic Decrement

### Given
Two successive peaks of an underdamped response at times $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$, separated by one full damped period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$.

### Step 1: Express Peak Amplitude
At a peak, $\sin(\omega_d t + \phi) = 1$, so the displacement is solely determined by the exponential envelope:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}$$

$$x_{n+1} = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_{n+1}}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Ratio

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}}{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_{n+1}}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n (t_{n+1} - t_n)} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

### Step 3: Take the Natural Logarithm

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}}$$

### Step 5: Invert for the Damping Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta^2(1 - \zeta^2) &= 4\pi^2 \zeta^2 \\
\delta^2 - \delta^2 \zeta^2 &= 4\pi^2 \zeta^2 \\
\delta^2 &= \zeta^2(4\pi^2 + \delta^2) \\
\zeta^2 &= \frac{\delta^2}{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

For small $\zeta$, $\delta \ll 2\pi$, so $\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2} \approx 2\pi$, giving the highly useful approximation:

$$\zeta \approx \frac{\delta}{2\pi}$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Demonstration (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Problem
A vehicle suspension has equivalent mass $m = 320$ kg (sprung mass quarter-car), spring stiffness $k = 28{,}000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 2{,}400$ Ns/m. Determine:
(a) Natural frequency and period
(b) Critical damping, damping ratio, and damped frequency
(c) Whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped
(d) Number of cycles for the amplitude to decay to 10% of its initial value

### Solution

**(a)** Natural angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{28{,}000}{320}} = \sqrt{87.5} = 9.354 \text{ rad/s}$$

Frequency in Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{9.354}{2\pi} = 1.489 \text{ Hz}$$

Period:

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{9.354} = 0.672 \text{ s}$$

**(b)** Critical damping:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{28{,}000 \times 320} = 2\sqrt{8{,}960{,}000} = 5986.6 \text{ Ns/m}$$

Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{2400}{5986.6} = 0.401$$

Damped frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 9.354 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.161} = 9.354 \times 0.916 = 8.571 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(c)** Since $0 < \zeta = 0.401 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it will oscillate with exponentially decaying amplitude.

**(d)** Logarithmic decrement:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi(0.401)}{0.916} = 2.751$$

After $n$ cycles, the amplitude is reduced by factor $e^{n\delta}$. Setting this equal to $0.10$ (10% of initial):

$$e^{n\delta} = 10 \implies n\delta = \ln 10 = 2.3026$$

$$n = \frac{2.3026}{2.751} = 0.837 \text{ cycles}$$

Hence the amplitude drops to 10% in **less than one full cycle** — confirming the suspension damps out road-excitation disturbances very effectively.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Energy Method Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify System Geometry and DOF] --> B[List All Masses mi and Stiffnesses ki]
    B --> C[Compute Equivalent m_eq and k_eq]
    C --> D[Assume Harmonic Mode Shape x sub t = X sin omega t]
    D --> E[Compute T_max equals half m_eq X squared omega squared]
    D --> F[Compute U_max equals half k_eq X squared]
    E --> G[Apply Conservation T_max = U_max]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Solve for omega_n = sqrt k_eq over m_eq]
    H --> I[Convert to f_n and T_n for Engineering Use]
    I --> J[Validate Against Differential Equation Solution]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Damped Vibration Response Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Parameters m, k, c] --> B[Compute zeta = c over 2 sqrt km]
    B --> C{Zeta Comparison}
    C -->|zeta equals 0| D1[Undamped Pure Oscillation x = X sin omega t]
    C -->|0 less than zeta less than 1| D2[Underdamped Decaying Oscillation]
    C -->|zeta equals 1| D3[Critically Damped Non Oscillatory]
    C -->|zeta greater than 1| D4[Overdamped Slow Non Oscillatory]
    D2 --> E[Amplitude Envelope X e power minus zeta omega t]
    D2 --> F[Damped Frequency omega_d = omega_n sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    D2 --> G[Logarithmic Decrement delta = 2 pi zeta over sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Logarithmic Decrement Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Apply Initial Displacement to System] --> B[Record Free Decay Response via Accelerometer]
    B --> C[Identify Consecutive Peaks x_n and x sub n+1]
    C --> D[Compute Ratio x_n over x sub n+1]
    D --> E[Take Natural Log delta = ln ratio]
    E --> F[Back Calculate zeta = delta over sqrt 4 pi squared + delta squared]
    F --> G[Validate Against Designed Damping Target]
```

## 4.4 Free-Body Schematic Representation (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mass m with Displacement x] -->|Inertia Force m x double dot| SUM[Dynamic Equilibrium Node]
    M -->|Damping Force c x dot| SUM
    M -->|Spring Force k x| SUM
    SUM -->|Net Force equals 0| EOM[Equation of Motion m x double dot + c x dot + k x = 0]
    EOM -->|Substitute omega_n and zeta| CAN[Canonical Form x double dot + 2 zeta omega_n x dot + omega_n squared x = 0]
```
