<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the principle of conservation of energy as applied to a vibrating system and write the Rayleigh's energy method equation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The principle of conservation of energy states that in a conservative (lossless) vibrating system, the **total mechanical energy remains constant** at all times. It oscillates between kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE), with the sum $T + U = \text{constant}$.

For a linear conservative system, the maximum kinetic energy equals the maximum potential energy:

$$T_{max} = U_{max}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}_{max}^2 = \frac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$$

This relation, when the assumed mode shape $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t)$ is substituted, yields the natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

The advantage of Rayleigh's method is that an *approximate* assumed mode shape still yields a very accurate $\omega_n$ (error of the **second order**).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define damping ratio. What are its values for underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped systems?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ is defined as the ratio of the actual viscous damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_c$:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity that characterises the nature of the system's free response:

| Condition | Value of $\zeta$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Sustained sinusoidal oscillation |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the damped natural frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ with the undamped natural frequency $\omega_n$. Examiners specifically test whether students report the correct frequency in a damped context.
> 2. **Always quote units** — natural frequency in **rad/s**, frequency in **Hz**, time period in **s**.
> 3. **Show the substitution step** in logarithmic decrement derivation; skipping the algebra loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Distinguish** between *underdamped* (oscillates) and *overdamped* (no oscillation) — a common conceptual error.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the natural frequency of an undamped single-degree-of-freedom spring-mass system using **Rayleigh's energy method**. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle suspension component is modeled as a mass-spring-damper system with $m = 250$ kg, $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, and $c = 1{,}500$ Ns/m. Determine:
   (i) Natural angular frequency $\omega_n$ and natural frequency $f_n$
   (ii) Critical damping coefficient $c_c$ and damping ratio $\zeta$
   (iii) Damped natural frequency $\omega_d$
   (iv) Whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The system is linear (spring obeys Hooke's law, damping is viscous).
2. The motion is a simple harmonic oscillation: $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t)$.
3. The system is conservative (no energy dissipation) for the energy method application.
4. The mass is rigid and the spring is massless.

**Step 1 — Displacement and Velocity:**

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t) \qquad \dot{x}(t) = X\omega_n \cos(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy:**

$$T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2 \cos^2(\omega_n t)$$

Maximum occurs when $\cos(\omega_n t) = \pm 1$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2$$

**Step 3 — Potential Energy:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}k X^2 \sin^2(\omega_n t)$$

Maximum occurs when $\sin(\omega_n t) = \pm 1$:

$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2}k X^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Energy Conservation:**

$$T_{max} = U_{max}$$

$$\frac{1}{2}m X^2 \omega_n^2 = \frac{1}{2}k X^2$$

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \qquad \text{(rad/s)}$$

This result is **independent of amplitude** $X$, confirming that the natural frequency is a system property.

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**(i) Natural angular frequency and natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} = 8.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{8.944}{2\pi} = 1.423 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Critical damping and damping ratio:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{20{,}000 \times 250} = 2\sqrt{5{,}000{,}000} = 4472.1 \text{ Ns/m}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{4472.1} = 0.335$$

**(iii) Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 8.944 \times \sqrt{1 - (0.335)^2} = 8.944 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.1122}$$

$$\omega_d = 8.944 \times \sqrt{0.8878} = 8.944 \times 0.9422 = 8.427 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iv) Damping regime:**
Since $0 < \zeta = 0.335 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it oscillates with exponentially decaying amplitude.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Derive the differential equation of motion for a damped free vibration system. Solve it completely for the **underdamped case** and discuss the significance of the term $\omega_d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a free vibration test on a vehicle quarter-car suspension, the amplitude reduces from $15$ mm to $2.5$ mm in **4 complete cycles**. Calculate:
   (i) The logarithmic decrement $\delta$
   (ii) The damping ratio $\zeta$
   (iii) If $m = 280$ kg, determine the damping coefficient $c$ and the spring stiffness $k$ given that $\omega_n = 9.0$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Equation of Motion (Newton's Second Law):**
For a mass $m$ subject to spring force $-kx$, damping force $-c\dot{x}$, and no external force:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Normalization:** Divide by $m$, define $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Characteristic Equation:** Assume $x = Ce^{st}$:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Underdamped Case ($\zeta < 1$):**
The radicand is negative: $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} = i\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$

Define the **damped natural frequency**:

$$\boxed{\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

The roots are complex conjugates:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

**General Solution:**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

or equivalently:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

**Significance of $\omega_d$:**
- It is the **actual oscillation frequency** of the damped system (always less than $\omega_n$).
- The oscillation decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = 1/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- As $\zeta \to 1$, $\omega_d \to 0$, confirming the cessation of oscillation at critical damping.
- For typical vehicle suspensions ($\zeta \approx 0.3$), $\omega_d \approx 0.95\,\omega_n$ — only a 5% reduction in frequency.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**(i) Logarithmic Decrement:**
For $n = 4$ cycles, the amplitude reduces from $x_0 = 15$ mm to $x_4 = 2.5$ mm. Successive peaks satisfy:

$$\frac{x_0}{x_n} = e^{n\delta}$$

$$e^{4\delta} = \frac{15}{2.5} = 6$$

$$4\delta = \ln 6 = 1.7918$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 0.4479}$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.4479}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + 0.2006}} = \frac{0.4479}{\sqrt{39.478 + 0.2006}} = \frac{0.4479}{6.302}$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.0711}$$

**Check (small-$\zeta$ approximation):** $\zeta \approx \delta/(2\pi) = 0.4479/6.283 = 0.0713$ ✓ (matches to 3 s.f.)

**(iii) Damping Coefficient and Spring Stiffness:**
Given $m = 280$ kg and $\omega_n = 9.0$ rad/s:

$$k = m\omega_n^2 = 280 \times 81 = \boxed{22{,}680 \text{ N/m}}$$

$$c = 2\zeta m\omega_n = 2 \times 0.0711 \times 280 \times 9.0 = \boxed{358.3 \text{ Ns/m}}$$

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Rayleigh's Energy Method** rests on $T_{max} = U_{max}$; it gives $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ assuming a sinusoidal mode shape.
- [x] **Rayleigh's Quotient** for distributed systems (e.g., drive shafts, leaf springs) is the energy-method generalization.
- [x] **Undamped natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — a property of the system, **independent of amplitude**.
- [x] **Critical damping** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ separates oscillatory from non-oscillatory behaviour.
- [x] **Damping ratio** $\zeta = c/c_c$ classifies the response:
  - $\zeta = 0$ → undamped
  - $0 < \zeta < 1$ → underdamped (decaying oscillation)
  - $\zeta = 1$ → critically damped
  - $\zeta > 1$ → overdamped
- [x] **Damped natural frequency** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — always **less than** $\omega_n$.
- [x] **Logarithmic decrement** $\delta = (2\pi\zeta)/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is used **experimentally** to find $\zeta$.
- [x] **Equivalence rules**: Springs in **series** → $1/k_{eq} = \sum 1/k_i$; springs in **parallel** → $k_{eq} = \sum k_i$.
- [x] **Common vehicle-dynamics targets**: Passenger car suspension damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ (ride comfort); engine mount $\zeta \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ (vibration isolation); seat suspension $\zeta \approx 0.5$–$0.7$.
- [x] **Approximation** for small damping: $\zeta \approx \delta/(2\pi)$ — memorize this for quick exam calculations.
- [x] **Energy method error**: Rayleigh's error in $\omega_n$ is of **second order** with respect to error in the assumed mode shape — hence it is exceptionally accurate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscous Damping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscous Damping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Viscous Damping** is the mechanism of energy dissipation in a dynamic system where the damping force is **directly proportional to the relative velocity** between the two ends of the damper. Mathematically, the viscous damping force is expressed as:

$$F_d = c \cdot v$$

where:
- $F_d$ = Damping force (in Newtons, **N**)
- $c$ = Viscous damping coefficient (in **N·s/m**)
- $v$ = Relative velocity across the damper (in **m/s**)

In vehicle dynamics, viscous damping is the dominant energy dissipation mechanism in **hydraulic shock absorbers**, **engine mounts**, and **bushings**. It arises from the fluid shearing action inside a piston-cylinder arrangement, where a viscous fluid (typically oil) resists motion with a force proportional to the flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *viscous* specifically refers to **velocity-proportional** resistance. Do not confuse with **Coulomb (dry friction) damping**, where the force is constant and independent of velocity.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing your hand through a tub of water. The faster you move your hand, the harder the water pushes back. That "push-back" force from the water is **viscous damping**.

In a car suspension, the **shock absorber** works exactly like this. Inside it, a piston moves through thick oil. When the car hits a bump, the piston is forced to move through the oil quickly, and the oil resists this motion. The faster the suspension tries to move, the stronger the resistance. This converts the unwanted kinetic energy of bouncing into **heat** in the oil, calming the ride.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Units:**
> - Damping coefficient $c$ has units of **N·s/m** (equivalent to **kg/s**).
> - Typical automotive damper coefficients range from **1000 N·s/m** (soft, comfort) to **5000+ N·s/m** (firm, sport).
> - Energy dissipated per cycle = area enclosed by the force-velocity hysteresis loop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown — How Viscous Damping Works

### Step 1: The Physical Mechanism
- A viscous damper contains a **piston** sliding inside a **cylinder filled with oil**.
- The piston has small **orifices (holes)** that allow oil to flow from one side to the other.
- As the piston moves, oil is forced through these orifices at a rate proportional to velocity.
- The pressure difference created across the piston generates a **resisting force** that opposes the motion.

### Step 2: The Linear Viscous Law
- For low velocities (laminar flow), the pressure-velocity relationship is linear.
- This is called **linear viscous damping**, governed by Newton's law of viscosity.
- The damping force always **opposes** the direction of motion, hence the negative sign:
$$F_d = -c \cdot \dot{x}$$

### Step 3: Why "Viscous" and Not "Frictional"?
- **Viscous damping**: Force depends on **velocity** (zero at rest, maximum at peak velocity).
- **Coulomb/friction damping**: Force is **constant** regardless of velocity.
- The velocity-dependence makes viscous damping ideal for **vibration control** because it scales with the intensity of motion.

### Step 4: Real-World Utility in Vehicle Engineering
- **Ride comfort**: Damps body bounce after road disturbances.
- **Handling stability**: Controls pitch during braking and roll during cornering.
- **Tire contact**: Maintains tire-road contact by suppressing wheel hop.
- **NVH reduction**: Attenuates structure-borne noise and vibrations.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter / Equation** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Standard Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Damping Force | $F_d = c \cdot v$ | N | Force resisting motion |
| 2 | Damping Coefficient | $c$ | N·s/m | Resistance of damper |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{m \cdot k}}$ | dimensionless | Degree of damping vs critical |
| 4 | Natural Frequency (undamped) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | System's natural oscillation rate |
| 5 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Actual oscillation rate with damping |
| 6 | Critical Damping Coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{m \cdot k}$ | N·s/m | Minimum $c$ to prevent oscillation |
| 7 | Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless | Decay per cycle |
| 8 | Energy Dissipated per Cycle | $\Delta E = \pi c \omega X^2$ | J | Energy lost as heat |
| 9 | SDOF Equation of Motion | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | — | Free vibration with damping |
| 10 | Non-linear Damping (high velocity) | $F_d = c_1 v + c_2 v \vert v \vert$ | N | Bilinear damper model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Damping Condition:** When $\zeta = 1$, the system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time without oscillating**. For passenger cars, designers typically target $\zeta = 0.2$ to $0.4$ (underdamped) for ride comfort.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Equation of Motion for a Damped SDOF System

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper $c$ in parallel, free to move on a frictionless surface.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram and Force Balance
Three forces act on the mass:
- **Inertia force** (Newton's 2nd law): $F_i = m\ddot{x}$
- **Spring force** (restoring): $F_s = kx$
- **Damping force** (velocity-proportional): $F_d = c\dot{x}$

Applying Newton's second law (sum of forces = inertia):

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

This is the **standard form of the damped free vibration equation**.

### Step 2: Assume a Solution of Exponential Form
We try a solution of the form:

$$x(t) = X e^{st}$$

### Step 3: Compute Derivatives
The first and second time derivatives are:

$$\dot{x}(t) = s X e^{st}$$
$$\ddot{x}(t) = s^2 X e^{st}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the Equation of Motion
Plugging into $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$:

$$m s^2 X e^{st} + c s X e^{st} + k X e^{st} = 0$$

### Step 5: Factor Out the Common Terms
Since $X e^{st} \neq 0$, we divide both sides by it:

$$m s^2 + c s + k = 0$$

This is the **characteristic equation** of the system.

### Step 6: Solve the Quadratic for s
Using the quadratic formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m} \\
s &= \frac{-c}{2m} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{c}{2m}\right)^2 - \frac{k}{m}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Introduce the Standard Parameters
Define the **undamped natural frequency**:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

And the **damping ratio**:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n}$$

### Step 8: Rewrite the Roots in Standard Form
Substituting $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$:

$$s = -\zeta \omega_n \pm \omega_n \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

### Step 9: Analyze the Three Cases

**Case 1 — Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** Roots are complex conjugates.
Let $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$, the **damped natural frequency**. The solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

The system oscillates with a decaying amplitude (exponential envelope).

**Case 2 — Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):** Roots are real and equal: $s = -\omega_n$. The solution is:

$$x(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

No oscillation; fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium.

**Case 3 — Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** Roots are real and distinct.
$s_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm \omega_n \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$. The solution is:

$$x(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$$

Slow return to equilibrium without oscillation.

## Derivation 2: Energy Dissipated per Cycle (Underdamped Case)

For a harmonically oscillating mass with displacement $x(t) = X \sin(\omega_d t)$ and velocity $\dot{x}(t) = \omega_d X \cos(\omega_d t)$:

### Step 1: Instantaneous Power Dissipated by the Damper
The damper's instantaneous power dissipation is:

$$P(t) = F_d \cdot \dot{x} = c \dot{x}^2 = c \omega_d^2 X^2 \cos^2(\omega_d t)$$

### Step 2: Integrate Over One Period T
The energy dissipated per cycle is:

$$\Delta E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Period $T = 2\pi / \omega_d$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \int_{0}^{2\pi/\omega_d} c \omega_d^2 X^2 \cos^2(\omega_d t) \, dt \\
&= c \omega_d^2 X^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_d} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta \quad \text{where } \theta = \omega_d t \\
&= c \omega_d^2 X^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_d} \cdot \pi \\
&= \pi c \omega_d X^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Final Result
For lightly damped systems where $\omega_d \approx \omega_n$:

$$\boxed{\Delta E \approx \pi c \omega_n X^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dissipated energy is proportional to the **square of the amplitude** $X^2$ and the **damping coefficient** $c$. This is why doubling the damping coefficient doubles the energy removed per bounce, while doubling the amplitude quadruples it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Physical Model of a Viscous Damper

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional architecture and flow paths of a typical automotive viscous damper (telescopic shock absorber).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROAD INPUT BUMP"] --> B["PISTON ROD TOP MOUNT"]
    B --> C["PISTON HEAD MOVES DOWN"]
    C --> D{"CHECK VALVE STATUS"}
    D --> E["COMPRESSION STROKE OIL FLOW"]
    E --> F["OIL THROUGH LOWER ORIFICES"]
    F --> G["RESERVOIR CHAMBER BOTTOM"]
    G --> H["DAMPING FORCE F equals c times v"]
    H --> I["VEHICLE BODY SETTLES"]
    I --> J["REBOUND STROKE OIL RETURNS"]
    J --> K["FLOW REVERSES VIA REBOUND VALVES"]
    K --> L["DAMPING FORCE OPPOSES MOTION"]
    L --> M["KINETIC ENERGY CONVERTED TO HEAT"]
```

## Diagram 2: SDOF Damped System Architecture

The following block diagram maps the signal/force flow in a mass-spring-damper system — the foundational model for vehicle suspension analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["EXCITATION FORCE F OF T"] --> sum1(("SUMMATION NODE"))
    sum1 --> integ1["INTEGRATOR 1 OVER s"]
    integ1 --> out1["VELOCITY V OF T"]
    out1 --> out2["DAMPING FEEDBACK c times v"]
    out2 --> sum1
    sum1 --> integ2["INTEGRATOR 2 OVER s"]
    integ2 --> out3["DISPLACEMENT X OF T"]
    out3 --> out4["SPRING FEEDBACK k times x"]
    out4 --> sum1
    out1 --> out5["MASS ELEMENT m times a"]
    out5 --> sum1
```

## Diagram 3: Damping Regimes Classification

The following tree diagram classifies the three damping regimes based on the damping ratio $\zeta$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["DAMPING REGIME BASED ON ZETA"] --> br1["ZETA LESS THAN 1 UNDERDAMPED"]
    root --> br2["ZETA EQUALS 1 CRITICAL"]
    root --> br3["ZETA GREATER THAN 1 OVERDAMPED"]
    br1 --> res1["OSCILLATES WITH DECAYING AMPLITUDE"]
    br1 --> f1["FORMULA omega_d equals omega_n sqrt of 1 minus zeta squared"]
    br2 --> res2["FASTEST RETURN WITHOUT OSCILLATION"]
    br2 --> f2["FORMULA c equals 2 sqrt of m times k"]
    br3 --> res3["SLOW EXPONENTIAL DECAY NO OSCILLATION"]
    br3 --> f3["FORMULA s equals minus zeta omega_n plus minus omega_n sqrt of zeta squared minus 1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Vehicle Context:** Most passenger cars operate in the **underdamped regime** ($\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$). Critical damping is generally undesirable because it feels "harsh" — the body settles too quickly and transmits road texture to passengers. Overdamping causes the suspension to "pack up" during successive bumps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define viscous damping. How does it differ from Coulomb damping?**

**Model Answer:**
Viscous damping is a form of energy dissipation in which the damping force is **directly proportional to the relative velocity** across the damper, expressed as $F_d = cv$.

**Differences:**

| **Aspect** | **Viscous Damping** | **Coulomb Damping** |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Force-velocity relationship | Linear: $F_d = cv$ | Constant: $F_d = \mu N$ |
| Velocity dependence | Force varies with velocity | Force independent of velocity |
| Physical source | Fluid shear (oil) | Dry sliding friction |
| Behavior at rest | Zero force | Sticking force |

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the damping ratio $\zeta$ in vehicle suspension design?**

**Model Answer:**
The damping ratio $\zeta$ is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the level of damping in a system relative to critical damping: $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{mk})$.

**Significance in vehicle suspensions:**
- $\zeta = 0$: Undamped — oscillations continue forever (unacceptable).
- $\zeta < 1$ (typically 0.2–0.4): **Underdamped** — preferred for ride comfort, allows controlled decay.
- $\zeta = 1$: **Critically damped** — fastest non-oscillatory return; feels harsh.
- $\zeta > 1$: **Overdamped** — suspension packs up; poor road holding.

**[3 Marks: 1 for formula + 2 for design significance]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a single degree of freedom (SDOF) damped vibrating system and obtain its solution for the underdamped case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle suspension is modeled as a SDOF system with mass $m = 250$ kg, stiffness $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 1500$ N·s/m. Determine:
  (i) The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and the damping ratio $\zeta$.
  (ii) Whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped.
  (iii) The damped natural frequency $\omega_d$ and the logarithmic decrement $\delta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The free body diagram of a mass $m$ with spring $k$ and damper $c$ gives, by Newton's second law:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Assuming a solution $x(t) = X e^{st}$, we obtain the characteristic equation:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

Solving for $s$:

$$s = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

For the **underdamped case** ($\zeta < 1$), the discriminant is negative. Defining $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c/(2m\omega_n)$:

$$s = -\zeta \omega_n \pm i \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm i \omega_d$$

The general solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

This shows an **oscillation with exponentially decaying amplitude** of envelope $X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$. **[7 Marks: 1 for EOM, 2 for characteristic equation, 2 for roots, 2 for final solution]**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 250$ kg, $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, $c = 1500$ N·s/m.

**(i) Natural frequency and damping ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} = 8.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{1500}{2\sqrt{250 \times 20{,}000}} = \frac{1500}{2\sqrt{5{,}000{,}000}} = \frac{1500}{2 \times 2236.07} = 0.335$$

**(ii) Damping regime:**
Since $\zeta = 0.335 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it will oscillate with decaying amplitude. This is typical and desirable for vehicle suspensions.

**(iii) Damped natural frequency and logarithmic decrement:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 8.944 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.1124} = 8.944 \times 0.9421 = 8.426 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.335}{0.9421} = \frac{2.105}{0.9421} = 2.234$$

**[7 Marks: 2 for (i), 1 for (ii), 2 for $\omega_d$, 2 for $\delta$]**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Define damping ratio. For a vehicle suspension with $m = 300$ kg, $k = 25{,}000$ N/m, and $c = 1800$ N·s/m, find the critical damping coefficient, damping ratio, and the logarithmic decrement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three regimes of damping (underdamped, critically damped, overdamped) with suitable sketches of displacement-time responses. State which regime is preferred for passenger car suspension and why. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{300 \times 25{,}000} = 2\sqrt{7{,}500{,}000} = 2 \times 2738.6 = 5477.2 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1800}{5477.2} = 0.3286$$

**Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{25{,}000}{300}} = 9.129 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.3286}{\sqrt{1 - 0.108}} = \frac{2.0645}{0.9438} = 2.187$$

**[7 Marks: 2 for $c_c$, 2 for $\zeta$, 3 for $\delta$ with intermediate steps]**

**(b) Conceptual Answer (7 marks):**

**Regime 1 — Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** The mass oscillates about equilibrium with amplitude decreasing exponentially. Mathematically $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$.

**Regime 2 — Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):** The mass returns to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without oscillating. Mathematically $x(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$.

**Regime 3 — Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** The mass returns to equilibrium slowly without oscillating, slower than the critical case. Mathematically $x(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ with both $s_1, s_2$ real and negative.

**Preferred Regime for Passenger Cars: Underdamped ($\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$):**
- Provides a comfortable ride by allowing gentle oscillation and decay.
- Critical damping feels "harsh" — the body settles too abruptly.
- Overdamping causes the suspension to "pack up" during successive bumps, reducing tire contact and safety.

**[7 Marks: 2 per regime explanation + 1 for preferred regime justification]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. Always state **rad/s** for frequency, **N·s/m** for damping coefficient.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega_n$ and $\omega_d$.** $\omega_n$ is undamped; $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ is the damped frequency actually observed.
> 3. **Logarithmic decrement formula** — students often write $\delta = 2\pi\zeta$ (which is an approximation valid only for $\zeta \ll 1$). Use the exact form unless the problem specifies small damping.
> 4. **Sign conventions** — damping force is always **opposing** motion; do not drop the negative sign in derivations.
> 5. **Confusing critical damping coefficient $c_c$ with damping coefficient $c$** — $c_c$ is a *reference value*, not the actual damping in the system.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscous damping** is **velocity-proportional**: $F_d = cv$.
- Damping coefficient $c$ has units **N·s/m** = **kg/s**.
- Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ is the master parameter governing system response.
- **Three regimes**: Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$), Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
- **Passenger car target**: $\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ (underdamped) — balances comfort and road holding.
- The **characteristic equation** $ms^2 + cs + k = 0$ governs all free-vibration analysis.
- **Damped natural frequency**: $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ — always less than $\omega_n$.
- **Logarithmic decrement** $\delta$ measures decay rate between successive oscillation peaks.
- **Energy dissipated per cycle**: $\Delta E = \pi c \omega_d X^2$ — proportional to $c$ and $X^2$.
- Real-world dampers are often **non-linear** (bilinear or velocity-squared) at high speeds; the linear model is an approximation valid for small oscillations.
- The viscous damping force always **opposes** velocity — this sign convention is critical in derivations.
- **Critical damping coefficient** $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ is the threshold value separating oscillatory from non-oscillatory behavior.
- Engine mounts use **high damping** (to absorb vibration), while suspension dampers use **moderate damping** (to allow controlled body motion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Damping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Critical Damping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Critical Damping** is the specific boundary condition of a second-order damped vibrating system in which the **damping ratio (ζ)** equals exactly **unity (ζ = 1)**. It represents the precise threshold at which the system's transient response transitions from oscillatory (underdamped) behavior to purely exponential (non-oscillatory) decay, returning to equilibrium in the **minimum possible time without any overshoot**.

In vehicle dynamics, the governing equation of a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) suspension mass-spring-damper system is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

At critical damping, the damping coefficient equals the **critical damping coefficient**:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} \quad \text{with damping ratio} \quad \zeta = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Critical damping is the dividing boundary between **underdamped (ζ < 1)** and **overdamped (ζ > 1)** response. It is the fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium — a vital benchmark when designing vehicle suspension absorbers and seat dampers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Cork in Honey

Imagine pushing a **cork suspended in honey** and then releasing it:
- **No damping (ζ = 0):** The cork would oscillate up and down forever (pure simple harmonic motion).
- **Light damping (ζ < 1):** The cork bobs up and down a few times before settling — this is a typical car suspension.
- **Critical damping (ζ = 1):** The cork returns to its original position **as fast as possible without ever overshooting or oscillating** — like a perfectly designed hydraulic door-closer.
- **Heavy damping (ζ > 1):** The cork slowly creeps back to its position without overshooting — sluggish and lazy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** A critically damped **car suspension** would return the body to its rest height after a bump in the shortest possible time. However, most passenger cars use **underdamped suspensions (ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4)** because slight overshoot improves ride comfort by allowing a brief secondary body motion.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Damping Ratio (ζ):** Dimensionless, defined as actual damping to critical damping — **ζ = c / c_c**.
- **Natural Frequency (ωₙ):** √(k/m) in **rad/s**.
- **Damped Natural Frequency (ω_d):** ωₙ√(1 − ζ²) — becomes **zero** at critical damping.
- **Settling Time (for ζ = 1):** Approximately **4.6 / ωₙ** seconds to within 1% of equilibrium.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Damped Oscillator Framework

A vehicle body modeled as an SDOF mass-spring-damper obeys Newton's second law. Rearranging the equation into **standard form** gives:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where the parameters are defined as:

- **ωₙ = √(k/m)** — undamped natural frequency (rad/s)
- **ζ = c / (2√(km))** — damping ratio (dimensionless)
- **c** — actual damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- **k** — spring stiffness (N/m)
- **m** — suspended mass (kg)

### 2.2 The Three Damping Regimes

The character of the solution depends entirely on the discriminant of the characteristic equation:

| Regime | Condition | Roots of Characteristic Equation | Response Type | Vehicle Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underdamped** | ζ < 1 | Complex conjugates | Oscillatory decay | Passenger car suspension (ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4) |
| **Critically Damped** | ζ = 1 | Real, repeated (−ωₙ, −ωₙ) | Fastest non-oscillatory return | Seat dampers, door-closers, precision instruments |
| **Overdamped** | ζ > 1 | Real, distinct, negative | Slow exponential creep | Rare; gun recoil mechanisms |

### 2.3 Solution of the Critically Damped System

For ζ = 1, the characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + 2\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0 \implies (s + \omega_n)^2 = 0$$

The **repeated root** s = −ωₙ requires the general solution form:

$$x(t) = (A + B t)\,e^{-\omega_n t}$$

where A and B are determined by initial conditions x(0) = x₀ and ẋ(0) = v₀:

$$x(t) = \left[ x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0)\,t \right] e^{-\omega_n t}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize the column boundaries and the corresponding response form.

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Condition at Critical Damping | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Damping ratio | ζ = c ∕ (2√(km)) | ζ = 1 | dimensionless |
| Critical damping coefficient | c_c = 2√(km) | c = c_c | N·s/m |
| Natural frequency | ωₙ = √(k/m) | unchanged | rad/s |
| Damped frequency | ω_d = ωₙ√(1 − ζ²) | ω_d = 0 | rad/s |
| Characteristic roots | s = −ζωₙ ± ωₙ√(ζ² − 1) | s₁ = s₂ = −ωₙ | 1/s |
| Time-domain response | x(t) = e^(−ζωₙt)[A cos(ω_d t) + B sin(ω_d t)] | x(t) = (A + Bt)e^(−ωₙt) | m |
| Settling time (2% band) | t_s ≈ 4 ∕ (ζωₙ) | t_s ≈ 4 ∕ ωₙ | s |
| Logarithmic decrement | δ = (2πζ) ∕ √(1 − ζ²) | δ → ∞ (no oscillations) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In vehicle **ride and handling trade-off studies**, critical damping is the *theoretical optimum* for speed of recovery. However, automotive engineers deliberately move to **underdamped** regions (ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4) because:
> 1. A small overshoot reduces subjective harshness on sharp bumps.
> 2. Tire-road contact loads are better maintained with controlled oscillation.
> 3. **Sky-hook** and **ground-hook** semi-active control strategies switch the effective damping ratio across the critical boundary in real time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Damping Condition

**Starting Equation (Free vibration of SDOF suspension mass):**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 1 — Normalize by mass m:**

Divide every term by m:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 2 — Identify the natural frequency and the damping term:**

By definition:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \text{and} \quad 2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{c}{m}$$

Substitute these into the normalized equation:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

This is the **standard form of the second-order linear homogeneous ODE**.

**Step 3 — Assume an exponential solution:**

Try x(t) = e^(st). Then ẋ = se^(st) and ẍ = s²e^(st). Substituting:

$$s^2 e^{st} + 2\zeta\omega_n s e^{st} + \omega_n^2 e^{st} = 0$$

Factor out e^(st) (which is never zero):

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

This is the **characteristic (auxiliary) equation**.

**Step 4 — Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2}$$

Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by 2:

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the critical damping condition:**

The expression under the square root, **ζ² − 1**, controls the root type:
- ζ² − 1 < 0  ⇒  complex roots (oscillatory)
- ζ² − 1 = 0  ⇒  **repeated real root** (critical damping)
- ζ² − 1 > 0  ⇒  two distinct real roots (overdamped)

Setting ζ² − 1 = 0 gives **ζ = 1**, hence the damping coefficient must be:

$$c_c = 2\zeta\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{km}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the repeated root:**

$$s_{1,2} = -\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{0} = -\omega_n \quad (\text{repeated root})$$

**Step 7 — Construct the general solution:**

When a characteristic equation has a *repeated* real root s = −ωₙ, the standard exponential ansatz e^(st) yields only one independent solution. The second independent solution is **t·e^(st)** (a consequence of the theory of linear ODEs). The general solution is therefore:

$$x(t) = A e^{-\omega_n t} + B t e^{-\omega_n t} = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Step 8 — Apply initial conditions:**

Let x(0) = x₀ and ẋ(0) = v₀.

From x(0) = x₀:

$$x(0) = (A + B \cdot 0)e^{0} = A = x_0$$

Differentiate the solution:

$$\dot{x}(t) = B e^{-\omega_n t} - \omega_n(A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

Evaluate at t = 0:

$$\dot{x}(0) = B - \omega_n A = v_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$$

Therefore, the **fully determined critically damped response** is:

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = \left[\,x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0)\,t\,\right]e^{-\omega_n t}\,}$$

### 3.2 Verification: Why This is the *Fastest* Non-Oscillatory Return

**Comparative settling times** (to within 1% of equilibrium):

| Damping Ratio ζ | Settling Time t_s (approx.) | Overshoot? |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 (undamped) | ∞ (oscillates forever) | Yes — constant |
| 0.1 | ≈ 40 ∕ ωₙ | ~73% |
| 0.4 | ≈ 10 ∕ ωₙ | ~25% |
| 0.7 | ≈ 5.7 ∕ ωₙ | ~4.6% |
| **1.0 (critical)** | **≈ 4.6 ∕ ωₙ** | **None** |
| 1.5 (overdamped) | ≈ 6.7 ∕ ωₙ | None |
| 2.0 (overdamped) | ≈ 9.2 ∕ ωₙ | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Proof of Optimality:** For overdamped systems (ζ > 1), the slower root s₂ = −ζωₙ + ωₙ√(ζ² − 1) dominates the long-time decay. As ζ increases, this slower root approaches zero, *increasing* settling time. Thus ζ = 1 minimizes settling time among all non-oscillatory cases.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A vehicle's quarter-car suspension has **m = 250 kg**, **k = 20,000 N/m**, and the damper is set to its **critical** value. The car hits a 0.05 m bump at t = 0 with v₀ = 0.

**Step 1 — Compute ωₙ:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute c_c:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{20000 \times 250} = 2\sqrt{5{,}000{,}000} = 2 \times 2236.07 \approx 4472.14\ \text{N·s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply response formula with x₀ = 0.05 m, v₀ = 0:**

$$x(t) = \left[0.05 + (0 + 8.944 \times 0.05)\,t\right]e^{-8.944 t}$$

$$x(t) = (0.05 + 0.4472\,t)\,e^{-8.944 t}$$

**Step 4 — Find peak time of decay (max magnitude after t = 0):**

For overdamped/critical, max occurs at t = 0 if x₀ > 0. Otherwise, compute dx/dt = 0:

$$\dot{x}(t) = 0.4472\,e^{-8.944 t} - 8.944(0.05 + 0.4472 t)e^{-8.944 t} = 0$$

$$0.4472 = 8.944(0.05 + 0.4472 t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.05 + 0.4472 t = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 0$$

So the body simply decays monotonically from 0.05 m — no overshoot, as expected.

**Step 5 — Compute settling time (1% band):**

$$t_s \approx \frac{4.6}{\omega_n} = \frac{4.6}{8.944} \approx 0.514\ \text{seconds}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Damping Regime Classification

The following functional architecture maps the classification logic of a vehicle suspension response based on the damping ratio ζ.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Suspension SDOF System] --> B[Compute Damping Ratio zeta]
    B --> C{zeta Value}
    C -->|zeta less than 1| D[UNDERDAMPED]
    C -->|zeta equals 1| E[CRITICALLY DAMPED]
    C -->|zeta greater than 1| F[OVERDAMPED]
    D --> D1[Oscillatory Decay]
    D --> D2[Passenger Cars zeta 0.2 to 0.4]
    D --> D3[Roots are complex conjugates]
    E --> E1[Fastest Non-Oscillatory Return]
    E --> E2[Seat Dampers and Door Closers]
    E --> E3[Repeated Real Root minus omega_n]
    F --> F1[Slow Exponential Creep]
    F --> F2[Gun Recoil Buffers]
    F --> F3[Two Distinct Negative Real Roots]
    E1 --> E4[Time Response A plus B t times exp minus omega_n t]
    D1 --> D5[Time Response exp decay times harmonic]
    F1 --> F6[Time Response sum of two exp decays]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Graph — Response Curve Morphology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DAMPING_REGIMES [Damping Response Curves]
        direction TB
        nodeA[UNDAMPED zeta equals 0] --> nodeB[Constant Amplitude Oscillation]
        nodeC[UNDERDAMPED zeta less than 1] --> nodeD[Decaying Sinusoid]
        nodeE[CRITICAL zeta equals 1] --> nodeF[Monotonic Exponential Decay]
        nodeG[OVERDAMPED zeta greater than 1] --> nodeH[Sluggish Exponential Return]
    end
    nodeB --> nodeI[NEVER SETTLES]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Oscillates then settles]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Fastest non oscillatory return to equilibrium]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Slow non oscillatory return]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Quarter-Car Critical Damping Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m1[Mass m quarter car body] --> s1[Spring k]
    m1 --> d1[Damper c equals c sub c equals 2 sqrt km]
    s1 --> g1[Ground Input x sub g]
    d1 --> g1
    c1[Critical Condition zeta equals 1] --> m1
    c1 --> s1
    c1 --> d1
    o1[Output Body Displacement x of t] --> m1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are **functional architecture flows**, not physical scale drawings. They map the logical relationships between damping regimes, response types, and their real-world vehicle applications — directly relevant to KTU module-end viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define critical damping. State the condition for critical damping in terms of the damping coefficient and the system parameters m and k.*

**Model Answer:**

**Critical damping** is the condition of a second-order damped system in which the damping ratio **ζ = 1**, representing the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory response. At this condition, the system returns to equilibrium in the **minimum possible time without overshoot**.

The condition is expressed as:

$$c = c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$$

where **c** is the actual damping coefficient, **k** is the spring stiffness, and **m** is the equivalent mass. The damping ratio is defined as ζ = c / c_c, hence at critical damping ζ = 1.

The time-domain response becomes:

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

where ωₙ = √(k/m). The two characteristic roots coincide at s = −ωₙ.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for condition formula, 1 for time-response form]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why are passenger car suspensions designed to be underdamped (ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4) and not critically damped, even though critical damping gives the fastest return?*

**Model Answer:**

Although critical damping gives the fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium, it is **not preferred for passenger car suspensions** for the following reasons:

1. **Ride Comfort:** A small amount of overshoot in underdamped systems allows the sprung mass to settle with reduced harshness. Pure exponential decay (critical damping) feels "dead" and transmits high-frequency content to passengers.
2. **Tire Load Variation:** Underdamped oscillations help maintain a more consistent dynamic tire-road contact load during transient disturbances.
3. **Subjective Preference:** Human主观 comfort studies show that a lightly underdamped response (ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4) feels more compliant than a critically damped one.
4. **Practical Design Constraint:** Critical damping corresponds to a relatively high damping force, which would require larger damper piston areas, increasing unsprung mass.

Critical damping *is* used in **seat dampers, instrument mounts, and door-closers** where zero overshoot is mandatory.

**[3 Marks: 1 per valid reason, max 3]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the condition for critical damping in a single-degree-of-freedom damped vibrating system. Show that at critical damping the characteristic equation has a repeated root and the response is of the form x(t) = (A + Bt)e^(−ωₙt). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quarter-car model has sprung mass **m = 320 kg** and suspension stiffness **k = 28,000 N/m**. The damper is set to its critical value. The vehicle is subjected to an initial displacement of **0.08 m** with zero initial velocity. Compute:
   (i) The critical damping coefficient c_c.
   (ii) The natural frequency ωₙ.
   (iii) The displacement response x(t).
   (iv) The time required for the displacement to fall below 1% of the initial value. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Critical Damping Condition:**

**Step 1:** Equation of motion for SDOF damped system:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 2:** Divide by m:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute ωₙ² = k/m and 2ζωₙ = c/m:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 4:** Characteristic equation:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Quadratic formula:

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 6:** For critical damping, the discriminant must be zero:

$$\zeta^2 - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = 1$$

**Step 7:** Damping condition:

$$c = c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$$

**Step 8:** Repeated root s = −ωₙ. Standard ODE theory for repeated roots gives the general solution:

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

Applying x(0) = x₀ and ẋ(0) = v₀:

$$A = x_0, \quad B = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$$

Hence:

$$x(t) = \left[x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0)t\right]e^{-\omega_n t} \quad \blacksquare$$

**[7 marks awarded: 1 per step, with the boxed final answer carrying 1 mark]**

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** m = 320 kg, k = 28,000 N/m, x₀ = 0.08 m, v₀ = 0.

**(i) Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{28000 \times 320} = 2\sqrt{8{,}960{,}000}$$

$$c_c = 2 \times 2993.33 \approx 5986.66\ \text{N·s/m}$$

**(ii) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{28000}{320}} = \sqrt{87.5} \approx 9.354\ \text{rad/s}$$

**(iii) Displacement response:**

Substituting x₀ = 0.08, v₀ = 0, ωₙ = 9.354:

$$B = v_0 + \omega_n x_0 = 0 + 9.354 \times 0.08 = 0.7483$$

$$x(t) = (0.08 + 0.7483\,t)\,e^{-9.354\,t}\ \text{meters}$$

**(iv) Time to fall below 1% of initial value:**

We require |x(t)| < 0.01 × 0.08 = 8 × 10⁻⁴ m.

For small t, the linear term dominates; for large t, the envelope is the linear term 0.7483 t e^(−9.354 t). The maximum of this envelope occurs when:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(te^{-\omega_n t}\right) = e^{-\omega_n t}(1 - \omega_n t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{peak} = \frac{1}{\omega_n} = \frac{1}{9.354} \approx 0.107\ \text{s}$$

The settling time (1% criterion) for critical damping is:

$$t_s \approx \frac{4.6}{\omega_n} = \frac{4.6}{9.354} \approx 0.492\ \text{seconds}$$

Verification: at t = 0.492 s,

$$x(0.492) = (0.08 + 0.7483 \times 0.492)e^{-9.354 \times 0.492}$$

$$= (0.08 + 0.3682)e^{-4.602} = 0.4482 \times 0.01 \approx 0.00448\ \text{m}$$

This is 5.6% of x₀. To reach 1%, t is slightly longer. Using t_s ≈ 5.3/ωₙ (a refined 1% formula for ζ = 1):

$$t_s \approx \frac{5.3}{9.354} \approx 0.567\ \text{seconds}$$

At t = 0.567 s:

$$x(0.567) = (0.08 + 0.7483 \times 0.567)e^{-9.354 \times 0.567}$$

$$= (0.08 + 0.4243)e^{-5.304} = 0.5043 \times 0.00496 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 3.1\%$$

The 1% threshold is reached around t ≈ 0.62 s. **[Approximate answer acceptable within KTU valuation norms.]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a vehicle suspension modeled as a damped SDOF system, derive expressions for the three damping cases: underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped. Compare their time responses with suitable sketches of displacement vs. time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's seat suspension has m = 40 kg, k = 8,000 N/m, and damping ratio ζ = 1.0 (critical). Find:
   (i) The natural frequency.
   (ii) The damping coefficient.
   (iii) The time to reach half the initial displacement (released from x₀ = 0.05 m, v₀ = 0). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the three damping cases:**

Starting from the standard form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

with roots:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Case 1 — Underdamped (ζ < 1):**

Define damped frequency ω_d = ωₙ√(1 − ζ²). The solution is:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

This is a **decaying sinusoid** that oscillates about the equilibrium with amplitude envelope e^(−ζωₙt).

**Case 2 — Critically Damped (ζ = 1):**

Roots coincide at s = −ωₙ. The solution is:

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

This is a **monotonic exponential decay** with no oscillations — the **fastest non-oscillatory return**.

**Case 3 — Overdamped (ζ > 1):**

Two distinct negative real roots:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

The solution is a **sum of two decaying exponentials**:

$$x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$$

The slower root dominates the long-time behavior, leading to a **sluggish return** to equilibrium.

**Comparison Table:**

| Case | Roots | Response | Overshoot | Settling Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Underdamped | Complex | Decaying sinusoid | Yes | Moderate |
| **Critical** | **Real repeated** | **(A+Bt)e^(−ωₙt)** | **No** | **Fastest** |
| Overdamped | Real distinct | Two exponentials | No | Slow |

The sketch of displacement vs. time should show:
- **Underdamped:** Sinusoidal oscillations decaying toward zero.
- **Critical:** A single hump decaying monotonically — the curve never crosses the time axis after t = 0.
- **Overdamped:** A flatter, slower decay than the critical case.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** m = 40 kg, k = 8,000 N/m, ζ = 1, x₀ = 0.05 m, v₀ = 0.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{8000}{40}} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.142\ \text{rad/s}$$

**(ii) Damping coefficient (critical):**

$$c = 2\zeta\sqrt{km} = 2 \times 1 \times \sqrt{8000 \times 40} = 2 \times \sqrt{320{,}000}$$

$$c = 2 \times 565.685 \approx 1131.37\ \text{N·s/m}$$

**(iii) Time to reach half initial displacement:**

We need x(t) = 0.025 m. The response is:

$$x(t) = (0.05 + 14.142 \times 0.05 \times t)\,e^{-14.142\,t}$$

$$x(t) = (0.05 + 0.7071\,t)\,e^{-14.142\,t}$$

Set x(t) = 0.025 and solve numerically:

$$(0.05 + 0.7071 t)\,e^{-14.142 t} = 0.025$$

Using iterative trial:

- At t = 0.04 s:  x = (0.05 + 0.02828) × e^(−0.5657) = 0.07828 × 0.5681 = 0.0445 m (too high)
- At t = 0.08 s:  x = (0.05 + 0.05657) × e^(−1.1314) = 0.1066 × 0.3224 = 0.0344 m (too high)
- At t = 0.10 s:  x = (0.05 + 0.07071) × e^(−1.4142) = 0.1207 × 0.2431 = 0.0293 m (slightly high)
- At t = 0.11 s:  x = (0.05 + 0.0778) × e^(−1.5556) = 0.1278 × 0.2112 = 0.0270 m (close)
- At t = 0.12 s:  x = (0.05 + 0.0849) × e^(−1.6970) = 0.1349 × 0.1832 = 0.0247 m (just below 0.025)

**Therefore, t ≈ 0.118 seconds** (interpolating between 0.11 and 0.12 s).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize by m:** Students often write c/m as 2ζωₙ but lose a mark by not explicitly stating the standard form derivation step.
> 2. **Confusing ζ with c:** The damping ratio ζ is dimensionless; c has units of N·s/m. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if these are interchanged in the final answer.
> 3. **Missing the "repeated root" argument:** For the ζ = 1 case, simply writing s = −ωₙ without explaining that it is a *repeated* root loses 1 mark. The form (A + Bt)e^(−ωₙt) is *not* obvious from the standard exponential ansatz — it requires justification.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply initial conditions:** A common error is leaving the response as x(t) = (A + Bt)e^(−ωₙt) without computing A and B. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Settling time formula:** Use t_s ≈ 4/(ζωₙ) for the 2% band and t_s ≈ 4.6/(ζωₙ) for the 1% band. Mixing them up costs marks.
> 6. **Unit mismatch in numerical problems:** Always include units (rad/s, N·s/m, seconds) in the final answer — KTU examiners explicitly look for this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Critical damping is the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory response**, defined by **ζ = 1** or equivalently **c = c_c = 2√(km)**.
- The **characteristic equation** at critical damping has a **repeated real root** s = −ωₙ, leading to the response form **x(t) = (A + Bt)e^(−ωₙt)**.
- **No overshoot** occurs in critical damping, and the system returns to equilibrium in the **minimum time** for any non-oscillatory damper setting.
- **Damped natural frequency ω_d = 0** at critical damping — the system cannot complete even one cycle.
- **Settling time** at ζ = 1 is approximately **4.6/ωₙ** for the 1% criterion.
- **Passenger car suspensions use ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4** (underdamped) for ride comfort — *not* critical damping.
- **Critical damping is preferred** in seat dampers, instrument mounts, gun recoil buffers, and door closers where zero overshoot is mandatory.
- The **standard form of the SDOF equation** is **ẍ + 2ζωₙẋ + ωₙ²x = 0**, where **ωₙ = √(k/m)**.
- The **damping ratio** is defined as the ratio of actual to critical damping: **ζ = c / c_c**.
- Always **normalize the equation of motion by m** before identifying ωₙ and ζ — this is a 1-mark step in KTU solutions.
- The **discriminant of the characteristic equation, ζ² − 1**, determines the response regime: negative ⇒ underdamped, zero ⇒ critical, positive ⇒ overdamped.
- In **MATLAB/Simulink simulations** of vehicle suspension, the damping coefficient is often specified directly as c in N·s/m, not as ζ — convert using c = 2ζ√(km) before simulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Takeaway:** Critical damping is a **theoretical benchmark**, not a practical design point for passenger vehicles. Always state in answers *why* a particular damping ratio is chosen for a given automotive application, and *quantify* the trade-off between settling time, overshoot, and ride comfort.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logarithmic Decrement (Numericals Only), Transmissibility
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logarithmic Decrement (Numericals Only), Transmissibility

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 1 of *Vehicle Stability and Dynamics* lays the analytical foundation of **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF)** vibratory systems. The two key analytical tools for characterising damping in such systems are the **Logarithmic Decrement** (time-domain technique) and **Transmissibility** (frequency-domain technique).

---

## 1.1 Logarithmic Decrement — Formal Definition

The **logarithmic decrement (δ)** is defined as the **natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive peak amplitudes** of a damped free-vibration response, separated by one complete cycle of oscillation.

Mathematically, for a viscously damped underdamped SDOF system:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{1}{n}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

where $x_0, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ are successive peak amplitudes and $n$ is the number of cycles between the chosen peaks.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Fading Swing:** Imagine a child on a playground swing. Each oscillation is slightly smaller than the previous one because air resistance and friction at the pivot "leak" energy. The **logarithmic decrement** is simply a mathematical way of *quantifying how fast the swing dies down*. A swing that loses 50 % of its height per cycle has a *high* δ; a near-frictionless swing has a *tiny* δ. In a car suspension test, engineers measure the oscillation of a sprung mass after a bump — the rate of decay of that bounce directly reveals the damping coefficient of the shock absorber.

---

## 1.2 Transmissibility — Formal Definition

**Transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the **ratio of the amplitude of the steady-state transmitted response of a system to the amplitude of the input (forcing or base) excitation**.

$$TR = \frac{X_{\text{transmitted}}}{X_{\text{input}}}$$

For **base excitation** (the dominant case in vehicle dynamics — road undulations transmitted to the chassis through the suspension):

$$TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where $r = \omega / \omega_n$ is the **frequency ratio** and $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car Over a Speed Bump:** When a car wheel hits a speed bump, the suspension isolates the cabin from the road input. **Transmissibility** tells us *how much* of that bump's amplitude actually reaches the passengers. A car with soft, well-damped suspension has $TR < 1$ at low driving speeds → the cabin is *shielded*. A stiff, lightly-damped sports car at high speed over rough tarmac may have $TR > 1$ → the cabin *amplifies* the bump! This is precisely why transmissibility is at the heart of ride-comfort engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Students must NOT confuse **Transmissibility (TR)** with **Magnification Factor (MF or Q)**. MF is the ratio of dynamic-to-static deflection under a *direct harmonic force*; TR is the ratio under *base excitation*. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 1.3 The Three Critical Frequency Zones of Transmissibility

| Zone | Frequency Range | Behaviour | Vehicle Implication |
|------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Isolation Zone** | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $TR < 1$ and decreases with $r$ | Suspension works as a vibration isolator — good for ride comfort |
| **Resonance Zone** | $r = 1$ | $TR$ reaches a **maximum** (peak) | Damping is the *only* thing preventing catastrophic amplitudes |
| **Amplification Zone** | $0 < r < \sqrt{2}$ | $TR > 1$ | Suspension *amplifies* road inputs — must be avoided in normal driving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Isolation (KTU Favourite):** Effective vibration isolation is achieved **only when** $r > \sqrt{2}$. Below this, the suspension makes things worse. This single line has appeared in multiple KTU university examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logarithmic Decrement — Conceptual Mechanics

For a viscously damped underdamped SDOF system, the free-vibration response is:

$$x(t) = X_0\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\,\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

### Successive peaks occur at:
- $t_1 = T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$  
- $t_2 = 2T_d$  
- $\dots$  
- $t_n = nT_d$

### Step-by-step logical deduction:

1. At $t_1$: peak amplitude $x_1 = X_0\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n T_d}$  
2. At $t_2$: peak amplitude $x_2 = X_0\,e^{-2\zeta\omega_n T_d}$  
3. The ratio $x_1 / x_2 = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$  
4. Taking natural log: $\delta = \zeta\omega_n T_d$  
5. Substituting $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ and $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

6. **Inverting** this to find damping ratio from a measured δ:

$$\boxed{\;\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}\;}$$

7. For **small damping** ($\zeta < 0.2$), the approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ is highly accurate and is **the version KTU examiners prefer** for quick numerical work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why logarithmic?** Because the energy dissipated per cycle is proportional to the *square* of the amplitude. Taking the natural log linearises an exponential decay — a powerful mathematical tool that converts multiplicative decay into an additive constant.

---

## 2.2 Transmissibility — Conceptual Mechanics

For a mass $m$ on a base that moves as $y(t) = Y\sin\omega t$, with spring $k$ and damper $c$ between mass and base, the equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x}-\dot{y}) + k(x-y) = 0$$

Assuming steady-state response $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$, substituting and solving yields the **transmitted displacement** amplitude ratio:

$$TR = \frac{X}{Y} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle suspension tuning:** Choose $k$ and $c$ so that at the dominant engine-idle frequency $r > \sqrt{2}$.
- **Engine mount design:** Minimise transmission of engine firing-frequency vibrations to the chassis.
- **Seat cushion analysis:** Quantify how much road noise reaches the driver.
- **Building base-isolation systems:** Protect structures from earthquake inputs (analogous concept).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single table is the most-tested content in Module 1 — memorise it!**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Special Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Logarithmic Decrement (general) | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$ | Used when peaks are $n$ cycles apart |
| 2 | Logarithmic Decrement (successive) | $\delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1}{x_2}\right)$ | Most common KTU form |
| 3 | δ–ζ exact relation | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Rigorous form |
| 4 | δ–ζ small-damping approx. | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | Valid for $\zeta < 0.2$ |
| 5 | ζ from measured δ | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2+\delta^2}}$ | **Most asked inversion** |
| 6 | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Used when converting $T_d$ |
| 7 | Transmissibility (base excit.) | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | **KTU 2024 focus** |
| 8 | Magnification Factor (force excit.) | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | **Do not confuse with TR** |
| 9 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | Engine speed / natural frequency |
| 10 | Critical isolation frequency | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | Threshold: $TR=1$ at $\zeta=0$ |
| 11 | Max. TR frequency | $r_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}}$ | Valid for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| 12 | Max. TR value | $TR_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r_{\text{max}})^2}}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Evaluate at $r_{\text{max}}$ |
| 13 | Unit relationships | $f_n = \omega_n/2\pi$, $\;f_d = \omega_d/2\pi$ | For RPM-to-Hz conversions |
| 14 | N to RPM | $N\,[\text{rpm}] = \dfrac{60\,\omega}{2\pi}$ | For engine excitation |
| 15 | Energy decay per cycle | $\dfrac{E_n}{E_0} = e^{-2\delta}$ | Useful for advanced problems |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** If the problem gives data **$n$ cycles apart** (e.g., "after 20 cycles"), always use the **$n$-cycle form**: $\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$. Using the successive-peaks formula will give a wrong answer worth 7 marks. Read the problem carefully!

---

## 2.4 Physical Constants & Units (Bold Per Protocol)

- **Logarithmic Decrement (δ):** **dimensionless** (a ratio of amplitudes → ratio is unitless → log is unitless).
- **Damping ratio (ζ):** **dimensionless**, always in the range $\zeta \in [0, 1]$ for underdamped systems.
- **Transmissibility (TR):** **dimensionless** ratio.
- **Frequency ratio (r):** **dimensionless** ratio.
- **Damped natural frequency ($\omega_d$):** **rad/s**.
- **Undamped natural frequency ($\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$):** **rad/s**.
- **Critical damping coefficient ($c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$):** **N·s/m**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** All numerical answers should be reported to **three significant figures** unless the data justifies more. Units are mandatory for dimensional quantities.

---

## 3.1 Complete Numerical — Logarithmic Decrement (Standard KTU Format)

### **Problem 3.1.A** — *Find the Damping Ratio from Decaying Amplitudes*

A vehicle suspension strut, when excited by a sharp impulse, produces decaying oscillations. The peak amplitudes measured in successive cycles are:

| Cycle $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Amplitude $x_n$ (mm) | 50.0 | 44.5 | 39.6 | 35.2 | 31.4 | 27.9 |

Determine:
1. The logarithmic decrement (use first and last readings — i.e., 5 cycles apart).  
2. The damping ratio $\zeta$ of the system.  
3. The actual natural frequency $\omega_n$ if the damped period $T_d = 0.40\,\text{s}$.

---

### **Solution (Exhaustive — No Step Skipped)**

**Given data:**
$$x_0 = 50.0\,\text{mm},\quad x_5 = 27.9\,\text{mm},\quad n = 5,\quad T_d = 0.40\,\text{s}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the $n$-cycle formula:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{5}\ln\!\left(\frac{50.0}{27.9}\right)$$

Compute the ratio first:

$$\frac{50.0}{27.9} = 1.7921$$

Compute the natural log:

$$\ln(1.7921) = 0.5839$$

Divide by $n = 5$:

$$\delta = \frac{0.5839}{5} = 0.1168$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 0.117\,\text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**Step 2 — Find damping ratio $\zeta$** using the rigorous inversion formula:

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

Compute $4\pi^2$:

$$4\pi^2 = 4 \times 9.8696 = 39.4784$$

Compute $\delta^2$:

$$\delta^2 = (0.1168)^2 = 0.01364$$

Add them:

$$4\pi^2 + \delta^2 = 39.4784 + 0.01364 = 39.4920$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{39.4920} = 6.2843$$

Divide $\delta$ by the result:

$$\zeta = \frac{0.1168}{6.2843} = 0.01858$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.0186\,\text{(dimensionless, or about 1.86 \%)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with small-damping approximation** ($\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$):
> $\zeta \approx \delta/(2\pi) = 0.1168/6.2832 = 0.01859$. The match is to four decimal places — confirming $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ is excellent for $\zeta < 0.05$.

**Step 3 — Find $\omega_n$ from $T_d$:**

The damped natural frequency is:

$$\omega_d = \frac{2\pi}{T_d} = \frac{2\pi}{0.40} = 15.708\,\text{rad/s}$$

Relate $\omega_d$ and $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

Solve for $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_n = \frac{\omega_d}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Compute $\zeta^2$:

$$\zeta^2 = (0.0186)^2 = 3.46\times 10^{-4}$$

Compute $1 - \zeta^2$:

$$1 - \zeta^2 \approx 1 - 0.000346 = 0.99965$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{0.99965} = 0.99983$$

Divide:

$$\omega_n = \frac{15.708}{0.99983} = 15.711\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 15.71\,\text{rad/s} \quad \text{and} \quad f_n = 2.50\,\text{Hz}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Take-away:** When $\zeta$ is very small, $\omega_n \approx \omega_d$. Always compute the difference to demonstrate awareness; it is the *process* of computing $1-\zeta^2$ that scores the methodology marks.

---

### **Problem 3.1.B** — *Find δ Given Time Between Peaks*

The amplitude of a vehicle body bouncing on its suspension decays from **80 mm to 32 mm** in **8 seconds**. The measured period of damped oscillation is **0.5 s**.

Find: (a) the number of cycles in 8 s, (b) the logarithmic decrement, (c) the damping ratio.

---

### **Solution**

**Step (a) — Number of cycles:**

$$n = \frac{t}{T_d} = \frac{8.0}{0.5} = 16\,\text{cycles}$$

**Step (b) — Logarithmic decrement (using the $n$-cycle form because $n=16$):**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \frac{1}{16}\ln\!\left(\frac{80}{32}\right)$$

Compute ratio: $80/32 = 2.5$

Compute $\ln$: $\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$

$$\delta = \frac{0.9163}{16} = 0.05727$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 0.0573}$$

**Step (c) — Damping ratio:**

Using $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$:

$$4\pi^2 + \delta^2 = 39.4784 + 0.00328 = 39.4817$$

$$\sqrt{39.4817} = 6.2835$$

$$\zeta = \frac{0.05727}{6.2835} = 0.00911$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.00911 \text{ (or about 0.91 \%)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A student might incorrectly use $\delta = \ln(80/32) = 0.9163$ instead of dividing by $n=16$. This is the *single most common mistake* in KTU logarithmic decrement problems. **Always check the wording: "successive peaks" vs. "after $n$ cycles"!**

---

## 3.2 Complete Numerical — Transmissibility (Standard KTU Format)

### **Problem 3.2.A** — *Base Excitation: Calculate Transmissibility*

A car of mass **$m = 1200$ kg** is modelled as a single-degree-of-freedom system with a suspension of stiffness **$k = 4.0 \times 10^5$ N/m** and damping coefficient **$c = 1.8 \times 10^4$ N·s/m**. The car is driven over a road whose surface varies sinusoidally at a frequency of **$f = 1.5$ Hz** with an amplitude of **$Y = 25$ mm**.

Find:
1. The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$.
2. The frequency ratio $r$.
3. The transmissibility $TR$.
4. The amplitude of vibration experienced by the car body $X$.

---

### **Solution**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency $\omega_n$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.0\times 10^5}{1200}} = \sqrt{333.33} = 18.257\,\text{rad/s}$$

Corresponding $f_n$:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{18.257}{6.2832} = 2.906\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio $\zeta$:**

Critical damping:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{(4.0\times 10^5)(1200)} = 2\sqrt{4.8\times 10^8}$$

$$c_c = 2 \times 21908.9 = 43817.8\,\text{N·s/m}$$

Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1.8\times 10^4}{43817.8} = 0.4108$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.411}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio $r$:**

Forcing frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(1.5) = 9.4248\,\text{rad/s}$$

Frequency ratio:

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{9.4248}{18.257} = 0.5162$$

$$\boxed{r = 0.516}$$

**Step 4 — Transmissibility $TR$:**

First, compute all sub-terms:

$$r^2 = (0.5162)^2 = 0.2665$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 0.2665 = 0.7335$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 = (0.7335)^2 = 0.5380$$

$$2\zeta r = 2(0.4108)(0.5162) = 0.4241$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (0.4241)^2 = 0.1799$$

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 1.1799$$

**Numerator of $TR$:**

$$\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2} = \sqrt{1.1799} = 1.0863$$

**Denominator of $TR$:**

$$\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2} = \sqrt{0.5380 + 0.1799} = \sqrt{0.7179} = 0.8473$$

**Transmissibility:**

$$TR = \frac{1.0863}{0.8473} = 1.282$$

$$\boxed{TR = 1.282}$$

**Step 5 — Body amplitude $X$:**

$$X = TR \cdot Y = 1.282 \times 25 = 32.05\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{X = 32.1\,\text{mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** $TR = 1.282 > 1$ and $r = 0.516 < \sqrt{2} = 1.414$ — the suspension is in the **amplification zone**. The road bump is *magnified* by ~28 % at the cabin! This explains why a poorly tuned suspension feels *worse* than driving on smooth roads.

---

### **Problem 3.2.B** — *Find Frequency Ratio for Unity Transmissibility*

For a vehicle suspension system with damping ratio **$\zeta = 0.25$**, determine the **frequency ratio $r$** at which the transmissibility $TR = 1$. Also find the **range of $r$** for which effective isolation ($TR < 1$) is achieved.

---

### **Solution**

**Set $TR = 1$:**

$$1 = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Square both sides:

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 1 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

Cancel $(2\zeta r)^2$ from both sides:

$$(1-r^2)^2 = 1$$

Take square root:

$$1 - r^2 = \pm 1$$

**Case 1:** $1 - r^2 = +1 \Rightarrow r^2 = 0 \Rightarrow r = 0$ (trivial, no oscillation).

**Case 2:** $1 - r^2 = -1 \Rightarrow r^2 = 2 \Rightarrow r = \sqrt{2}$.

$$\boxed{r = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414}$$

**For effective isolation $TR < 1$:**

From $(1-r^2)^2 > 1$:

$$r^2 - 1 > 1 \quad (\text{since } r>1)$$

$$r^2 > 2 \Rightarrow r > \sqrt{2}$$

$$\boxed{r > 1.414 \text{ for } TR < 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Beautiful Result:** The frequency ratio at which $TR = 1$ is **independent of damping ratio $\zeta$**! This is a hallmark KTU result — students should derive it from scratch and note the independence explicitly for full marks.

---

### **Problem 3.2.C** — *Engine Excitation Transmissibility*

A car engine weighs **$M = 200$ kg** and runs at **$N = 3000$ rpm**. A four-cylinder engine produces primary firing frequency $f = 2N/60$ Hz. The engine mount has stiffness **$k = 5\times 10^5$ N/m** and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.10$. Calculate the transmissibility of vibration to the chassis.

---

### **Solution**

**Step 1 — Firing frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi\left(\frac{2 \times 3000}{60}\right) = 2\pi(100) = 628.32\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency of mount:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{5\times 10^5}{200}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{628.32}{50} = 12.566$$

> [!NOTE]
> $r = 12.566 \gg \sqrt{2}$ — we are deep in the **isolation zone**.

**Step 4 — Compute $TR$:**

$$r^2 = 157.96, \quad 1-r^2 = -156.96, \quad (1-r^2)^2 = 24635$$

$$2\zeta r = 2(0.10)(12.566) = 2.513, \quad (2\zeta r)^2 = 6.316$$

**Numerator:** $\sqrt{1 + 6.316} = \sqrt{7.316} = 2.705$

**Denominator:** $\sqrt{24635 + 6.316} = \sqrt{24641} = 156.97$

$$TR = \frac{2.705}{156.97} = 0.01723$$

$$\boxed{TR = 0.0172}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Meaning:** Only **1.72 %** of engine vibration reaches the chassis — the mount is performing brilliantly as an isolator. This is exactly the design intent: stiff enough to hold the engine, but with $r \gg \sqrt{2}$ to isolate the cabin from high-frequency engine harmonics.

---

### **Problem 3.2.D** — *Find Resonance Peak Transmissibility*

For a vehicle suspension with $\zeta = 0.30$, find:
1. The frequency ratio at which transmissibility is **maximum**.
2. The maximum transmissibility value.

---

### **Solution**

**Step 1 — Frequency of maximum $TR$:**

The maximum of $TR$ occurs at:

$$r_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}}$$

Check validity: $2\zeta^2 = 2(0.09) = 0.18 < 1$ ✓

$$1 - 2\zeta^2 = 0.82$$

$$r_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.82}} = \frac{1}{0.9055} = 1.1044$$

$$\boxed{r_{\text{max}} = 1.104}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum $TR$ value:**

Substitute $r = r_{\text{max}}$ into $TR$ formula. The expression simplifies to:

$$TR_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\cdot\sqrt{1 + \frac{(2\zeta)^2}{1-2\zeta^2}}$$

Simpler direct substitution approach:

Compute $2\zeta r_{\text{max}}$:

$$2\zeta r_{\text{max}} = 2(0.30)(1.1044) = 0.6626$$

$$(2\zeta r_{\text{max}})^2 = 0.4391$$

Compute $1 - r_{\text{max}}^2$:

$$r_{\text{max}}^2 = 1.2197, \quad 1 - r_{\text{max}}^2 = -0.2197$$

$$(1 - r_{\text{max}}^2)^2 = 0.04827$$

**Numerator:** $\sqrt{1 + 0.4391} = \sqrt{1.4391} = 1.1996$

**Denominator:** $\sqrt{0.04827 + 0.4391} = \sqrt{0.4874} = 0.6981$

$$TR_{\text{max}} = \frac{1.1996}{0.6981} = 1.7184$$

$$\boxed{TR_{\text{max}} = 1.72}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often use $r_{\text{max}} = 1$ (the undamped resonance) when computing $TR_{\text{max}}$. This gives a slightly wrong value. The correct maximum occurs at $r_{\text{max}} = 1/\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$, which only equals 1 when $\zeta = 0$. Use the corrected formula for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C -- No --> E[Use delta = ln x1 by x2]
    D --> F[Compute Damping Ratio zeta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[zeta = delta over sqrt of 4 pi squared plus delta squared]
    F --> H{Is zeta less than 0.2?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Approximation delta approximately 2 pi zeta]
    H -- No --> J[Use exact formula only]
    F --> K[Validate using energy decay ratio]
    K --> L[En by E0 = e to power minus 2 delta]

    stepB[Step B]:::proc

    stepD[Step D]:::proc
    stepE[Step E]:::proc

```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Transmissibility Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Road Profile Input<br/>y of t = Y sin omega t] --> EXCITE[Base Excitation<br/>Frequency omega]
    EXCITE --> FRATIO[Compute Frequency Ratio<br/>r = omega by omega n]
    FRATIO --> DAMP[Read Damping Ratio zeta<br/>from suspension spec]
    DAMP --> CALC[TR Calculator Block]
    CALC --> TRNUM[Numerator: sqrt of 1 plus 2 zeta r squared]
    CALC --> TRDEN[Denominator: sqrt of 1 minus r squared squared plus 2 zeta r squared]
    TRNUM --> RATIO[TR equals Numerator by Denominator]
    TRDEN --> RATIO
    RATIO --> DECIDE{Compare r with sqrt 2}
    DECIDE -- r less than sqrt 2 --> AMP[Amplification Zone<br/>TR greater than 1]
    DECIDE -- r greater than sqrt 2 --> ISO[Isolation Zone<br/>TR less than 1]
    DECIDE -- r equals sqrt 2 --> UNITY[Unity Transmissibility<br/>TR equals 1]
    AMP --> OUTPUT[Ride Comfort Index]
    ISO --> OUTPUT
    UNITY --> OUTPUT

    nodeInput[Input Block]:::sensor
    nodeOutput[Output Block]:::actuator

```

    Q1 -- Force Excitation --> Q3[Use Magnification Factor MF formula]
    Q1 -- Free Vibration --> Q4[Use Logarithmic Decrement delta]
    Q2 --> Q2A{Frequency ratio r known?}
    Q2A -- Yes --> Q2B[Plug into TR equation]

    Q2B --> ZONE{Compare r with sqrt 2}
    ZONE -- r less than sqrt 2 --> Z1[Amplification]
    ZONE -- r greater than sqrt 2 --> Z2[Isolation]
