# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Bicycle Model — Foundation of Cornering Analysis

The bicycle model lumps the left and right wheels on each axle into a single equivalent wheel at the centerline. This 2-DOF model is the **workhorse** of vehicle dynamics and is the starting point for KTU problems.

**Assumptions of the Bicycle Model:**
1. Small angles (linearization valid).
2. Constant forward speed $V$ (no longitudinal acceleration).
3. Symmetric vehicle; left and right tires combined.
4. Steering input $\delta$ applied only to front wheel.
5. Tire forces are linear in slip angle ($F_y = -C_\alpha \alpha$).

### Equilibrium Equations in the Linear Range

The two fundamental equations of motion for steady-state and transient cornering (bicycle model, SAE coordinate system with x forward, y to left) are:

$$
m V \left(\dfrac{d\beta}{dt} + r\right) = F_{yf}\cos\delta + F_{yr}
$$

$$
I_{zz} \dfrac{dr}{dt} = a\,F_{yf}\cos\delta - b\,F_{yr}
$$

where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $I_{zz}$ = yaw moment of inertia about z-axis (kg·m²)
- $\beta$ = sideslip angle at CG (rad)
- $r$ = yaw rate (rad/s)
- $a$ = distance from CG to front axle (m)
- $b$ = distance from CG to rear axle (m)
- $V$ = longitudinal velocity (m/s)
- $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ = lateral tire forces at front and rear axles (N)

The tire forces for small slip angles (with $C_\alpha$ per axle, i.e., $C_{\alpha f} = C_{\alpha f,L} + C_{\alpha f,R}$) are:

$$
F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}\,\alpha_f = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \theta_f\right)
$$

$$
F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}\,\alpha_r = C_{\alpha r}\left(-\theta_r\right)
$$

The slip angles $\theta_f$ and $\theta_r$ at the tires relate to $\beta$ and $r$ as:

$$
\theta_f = \beta + \dfrac{a\,r}{V} \quad ; \quad \theta_r = \beta - \dfrac{b\,r}{V}
$$

### Steady-State Cornering Solution

In **steady-state**, $\dot{r} = 0$ and $\dot{\beta} = 0$. Substituting and solving the equilibrium equations yields the famous steady-state relations:

$$
\dfrac{r}{\delta_{sw}} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K V^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{i_s}
$$

$$
\beta = \delta \cdot \dfrac{b - \dfrac{m a V^2}{C_{\alpha r} L}}{L(1 + K V^2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{i_s}
$$

where $i_s$ is the steering ratio (steering wheel angle to road wheel angle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Understeer Gradient Definition:**
> 
> $$K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} \quad [\text{rad/g or s²/m²}]$$
> 
> - $K > 0$: **Understeer** — vehicle needs *more* steering as speed rises.
> - $K < 0$: **Oversteer** — vehicle needs *less* steering; potentially unstable.
> - $K = 0$: **Neutral steer** — Ackermann geometry perfect; constant steering.

### Critical Speed and Characteristic Speed

**For an understeer vehicle ($K > 0$):** No critical speed; $r/\delta$ asymptotically approaches zero as $V \to \infty$. The vehicle remains controllable.

**For an oversteer vehicle ($K < 0$):** The denominator $1 + K V^2 = 0$ at:

$$
V_{\text{cr}} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L\,g}{W_r/C_{\alpha r} - W_f/C_{\alpha f}}}
$$

This is the **Critical Speed**. Above $V_{cr}$, the denominator becomes negative, $r/\delta$ flips sign (the vehicle turns the wrong way relative to steering), and yaw rate grows uncontrollably.

The **Characteristic Speed** $V_{ch}$ is defined as the speed at which the yaw rate gain is half its low-speed value:

$$
V_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{K \tan(60°)}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad V_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\alpha r}\,L}{W_f\,\vert K \vert}}
$$

### Transient (Dynamic) Cornering Response

For non-steady cornering (e.g., step steering input), the yaw rate response is governed by a **second-order ODE**:

$$
I_{zz} \dot{r} + \left(\dfrac{m\,a\,b\,C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}\,V}{L\,(C_{\alpha r}+m V^2/L)} + \dfrac{(C_{\alpha r}\,b^2 + C_{\alpha f}\,a^2)V}{L}\right) r + \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}C_{\alpha r}\,L}{V} r = \dfrac{a\,C_{\alpha f}\,C_{\alpha r}}{L\,(C_{\alpha r}+mV^2/L)}\,\delta
$$

The standard form yields:

$$
\tau_r \dot{r} + r = K_r\,\delta \quad \text{(first-order approx.)}
$$

with **yaw time constant**:

$$
\tau_r \approx \dfrac{I_{zz}\,V}{C_{\alpha f}\,a^2 + C_{\alpha r}\,b^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Yaw Damping ($C_r$):** A higher yaw time constant means *less* damping and a slower, more oscillatory yaw response. Yaw damping is essential for stability — without it, the vehicle would oscillate indefinitely after a steering input.

### Steady-State Turn Radius and Steering Sensitivity

The **Ackermann turn radius** (geometric, ignoring tire slip):

$$
R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}
$$

With tire compliance, the effective turning radius is:

$$
R = \dfrac{L}{\delta}\left(1 + K V^2\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the design language for chassis engineers tuning suspension bushings, anti-roll bars, and tire selection. A race car is engineered to have slight understeer for predictability, while a Formula 1 car may flirt with oversteer for agility.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking tables. All table cells below use plain mathematical notation without internal pipes.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_y = C_\alpha \,\alpha$ | Linear cornering force at a tire | N |
| 2 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - ar/V$ | Front slip angle | rad |
| 3 | $\alpha_r = -\beta + br/V$ | Rear slip angle | rad |
| 4 | $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$ | Understeer gradient | rad/g |
| 5 | $r/\delta = V/[L(1+KV^2)]$ | Steady-state yaw rate gain | rad/s per rad |
| 6 | $\beta = \delta \cdot [b - m a V^2/(C_{\alpha r} L)] / [L(1+KV^2)]$ | Steady-state sideslip | rad |
| 7 | $V_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ | Critical speed (oversteer) | m/s |
| 8 | $V_{ch} = \sqrt{L/\vert K\vert}$ | Characteristic speed | m/s |
| 9 | $R_{ss} = L/\delta \cdot (1+KV^2)$ | Steady-state turn radius | m |
| 10 | $\tau_r \approx I_{zz} V / (C_{\alpha f} a^2 + C_{\alpha r} b^2)$ | Yaw time constant | s |
| 11 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} L^2/[I_{zz} V^2 \cdot (\dots)]}$ | Yaw natural frequency | rad/s |
| 12 | $C_{r\phi} = C_\phi + m g h_r$ | Total roll stiffness cornering gain | Nm/rad |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for validity:** Slip angles must remain small ($\alpha < 4°$ to $6°$); $V$ must be sub-critical for the linear model. Beyond $V_{cr}$ or in large-slip regime, use nonlinear Magic Formula (Pacejka) tire models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Steady-State Yaw Rate Gain from Bicycle Model

**Problem Setup:** A 2-DOF bicycle model is in steady-state cornering. Front wheel steered by angle $\delta$ (small). Derive the steady-state yaw rate gain $r/\delta$.

### Step 1: Write down the equilibrium equations

For steady-state, $\dot{r} = 0$ and $\dot{\beta} = 0$. The lateral force balance becomes:

$$
m V r = F_{yf}\cos\delta + F_{yr}
$$

Since $\delta$ is small, $\cos\delta \approx 1$. So:

$$
m V r \approx F_{yf} + F_{yr} \tag{1}
$$

The yaw moment balance (about CG):

$$
0 = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr} \tag{2}
$$

### Step 2: Express cornering forces in terms of slip angles

For linear tires, $F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}\,\alpha_f$ and $F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}\,\alpha_r$. Using the geometric relations for small angles:

$$
\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \dfrac{a r}{V} \quad ; \quad \alpha_r = -\beta + \dfrac{b r}{V}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \beta - \dfrac{a r}{V}\right) \tag{3}
$$

$$
F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}\left(-\beta + \dfrac{b r}{V}\right) \tag{4}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\beta$ from the moment equation (2)

Substituting (3) and (4) into (2):

$$
a\,C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \beta - \dfrac{a r}{V}\right) = b\,C_{\alpha r}\left(-\beta + \dfrac{b r}{V}\right)
$$

Expand:

$$
a C_{\alpha f}\,\delta - a C_{\alpha f}\,\beta - \dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f}\,r}{V} = -b C_{\alpha r}\,\beta + \dfrac{b^2 C_{\alpha r}\,r}{V}
$$

Collect terms in $\beta$:

$$
\beta\left(b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f}\right) = \dfrac{b^2 C_{\alpha r}\,r}{V} - \dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f}\,r}{V} - a C_{\alpha f}\,\delta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{(b^2 C_{\alpha r} - a^2 C_{\alpha f})\,r/V - a C_{\alpha f}\,\delta}{b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f}} \tag{5}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\beta$ into force balance (1)

Insert (3), (4), and (5) into (1). After algebraic manipulation (dropping the small $\beta V r$ cross-term), the steady-state solution reduces to:

$$
r = \dfrac{V \delta / L}{1 + K V^2} \tag{6}
$$

where the **wheelbase** $L = a + b$ and the **understeer gradient** $K$ is defined as:

$$
K = \dfrac{m b}{C_{\alpha r}\, L} - \dfrac{m a}{C_{\alpha f}\,L} = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}
$$

using $W_f = m g\, b/L$ and $W_r = m g\, a/L$ (static weight distribution).

**Verification:** When $K = 0$ (neutral steer), $r = V\delta/L$, which is the Ackermann result. ✓ When $K > 0$ (understeer), increasing $V$ reduces $r/\delta$ — more steering needed. ✓ When $K < 0$ (oversteer), at $V_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$, the denominator vanishes. ✓

---

## Derivation 2: Critical Speed for Oversteered Vehicle

**Setup:** Same bicycle model. An oversteer vehicle has $K < 0$. Find the speed at which the yaw rate gain $r/\delta \to \infty$.

### Step 1: Identify the denominator singularity

From equation (6):

$$
\dfrac{r}{\delta} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K V^2}
$$

For finite, non-zero steering input $\delta$, the gain diverges when:

$$
1 + K V^2 = 0
$$

### Step 2: Solve for $V$

$$
V^2 = -\dfrac{1}{K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{cr} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}}
$$

### Step 3: Express in physical parameters

Substituting $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$:

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} - \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}}}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $g$ (where $W = mg$):

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} - \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L\,g}{\dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}}(W_f) - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}(W_r)}}\quad \text{(simplified form)}
$$

A cleaner expression using $a$ and $b$:

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\alpha f}\,C_{\alpha r}\,L}{m (a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r})}}
$$

This speed is the **Absolute Stability Limit** — beyond it, the oversteered vehicle cannot maintain steady-state cornering and will spin out.

---

## Derivation 3: Yaw Time Constant (Transient Response)

**Setup:** Apply a step steering input $\delta$ at $t=0$ and observe yaw rate $r(t)$.

### Step 1: Linearized state-space form

For small slip angles, the 2-DOF bicycle equations can be written as:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \dot{\beta} \\ \dot{r} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f}+C_{\alpha r}}{m V} & -1 - \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{m V^2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f}}{I_{zz}} & -\dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{I_{zz} V} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m V} \\[6pt] \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f}}{I_{zz}} \end{Bmatrix} \delta
$$

### Step 2: Compute the eigenvalue for yaw decay

The yaw equation, when decoupled for slow transient, gives a first-order response:

$$
\dot{r} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_r}\,r = \dfrac{K_r}{\tau_r}\,\delta
$$

The yaw damping coefficient is:

$$
C_r = \dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{V}
$$

So the time constant is:

$$
\tau_r = \dfrac{I_{zz}}{C_r} = \dfrac{I_{zz}\,V}{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}
$$

### Step 3: Response to step input

For step steering $\delta_0$ applied at $t=0$:

$$
r(t) = K_r\,\delta_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_r}\right)
$$

This is a **first-order lag** with steady-state gain $K_r$ and time constant $\tau_r$.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical passenger car: $\tau_r \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ s at $V = 20$ m/s. A larger $\tau_r$ means sluggish yaw response — a sign of under-damped or low-stiffness tires.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Behavior Classification Table

| Condition | $K$ | $V < V_{cr}$ Behavior | Yaw Response | Sideslip $\beta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Understeer ($K > 0$) | Positive | Stable, less yaw per steering | Smooth, asymptotic | Slight inward then outward |
| Neutral ($K = 0$) | Zero | Ideal Ackermann tracking | $r = V\delta/L$ exactly | $\beta = b\delta/L$ |
| Oversteer ($K < 0$) | Negative | Stable only below $V_{cr}$ | Increases with speed | Grows large near $V_{cr}$ |
| Above $V_{cr}$ | — | Unstable, yaw diverges | Opposite to steering | Large, divergent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Bicycle Model Geometry (Free-Body)

```
        Front Tire
            O
           /|
          / |
   Fyf   /  |  delta  <-- steering angle
        /   |
       /    V (velocity)
      /     |
   ---CG----O----> R (turn radius)
      \  a  |  b  /
       \    |    /
   Fyr  \   |   /
         \  |  /
          \ | /
           \|/
            O
        Rear Tire
```

**ASCII Art Representation (formal):**

```
                    δ (steer)
              ↓
       ┌──────────┐
       │    FRONT │
       │   TIRE   │ ← α_f (front slip angle)
       └────┬─────┘
            │
       ─────┼─────  ← x-axis (longitudinal)
      a     │
   ←────────┼────────→ V (forward velocity)
            │    b
            │
       ┌────┴─────┐
       │   REAR   │
       │   TIRE   │ ← α_r (rear slip angle)
       └──────────┘

  ↑ y-axis (lateral)
  
  CG = center of gravity at distance 'a' from front, 'b' from rear
  β = sideslip angle at CG (angle between CG velocity and x-axis)
  r = yaw rate (rotation about z-axis at CG)
```

## Diagram 2: Mermaid Block Diagram — Cornering Force Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Steering Input delta] --> B[Front Wheel Steered Angle delta]
    B --> C[Front Slip Angle alpha_f]
    B --> D[Vehicle Lateral Velocity v at CG]
    D --> E[Sideslip Angle beta]
    C --> F[Front Cornering Force Fyf]
    C --> G[Rear Slip Angle alpha_r]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Rear Cornering Force Fyr]
    F --> I[Yaw Moment Balance]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Lateral Force Balance]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Yaw Rate r Output]
    J --> L[Sideslip beta Output]
    K --> M[Vehicle Curved Path R]
    L --> M
```

## Diagram 3: Mermaid — Steady-State vs Transient Cornering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SS[Steady-State Cornering]
        A1[Constant Steering] --> B1[Constant Yaw Rate]
        B1 --> C1[Constant Turn Radius]
        C1 --> D1[Equilibrium Forces]
    end
    subgraph TR[Transient Cornering]
        A2[Step Steering] --> B2[First-Order Yaw Lag]
        B2 --> C2[Time Constant tau_r]
        C2 --> D2[Asymptotic Yaw Rate]
    end
    D1 --> E[Yaw Rate Gain r slash delta]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F{Vehicle Type}
    F --> G1[Understeer K greater than 0]
    F --> G2[Neutral K equals 0]
    F --> G3[Oversteer K less than 0]
```

## Diagram 4: Mermaid — Critical Speed Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Understeer Gradient K] --> B{K sign check}
    B -->|K greater than 0| C[Understeer Vehicle]
    B -->|K equals 0| D[Neutral Steer]
    B -->|K less than 0| E[Oversteer Vehicle]
    C --> F[No critical speed; stable at all V]
    D --> G[Pure Ackermann; r = V delta slash L]
    E --> H[Critical speed Vcr = sqrt of minus 1 slash K]
    H --> I{V versus Vcr}
    I -->|V less than Vcr| J[Stable cornering]
    I -->|V greater than Vcr| K[Unstable; yaw diverges]
```

## Diagram 5: Mermaid — Tire Slip Angle Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tire Heading Direction] --> B[Tire Slip Angle alpha]
    C[Tire Velocity Vector] --> B
    B --> D[Lateral Force Fy = C alpha times alpha]
    D --> E[Acts Perpendicular to Tire Heading]
    D --> F[Opposes Sideways Velocity]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Define understeer gradient. Explain how it classifies the directional behavior of a vehicle during cornering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **understeer gradient $K$** is defined as the rate of change of the slip angle difference between front and rear axles with respect to lateral acceleration. Mathematically:

$$
K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}
$$

where $W_f$ and $W_r$ are the static weights on the front and rear axles, and $C_{\alpha f}, C_{\alpha r}$ are the cornering stiffnesses of the front and rear axles.

**Classification:**
- If $K > 0$: **Understeer** — front tires saturate first; vehicle needs *increasing* steering angle with speed.
- If $K = 0$: **Neutral steer** — steering angle is independent of speed (Ackermann ideal).
- If $K < 0$: **Oversteer** — rear tires saturate first; vehicle needs *decreasing* steering angle with speed; potentially unstable.

(3 marks for definition, classification, and units)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is the significance of the critical speed of a vehicle? Derive its expression for an oversteer vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical speed $V_{cr}$** is the forward speed beyond which a vehicle (typically oversteered) becomes directionally unstable and cannot maintain steady-state cornering. It marks the absolute limit of stable handling.

For an oversteer vehicle, the yaw rate gain is:

$$
\dfrac{r}{\delta} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K V^2}
$$

Since $K < 0$ for oversteer, the denominator $1 + KV^2$ becomes zero at:

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}}
$$

Above $V_{cr}$, the denominator becomes negative, and $r/\delta$ reverses sign — the vehicle yaws opposite to the steering input, causing an uncontrollable spin.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Develop the two-degree-of-freedom bicycle model equations for a vehicle during steady-state cornering. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a passenger car with the following data, compute the steady-state yaw rate gain, the sideslip angle, and the turn radius at a forward speed of 25 m/s. Indicate whether the vehicle is understeer, neutral, or oversteer. **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Vehicle mass $m$ | 1500 kg |
| Wheelbase $L$ | 2.6 m |
| Distance CG to front $a$ | 1.2 m |
| Distance CG to rear $b$ | 1.4 m |
| Front cornering stiffness $C_{\alpha f}$ | 80,000 N/rad |
| Rear cornering stiffness $C_{\alpha r}$ | 70,000 N/rad |
| Steering wheel angle $\delta_{sw}$ | 90 deg |
| Steering ratio $i_s$ | 18 |

#### Model Solution for Part A-(a):

**Assumptions of Bicycle Model:**
1. Vehicle travels at constant forward speed $V$.
2. Steering angle $\delta$ and slip angles are small (linear regime).
3. Left and right tires combined into single equivalent wheel per axle.
4. No longitudinal weight transfer or aerodynamic forces.
5. Tire cornering forces are linear: $F_y = -C_\alpha \alpha$.
6. Constant yaw rate $r$ and sideslip $\beta$ in steady state.

**Equations of motion (lateral and yaw):**

$$
m V \left(\dot{\beta} + r\right) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}
$$

$$
I_{zz} \dot{r} = a F_{yf} \cos\delta - b F_{yr}
$$

**Tire force relations:**

$$
F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \beta - \dfrac{a r}{V}\right)
$$

$$
F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r}\left(-\beta + \dfrac{b r}{V}\right)
$$

**For steady-state:** $\dot{r} = 0$, $\dot{\beta} = 0$, small $\delta$ so $\cos\delta \approx 1$.

Solving yields:

$$
r = \dfrac{V \delta / L}{1 + K V^2} \quad ; \quad \beta = \dfrac{b\delta - m a V^2 \delta / (C_{\alpha r} L)}{L(1 + K V^2)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part A-(b):

**Step 1: Convert steering wheel angle to road wheel angle.**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{\delta_{sw}}{i_s} = \dfrac{90° \times \pi/180}{18} = \dfrac{\pi/2}{18} = 0.0873 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Compute static axle weights.**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{m g\, b}{L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.4}{2.6} = 7921 \text{ N}
$$

$$
W_r = \dfrac{m g\, a}{L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.6} = 6790 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Compute understeer gradient.**

$$
K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \dfrac{7921}{80{,}000} - \dfrac{6790}{70{,}000}
$$

$$
K = 0.0990 - 0.0970 = 0.0020 \text{ rad/(m/s)²}
$$

Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is **Understeered**. ✓

**Step 4: Compute steady-state yaw rate gain $r/\delta$.**

$$
1 + K V^2 = 1 + 0.0020 \times (25)^2 = 1 + 1.25 = 2.25
$$

$$
\dfrac{r}{\delta} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K V^2} = \dfrac{25/2.6}{2.25} = \dfrac{9.615}{2.25} = 4.274 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Compute yaw rate $r$.**

$$
r = (r/\delta) \times \delta = 4.274 \times 0.0873 = 0.373 \text{ rad/s} = 21.4 \text{ deg/s}
$$

**Step 6: Compute sideslip $\beta$.**

Numerator:

$$
b\,\delta - \dfrac{m a V^2 \delta}{C_{\alpha r}\,L} = 1.4(0.0873) - \dfrac{1500 \times 1.2 \times 625 \times 0.0873}{70{,}000 \times 2.6}
$$

$$
= 0.1222 - \dfrac{1500 \times 1.2 \times 625 \times 0.0873}{182{,}000} = 0.1222 - 0.5396 = -0.4174
$$

Denominator:

$$
L(1+KV^2) = 2.6 \times 2.25 = 5.85
$$

$$
\beta = -0.4174/5.85 = -0.0714 \text{ rad} = -4.09° \text{ (negative means inward/outward depending on convention)}
$$

**Step 7: Compute steady-state turn radius.**

$$
R_{ss} = \dfrac{L}{\delta}(1 + K V^2) = \dfrac{2.6}{0.0873} \times 2.25 = 29.78 \times 2.25 = 67.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Summary of Results:**
- $r/\delta = 4.27$ s⁻¹
- $r = 0.373$ rad/s
- $\beta = -0.0714$ rad (≈ −4.09°)
- $R_{ss} = 67.0$ m
- **Classification: Understeer** (positive $K$)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** What is the yaw rate gain of a vehicle? Derive the steady-state expression for yaw rate gain using the bicycle model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle has $a = 1.3$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 90{,}000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 75{,}000$ N/rad, $m = 1600$ kg. Calculate (i) understeer gradient, (ii) critical speed, and (iii) characteristic speed. Is this vehicle safe at 35 m/s? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part B-(a):

**Definition:** Yaw rate gain $r/\delta$ is the ratio of vehicle yaw rate $r$ (rad/s) to the steering input $\delta$ (rad). It describes how responsive the vehicle's rotation is to steering commands at a given speed.

**Derivation:** See Derivation 1 above. Result:

$$
\dfrac{r}{\delta} = \dfrac{V/L}{1 + K V^2}
$$

(7 marks for definition, derivation, and final expression)

---

#### Model Solution for Part B-(b):

**Step 1: Compute weights and understeer gradient.**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{m g\,b}{L} = \dfrac{1600 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}{2.8} = 8408.6 \text{ N}
$$

$$
W_r = \dfrac{m g\,a}{L} = \dfrac{1600 \times 9.81 \times 1.3}{2.8} = 7287.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \dfrac{8408.6}{90{,}000} - \dfrac{7287.4}{75{,}000}
$$

$$
K = 0.0934 - 0.0972 = -0.0038 \text{ rad/(m/s)²}
$$

**Step 2: Critical speed (since $K < 0$, vehicle is oversteered).**

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{-0.0038}} = \sqrt{263.16} = 16.22 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Characteristic speed.**

$$
V_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{\vert K\vert}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.8}{0.0038}} = \sqrt{736.84} = 27.14 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Safety check at $V = 35$ m/s.**

Since $V = 35$ m/s > $V_{cr} = 16.22$ m/s, the vehicle is **above its critical speed and is directionally UNSTABLE**. The driver would lose control during steady-state cornering. The vehicle is **NOT safe** at 35 m/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of $K$:** Students often forget that oversteer requires $K < 0$; double-check weight distribution and stiffness values.
> 2. **Units of $K$:** If you get $K$ in **deg/g**, conversion factor is $K[\text{rad/g}] = K[\text{deg/100m}] \times 0.001745$. Many students miss this and get wrong $V_{cr}$.
> 3. **$V_{cr}$ formula:** Some textbooks use $V_{cr} = \sqrt{C_{\alpha r}\,L/(W_f \vert K \vert)}$ for "characteristic" speed and $\sqrt{-1/K}$ for "critical". Don't confuse the two.
> 4. **Steering ratio:** Always remember $i_s = \delta_{sw}/\delta_{road}$. Many errors arise from using $\delta_{sw}$ directly in yaw-rate formulas.
> 5. **Sideslip sign convention:** SAE convention has $\beta$ positive for CG velocity pointing to the *left* of x-axis. Some textbooks use opposite sign. Stick to one in your answer.
> 6. **Steady-state vs Transient:** Mark partial credit only if you clearly state which regime you're analyzing. Don't apply $\dot{r} = 0$ conditions to a transient step input problem.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cornering behavior** is the lateral and yaw response of a vehicle under steering input, studied via the **2-DOF bicycle model**.
- The two governing equations are **lateral force balance** and **yaw moment balance** about CG.
- **Slip angle** $\alpha$ is the angle between tire heading and velocity vector; small-angle assumption gives linear tire model $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$.
- **Understeer gradient $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$** is the key descriptor of cornering character:
  - $K > 0$: Understeer (safe, predictable).
  - $K = 0$: Neutral steer (ideal Ackermann).
  - $K < 0$: Oversteer (potentially unstable).
- **Steady-state yaw rate gain:** $r/\delta = (V/L)/(1 + K V^2)$.
- **Sideslip angle at CG** in steady-state: $\beta = [b\delta - m a V^2 \delta/(C_{\alpha r} L)] / [L(1+KV^2)]$.
- **Critical speed** $V_{cr} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ applies only when $K < 0$; beyond it, the vehicle is directionally unstable.
- **Characteristic speed** $V_{ch} = \sqrt{L/\vert K \vert}$ is the speed at which yaw rate gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of low-speed value.
- **Yaw time constant** $\tau_r \approx I_{zz} V / (a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r})$ governs how fast yaw builds up after a steering input.
- **Steady-state turn radius** $R_{ss} = (L/\delta)(1 + K V^2)$ is the actual radius versus Ackermann $L/\tan\delta$.
- Always convert **steering wheel angle** $\delta_{sw}$ to **road wheel angle** $\delta = \delta_{sw}/i_s$ before using cornering formulas.
- The **bicycle model** is valid for small slip angles and sub-critical speeds; for high-speed or large-slip scenarios, use 4-DOF or Magic Formula tire models.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** Cornering behavior, $K$, $V_{cr}$, and steady-state $r/\delta$ are exam favorites — expect at least one 7-mark derivation and one 7-mark numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slip Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of vehicle dynamics, the **Slip Angle (α)** of a pneumatic tire is formally defined as the **angle between the direction in which the wheel is pointing (its plane of symmetry / heading vector)** and the **actual direction of motion of the tire's contact patch (its velocity vector / trajectory vector)**.

Mathematically, the slip angle is the angle formed between:
- The **wheel plane (steered direction)**, and
- The **instantaneous velocity vector (V)** of the wheel's centre, projected onto the road plane.

For a vehicle moving with longitudinal velocity $V_x$ and lateral velocity $V_y$ at a wheel, the slip angle is expressed as:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Slip angle is a fundamental tire parameter in Module 2 (Tire Dynamics) of PCAUT602. It is the *primary input variable* that generates lateral (cornering) force in a tire. Without slip angle, a tire cannot generate any meaningful cornering force — this is a critical board-exam concept.

### 1.2 Slip Angle Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Tire Position | Sign Convention | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Left Tire** | Positive (+) | When velocity vector lies to the **left** of the wheel plane |
| **Right Tire** | Positive (+) | When velocity vector lies to the **right** of the wheel plane |
| **Standard SAE** | Symmetric (left positive, right positive) | Used in most vehicle dynamics textbooks |
| **KTU / ISO** | Symmetric positive convention | Same as SAE |

> [!NOTE]
> Always follow the **symmetric sign convention** preferred in KTU board examinations unless otherwise specified by the problem statement. This is consistent with the standard **Milliken & Milliken** convention followed in the textbook *Race Car Vehicle Dynamics*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Rowing Boat

Imagine you are **rowing a small boat** across a flowing river:
- The oars are pointed in one direction (the heading).
- The river current pushes the boat sideways.
- Your *actual* path of travel is the **resultant** of your forward rowing effort and the sideways current.

The **angle between where your oars point and where you actually move** is essentially the *slip angle*. 

In a tire, this happens because:
- The wheel is steered (oars pointing in a direction).
- The tire has rubber that **deforms laterally** under side load (the current pushing you).
- The contact patch is not exactly aligned with the wheel hub due to elastic deformation.
- The resulting motion vector deviates from the heading — and that deviation **is** the slip angle.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** If the tire is rolling perfectly straight with no side load, slip angle = 0. If the tire is sliding sideways (skidding), slip angle approaches 90°. Real driving scenarios produce slip angles between **1° to 10°** typically in passenger cars, and up to **12°–15°** in racing tires at the limit of grip.

### 1.4 Why Slip Angle Exists — The Physical "Why"

A tire is not a rigid wheel. It is a **viscoelastic composite structure** made of rubber, cords (polyester, steel, nylon), and inflated air. When subjected to a lateral force:

1. The contact patch **shears** against the road.
2. The rubber in the contact zone **deforms laterally** (this is called *lateral distortion*).
3. The trailing edge of the contact patch moves laterally relative to the leading edge.
4. The wheel heading and motion vector therefore **decouple**.

This decoupling — the *slip angle* — is the mechanism by which the tire generates **cornering force**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse *slip angle* with *sideslip angle (β)*. They are NOT the same. **Slip angle (α)** is a *tire-level* property. **Sideslip angle (β)** is a *vehicle-level* property at the center of gravity. A car can have zero vehicle sideslip angle but each individual tire can still have a non-zero slip angle!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Breakdown of Slip Angle

The slip angle arises from the resolution of the wheel's velocity into two orthogonal components at the contact patch:

- **Longitudinal velocity component** along the wheel plane: $V_x$
- **Lateral velocity component** perpendicular to the wheel plane: $V_y$

The slip angle is the angle whose:
- **Tangent** = (lateral velocity) / (longitudinal velocity) = $V_y / V_x$

For most vehicle dynamics applications, slip angles are small (typically $< 10°$), and we can use the **small angle approximation** $\tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha$ (in radians).

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Slip Angle Regimes

| Regime | Range (Approx.) | Tire Behavior | Vehicle Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $0° \leq \alpha \leq 4°$–$6°$ | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ (linear cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$) | Normal driving; predictable handling |
| **Non-linear** | $4° \leq \alpha \leq 10°$ | $F_y$ grows slower than $\alpha$ (stiffness drops) | Approaching grip limit; understeer becomes evident |
| **Saturation** | $\alpha \geq 10°$–$12°$ | $F_y$ plateaus, then decreases (full sliding) | Tire sliding; no more grip; spin-out risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Most numerical problems in PCAUT602 ask you to work in the **linear regime** unless otherwise stated. Always verify whether the slip angle is small enough to use $\tan \alpha \approx \alpha$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-critical equations** for the Slip Angle topic. Memorize these — they appear in almost every KTU board paper for this module.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$ | **Exact** slip angle definition | radians or degrees |
| 2 | $\alpha \approx \dfrac{V_y}{V_x}$ | **Small angle approximation** ($\alpha < 10°$) | radians |
| 3 | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | **Linear cornering force** (linear regime) | Newtons [N] |
| 4 | $C_\alpha = \left.\dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial \alpha}\right\vert_{\alpha=0}$ | **Cornering stiffness** definition | N/rad or N/deg |
| 5 | $M_z = -C_\alpha \cdot t \cdot \alpha$ | **Aligning moment** ($t$ = pneumatic trail) | N·m |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{M_z}{F_y}$ | **Pneumatic trail** (lateral offset of force resultant) | meters [m] |
| 7 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}\right)$ | **Front slip angle** (bicycle model, $a$ = CG to front axle) | radians |
| 8 | $\alpha_r = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x}\right)$ | **Rear slip angle** (bicycle model, $b$ = CG to rear axle) | radians |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$ | **Understeer gradient** (stability indicator) | rad/g or deg/g |
| 10 | $\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} \cdot C_{\alpha r} \cdot L^2}{W \cdot V_x}$ | **Critical speed** denominator term (for stability analysis) | varies |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** In equations (7) and (8), $\delta$ is the steering angle at the road wheel, $r$ is the yaw rate, $a$ and $b$ are distances from CG to front and rear axles respectively, and $V_y$ is the lateral velocity at the CG. Pay close attention to signs — KTU evaluators **deduct marks** for sign errors in slip angle calculations.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Slip Angle

| Application Area | Why Slip Angle Matters |
|---|---|
| **Suspension Design** | King-pin inclination, caster trail, and compliance are tuned to manage slip angle buildup |
| **Steering System Tuning** | Variable ratio racks use slip angle feedback in modern steer-by-wire systems |
| **Electronic Stability Control (ESC/ESP)** | Compares **target slip angle** vs **actual slip angle** and applies differential braking |
| **Tire Compound Selection** | Soft racing compounds have higher peak slip angle before saturation |
| **Vehicle Sizing & Architecture** | Determines front/rear weight distribution and cornering balance (understeer/oversteer) |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Slip angle estimation is critical for path-following controllers |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Tie-In:** Modern Tesla, BMW, and Bosch ESC systems compute the slip angle **in real-time** using an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** that fuses IMU, wheel-speed, and steering-angle sensor data. This is the same α you're studying — but computed at 100 Hz inside the car's stability controller.

### 2.5 Pneumatic Trail & Aligning Moment — The Hidden Connection

The **aligning moment** ($M_z$) is the self-aligning torque generated by the tire that tries to **restore the wheel to its original heading**. It arises because the resultant lateral force in the contact patch does not pass through the wheel centre — instead, it acts at a small distance **behind** the wheel centre. This distance is the **pneumatic trail ($t$)**.

$$M_z = -t \cdot F_y = -t \cdot C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$$

The pneumatic trail is typically between **30 mm and 60 mm** for passenger car tires, and is highest at small slip angles. As $\alpha$ increases, $t$ decreases, eventually becoming negative (which contributes to the aligning moment reversal at very high slip angles).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Slip Angle from Velocity Components (Geometric Origin)

**Given:**
- A wheel is moving in a plane with a velocity vector $\vec{V}$ that has magnitude $V$.
- The wheel plane makes an angle $\delta$ with the longitudinal (X) axis of the vehicle.
- The longitudinal velocity component along the wheel plane is $V_x = V \cos\alpha$.
- The lateral velocity component perpendicular to the wheel plane is $V_y = V \sin\alpha$.

**To Prove / Derive:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Resolve the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ into two orthogonal components along the wheel's local coordinate frame.

By definition of the slip angle, the velocity vector deviates from the wheel plane by exactly $\alpha$. We resolve along the wheel plane (longitudinal) and perpendicular to it (lateral):

$$V_x = V \cos\alpha \quad \text{(component along wheel plane)}$$

$$V_y = V \sin\alpha \quad \text{(component perpendicular to wheel plane)}$$

**Step 2:** Form the ratio of lateral to longitudinal components to eliminate the magnitude $V$.

$$\frac{V_y}{V_x} = \frac{V \sin\alpha}{V \cos\alpha}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common factor $V$ from numerator and denominator.

$$\frac{V_y}{V_x} = \frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\dfrac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = \tan\alpha$.

$$\frac{V_y}{V_x} = \tan\alpha$$

**Step 5:** Take the inverse tangent of both sides to solve for $\alpha$.

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6:** Small Angle Approximation.

For slip angles $\alpha < 10°$ (i.e., $\alpha < 0.1745$ rad), we can use the Taylor series expansion:

$$\tan\alpha = \alpha + \frac{\alpha^3}{3} + \frac{2\alpha^5}{15} + \dots$$

Neglecting higher-order terms:

$$\tan\alpha \approx \alpha \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha \approx \frac{V_y}{V_x} \quad \text{(small angle approximation)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When you write the small-angle approximation, **always state the assumption** (i.e., "assuming $\alpha < 10°$"). This shows the examiner you understand the underlying physics, and is worth 0.5–1 bonus mark in valuations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Front and Rear Slip Angles for the Bicycle Model

**Context:** The **bicycle model** is the simplest vehicle model used in KTU board problems. It lumps both front wheels into a single front "bicycle wheel" and both rear wheels into a single rear "bicycle wheel."

**Coordinate System:**
- $X$ — longitudinal axis, pointing forward
- $Y$ — lateral axis, pointing left
- $Z$ — vertical axis, pointing up
- Origin at the **vehicle's center of gravity (CG)**

**Vehicle Parameters:**
- $a$ = distance from CG to front axle [m]
- $b$ = distance from CG to rear axle [m]
- $L = a + b$ = wheelbase [m]
- $\delta$ = steering angle at the front wheel [rad]
- $r$ = yaw rate of the vehicle [rad/s]
- $V_x$ = longitudinal velocity at CG [m/s]
- $V_y$ = lateral velocity at CG [m/s]

**Velocity at Front Axle (Longitudinal Component):**

The longitudinal velocity at the front axle is the same as at the CG in a planar bicycle model:

$$V_{x,f} = V_x$$

**Velocity at Front Axle (Lateral Component):**

The lateral velocity at the front axle is the lateral velocity at the CG plus the contribution from yaw rotation. Since the front axle is **distance $a$ ahead** of the CG, a positive yaw rate $r$ creates an additional lateral velocity component in the **negative Y direction** (in standard convention):

$$V_{y,f} = V_y + a \cdot r$$

Wait — let us re-derive this carefully. The yaw rate creates a tangential velocity at the front axle. In the body-fixed frame, if yaw rate $r$ is positive (counter-clockwise about Z), then the lateral velocity at the front axle is:

$$V_{y,f} = V_y + a \cdot r$$

(Some texts use $-a \cdot r$ depending on sign convention. We follow the standard SAE convention where a positive $r$ creates a **positive** lateral velocity contribution at the front.)

**Direction of the Front Wheel:**

The front wheel is steered by angle $\delta$ from the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

**Slip Angle at the Front Wheel:**

The slip angle is the difference between the wheel heading direction and the actual velocity direction. At the front wheel, the heading is along $\delta$, so:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{y,f}}{V_{x,f}}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}\right)$$

**Slip Angle at the Rear Wheel:**

The rear wheel is **not** steered, so its heading is along the vehicle's longitudinal axis ($\delta = 0$). The lateral velocity at the rear axle is:

$$V_{y,r} = V_y - b \cdot r$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha_r = 0 - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{y,r}}{V_{x,r}}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x}\right)$$

Combining the two:

$$\boxed{\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}\right) \qquad \alpha_r = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x}\right)}$$

**Small-Angle Approximation Form:**

For small slip angles (typical in steady-state cornering), $\tan^{-1}(x) \approx x$:

$$\alpha_f \approx \delta - \frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}$$

$$\alpha_r \approx -\frac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x} = \frac{b\,r - V_y}{V_x}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** For **steady-state cornering** ($r = \text{const}$, $V_y = \text{const}$), the relationship $\delta \cdot V_x = L \cdot r$ (Ackermann condition) applies. Substituting this into the above gives $\alpha_f = \alpha_r \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}$, which is a useful **sanity check** during KTU board exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cornering Force from Cornering Stiffness

**Given:** In the linear regime, the cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$ is a constant. The tire generates a cornering force $F_y$ proportional to slip angle $\alpha$.

**To Prove / Show:** $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the **defining equation** of cornering stiffness as the slope of the cornering force vs slip angle curve at the origin:

$$C_\alpha = \left.\frac{dF_y}{d\alpha}\right\vert_{\alpha = 0}$$

**Step 2:** In the **linear regime**, $F_y$ vs $\alpha$ is a straight line through the origin, so $\dfrac{dF_y}{d\alpha}$ is constant and equal to $C_\alpha$ everywhere in that regime.

**Step 3:** Therefore, integrating the constant slope from $0$ to $\alpha$:

$$F_y(\alpha) = \int_0^{\alpha} C_\alpha \, d\alpha' = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\boxed{F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Typical Values of $C_\alpha$:**

| Vehicle Class | Tire Size | Approx. $C_\alpha$ (per tire) |
|---|---|---|
| Compact car | 185/65 R15 | ~30,000 N/rad |
| Mid-size sedan | 205/55 R16 | ~40,000 N/rad |
| Sports car | 245/40 R18 | ~60,000 N/rad |
| Formula-style race car | Slick racing | ~100,000+ N/rad |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A passenger car is negotiating a steady-state turn of radius $R = 100$ m at a constant speed of $V = 20$ m/s. The vehicle's wheelbase is $L = 2.5$ m, and the CG is located at $a = 1.2$ m from the front axle and $b = 1.3$ m from the rear axle. The steering angle is $\delta = 4°$. Calculate:
(a) The yaw rate of the vehicle.
(b) The slip angle at the front and rear tires (using the small-angle approximation).
(c) The cornering force at each axle, given cornering stiffness $C_{\alpha f} = 80{,}000$ N/rad and $C_{\alpha r} = 70{,}000$ N/rad (per axle).

### Solution

**Part (a): Yaw Rate**

For steady-state cornering at constant speed on a fixed radius:

$$r = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b): Slip Angles**

Steady-state lateral velocity at CG (assuming bicycle model, $V_y \approx 0$ for small angles):

$$V_y \approx 0 \quad \text{(steady-state Ackermann cornering)}$$

Slip angle at front axle (small-angle form):

$$\alpha_f \approx \delta - \frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x} = \delta - \frac{a \cdot r}{V}$$

Substituting values (convert $\delta$ to radians: $4° = 0.0698$ rad):

$$\alpha_f = 0.0698 - \frac{1.2 \times 0.2}{20} = 0.0698 - 0.0120 = 0.0578 \text{ rad}$$

Converting back to degrees:

$$\alpha_f = 0.0578 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 3.31°$$

Slip angle at rear axle:

$$\alpha_r \approx -\frac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x} = \frac{b \cdot r}{V} = \frac{1.3 \times 0.2}{20} = 0.0130 \text{ rad} = 0.745°$$

**Part (c): Cornering Forces**

Front cornering force (per axle):

$$F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f = 80{,}000 \times 0.0578 = 4{,}624 \text{ N}$$

Rear cornering force (per axle):

$$F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r = 70{,}000 \times 0.0130 = 910 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The front tires carry **5× more cornering load** than the rear tires in this configuration — this indicates a **front-heavy understeer tendency** (a stable, safe handling characteristic desirable in passenger cars). KTU board questions often follow up with a stability check using the understeer gradient $K$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Slip Angle Geometry — Free Body of a Steered Tire

This diagram illustrates the geometric definition of slip angle as the angle between the wheel plane and the velocity vector at the contact patch.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Vehicle CG Origin O"] -->|Vx Long Forward| B["Wheel Centre C"]
    B -->|Wheel Plane Heading| C["Steered Direction Delta"]
    B -->|Actual Velocity V| D["Velocity Vector V"]
    D -->|Perpendicular Component| E["Lateral Velocity Vy"]
    D -->|Parallel Component| F["Longitudinal Velocity Vx"]
    C -->|Alpha Slip Angle| D
    G["Contact Patch"] -->|Shear Deformation| H["Pneumatic Trail t"]
    H -->|Cornering Force Fy| I["Resultant Force Line of Action"]
    I -->|Aligning Moment Mz| B
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style I fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f
```

### Diagram Description (For Exam Use)

The geometry of slip angle can be described as follows:
- The **wheel centre (C)** is connected to the vehicle CG by a rigid member.
- The **wheel plane** (steered direction) is rotated by the steering angle $\delta$ from the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
- The **actual velocity vector $\vec{V}$** deviates from this wheel plane by the slip angle $\alpha$.
- The **velocity vector** can be decomposed into:
  - $V_x$ (along the wheel plane) — the rolling component.
  - $V_y$ (perpendicular to the wheel plane) — the sliding component.
- At the **contact patch**, the rubber shears, and the resultant lateral force $F_y$ acts at a small offset (pneumatic trail $t$) behind the wheel centre, generating the **aligning moment** $M_z$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Cornering Force vs Slip Angle Characteristic Curve

This block diagram represents the **functional relationship** between slip angle input and cornering force output, including the three operational regimes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Slip Angle alpha deg"] --> R1{"Alpha less than 4 to 6 deg"}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2["Linear Regime"]
    R2 --> R3["Fy equals C alpha times alpha"]
    R3 --> R4["Output Constant Cornering Stiffness"]
    R1 -->|No| R5{"Alpha less than 10 to 12 deg"}
    R5 -->|Yes| R6["Nonlinear Regime"]
    R6 --> R7["Fy grows sublinearly with alpha"]
    R7 --> R8["Output Decreasing Cornering Stiffness"]
    R5 -->|No| R9["Saturation Regime"]
    R9 --> R10["Fy plateaus then drops"]
    R10 --> R11["Output Tire sliding full slip"]
    style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style R1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style R2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style R3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style R4 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R5 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style R6 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100
    style R7 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100
    style R8 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#bf360c
    style R9 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style R10 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style R11 fill:#e57373,stroke:#7f0000
```

### Diagram Description

The diagram shows the **three regimes** of tire behavior:
1. **Linear Regime (α < 4°–6°):** Force grows linearly. Cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$ is constant. **Normal passenger car driving.**
2. **Non-linear Regime (4° < α < 10°–12°):** Force grows sub-linearly. Effective cornering stiffness decreases. **Approaching grip limit.**
3. **Saturation Regime (α > 10°–12°):** Force plateaus and may decrease. Tire is sliding. **Loss of control regime.**

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bicycle Model — Slip Angle Architecture

This block diagram maps the **flow of information and forces** in the bicycle model used in Module 2 of PCAUT602.

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT["Inputs delta Vx Vy r"] --> CALC_F["Compute Front Slip Angle alpha f"]
    INPUT --> CALC_R["Compute Rear Slip Angle alpha r"]
    CALC_F --> CF["Front Cornering Force Fy f equals C alpha f times alpha f"]
    CALC_R --> CR["Rear Cornering Force Fy r equals C alpha r times alpha r"]
    CF --> SUM_F["Total Lateral Force Fy total"]
    CR --> SUM_F
    SUM_F --> SS1{"Check Understeer Gradient K"}
    SS1 -->|K greater than 0| OUT1["Understeer Stable"]
    SS1 -->|K equals 0| OUT2["Neutral Steer"]
    SS1 -->|K less than 0| OUT3["Oversteer Unstable"]
    style INPUT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style CALC_F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style CALC_R fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style CF fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style CR fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style SUM_F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style SS1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style OUT1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style OUT2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style OUT3 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### Diagram Description

The bicycle model block flow:
1. **Inputs** are fed in: steering angle $\delta$, longitudinal velocity $V_x$, lateral velocity $V_y$, and yaw rate $r$.
2. The system computes **front slip angle** $\alpha_f$ and **rear slip angle** $\alpha_r$ using the derived formulas.
3. Each slip angle is multiplied by the **cornering stiffness** to get cornering force.
4. The total lateral force is summed.
5. The **understeer gradient** $K$ is computed and classified into one of three handling regimes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Use:** This block diagram is ideal for a **"draw and explain" type question** in the 14-mark section. Make sure to label all variables clearly and add units.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define slip angle. Explain its physical significance in tire force generation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **slip angle ($\alpha$)** of a tire is defined as the angle between the **direction in which the wheel is pointing** (the wheel plane or steered direction) and the **actual direction of travel** (the velocity vector) of the contact patch.

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$$

**Physical Significance:**
- A tire can only generate a **lateral (cornering) force** if a slip angle exists. The relationship in the linear regime is $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$.
- Slip angle arises from the **elastic shear deformation** of the tire's rubber in the contact patch.
- It is the **fundamental input variable** for analyzing vehicle cornering, stability, and understeer/oversteer behavior.
- Slip angle is essential for **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** systems, which continuously estimate $\alpha$ and intervene to keep the vehicle stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Physical significance (1).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between slip angle (α) and sideslip angle (β).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Slip Angle ($\alpha$) | Sideslip Angle ($\beta$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Tire-level property | Vehicle-level property |
| **Definition** | Angle between wheel plane and tire velocity vector | Angle between vehicle longitudinal axis and velocity vector at CG |
| **Location** | Measured at each tire contact patch | Measured at the vehicle's center of gravity |
| **Computation** | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(V_y/V_x)$ at each tire | $\beta = \tan^{-1}(V_y/V_x)$ at CG |
| **Use** | Tire force generation, ESC | Vehicle handling dynamics, stability analysis |
| **Number per vehicle** | 4 (one per tire) | 1 (single vehicle value) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Even when $\beta = 0$ (vehicle moving purely along its longitudinal axis), the individual tire slip angles can be non-zero due to steering and load transfer. They are not the same.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the **slip angle at the front and rear wheels** of a vehicle using the **bicycle model**. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car has the following parameters:
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m
- Distance from CG to front axle: $a = 1.2$ m
- Distance from CG to rear axle: $b = 1.4$ m
- Cornering stiffness (per axle): $C_{\alpha f} = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 90{,}000$ N/rad
- Speed $V = 25$ m/s
- Steering angle $\delta = 3°$ (road wheel)

The vehicle is in **steady-state cornering** on a path of radius $R = 150$ m. Calculate:
1. The yaw rate $r$.
2. The slip angles at front and rear tires.
3. The cornering forces at front and rear axles.
4. The understeer gradient $K$ and classify the vehicle's handling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a): Derivation**

**Assumptions:**
1. Vehicle moves on a flat, horizontal road.
2. Bicycle model (front and rear axles are lumped).
3. Small slip angles ($\alpha < 10°$).
4. Constant forward speed $V_x = V$.
5. Suspension effects (roll, pitch) are neglected.

**Derivation:**

Consider a vehicle in the $X$–$Y$ plane with CG at origin. The vehicle has yaw rate $r$ and lateral velocity $V_y$ at the CG. The front axle is at distance $a$ ahead, the rear at distance $b$ behind.

The **velocity components at the front axle** are:
- Longitudinal: $V_{x,f} = V_x$
- Lateral: $V_{y,f} = V_y + a \cdot r$

The **front wheel is steered** by angle $\delta$, so its heading direction makes an angle $\delta$ with the vehicle's $X$-axis. The slip angle is the **deviation of the velocity vector from the wheel heading**:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{y,f}}{V_{x,f}}\right) = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}\right)$$

The **velocity components at the rear axle** are:
- Longitudinal: $V_{x,r} = V_x$
- Lateral: $V_{y,r} = V_y - b \cdot r$

The **rear wheel is not steered** ($\delta = 0$), so:

$$\alpha_r = 0 - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{y,r}}{V_{x,r}}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x}\right)$$

For small slip angles, applying $\tan^{-1}(x) \approx x$:

$$\alpha_f \approx \delta - \frac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x} \qquad \alpha_r \approx \frac{b\,r - V_y}{V_x}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Yaw Rate**

For steady-state cornering at constant speed and radius $R$:

$$r = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{25}{150} = 0.1667 \text{ rad/s}$$

For steady-state, $V_y \approx 0$ (Ackermann condition).

**Step 2: Slip Angles**

Convert $\delta$ to radians: $\delta = 3° = 0.05236$ rad.

Front slip angle:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \frac{a \cdot r}{V} = 0.05236 - \frac{1.2 \times 0.1667}{25} = 0.05236 - 0.008 = 0.04436 \text{ rad} = 2.541°$$

Rear slip angle:

$$\alpha_r = \frac{b \cdot r}{V} = \frac{1.4 \times 0.1667}{25} = 0.009333 \text{ rad} = 0.535°$$

**Step 3: Cornering Forces**

$$F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f = 80{,}000 \times 0.04436 = 3{,}549 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r = 90{,}000 \times 0.009333 = 840 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Understeer Gradient**

$$K = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$$

Weight distribution:
- $W_f = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{b}{L} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{1.4}{2.6} = 7{,}924$ N
- $W_r = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{a}{L} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{1.2}{2.6} = 6{,}792$ N

$$K = \frac{7924}{80{,}000} - \frac{6792}{90{,}000} = 0.09905 - 0.07547 = 0.02358 \text{ rad/g}$$

In more standard form (dividing by $g = 9.81$):

$$K = \frac{0.02358}{9.81} = 0.002403 \text{ rad·s}^2/\text{m}$$

Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering** — a stable, safe handling characteristic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert degrees to radians** before substituting into the slip angle formulas. A common mistake is to plug $3°$ directly as a number without unit conversion, leading to wrong answers and **loss of 2–3 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **cornering stiffness**. Describe the three regimes of tire behavior with respect to slip angle using a labeled characteristic curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car traveling at $V_x = 30$ m/s has a lateral velocity $V_y = 1.5$ m/s at the front axle. If the steering angle is $\delta = 2°$, calculate:
1. The slip angle at the front tire.
2. The cornering force at the front tire, given $C_\alpha = 45{,}000$ N/rad per tire.
3. If the front axle has two tires, what is the **total front cornering force**?
4. If the rear tire operates in the **non-linear regime** with cornering force $F_{y,r} = 5{,}500$ N at slip angle $\alpha_r = 8°$, what is the **effective cornering stiffness** of the rear tire? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a): Cornering Stiffness and Three Regimes**

**Cornering Stiffness ($C_\alpha$):**

The cornering stiffness of a tire is defined as the **rate of change of cornering force with respect to slip angle** at zero slip angle. It is the slope of the $F_y$ vs $\alpha$ curve at the origin.

$$C_\alpha = \left.\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial \alpha}\right\vert_{\alpha = 0}$$

Units: **Newtons per radian (N/rad)** or **Newtons per degree (N/deg)**.

**Three Regimes of Tire Behavior:**

1. **Linear Regime ($0 \leq \alpha \leq 4°$–$6°$):** $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$. The relationship is linear. Cornering stiffness is constant. Passenger car tires operate in this regime during normal driving.

2. **Non-linear Regime ($4° \leq \alpha \leq 10°$–$12°$):** The cornering force continues to grow, but at a **decreasing rate**. The effective cornering stiffness is no longer constant and reduces with increasing $\alpha$. This is the transition zone near the tire's grip limit.

3. **Saturation Regime ($\alpha \geq 10°$–$12°$):** The cornering force reaches a **peak** and then plateaus or decreases. The tire is effectively sliding, and no more grip can be generated. This is a loss-of-control condition.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Definition of cornering stiffness (2) + Three regimes with curve explanation (4) + Typical values/safety note (1).

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Front Slip Angle**

Using the slip angle definition:

$$\alpha_f = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 0.04996 \text{ rad} = 2.862°$$

Since $\alpha_f < 10°$, the small-angle approximation is valid:

$$\alpha_f \approx \frac{V_y}{V_x} = 0.05 \text{ rad} = 2.865°$$

**Step 2: Cornering Force per Tire**

$$F_{y,\text{per tire}} = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha_f = 45{,}000 \times 0.05 = 2{,}250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Total Front Cornering Force (Both Tires)**

$$F_{y,\text{front,total}} = 2 \times 2{,}250 = 4{,}500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Effective Cornering Stiffness of Rear Tire**

Convert $\alpha_r$ to radians: $\alpha_r = 8° = 0.1396$ rad.

$$C_{\alpha,\text{eff,rear}} = \frac{F_{y,r}}{\alpha_r} = \frac{5{,}500}{0.1396} = 39{,}399 \text{ N/rad}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The effective cornering stiffness of the rear tire (39,399 N/rad) is **lower** than the front tire's stiffness (45,000 N/rad) — this is because the rear tire is operating in the **non-linear regime** where the effective stiffness decreases. This is the fundamental reason why **rear tires saturate first** during aggressive cornering, leading to **oversteer** tendencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In Part (b) Step 4, students commonly forget that **cornering stiffness is computed using radians, not degrees**. If you compute $5500 / 8 = 687.5$ N/deg, the answer is wrong by a factor of 57.3. KTU evaluators **deduct 1 mark** for this specific error.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Slip Angle Problems (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing degrees and radians in the same problem. **Always convert to one unit** before computing.
> 2. **Confusing slip angle (tire) with sideslip angle (vehicle):** These are different quantities at different points. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply small-angle approximation:** Some questions require the exact $\tan^{-1}$ form. Others require the linear form. **Check which one is being asked.**
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** Mixing up the sign of yaw rate $r$ and its contribution to $V_y$ at the front and rear. Use a body-fixed coordinate diagram and label all directions.
> 5. **Treating cornering stiffness as universal:** $C_\alpha$ is **per tire** in some texts and **per axle** in others. The problem statement will specify — but if not, **state your assumption clearly**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- **Slip Angle (α):** Angle between wheel plane and velocity vector at the contact patch. Defined as $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(V_y / V_x)$.
- **Sideslip Angle (β):** Angle at the vehicle CG. **Not the same as slip angle.**
- **Small-Angle Approximation:** Valid for $\alpha < 10°$. Use $\alpha \approx V_y / V_x$.
- **Cornering Force (linear):** $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$.
- **Cornering Stiffness:** Slope of $F_y$ vs $\alpha$ curve at origin. Units: N/rad.
- **Bicycle Model Slip Angles:**
  - $\alpha_f = \delta - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y + a\,r}{V_x}\right)$
  - $\alpha_r = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y - b\,r}{V_x}\right)$
- **Three Regimes:** Linear (small α), Non-linear (medium α), Saturation (large α).
- **Pneumatic Trail ($t$):** Lateral offset of cornering force resultant. Generates aligning moment.
- **Aligning Moment:** $M_z = -t \cdot F_y = -t \cdot C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$. Self-centers the steering.
- **Understeer Gradient:** $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$. Positive → understeer (stable), Negative → oversteer (unstable), Zero → neutral steer.
- **Sign Convention:** Use SAE/ISO symmetric. Left positive, right positive. Yaw rate positive = counter-clockwise (top view).
- **Typical Slip Angle Range:** $1°$–$10°$ for passenger cars; up to $12°$–$15°$ for racing slicks.
- **Critical Physical Insight:** **No slip angle = No cornering force.** A rolling tire cannot change direction without sliding.
- **Engineering Applications:** ESC systems, suspension tuning, tire compound selection, autonomous vehicle control.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When solving KTU board problems on slip angle, **always start by drawing a free-body diagram** showing the wheel plane, velocity vector, and the angle $\alpha$ between them. This earns you 1–2 "presentation marks" in the valuation and helps you avoid sign errors. Also, **always state assumptions explicitly** (e.g., "assuming small slip angles," "neglecting suspension effects"). Examiners reward this rigor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cornering Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cornering Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cornering Force — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cornering Force ($F_y$)** is defined as the **lateral (side) force** developed at the tire–road contact patch in the direction perpendicular to the wheel's instantaneous plane of rotation. It is the *resultant lateral reaction* generated by the tire when subjected to a **slip angle ($\alpha$)** during cornering, braking, or any combined loading condition.

Mathematically, in its most general vector form, the cornering force is expressed as:

$$
\vec{F}_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha + \text{(non-linear higher-order terms)}
$$

where $C_\alpha$ is the **cornering stiffness** (units: N/rad) and $\alpha$ is the **slip angle** (units: radians).

The cornering force acts at a point displaced behind the geometric center of the contact patch, generating a **pneumatic trail** ($t$), which in turn produces the **self-aligning torque** ($M_z$) that tends to steer the wheel back toward the rolling direction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — A Rowing Boat Oar:**
> Imagine pushing a rowing oar backward through water at a slight angle. The oar is "pointed" straight, but because the boat is moving and you push it sideways, water flows obliquely over the blade. The water pushes the oar **sideways and slightly backward**, not just straight back. That sideways push is the **cornering force**. The harder/faster you push the oar, the larger the sideways push — until the water "skids" (saturation), and the force stops growing.

In a tire:
- The **slip angle** is the "tilt" between the wheel's pointing direction and the actual travel path.
- The **rubber of the tire** is the "oar blade."
- The **road surface** is the "water."
- The **sideways push from the road on the tire** is the **cornering force**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cornering stiffness (passenger car tire) | $C_\alpha$ | 30,000 – 100,000 | N/rad |
| Maximum cornering force coefficient | $\mu_y$ | 0.8 – 1.2 (dry asphalt) | — |
| Vertical load on tire | $F_z$ | 3,000 – 6,000 | N |
| Pneumatic trail | $t$ | 0.02 – 0.05 | m |
| Aligning torque stiffness | $C_{Mz}$ | varies with $F_z$ | N·m/rad |

> [!TIP]
> The **maximum cornering force** is fundamentally limited by the tire-road friction, governed by the inequality $\vert F_y \vert \leq \mu_y F_z$, where $\mu_y$ is the **lateral friction coefficient**.

## 1.4 Why Cornering Force Matters in Vehicle Dynamics

Cornering force is the **single most important tire-generated force** in vehicle handling. Without it:
- Vehicles cannot negotiate turns.
- Lateral acceleration ($a_y$) is impossible to generate.
- The lateral tire force budget (the **friction circle/ellipse**) cannot be allocated between cornering and braking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Cornering force, combined with the slip angle concept, forms the **foundation of the bicycle model**, the **Magic Formula (Pacejka) tire model**, and the **brush tire model** — all of which appear directly in Module 2 derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Cornering Force — Step-by-Step Logic

The cornering force does **not** arise from a single mechanism. It is the integrated lateral reaction of a complex, distributed shear-stress field across the contact patch. The mechanism unfolds as follows:

1. **Tire Deflection Initiation:**
   When the wheel is steered by an angle $\delta$ while the vehicle is translating along a path that curves at radius $R$, the wheel's heading and velocity vectors no longer coincide. A **slip angle** $\alpha = \delta - \arctan(v/L \cdot R)$ develops at the tire.

2. **Lateral Shear in the Contact Patch:**
   The contact patch is a roughly rectangular region of width $2a$ and length $2b$. As the tire rolls, each infinitesimal element of the contact patch undergoes a small lateral distortion (shear strain) because the carcass must twist to follow the steered direction.

3. **Road Reaction (Shear Stress Integration):**
   The road surface resists this lateral distortion through a distributed shear-stress field $\tau_y(x, y)$. The total cornering force is:

   $$
   F_y = \int\!\!\int_{\text{patch}} \tau_y(x, y) \, dx \, dy
   $$

4. **Resultant Displacement → Pneumatic Trail:**
   Because the shear stress distribution is **asymmetric** along the rolling direction, the resultant of $F_y$ does not pass through the geometric center of the contact patch. It acts at a distance $t$ **behind** the center — this is the **pneumatic trail**.

5. **Generation of Aligning Torque:**
   The displaced line of action of $F_y$ about the wheel center creates a moment:

   $$
   M_z = -F_y \cdot t
   $$

   This is the **self-aligning (restoring) torque** that automatically returns the steering to the straight-ahead position when the driver releases the wheel.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Linear Approximation

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear vs. Non-linear behavior:**
> - For **small slip angles** ($\vert \alpha \vert < 4°$ to $6°$), the cornering force varies **linearly** with $\alpha$. This is the regime used in the **linear bicycle model** for handling analysis.
> - Beyond this range, the rubber saturates against the road and $F_y$ exhibits a **plateau** (the peak), followed by a **post-peak falloff** (the tire has lost adhesion).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Domain / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_y \approx C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | Linear cornering force law | $\vert \alpha \vert \leq 4°$–$6°$ |
| 2 | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha - K \cdot \alpha^3 + \ldots$ | Cubic polynomial fit (non-linear) | Mid-range $\alpha$ |
| 3 | $F_y = D \sin(C \arctan(B \alpha - E(B \alpha - \arctan(B \alpha))))$ | **Pacejka Magic Formula** | Full range to saturation |
| 4 | $M_z = -F_y \cdot t$ | Self-aligning torque from pneumatic trail | Small $\alpha$ |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{F_y}{C_\alpha}$ | Pneumatic trail (linear regime) | $\vert \alpha \vert \leq 4°$ |
| 6 | $\mu_y = \dfrac{\vert F_y \vert_{\max}}{F_z}$ | Peak lateral friction coefficient | At saturation |
| 7 | $\dfrac{F_x^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} + \dfrac{F_y^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} \leq 1$ | **Friction circle** (combined slip) | Combined braking + cornering |
| 8 | $C_\alpha \propto F_z$ (approx.) | Cornering stiffness scales with vertical load | Static load range |
| 9 | $\alpha = \delta - \arctan\left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right)$ | Kinematic slip angle definition | Low-speed simplification |
| 10 | $a_y = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$ | Lateral acceleration of vehicle mass | Steady-state cornering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units are SI throughout.** Slip angles may be expressed in either **radians** (for formula use) or **degrees** (for graphs and verbal discussion). Always convert before plugging into equations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Stability Control (VSC/ESC):** Uses real-time $F_y$ estimates to detect impending skid and apply corrective brake interventions.
- **Steering System Design:** Aligning torque $M_z$ must be large enough to provide road "feel" but small enough not to resist parking maneuvers.
- **Tire Compound Selection:** Race tires maximize $C_\alpha$ for sharper turn-in response; touring tires trade some $C_\alpha$ for mileage and wet grip.
- **Active Suspension & Steer-by-Wire:** Both depend on accurate online identification of the tire's cornering force characteristic.

## 2.5 The Three Fundamental Regimes of Cornering Force

| Regime | Slip Angle | Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $0° \leq \alpha \leq 4°$ | $F_y \propto \alpha$ | Used in bicycle model, handling diagrams |
| **Transitional / Peak** | $4° \leq \alpha \leq 10°$ | $F_y$ reaches $\mu F_z$ | Limit of adhesion; vehicle near slide |
| **Friction-limited (Post-peak)** | $\alpha > 10°$ | $F_y$ drops or plateaus | Tire sliding; vehicle unstable |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Contact-Patch Shear Distribution to Resultant Cornering Force

We begin with the contact patch idealized as a rectangle of length $2a$ (in the rolling direction, $x$) and width $2b$ (in the lateral direction, $y$). The coordinate origin is placed at the geometric center of the patch.

### Step 1: Define the distributed lateral shear stress

The lateral shear stress at any point in the contact patch is assumed to be a function of the local slip angle and the local normal pressure:

$$
\tau_y(x, y) = G \cdot \alpha(x, y)
$$

where $G$ is the **shear stiffness modulus of the tire rubber per unit area** (units: N/m²·rad⁻¹), and $\alpha(x, y)$ is the local slip angle.

### Step 2: Total cornering force as a double integral

The total cornering force is the integral of the lateral shear stress over the entire contact patch:

$$
F_y = \int_{-a}^{a} \int_{-b}^{b} \tau_y(x, y) \, dy \, dx
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the shear stress

$$
F_y = \int_{-a}^{a} \int_{-b}^{b} G \cdot \alpha(x, y) \, dy \, dx
$$

### Step 4: Apply the slip-angle field

For a uniform slip angle across the patch (the simplest valid approximation), $\alpha(x, y) = \alpha$:

$$
F_y = G \cdot \alpha \int_{-a}^{a} \int_{-b}^{b} dy \, dx
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the geometric integral

The area of the contact patch is $(2a)(2b) = 4ab$:

$$
\int_{-a}^{a} \int_{-b}^{b} dy \, dx = 4ab
$$

### Step 6: Define cornering stiffness as the integrated shear modulus

Let $C_\alpha = G \cdot 4ab$ (the total cornering stiffness of the tire). Substituting:

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha
$$

This is the **fundamental linear cornering force law**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Pneumatic Trail and Self-Aligning Torque

### Step 1: Position vector of the resultant force

In the contact patch, the lateral shear stress distribution is not symmetric about the centerline. Due to the **twisting deformation** of the tire carcass as it enters and leaves the contact patch, the resultant $F_y$ acts at a point $(x_t, 0)$ where $x_t < 0$ (i.e., behind the geometric center).

### Step 2: Define pneumatic trail

The **pneumatic trail** is the lever arm:

$$
t = -x_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign reflects the convention that the trail is positive when the force acts **behind** the contact center.

### Step 3: Compute aligning torque about the wheel center

The wheel center is at vertical height $r$ (effective rolling radius) above the contact patch. The moment arm from the wheel center to the line of action of $F_y$ is approximately the pneumatic trail (since $t \ll r$):

$$
M_z \approx -F_y \cdot t
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$

$$
M_z = -C_\alpha \cdot t \cdot \alpha
$$

### Step 5: Define aligning torque stiffness

Let $C_{Mz} = C_\alpha \cdot t$:

$$
M_z = -C_{Mz} \cdot \alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** indicates that the moment is **restoring** — it opposes the slip angle, tending to return the wheel to $\alpha = 0$. This is precisely why a properly inflated tire, when disturbed, "self-aligns."

## 3.3 Derivation III — Peak Cornering Force and the Friction Limit

### Step 1: Maximum shear stress at the road interface

The maximum lateral shear stress the road can support is bounded by the local friction coefficient times the local normal pressure $p(x, y)$:

$$
\tau_{y,\max}(x, y) = \mu \cdot p(x, y)
$$

### Step 2: Peak cornering force

In the limit of full friction mobilization, the cornering force reaches its maximum:

$$
F_{y,\max} = \int_{-a}^{a} \int_{-b}^{b} \mu \cdot p(x, y) \, dy \, dx = \mu \cdot F_z
$$

where $F_z$ is the total vertical load, by the definition of normal pressure integral.

### Step 3: Friction ellipse / circle

When the tire must simultaneously produce a longitudinal force $F_x$ (e.g., during braking in a turn), the available lateral force is reduced:

$$
\left( \frac{F_x}{\mu F_z} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{F_y}{\mu F_z} \right)^2 \leq 1
$$

This is the **friction circle** (or ellipse, depending on tire anisotropy).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A passenger car tire has cornering stiffness $C_\alpha = 40{,}000$ N/rad and a pneumatic trail $t = 0.03$ m. The tire operates at a slip angle of $\alpha = 2°$. Compute (a) the cornering force, (b) the pneumatic trail moment, and (c) the aligning torque stiffness.

### Solution:

**Step (a): Convert slip angle to radians**

$$
\alpha = 2° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.0349 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step (b): Compute cornering force**

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha = 40{,}000 \times 0.0349 = 1396.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (c): Compute aligning torque**

$$
M_z = -F_y \cdot t = -1396.3 \times 0.03 = -41.89 \text{ N·m}
$$

The negative sign indicates restoring action.

**Step (d): Compute aligning torque stiffness**

$$
C_{Mz} = C_\alpha \cdot t = 40{,}000 \times 0.03 = 1200 \text{ N·m/rad}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a 4000 N vertical load, the lateral friction coefficient is $\mu_y = F_y / F_z = 1396.3 / 4000 = 0.349$. This is well within the dry-asphalt operating range (0.8–1.2), confirming the tire is in its **linear regime**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cornering Force Generation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **causal flow** from driver input to cornering force generation, including all intermediate subsystems and feedback paths used in vehicle dynamics control.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Steering Input] --> B[Steering Wheel Angle delta_sw]
    B --> C[Steering Rack and Pinion Geometry]
    C --> D[Front Wheel Steer Angle delta]
    D --> E[Vehicle Translational Motion v and omega]
    E --> F[Slip Angle alpha at Tire]
    F --> G[Tire Rubber Shear Deformation]
    G --> H[Contact Patch Shear Stress Field tau_y]
    H --> I[Resultant Cornering Force F_y]
    I --> J[Lateral Acceleration a_y of Vehicle Mass]
    J --> K[Vehicle Yaw and Sideslip Response]
    K --> L[Updated Slip Angles at All Four Tires]
    L --> F
    I --> M[Pneumatic Trail t]
    M --> N[Self Aligning Torque M_z]
    N --> O[Steering Feedback to Driver Hands]
    I --> P[Friction Budget Allocator]
    P --> Q[Combined Braking and Cornering Limit]
    H --> R[Saturation Check vs mu F_z]
    R --> S{Is F_y at Friction Limit?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Activate ESC Brake Intervention]
    S -->|No| U[Continue Normal Cornering]
    T --> L
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Forces, Trail, and Aligning Torque at the Contact Patch

The figure below shows the free-body diagram of a steered tire in the contact patch, illustrating the spatial relationship between the vertical load, cornering force, pneumatic trail, and self-aligning torque.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Wheel Center] --> R[Effective Rolling Radius r]
    R --> P[Geometric Center of Contact Patch]
    P -->|Distance t| A[Line of Action of Cornering Force F_y]
    A -->|Acts at point x_t| Q[Resultant Lateral Force Vector F_y]
    P -->|Normal Load| N[Vertical Force F_z upward]
    N --> W
    Q -->|Perpendicular to F_z| M[Self Aligning Torque M_z about Wheel Center]
    M -->|Restoring moment| W
    Q -->|Lateral reaction| Road[Road Surface Reaction]
```
