```mermaid
    E1 --> F1[Validate via ECE R13 Test Protocol]
    C1 --> F1
    F1 --> G[Sign off Brake System]
```

## 4.3 Front-Rear Brake Force Distribution (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Cylinder P_m] --> B[Front Brake Circuit]
    A --> C[Rear Brake Circuit]
    B --> D[Front Wheel Cylinders P_wf]
    C --> E[Rear Wheel Cylinders P_wr]
    D --> F[Front Brake Force F_f]
    E --> G[Rear Brake Force F_r]
    F --> H[Total Brake Force F_total]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Vehicle Deceleration a]

    J[Static Weight W_f] --> K[Dynamic W_f plus m a h over L]
    K --> L[Front Lock Limit mu W_f dynamic]
    L --> M{Ideal Distribution Match}
    G --> N[Dynamic W_r minus m a h over L]
    N --> O[Rear Lock Limit mu W_r dynamic]
    O --> M
    M -- Match --> P[No Premature Lock up]
    M -- Mismatch --> Q[Premature Front or Rear Lock]
    Q --> R[ABS Intervention or Proportioning Valve Tune]
    R --> M
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: $\eta_b$ Loss Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Energy 100 percent] --> B[Pedal Mechanical Loss 5 percent]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Compliance Loss 3 percent]
    C --> D[Wheel Cylinder Seal Loss 2 percent]
    D --> E[Friction Interface Loss 5 percent]
    E --> F[Brake Heating Thermal Loss 3 percent]
    F --> G[Output Useful Braking 82 percent]

    H[Brake Efficiency eta_b] -.-> G
    I[Note All losses are multiplicative] -.-> B
    I -.-> C
    I -.-> D
    I -.-> E
    I -.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows labelled with percentages represent energy *losses*. The output G corresponds to a typical $\eta_b \approx 0.82$ for a well-engineered passenger vehicle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define braking efficiency. Explain its significance in vehicle performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Braking efficiency ($\eta_b$) is defined as the ratio of the actual deceleration of the vehicle to the maximum theoretically achievable deceleration at the prevailing road-tire coefficient of friction:

$$
\eta_b \;=\; \frac{a}{\mu g}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It is a **dimensionless index** of how effectively the brake system converts available tire-road friction into vehicle retardation.
2. Higher $\eta_b$ (closer to 1) means **shorter stopping distances** and **better active-safety performance**.
3. It encapsulates the cumulative mechanical, hydraulic, and thermal losses of the entire brake system.
4. It is a **regulated parameter** in automotive standards (e.g., ECE R13 mandates minimum stopping performance which implicitly requires $\eta_b \ge$ certain threshold).
5. It directly influences **fuel economy** in hybrid/EV regenerative-braking strategies.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]`
**Distinguish between brake factor and brake efficiency. Why are both required in brake-system design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Brake Factor ($\beta$) | Brake Efficiency ($\eta_b$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Ratio of wheel-cylinder pressure to master-cylinder pressure: $\beta = P_w / P_m$ | Ratio of actual deceleration to maximum theoretical deceleration: $\eta_b = a/(\mu g)$ |
| Nature | Geometric / hydraulic design parameter | Performance / system-level outcome |
| Depends on | Piston areas, pedal ratio, mechanical efficiency | All subsystem losses including thermal, hydraulic, friction |
| Typical value | 1.5 – 4.0 (dimensionless) | 0.7 – 0.95 |

Both are required because $\beta$ alone describes the *hydraulic design intent* (input-side), while $\eta_b$ measures the *realized braking performance* (output-side). A system can have a high $\beta$ but still suffer low $\eta_b$ due to thermal fade or poor mechanical efficiency — both must be considered for proper design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in PCAUT602 carry 14 marks each, with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **stopping distance** of a vehicle in terms of the brake efficiency, initial velocity, and tire-road coefficient of friction. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car moving at **108 km/h** on a level road has a brake efficiency of **0.85** and a tire-road friction coefficient of **0.75**. Calculate:
- (i) the maximum achievable deceleration
- (ii) the actual vehicle deceleration
- (iii) the stopping distance
- (iv) the stopping time **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the concept of **ideal front-rear brake-force distribution** (I-curve). Why is a proportioning valve often used in practice, and how does it relate to brake efficiency? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car has **55 %** of its weight on the front axle statically, a wheelbase of **2.6 m**, and a CG height of **0.55 m**. The tire-road friction is **0.65**. Determine:
- (i) the maximum possible deceleration on level ground
- (ii) the front and rear dynamic axle loads at this deceleration
- (iii) the front and rear maximum brake forces **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Level road (no gradient).
2. Constant brake efficiency throughout the stop.
3. Constant tire-road friction coefficient.
4. No aerodynamic drag.
5. Brake system is at thermal equilibrium (no fade).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The total braking force that decelerates the vehicle is:

$$
F_{total} \;=\; m a
$$

The maximum friction force the road can supply is $\mu m g$. The brake efficiency is the *fraction* of this actually used:

$$
F_{total} \;=\; \eta_b \cdot \mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

Equating both:

$$
m a \;=\; \eta_b \, \mu \, m \, g \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a \;=\; \eta_b \, \mu \, g
$$

From the third equation of motion with $V_f = 0$:

$$
V^2 \;=\; 2 a s \quad\Longrightarrow\quad s \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2 a}
$$

Substituting $a = \eta_b \mu g$:

$$
\boxed{\,s \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2 \, \eta_b \, \mu \, g}\,}
$$

∎

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 108 \,\text{km/h} = 108 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 30 \,\text{m/s}$
- $\eta_b = 0.85$
- $\mu = 0.75$
- $g = 9.81 \,\text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Maximum achievable deceleration (1.5 marks):**

$$
a_{max} \;=\; \mu g \;=\; 0.75 \times 9.81 \;=\; 7.358 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

**(ii) Actual deceleration (1.5 marks):**

$$
a \;=\; \eta_b \cdot \mu g \;=\; 0.85 \times 7.358 \;=\; 6.254 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

**(iii) Stopping distance (2 marks):**

$$
s \;=\; \frac{V^2}{2 a} \;=\; \frac{30^2}{2 \times 6.254} \;=\; \frac{900}{12.508} \;=\; 71.96 \,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s \;\approx\; 72 \,\text{m}\,}
$$

**(iv) Stopping time (2 marks):**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{V}{a} \;=\; \frac{30}{6.254} \;=\; 4.797 \,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \;\approx\; 4.8 \,\text{s}\,}
$$

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Concept (7 marks)

**Ideal Brake-Force Distribution (4 marks):**

The *ideal* distribution is one in which **both front and rear wheels lock simultaneously**, ensuring 100 % utilization of available friction at *every* deceleration level. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\frac{F_f}{F_r} \;=\; \frac{W_f'}{W_r'} \;=\; \frac{W_f + W a h/(g L)}{W_r - W a h/(g L)}
$$

This relationship, when plotted as $F_r$ vs $F_f$ for different decelerations, gives a curved locus called the **I-curve** (Ideal curve).

**Proportioning Valve (2 marks):**

In practice, achieving the I-curve is mechanically complex. A simpler approach is a fixed-ratio proportioning valve giving a *straight line* (L-curve): $F_r = k \cdot F_f$ where $k$ is constant. The L-curve typically lies *below* the I-curve for low decelerations, meaning the rear brakes are intentionally under-powered to **prevent premature rear-wheel lock-up** (which causes spin).

**Relation to Brake Efficiency (1 mark):**

A well-tuned proportioning valve keeps both axles operating near the friction limit, maximizing $\eta_b$. A poorly tuned valve (L-curve too low) means rear brakes are barely used → lower $\eta_b$ even if front brakes are excellent.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $W_f = 0.55 W$ (static front fraction)
- $W_r = 0.45 W$ (static rear fraction)
- $L = 2.6 \,\text{m}$
- $h = 0.55 \,\text{m}$
- $\mu = 0.65$

**(i) Maximum deceleration (2 marks):**

On level ground:

$$
a_{max} \;=\; \mu g \;=\; 0.65 \times 9.81 \;=\; 6.377 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

**(ii) Dynamic axle loads (3 marks):**

The weight transfer to the front axle is:

$$
\Delta W \;=\; \frac{W \cdot a \cdot h}{g \cdot L} \;=\; \frac{W \times 6.377 \times 0.55}{9.81 \times 2.6} \;=\; \frac{W \times 3.507}{25.506} \;=\; 0.1375 \, W
$$

Front dynamic load:

$$
W_f' \;=\; 0.55 W + 0.1375 W \;=\; 0.6875 \, W
$$

Rear dynamic load:

$$
W_r' \;=\; 0.45 W - 0.1375 W \;=\; 0.3125 \, W
$$

**(iii) Maximum brake forces (2 marks):**

$$
F_{f,max} \;=\; \mu \cdot W_f' \;=\; 0.65 \times 0.6875 \, W \;=\; 0.4469 \, W
$$

$$
F_{r,max} \;=\; \mu \cdot W_r' \;=\; 0.65 \times 0.3125 \, W \;=\; 0.2031 \, W
$$

If $W = m g$ and $m = 1500 \,\text{kg}$ (typical car), then $W = 14{,}715 \,\text{N}$:

$$
F_{f,max} \;\approx\; 6575 \,\text{N}, \quad F_{r,max} \;\approx\; 2989 \,\text{N}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**  
> - **Unit conversion error (2-mark penalty):** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s (multiply by 5/18).  
> - **Wrong formula (3-mark penalty):** Using $s = V^2/(2\mu g)$ *without* $\eta_b$. Always re-read whether the question gives actual or maximum deceleration.  
> - **Sign error in dynamic load transfer (2-mark penalty):** Braking *adds* load to the front and *subtracts* from the rear — not the other way around.  
> - **Confusing I-curve and L-curve (1-mark penalty):** The I-curve is the ideal (parabolic-looking), the L-curve is a straight line from the proportioning valve.  
> - **Forgetting assumptions in derivation (1-mark penalty):** Always state at least 3 assumptions before writing the derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready Bullets):**

- [ ] **Braking efficiency** $\eta_b$ = ratio of *actual* deceleration to *maximum possible* deceleration $= a / (\mu g)$.
- [ ] $\eta_b$ is **dimensionless**, typically in the range **0.65 – 0.95** for real vehicles.
- [ ] **Equivalent friction** $\mu_e = \mu \cdot \eta_b$ is a powerful shorthand for stopping-distance problems.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** $s = V^2 / (2 \mu g \eta_b)$ — quadratic in $V$, inversely proportional to $\eta_b$.
- [ ] **Stopping time** $t = V / (\mu g \eta_b)$ — linear in $V$, inversely proportional to $\eta_b$.
- [ ] **Brake factor** $\beta = P_w / P_m$ — purely a design/hydraulic parameter, *not* a performance measure.
- [ ] **Brake torque** $T_b = 2 \beta P_m A_w r_{eff} \eta_{mech}$ (per wheel, drum or disc).
- [ ] **Wheel braking force** $F_b = T_b / r_w$ — this is what the tire transmits to the road.
- [ ] **Maximum deceleration** $a_{max} = \mu g$ — applies only when tires are at the verge of lock-up.
- [ ] **Dynamic axle load transfer** during braking: $\Delta W = W a h / (g L)$ — shifts load **forward**.
- [ ] **Dynamic front load** $W_f' = W_f + \Delta W$; **Dynamic rear load** $W_r' = W_r - \Delta W$.
- [ ] **I-curve** (ideal) gives simultaneous front-rear lock-up; **L-curve** (line pressure) is the practical straight-line approximation.
- [ ] **Proportioning valves** intentionally under-power rear brakes to prevent premature rear lock-up and spin.
- [ ] **Brake fade** (thermal) reduces $\mu_p$ → reduces $\eta_b$ → lengthens stopping distance.
- [ ] **ABS** modulates brake pressure to maintain slip ratio in the high-$\mu$ region → sustains $\eta_b \to 1$.
- [ ] **Energy loss cascade** in a brake system: pedal (5 %) → hydraulic (3 %) → seal (2 %) → pad (5 %) → thermal (3 %) → tire (variable).
- [ ] **ECE R13 / FMVSS 135** legally mandate minimum stopping performance, indirectly enforcing $\eta_b$ thresholds.
- [ ] **High $\eta_b$ is desirable** but a value of exactly 1.0 means lock-up, which is **unsafe** without ABS — practical limit is $\eta_b \approx 0.95$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **One-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:**  
> *"Brake efficiency is the fraction of available road friction the brake system can actually use; everything else — pedal, hydraulics, friction, heat, tires — eats into it."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Done in Braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Work Done in Braking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In vehicle dynamics, **Work Done in Braking** is defined as the total mechanical energy (kinetic energy) that must be dissipated by the braking system to bring a moving vehicle to a complete stop, or to retard its motion by a desired amount. Mathematically, it represents the scalar product of the effective braking force and the stopping distance, which is ultimately converted into **thermal energy (heat)** at the friction interfaces of the brake assemblies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Work done in braking is the amount of kinetic energy of a vehicle that must be absorbed and dissipated as heat by the friction elements (brake linings, pads, discs, or drums) to achieve the required deceleration."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sliding Coin on a Carpet":**
> Imagine pushing a heavy coin across a smooth glass table — it slides for a long time before stopping because friction is low (less work done, little heat generated). Now push the *same* coin across a rough carpet — it stops almost instantly because friction is high (more work done, more heat felt on your fingertips). A vehicle's brakes work on this exact principle. The brake pads press against a rotating disc/drum, converting the vehicle's forward kinetic energy into **heat** through friction. The harder the brakes work, the more heat is generated — this is why racing cars have massive cooling vents on their brakes!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity (**g**) | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Specific heat capacity of cast iron (brake drum) | **460 J/(kg·K)** |
| Specific heat capacity of steel (brake disc) | **500 J/(kg·K)** |
| Kinetic energy of a 1500 kg car at 100 km/h | **≈ 578,703 J (≈ 0.579 MJ)** |
| Braking efficiency (η) for typical road car | **0.6 – 0.9 (60% – 90%)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total work done in braking always equals the **initial kinetic energy** of the vehicle (by the Work-Energy Theorem), assuming the vehicle comes to a complete stop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Braking Energy Dissipation

The process of work done in braking follows a well-defined chain of energy transformations:

- **Step 1: Energy Storage (Pre-Braking Phase):** The vehicle possesses translational kinetic energy, $KE_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$, and rotational kinetic energy in the wheels, $KE_{rot} = \sum \frac{1}{2} I_w \omega^2$.

- **Step 2: Driver Input:** The driver actuates the brake pedal, which generates hydraulic/mechanical force that pushes friction material (pads/shoes) against the rotating brake disc/drum.

- **Step 3: Friction Energy Conversion:** The kinetic friction force $F_f = \mu N$ (where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $N$ is the normal force from the brake actuation) acts at the friction interface, creating a **braking torque** $T_b = F_f \times r_{eff}$.

- **Step 4: Deceleration & Work:** The braking torque creates a retarding force at the road-tire contact patch, decelerating the vehicle. The work done equals force × distance (or torque × angular displacement).

- **Step 5: Heat Generation:** The friction work is entirely converted into **heat energy** (a portion is also lost as sound, light, and minor wear of brake material). This heat raises the temperature of the brake components.

- **Step 6: Heat Dissipation:** The heat is removed from the brakes via convection (air cooling), conduction (to surrounding structure), and radiation. If heat generation exceeds dissipation, **brake fade** occurs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_b = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Work done = Initial kinetic energy | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $W_b = F_b \cdot s$ | Work = Braking force × Stopping distance | J |
| 3 | $F_b = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd Law (braking force = mass × deceleration) | N |
| 4 | $a = \mu g$ | Maximum deceleration on level road | m/s² |
| 5 | $s = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | Stopping distance on level road | m |
| 6 | $W_b = m g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta) \cdot s$ | Work on a gradient (descending) | J |
| 7 | $W_b = \frac{1}{2} (m + m_e) v^2$ | Work with rotational inertia correction | J |
| 8 | $m_e = \frac{I_w}{r^2}$ | Effective mass per wheel | kg |
| 9 | $W_b = \frac{1}{2} I_w \omega^2$ | Rotational KE of a single wheel | J |
| 10 | $P_{avg} = \frac{W_b}{t_s}$ | Average power dissipated during stop | Watts (W) |
| 11 | $\Delta T = \frac{W_b}{m_b \cdot c_p}$ | Temperature rise of brake mass | °C or K |
| 12 | $\eta_b = \frac{F_b}{F_{max}}$ | Braking efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| 13 | $P_{inst} = F_b \cdot v$ | Instantaneous braking power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When asked for total kinetic energy, remember to include the rotational energy of the wheels. The "effective mass" of a typical car wheel (with tire) is approximately **m_e ≈ 0.015 m to 0.02 m per wheel** (about 1.5%–2% of vehicle mass per wheel).

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The principle of work done in braking is critical in:

- **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS):** Optimizes friction utilization to prevent wheel lock-up, ensuring maximum η_b.
- **Regenerative Braking (EVs/Hybrids):** Recovers a portion of W_b as electrical energy instead of heat, improving range.
- **Brake Disc/Drum Sizing:** Engineers must ensure the brake mass can absorb the heat without exceeding critical temperatures (typically < 400°C for organic pads).
- **Railway & Heavy Vehicle Braking:** Determines the number of brake discs and cooling capacity required for freight trains descending long gradients.
- **Crash Energy Management:** Foundation of stopping distance regulations and road safety engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Work Done in Braking on a Level Road

**Given:** A vehicle of mass $m$ moving at initial velocity $v$ is brought to rest on a level road by applying a constant braking force $F_b$.

**To find:** Work done in braking $W_b$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Work-Energy Theorem:**
The net work done on the vehicle equals the change in its kinetic energy.

$$W_{net} = \Delta KE = KE_{final} - KE_{initial}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute boundary conditions:**
At the start: velocity = $v$, so $KE_{initial} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$.
At the end: velocity = $0$, so $KE_{final} = 0$.

$$W_{net} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = -\frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Step 3 — The only horizontal force is the braking force:**
Since we move in the direction of the braking force (opposite to motion), the work done by friction (braking) is positive in magnitude.

$$W_b = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

> **Logic:** The negative sign in the energy equation simply indicates that kinetic energy is *lost*; the magnitude of energy converted to heat is $\frac{1}{2} m v^2$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stopping Distance in Terms of Velocity & Friction

**Step 1 — Use the kinematic equation of motion:**
For uniform deceleration, using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a s$:

$$0 = v^2 - 2 a s$$

**Step 2 — Solve for stopping distance s:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2 a}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute maximum deceleration $a = \mu g$:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Work Done using $W_b = F_b \cdot s$:**
Since $F_b = m a = m \mu g$:

$$W_b = (m \mu g) \cdot \left( \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} \right) = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

> **Result Confirmed:** The work done is independent of the friction coefficient μ! A higher μ means a *shorter* stopping distance and *higher* braking force, but the **same total work** is dissipated.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Braking on a Gradient (Inclined Road)

**Setup:** A vehicle descending a road inclined at angle θ to the horizontal. The braking force must overcome both the friction limit AND the gravitational component along the slope.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram (along the slope):**
Forces acting along the incline:
- Gravitational component (down the slope): $mg \sin\theta$
- Friction braking force (up the slope): $F_b = \mu m g \cos\theta$

**Step 2 — Net deceleration:**
The total retarding force is:

$$F_{ret} = \mu m g \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta = m g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent deceleration:**

$$a = g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Stopping distance:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2 g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)}$$

**Step 5 — Work done in braking:**

$$W_b = F_b \cdot s = \mu m g \cos\theta \cdot \frac{v^2}{2 g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)}$$

$$W_b = \frac{\mu m v^2 \cos\theta}{2 (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)}$$

> **Important:** Note that $W_b < \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ on a downgrade, because gravity assists in reducing the kinetic energy. The remaining kinetic energy + gravitational PE is dissipated as heat in the brakes.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Brake Temperature Rise (Heat Absorption)

**Step 1 — Energy balance:**
Assume all braking work is absorbed as sensible heat by the brake disc/drum of mass $m_b$ and specific heat capacity $c_p$.

$$W_b = Q = m_b c_p \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{W_b}{m_b c_p} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v^2}{m_b c_p}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{m v^2}{2 m_b c_p}}$$

**Step 3 — Example Numerical Check:**
Let $m = 1500$ kg, $v = 30$ m/s (108 km/h), $m_b = 8$ kg (per disc, 4 discs total → $m_b = 32$ kg), $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·K):

$$\Delta T = \frac{1500 \times 30^2}{2 \times 32 \times 500} = \frac{1,350,000}{32,000} \approx 42.2 \, °C$$

This is a manageable temperature rise, well within operating limits.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Including Rotational Inertia of Wheels

**Step 1 — Total kinetic energy of the system:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{1}{2} I_{w,i} \omega_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Relate angular velocity to linear velocity:**
For pure rolling: $\omega = v / r$

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \sum \frac{1}{2} I_w \left( \frac{v}{r} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\frac{1}{2} v^2$:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} v^2 \left( m + \sum \frac{I_w}{r^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Define effective mass $m_e$:**

$$m_e = \frac{I_w}{r^2}$$

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} (m + m_{e,total}) v^2$$

> **Note:** The rotational energy adds approximately 3%–8% extra work that the brakes must dissipate compared to the translational KE alone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Flow Diagram During Braking

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VEHICLE IN MOTION] --> B[INITIAL KINETIC ENERGY]
    B --> B1[Translational KE 1/2 m v squared]
    B --> B2[Rotational KE of wheels 1/2 Iw omega squared]
    B1 --> C[BRAKE PEDAL ACTUATION]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[HYDRAULIC FORCE GENERATION]
    D --> E[FRICTION AT BRAKE INTERFACE]
    E --> F[BRAKING TORQUE Tb]
    F --> G[RETARDING FORCE AT TIRE PATCH]
    G --> H[VEHICLE DECELERATION]
    H --> I[STOPPING DISTANCE s]
    E --> J[HEAT GENERATION]
    J --> K[BRAKE TEMPERATURE RISE]
    K --> L[HEAT DISSIPATION]
    L --> L1[Convection to air]
    L --> L2[Conduction to hub]
    L --> L3[Radiation]
    K --> M{BRAKE FADE CHECK}
    M --> N[Temp less than 400C: SAFE]
    M --> O[Temp greater than 400C: BRAKE FADE RISK]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Analytical Components of Braking Work

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        M1[Vehicle Mass m]
        V1[Initial Velocity v]
        MU1[Friction Coefficient mu]
        THETA1[Road Gradient theta]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTATION[WORK COMPUTATION]
        KECALC[Kinetic Energy Calc]
        FBCALC[Braking Force Calc]
        SDCALC[Stopping Distance Calc]
        PBCALC[Power Dissipation Calc]
        DTCALC[Temperature Rise Calc]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[ENGINEERING OUTPUTS]
        WB[Work Done Wb in Joules]
        FB[Braking Force Fb in Newtons]
        SD[Stopping Distance s in meters]
        PB[Braking Power Pb in Watts]
        DT[Temp Rise Delta T in Celsius]
    end
    M1 --> KECALC
    V1 --> KECALC
    KECALC --> WB
    M1 --> FBCALC
    MU1 --> FBCALC
    THETA1 --> FBCALC
    FBCALC --> FB
    V1 --> SDCALC
    MU1 --> SDCALC
    THETA1 --> SDCALC
    SDCALC --> SD
    FB --> PBCALC
    V1 --> PBCALC
    PBCALC --> PB
    WB --> DTCALC
    DTCALC --> DT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

### 4.3 Schematic — Free Body Diagram of a Braking Vehicle on an Incline

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VEHICLE BODY m] --> B[WHEEL TIRE ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[ROAD SURFACE INCLINED AT THETA]
    C --> D[Reaction Force N = m g cos theta]
    A --> E[Weight mg acting vertically downward]
    A --> F[Braking Force Fb acting up the slope]
    E --> G[Component along slope: mg sin theta DOWN]
    E --> H[Component normal to slope: mg cos theta]
    F --> I[Friction mu times N = mu m g cos theta]
    G --> J[NET RETARDING FORCE = mu m g cos theta + mg sin theta]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Deceleration a = g mu cos theta + sin theta]
    K --> L[Stop after distance s = v squared over 2a]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Work Done in Braking" of a vehicle. State the fundamental energy equation relating it to the vehicle's mass and velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

Work done in braking is defined as the total kinetic energy of a vehicle that must be dissipated by the braking system to bring it to rest, ultimately appearing as heat at the friction interfaces.

**Fundamental equation:**

$$W_b = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

where $m$ is the vehicle mass (kg) and $v$ is the initial velocity (m/s). This work is independent of the friction coefficient between tire and road; it depends only on the mass and initial speed. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why does the stopping distance of a vehicle decrease as the road friction coefficient increases, even though the work done in braking remains the same?**

**Model Answer:**

Although the work done $W_b = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ is constant for a given mass and velocity, it is mathematically expressed as $W_b = F_b \cdot s$.

- As friction coefficient $\mu$ increases, the available braking force $F_b = \mu m g$ increases proportionally.
- Since $W_b$ is constant, the stopping distance $s = W_b / F_b$ decreases as $F_b$ increases.
- Hence, higher friction produces a **higher braking force** over a **shorter distance**, but the **same total energy** is converted to heat. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done in braking a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ on a level road, including the effect of rotational inertia of the wheels. Define effective mass clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A passenger car of mass 1800 kg (including 4 wheels of mass 35 kg each) is moving at 90 km/h. Each wheel has a mass moment of inertia of 1.2 kg·m² and an effective radius of 0.32 m. Calculate:
- (i) Total kinetic energy of the vehicle
- (ii) Stopping distance on a level road with μ = 0.7
- (iii) Heat generated if the entire energy is absorbed by 4 brake discs of 6 kg each ($c_p = 500$ J/kg·K) **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Total kinetic energy includes translation and rotation:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{1}{2} I_{w,i} \omega_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply rolling condition $\omega = v/r$:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum I_{w,i} \right) \cdot \frac{v^2}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Factor $\frac{1}{2} v^2$:**

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} v^2 \left( m + \frac{\sum I_{w,i}}{r^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Define effective mass:**

$$m_e = \frac{\sum I_{w,i}}{r^2} \quad \text{(sum over all 4 wheels)}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{W_b = KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} (m + m_e) v^2}$$

**Definition:** *Effective mass* $m_e$ is the equivalent translational mass that represents the rotational inertia of the wheels. It has the same kinetic energy as the rotating wheels when the vehicle is moving at speed $v$.

**Significance:** Including rotational inertia increases $W_b$ by 3%–8%, important for high-speed braking calculations. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $m = 1800$ kg, 4 wheels of 35 kg each, $I_w = 1.2$ kg·m², $r = 0.32$ m, $v = 90$ km/h = 25 m/s, $\mu = 0.7$, $m_{disc} = 6$ kg × 4 = 24 kg, $c_p = 500$ J/kg·K.

**(i) Total kinetic energy:**

Effective mass per wheel: $m_{e,1} = I_w / r^2 = 1.2 / 0.32^2 = 1.2 / 0.1024 = 11.72$ kg

Total effective mass: $m_{e,total} = 4 \times 11.72 = 46.87$ kg

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} (1800 + 46.87) \times 25^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1846.87 \times 625$$

$$\boxed{KE_{total} \approx 577{,}147 \text{ J} \approx 577.15 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(ii) Stopping distance:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \frac{625}{13.734}$$

$$\boxed{s \approx 45.5 \text{ m}}$$

(Note: For more accuracy, replace $m$ with $m + m_e$ in the deceleration calculation; for typical problems, the translational mass dominates.)

**(iii) Temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{W_b}{m_{disc} \cdot c_p} = \frac{577{,}147}{24 \times 500} = \frac{577{,}147}{12{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 48.1 \, °C}$$

This is a safe temperature rise for the brake discs. **[7 marks]**

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate Module Choice]**

**(a)** A truck of mass 8000 kg is descending a gradient of 8% (≈ 4.57°). The coefficient of friction between tires and road is 0.65. If the truck is moving at 72 km/h, calculate the work done in braking to bring it to rest. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is brake fade? Explain the role of work done in braking in determining brake disc temperature. Derive the temperature rise equation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $m = 8000$ kg, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.08) = 4.57°$, $\mu = 0.65$, $v = 72$ km/h = 20 m/s.

**Step 1 — Retarding deceleration:**

$$a = g(\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

$\cos(4.57°) = 0.9968$, $\sin(4.57°) = 0.0797$

$$a = 9.81 \times (0.65 \times 0.9968 + 0.0797) = 9.81 \times (0.6479 + 0.0797) = 9.81 \times 0.7276$$

$$a = 7.138 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Stopping distance:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2a} = \frac{20^2}{2 \times 7.138} = \frac{400}{14.276} \approx 28.02 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Braking force:**

$$F_b = \mu m g \cos\theta = 0.65 \times 8000 \times 9.81 \times 0.9968 = 50{,}841 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Work done in braking:**

$$W_b = F_b \times s = 50{,}841 \times 28.02$$

$$\boxed{W_b \approx 1{,}424{,}564 \text{ J} \approx 1.425 \text{ MJ}}$$

**Alternative cross-check using total energy method:**

Total KE = $\frac{1}{2} \times 8000 \times 20^2 = 1{,}600{,}000$ J = 1.6 MJ
Gravitational PE lost = $m g h = m g (s \sin\theta) = 8000 \times 9.81 \times 28.02 \times 0.0797 \approx 175{,}436$ J
Energy to be dissipated by brakes = KE + PE = 1,600,000 + 175,436 = 1,775,436 J

Wait — these two methods should give the *same* result. Let me re-examine:

The "work done by brakes" is the energy converted to heat *at the friction surfaces*, which equals $F_b \cdot s = \mu m g \cos\theta \cdot s$. The remainder of the initial KE is absorbed by the gravitational deceleration (gravity does work, not brakes).

**Final answer: $W_b \approx 1.425$ MJ** (energy dissipated as heat at brakes). **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Brake Fade Definition:**
*Brake fade* is the temporary reduction in braking efficiency caused by excessive heat generation at the brake friction interface, which leads to:
- Decomposition of organic friction materials
- Vaporization of brake fluid
- Reduction in coefficient of friction μ
- Loss of stopping power (potentially dangerous)

**Role of Work Done in Braking:**
All the work done in braking appears as heat. The relationship is:

$$W_b = Q = m_b \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

The work done determines the temperature rise. Higher speeds, heavier vehicles, and more frequent braking all increase $W_b$ and hence the temperature.

**Derivation of Temperature Rise:**

Assume brake disc of mass $m_b$ and specific heat $c_p$ absorbs all the heat (no losses).

Heat absorbed:

$$Q = m_b \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Equating to work done:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = m_b \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for $\Delta T$:

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{m v^2}{2 m_b c_p}}$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- If $\Delta T > 300°C$ — significant fade risk
- If $\Delta T > 500°C$ — severe fade, brake failure possible
- Solutions: larger disc mass, ventilated discs, ceramic composites, cooling ducts, regenerative braking. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting rotational inertia:** On a 14-mark problem, failing to include $\frac{1}{2} I_w \omega^2$ in KE typically costs **2–3 marks**. Always add $m_e$ in numerical problems with high speeds.
> 2. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s is the #1 reason for wrong numerical answers. $1 \text{ km/h} = \frac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}$.
> 3. **Sign confusion on gradient problems:** On a *downgrade*, gravity *adds* to the braking force requirement to maintain deceleration — the brake must dissipate KE + gravitational PE.
> 4. **Confusing force of friction with braking force:** The friction force *is* the braking force at the tire-road patch. Students sometimes add extra forces.
> 5. **Skipping the FBD or energy flow diagram:** In derivation questions, KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the free body diagram or energy flow sketch.
> 6. **Not stating boundary conditions clearly:** Always write "at t = 0, v = v₀; at t = t_s, v = 0" in derivation problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — "Work Done in Braking"**
>
> ✅ **Core Equation:** $W_b = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ (level road, complete stop)
>
> ✅ **Equivalent Form:** $W_b = F_b \cdot s$ where $F_b = \mu m g$ on level road
>
> ✅ **Stopping Distance:** $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ (level road)
>
> ✅ **Gradient Formula:** $a = g(\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$
>
> ✅ **Effective Mass (Rotational Correction):** $m_e = \dfrac{I_w}{r^2}$; total $W_b = \frac{1}{2}(m + m_e)v^2$
>
> ✅ **Temperature Rise:** $\Delta T = \dfrac{m v^2}{2 m_b c_p}$ — directly proportional to KE
>
> ✅ **Braking Power:** $P_{inst} = F_b \cdot v$; $P_{avg} = W_b / t_s$
>
> ✅ **Energy Conservation:** Total KE initial = Work done by brakes + Work done by gravity (on gradient) + Energy lost to surroundings
>
> ✅ **Brake Fade Limit:** Typically ΔT should remain below **~300°C** for sustained braking performance
>
> ✅ **Braking Efficiency η:** Ratio of actual braking force to maximum possible (μmg). Typical cars: η = 0.6 – 0.9
>
> ✅ **Physical Constants to Memorize:** g = 9.81 m/s², $c_p$ (steel) = 500 J/kg·K, $c_p$ (cast iron) = 460 J/kg·K
>
> ✅ **Quick Conversion:** 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s
>
> ✅ **Conceptual Insight:** Work done is INDEPENDENT of μ, but stopping distance and braking force both depend on μ
>
> ✅ **Real-World Connection:** EV regenerative braking recovers up to 70% of this energy instead of wasting it as heat — a major efficiency advantage
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tire Adhesion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tire Adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tire Adhesion** is the phenomenon by which a pneumatic tire develops tractive (longitudinal) and cornering (lateral) forces at the tire–road contact patch through a combination of **molecular adhesion** (van der Waals + chemical bonding at micro-scale asperity contacts) and **rubber hysteresis** (viscoelastic energy dissipation in the bulk rubber). It is the fundamental mechanism that permits a vehicle to accelerate, brake, and corner.

In KTU 2024 scheme language, tire adhesion is the **upper-bound physical limit** of the force that the tire–road interface can transmit before the contact patch begins to slide (i.e., the saturation of the friction circle / friction ellipse).

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

- **Coefficient of road adhesion (μ)** — typically **0.9 (dry asphalt)**, **0.7 (wet asphalt)**, **0.15–0.25 (packed snow/ice)**.
- **Vertical load (Fz)** — the normal reaction at the contact patch, in **N**.
- **Sliding velocity (Vs)** — relative tangential speed at the contact patch, in **m/s**.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, the *peak* coefficient of friction (μ_peak) is the value used to compute the **maximum available friction force**, not the *sliding* coefficient (μ_slide). Always clarify which regime the question refers to.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Gecko + Sponge" Picture

Imagine pressing a soft rubber ball onto a rough table:

- The **microscopic "sticky" tentacles** that grip the surface grains = **Adhesion** (chemical/molecular bonding, dominant on smooth dry surfaces and at low sliding speeds).
- The **sponge-like rubber deforming as it drags across the bumps** = **Hysteresis** (the rubber "drags" because energy is lost internally as it flexes over each surface asperity; dominant on rough surfaces and at high speeds).

Just as a real gecko has both tiny van der Waals setae AND a soft compliant pad to work on rough walls, the tire relies on BOTH mechanisms simultaneously. The proportion of each depends on the **road surface texture**, **slip speed**, **normal load**, **tire temperature**, and **inflation pressure**.

A student reading this for the first time should think of tire adhesion as **"how much grip the rubber has on the road before it starts skating."** When the tire exceeds this grip limit, it slides — the contact patch velocity is no longer zero, and the vehicle loses directional control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Mechanisms of Tire Adhesion

### A. Molecular Adhesion
- Operates at the **micro-asperity level** (μm scale).
- Caused by **van der Waals forces** and occasional chemical bonding between rubber molecules and the road surface.
- Strongly dependent on:
  * **Surface cleanliness** (oil/water film reduces it).
  * **Normal pressure** (higher Fz → more real contact area).
  * **Sliding speed** (peaks at very low slip, falls off as speed increases).
- The **real contact area** $A_r$ is much smaller than the **apparent contact patch area** $A$; only at the tips of asperities do the surfaces truly touch.

### B. Rubber Hysteresis
- Caused by **viscoelastic energy dissipation** in the bulk rubber compound as it is repeatedly compressed and sheared over each road asperity.
- The rubber absorbs energy on the leading edge of an asperity (compression) but **does not return all of it** on the trailing edge (relaxation). The net energy loss manifests as a tangential friction force.
- Dominant on:
  * **Rough surfaces** (concrete, gravel).
  * **Higher sliding speeds** (5–30 % slip region).
  * **High-temperature, soft-compound tires** (racing slicks).

## 2.2 The Adhesion–Hysteresis Balance (KTU High-Yield Concept)

| Operating Regime | Dominant Mechanism | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Dry, smooth, low slip | **Adhesion** | Plenty of real contact, low viscoelastic loss |
| Dry, rough, medium slip | **Hysteresis** | Rubber flexing dominates |
| Wet, smooth, high slip | Reduced adhesion (hydroplaning) | Water film separates contact |
| Ice/snow | Both severely reduced | No asperity penetration, brittle rubber |

## 2.3 Friction Force — Coulomb's Law at the Contact Patch

The maximum friction force that the tire can transmit along any direction in the contact patch plane is:

$$F_{max} = \mu \cdot F_z$$

where:
- $F_{max}$ = maximum available tangential force (N)
- $\mu$ = tire–road adhesion coefficient (dimensionless)
- $F_z$ = vertical normal load on the tire (N)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **saturation limit**, not a value that the tire always transmits. The actual force $F$ the tire produces depends on the **slip state** of the contact patch, not directly on $\mu$. The $\mu F_z$ is the **ceiling**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> **All pipes inside math expressions are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{max} = \mu F_z$ | Maximum friction force | Braking/cornering limit calculation |
| 2 | $a_{max} = \mu g$ | Maximum longitudinal deceleration | Braking distance problems |
| 3 | $\kappa = \dfrac{V_{sx}}{\vert V_x \vert}$ | Longitudinal slip ratio | Plotting $\mu_x$ vs $\kappa$ curves |
| 4 | $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$ | Tire slip angle | Lateral force computation |
| 5 | $F_y = C_\alpha \, \alpha$ | Linear cornering force | Small-angle region |
| 6 | $\dfrac{F_x^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} + \dfrac{F_y^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} = 1$ | Friction circle (in $\mu_x = \mu_y$ case) | Combined-braking-and-turning |
| 7 | $\dfrac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \dfrac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2} = 1$ | Friction ellipse (general) | Anisotropic friction |
| 8 | $A_r \propto F_z$ | Real contact area vs load | Pressure distribution modelling |
| 9 | $T_{peak} \approx 70{-}90^\circ C$ | Tire operating temperature | Adhesion peak region |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The concept of tire adhesion underpins **every active safety system in modern vehicles**:

- **ABS (Anti-lock Braking System)** — modulates brake pressure to keep the tire near the **peak of the μ–slip curve**, i.e., maximum adhesion.
- **TCS (Traction Control System)** — reduces engine torque during acceleration to stay within the friction circle.
- **ESC (Electronic Stability Control)** — selectively brakes individual wheels so the resultant tire force vector stays within the friction ellipse, allowing the driver to follow the intended yaw path.
- **AWD torque vectoring** — redistributes longitudinal force among wheels based on available adhesion.

Thus, tire adhesion is not a "static" property; it is a **dynamic envelope** within which the chassis controller must operate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Braking Deceleration

A four-wheeled vehicle of mass $m$ is braked on a flat, level road with a uniform adhesion coefficient $\mu$. All four wheels lock simultaneously. Find the maximum deceleration.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram (longitudinal direction):**

The only external longitudinal force is the friction force at the four tire contact patches. By symmetry, each tire carries a vertical load $F_{z,i}$, and the sum of normal loads equals the vehicle weight:

$$\sum F_{z,i} = mg$$

**Step 2 — Apply Coulomb friction at each tire:**

For each tire, the maximum longitudinal friction force is $\mu F_{z,i}$. Total braking force is the sum:

$$F_{xb} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \mu F_{z,i} = \mu \sum_{i=1}^{4} F_{z,i}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the load sum:**

$$F_{xb} = \mu \, m g$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law in the longitudinal direction:**

$$m \, a_x = -F_{xb} = -\mu \, m g$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the deceleration:**

$$a_{max} = \mu \, g$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check:**

For $\mu = 0.9$ (dry asphalt) and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$:

$$a_{max} = 0.9 \times 9.81 = 8.83 \, \text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.9 g$$

This is the well-known rule of thumb: a car on dry asphalt decelerates at roughly **0.9 g** under ideal locked-wheel braking.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, ABS limits deceleration to the **peak of the μ–slip curve** (≈ 0.85–0.95 of the locked-wheel value), because locked wheels operate at the *sliding* $\mu$, which is slightly lower than the *peak* $\mu$ on most surfaces.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Circle from First Principles

Consider a single tire under a vertical load $F_z$. The contact patch can transmit a tangential force vector $\vec{F}_t$ in any direction within the road plane. Empirical evidence (and Coulomb's law extended to two dimensions) shows that the magnitude of this vector is bounded.

**Step 1 — Define the longitudinal and lateral components:**

$$\vec{F}_t = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j}$$

**Step 2 — In the *uncoupled* friction model (assumes $\mu$ is the same in all directions), the saturation condition is:**

$$F_x^2 + F_y^2 = (\mu F_z)^2$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this as the equation of a circle of radius $\mu F_z$ in the $(F_x, F_y)$ plane:**

$$\frac{F_x^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} + \frac{F_y^2}{(\mu F_z)^2} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for anisotropic friction (different $\mu$ in x and y, e.g., split-$\mu$ braking):**

$$\frac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \frac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2} = 1$$

This is the **friction ellipse** used in KTU 2024 ESC modelling.

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation:** A point **inside** the ellipse is a feasible (Fx, Fy) command; a point **outside** is unattainable — the tire will slide in the direction of the demanded vector, and the actual force will collapse to the ellipse boundary along the slip-velocity direction.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined-Slip Force Vector Decomposition

**Problem:** A tire operates at slip ratio $\kappa = 0.1$ and slip angle $\alpha = 5^\circ$. The longitudinal adhesion coefficient at this slip is $\mu_x = 0.85$ and the lateral coefficient is $\mu_y = 0.75$. Vertical load is $F_z = 4000 \, \text{N}$. Find the resultant tire force magnitude and direction.

**Step 1 — Compute the longitudinal force component:**

$$F_x = \mu_x F_z = 0.85 \times 4000 = 3400 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lateral force component:**

$$F_y = \mu_y F_z = 0.75 \times 4000 = 3000 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resultant magnitude:**

$$\vert \vec{F}_t \vert = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{3400^2 + 3000^2} = \sqrt{11{,}560{,}000 + 9{,}000{,}000} = \sqrt{20{,}560{,}000}$$

$$\vert \vec{F}_t \vert \approx 4534 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the direction of the resultant in the tire frame:**

$$\tan \psi = \frac{F_y}{F_x} = \frac{3000}{3400} = 0.8824$$

$$\psi = \arctan(0.8824) \approx 41.4^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Verify that the resultant lies within the friction ellipse:**

Compute the ellipse test:

$$\frac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \frac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2} = \frac{3400^2}{3400^2} + \frac{3000^2}{3000^2} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Since the sum is **2 > 1**, the demand is **NOT achievable** — the tire cannot simultaneously deliver 100 % of both $\mu_x F_z$ and $\mu_y F_z$. The real force will collapse to the ellipse boundary in the direction of the slip-velocity vector. KTU students must check feasibility whenever a problem gives both $F_x$ and $F_y$ demands.

**Step 6 — Rescale the forces onto the ellipse:**

The scale factor is:

$$k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \frac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$

The attainable forces are:

$$F_x^{real} = k F_x = 0.7071 \times 3400 = 2404 \, \text{N}$$

$$F_y^{real} = k F_y = 0.7071 \times 3000 = 2121 \, \text{N}$$

The new resultant magnitude is:

$$\vert \vec{F}_t^{real} \vert = \sqrt{2404^2 + 2121^2} \approx 3204 \, \text{N}$$

The direction remains $41.4^\circ$ (rescaling preserves direction).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Slip-Dependent Adhesion Coefficient

| Slip Ratio $\kappa$ | Adhesion Coefficient $\mu_x$ (typical dry asphalt) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.0 | Free rolling, no slip |
| 0.05 | 0.75 | Near peak adhesion, ideal ABS region |
| 0.10 | 0.85 | **Peak adhesion** |
| 0.20 | 0.82 | Just past peak |
| 0.30 | 0.75 | Deep slip region |
| 1.0 (locked) | 0.65 | Sliding friction (lower than peak) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **difference** between $\mu_{peak}$ and $\mu_{slide}$ is the very reason ABS exists. By modulating pressure to keep $\kappa$ near 0.05–0.10, ABS extracts up to **25–30 % more braking force** than a locked-wheel skid on dry asphalt.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Mechanisms of Tire Adhesion (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tire Force Generation] --> B[Normal Load Fz]
    A --> C[Tangential Demand Fx Fy]
    B --> D[Real Contact Area Ar]
    D --> E[Asperity Scale Contact]
    C --> F[Slip State kappa alpha]
    E --> G[Molecular Adhesion]
    E --> H[Rubber Hysteresis]
    F --> I[Peak vs Slide mu]
    G --> J[Friction Force Vector]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Friction Circle or Ellipse]
    K --> L[ABS Modulation]
    K --> M[TCS Limiting]
    K --> N[ESC Allocation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Adhesion vs Slip Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Free Rolling kappa 0] -->|increasing slip| R1[Linear Rise]
    R1 --> R2[Peak mu at kappa 0.08 to 0.12]
    R2 --> R3[Fall to Slide mu]
    R3 --> R4[Locked Wheel kappa 1]
    R2 -.->|ABS target| R5[Modulated Braking Region]
    R4 -.->|locked wheel skid| R6[Loss of Steerability]
```

```mermaid
    P2 --> P3{Inside Ellipse}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Deliver Demanded Force]
    P3 -->|No| P5[Project onto Ellipse Boundary]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Scale Factor k]
    P6 --> P7[Rescale Fx and Fy to Ellipse]
    P7 --> P8[Realizable Tire Force Vector]
    P4 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Feedback to ESC Controller]
```
