# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define tire adhesion and explain the difference between molecular adhesion and rubber hysteresis as mechanisms of friction generation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tire adhesion is the mechanism by which a tire develops longitudinal and lateral tractive forces at the contact patch through molecular bonding and viscoelastic energy dissipation.

**Difference (1 mark each):**

| Aspect | Molecular Adhesion | Rubber Hysteresis |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | van der Waals / chemical bonding at asperity tips | Viscoelastic energy loss in bulk rubber |
| Dominance | Smooth, dry surfaces, low slip | Rough surfaces, moderate-to-high slip |
| Speed dependence | Decreases with sliding speed | Increases with sliding speed |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the friction circle? Why is it called a "circle" and when does it become an "ellipse"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The friction circle (1 mark) is the locus of all achievable tire force vectors $(F_x, F_y)$ at a given vertical load, defined by:

$$F_x^2 + F_y^2 = (\mu F_z)^2$$

It is a circle (1 mark) when the tire–road friction coefficient is the same in all directions of the contact plane (isotropic, $\mu_x = \mu_y$).

It becomes an ellipse (1 mark) when the friction coefficients differ in the longitudinal and lateral directions, e.g., on a split-$\mu$ surface (one wheel on ice, one on dry asphalt):

$$\frac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \frac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2} = 1$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** A passenger car of mass **1500 kg** is braked from **100 km/h** to a standstill on a level road with uniform adhesion coefficient $\mu = 0.85$. Assuming all four wheels lock simultaneously and the braking force is constant:

1. Calculate the maximum achievable deceleration. **(3 marks)**
2. Calculate the stopping distance. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A tire operates at a slip ratio of 0.10 on a road with $\mu_x = 0.85$, slip angle of $4^\circ$ with $\mu_y = 0.70$, and a vertical load of $F_z = 4500 \, \text{N}$. The driver demands $F_x = 3500 \, \text{N}$ and $F_y = 3000 \, \text{N}$ simultaneously.

1. Check whether the demand lies within the friction ellipse. **(3 marks)**
2. If it does not, find the realizable force vector. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Solution

**(a-1) Maximum deceleration (3 marks):**

Apply Coulomb's law at the tire–road interface. The total maximum braking force is:

$$F_{xb,max} = \mu \, m g = 0.85 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{xb,max} = 12{,}508 \, \text{N}$$

Apply Newton's second law in the longitudinal direction:

$$m \, a_x = -F_{xb,max}$$

$$a_{max} = -\mu g = -0.85 \times 9.81 = -8.34 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

Magnitude: $\vert a_{max} \vert = 8.34 \, \text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.85 g$.

**(a-2) Stopping distance (4 marks):**

Initial speed $V_0 = 100 \, \text{km/h} = 27.78 \, \text{m/s}$. Final speed $V_f = 0$. Use the kinematic equation:

$$V_f^2 = V_0^2 + 2 a_x s$$

$$0 = (27.78)^2 + 2 \times (-8.34) \times s$$

$$s = \frac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 8.34} = \frac{771.7}{16.68}$$

$$s \approx 46.3 \, \text{m}$$

### Part (b) — Solution

**(b-1) Feasibility check (3 marks):**

Compute the longitudinal saturation:

$$\mu_x F_z = 0.85 \times 4500 = 3825 \, \text{N}$$

Compute the lateral saturation:

$$\mu_y F_z = 0.70 \times 4500 = 3150 \, \text{N}$$

Compute the ellipse test:

$$\left(\frac{F_x}{\mu_x F_z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{F_y}{\mu_y F_z}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{3500}{3825}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3000}{3150}\right)^2$$

$$= (0.9150)^2 + (0.9524)^2 = 0.8372 + 0.9071 = 1.744$$

Since $1.744 > 1$, **the demand is infeasible** — it lies outside the friction ellipse.

**(b-2) Realizable force (4 marks):**

The direction of the slip-velocity vector gives the projection direction. Compute the scale factor:

$$k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.744}} = \frac{1}{1.3205} = 0.7573$$

Rescale:

$$F_x^{real} = 0.7573 \times 3500 = 2651 \, \text{N}$$

$$F_y^{real} = 0.7573 \times 3000 = 2272 \, \text{N}$$

Verification — plug back into ellipse:

$$\left(\frac{2651}{3825}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{2272}{3150}\right)^2 = (0.6931)^2 + (0.7213)^2 = 0.4804 + 0.5203 = 1.0007 \approx 1$$

Confirmed on the ellipse boundary.

Realizable resultant magnitude:

$$\vert \vec{F}_t^{real} \vert = \sqrt{2651^2 + 2272^2} = \sqrt{7{,}027{,}801 + 5{,}161{,}984} = \sqrt{12{,}189{,}785}$$

$$\vert \vec{F}_t^{real} \vert \approx 3491 \, \text{N}$$

Direction in tire frame:

$$\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{2272}{2651}\right) = \arctan(0.8570) \approx 40.6^\circ$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled $\mu$–slip curve, explain the difference between **peak adhesion** and **sliding adhesion** coefficients. Why does ABS aim to operate near the peak? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1800 kg** travels on a road where the left wheels are on ice ($\mu_L = 0.15$) and the right wheels are on dry asphalt ($\mu_R = 0.90$). The static load distribution is 50/50. The driver brakes hard, locking all four wheels.

1. Calculate the yaw moment about the CG produced by the asymmetric friction force. The track width is **1.55 m** and the CG height is **0.55 m**. Assume a constant deceleration phase. **(4 marks)**
2. Briefly explain how ESC can use differential braking to counteract this moment. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

The $\mu$–slip curve plots the longitudinal friction coefficient $\mu_x$ against the longitudinal slip ratio $\kappa$.

**Peak adhesion** (3 marks):
- Occurs at a slip ratio typically between **0.08 and 0.12** on dry asphalt.
- Represents the maximum friction the tire can transmit.
- Arises from the combined optimum of molecular adhesion and rubber hysteresis.

**Sliding adhesion** (2 marks):
- Occurs at $\kappa = 1$ (wheel fully locked, contact patch sliding).
- Value is **lower** than the peak on most surfaces (μ_slide < μ_peak on dry asphalt; the opposite on ice).
- All adhesion is lost; only hysteresis and ploughing remain.

**Why ABS targets the peak** (2 marks):
- ABS modulates brake pressure in a rapid pulse cycle (typically 5–20 Hz) to keep $\kappa$ oscillating in the **0.05–0.15 region**, where $\mu_x$ is near maximum.
- This yields **20–30 % higher braking force** than a fully locked wheel.
- It also preserves **steerability**, because a rolling tire retains lateral grip, whereas a locked tire has $\mu_y \approx 0$ (a skidding tire cannot corner).

### Part (b) — Solution

**(b-1) Yaw moment calculation (4 marks):**

Total braking force on left side:

$$F_{xL} = \mu_L \cdot m g / 2 = 0.15 \times 1800 \times 9.81 / 2 = 1324 \, \text{N}$$

Total braking force on right side:

$$F_{xR} = \mu_R \cdot m g / 2 = 0.90 \times 1800 \times 9.81 / 2 = 7946 \, \text{N}$$

The asymmetric force creates a **yaw moment** about the CG. The track width $t = 1.55 \, \text{m}$. The yaw moment is the difference in braking forces times the half-track (the moment arm from CG to wheel):

$$M_z = (F_{xR} - F_{xL}) \cdot \frac{t}{2} = (7946 - 1324) \times \frac{1.55}{2}$$

$$M_z = 6622 \times 0.775 = 5132 \, \text{N·m}$$

Direction: the right side brakes harder, producing a **clockwise yaw moment** (vehicle tends to spin toward the high-$\mu$ side, i.e., toward the dry asphalt side).

**(b-2) ESC counter-strategy (3 marks):**

ESC detects the unintended yaw rate via the gyroscope and selectively applies **differential braking**:
1. **Brake the left (low-$\mu$) wheel harder** to create a counter-yaw moment that opposes the spin.
2. Alternatively, **reduce engine torque** to lower the total longitudinal demand.
3. If needed, brake an individual right wheel to create a yaw moment aligning the vehicle with the driver's intended path.

The ESC algorithm (typically a feedback loop on yaw rate error and sideslip angle) keeps the realized tire force vector within each wheel's friction ellipse while satisfying the driver-commanded trajectory.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Tire Adhesion Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing peak and sliding μ** — students often use $\mu_{slide}$ as if it were the maximum available, then claim the car can decelerate at $1.0g$ on ice (it cannot).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in stopping-distance problems — this is the single most common arithmetic error in KTU paper evaluation; partial credit is given only if the unit is shown in the substitution step.
> 3. **Not checking ellipse feasibility** — if a problem gives both $F_x$ and $F_y$, you MUST verify $(F_x/\mu_x F_z)^2 + (F_y/\mu_y F_z)^2 \le 1$ before declaring the answer. Failing to do this loses 2–3 marks per sub-question.
> 4. **Mixing tire frame and vehicle frame** — slip angle $\alpha$ is measured in the tire frame; vehicle yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$ is in the vehicle frame. Confusing the two is a fatal conceptual error.
> 5. **Drawing the friction circle as a square** — the saturated force boundary is a circle/ellipse, not a square. The square approximation is only valid for control-design conservatism, not for exam derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Tire Adhesion":**
>
> - **Definition** — friction force generation at the contact patch via adhesion + hysteresis.
> - **Two mechanisms** — (1) molecular adhesion (van der Waals, smooth surfaces, low slip); (2) rubber hysteresis (viscoelastic, rough surfaces, medium slip).
> - **Coulomb's law** — $F_{max} = \mu F_z$; this is a *saturation limit*, not the actual force.
> - **Maximum braking deceleration** — $a_{max} = \mu g$ (level road, locked-wheel).
> - **Slip ratio** $\kappa = V_{sx} / \vert V_x \vert$; **Slip angle** $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$.
> - **Peak μ** occurs at $\kappa \approx 0.08$–$0.12$ on dry asphalt; **sliding μ** is at $\kappa = 1$.
> - **Friction circle** — isotropic case $F_x^2 + F_y^2 = (\mu F_z)^2$.
> - **Friction ellipse** — anisotropic case $\dfrac{F_x^2}{(\mu_x F_z)^2} + \dfrac{F_y^2}{(\mu_y F_z)^2} = 1$.
> - **ABS strategy** — pulse-modulate brake pressure to keep $\kappa$ at peak; gain ~25 % braking force and preserve steerability.
> - **ESC strategy** — selective wheel braking to keep force vector inside ellipse while tracking driver yaw demand.
> - **Split-μ surfaces** — produce yaw moments that ESC must counteract.
> - **Tire operating temperature** — $\approx 70$–$90 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ is the adhesion peak window; cold or overheated tires lose grip.
> - **Hydroplaning** — water film separates contact; $\mu \to 0$ above a critical speed $V_{crit} \approx 10 \sqrt{P}$ (where $P$ is inflation pressure in psi, $V_{crit}$ in m/s, simplified formula).
> - **Real contact area** $A_r$ is proportional to normal load $F_z$, not to apparent contact patch area.
> - **Units** — $\mu$ is dimensionless, forces in N, slip ratios dimensionless, slip angles in radians (in formulas) or degrees (in figures).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Distribution (Three Wheeled and Four Wheeled Vehicles)
Calculation of Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of automotive engineering and KTU Module 3, **Acceleration Performance** refers to the vehicle's ability to increase its longitudinal velocity under the combined action of the **tractive force** (developed at the tyre-road contact patch) and the various **resistance forces** that oppose motion. Mathematically, it is governed directly by **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a translating rigid body on a road surface:

$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m_{eff}} = \frac{F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g}{m_{eff}}$$

Where:
- $a$ = longitudinal acceleration of the vehicle (m/s²)
- $F_t$ = total tractive force at the wheels (N)
- $F_r$ = rolling resistance force (N)
- $F_{ad}$ = aerodynamic drag force (N)
- $F_g$ = grade (gradient) resistance force (N)
- $m_{eff}$ = effective mass of the vehicle including rotating inertia effects (kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 Syllabus Highlight:** Calculation of Acceleration is a 2-tier problem in the KTU 2024 scheme. You are expected to compute the **maximum possible acceleration** (force-balance method) AND predict the **time/distance required to accelerate** between two given speeds (integration method).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping cart across a supermarket parking lot on a windy day.
- Your **arms pushing the cart** = the engine's tractive force ($F_t$).
- The **friction of the cart wheels on the asphalt** = rolling resistance ($F_r$).
- The **wind blowing against the cart** = aerodynamic drag ($F_{ad}$).
- If the parking lot is an uphill slope, **gravity pulling the cart backward** = grade resistance ($F_g$).
- The **acceleration of the cart** depends purely on what is left over after subtracting all the resisting "pushbacks" from your applied push.

The bigger the leftover (net) force, the faster the cart (vehicle) speeds up. As the cart speeds up, the wind push (drag) grows quadratically — so eventually there is nothing left to push you further, and acceleration drops to zero (this is the **maximum speed** of the vehicle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a vehicle cannot accelerate forever:** The engine produces finite power ($P$). As velocity $V$ increases, the tractive force $F_t = P/V$ decreases, while the resistance forces $F_r + F_{ad}$ increase. At some speed, $F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$, leaving zero net force and therefore **zero acceleration** — this speed is the **maximum or terminal velocity**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration of gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Air density (sea level, 25°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | $f_r$ | 0.010 – 0.020 | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of aerodynamic drag | $C_d$ | 0.25 – 0.45 | dimensionless |
| Vehicle frontal area | $A$ | 1.8 – 2.5 | m² |
| Driveline efficiency | $\eta$ | 0.85 – 0.92 | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Acceleration Problem

The calculation of acceleration in vehicle dynamics proceeds through the following structured logic chain. Mastering this flow guarantees full marks in KTU derivations.

**Step 1 — Identify the Force Providers (Sources of Tractive Effort):**
The engine produces torque, which is multiplied by the gearbox and final drive before reaching the wheels. Two distinct limits emerge from this process:
- **Force-Limited Region** (Low-to-mid speed): The engine torque × gear ratio is large enough that the wheel cannot transmit it without spinning the tyre. The tractive force is **clamped** by the tyre-road friction limit $\mu \cdot W$.
- **Power-Limited Region** (Mid-to-high speed): The engine can deliver all its rated power $P$ to the wheels. The tractive force becomes $F_t = P / V$ and naturally decreases with speed.

**Step 2 — Identify the Force Consumers (Resistance Forces):**
Four resistive forces always act against motion. Each has a distinct physical origin and a unique speed-dependence.
- **Rolling resistance ($F_r$):** Caused by tyre hysteresis and road deformation. Roughly constant with speed in the low-speed range, but slowly increases with $V$.
- **Aerodynamic drag ($F_{ad}$):** Caused by air displacement and viscous shearing. Grows with the **square of velocity** ($V^2$). Dominant at high speeds.
- **Grade resistance ($F_g$):** Gravitational component along the slope. Constant for a fixed gradient.
- **Inertial resistance ($m \cdot a$):** Pseudo-force needed to accelerate the vehicle's mass. Determined by the net force (the unknown itself).

**Step 3 — Formulate the Force Balance Equation:**
Apply Newton's Second Law along the longitudinal axis. The algebraic sum of tractive and resistive forces must equal the mass times acceleration.

**Step 4 — Solve for Acceleration:**
Rearrange the force balance to isolate $a$. This yields an **explicit expression** for acceleration as a function of velocity $V$ — this is the famous $a(V)$ curve.

**Step 5 — Integrate to Obtain Time and Distance:**
Because $a$ is a function of $V$, the time and distance to accelerate between two speeds are obtained via integration (since $a = dV/dt$ and $a = V \cdot dV/ds$).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL formulas you are expected to know for Module 3 KTU examinations.** Memorize the derivation logic, not just the expressions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (Longitudinal) | $F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g = m \cdot a$ | Forces in N, mass in kg, $a$ in m/s² |
| 2 | Tractive Force (Torque side) | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r}$ | $T_{eng}$ engine torque, $i_g$ gear ratio, $i_0$ final drive, $r$ tyre radius |
| 3 | Tractive Force (Power side) | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{V}$ | $P$ in W (watts), $V$ in m/s |
| 4 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = f_r \cdot W = f_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | $f_r \approx 0.013$ for car on asphalt |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$ | $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, $C_d A$ drag area |
| 6 | Grade Resistance | $F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = slope angle |
| 7 | Acceleration (explicit) | $a = \dfrac{F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g}{m}$ | Direct plug-and-play |
| 8 | Effective Mass | $m_{eff} = m \left(1 + \dfrac{J_{eng}}{m r^2} i_g^2 i_0^2 \right)$ | Accounts for rotating inertia |
| 9 | Maximum Speed | $V_{max} : F_t(V_{max}) = F_r + F_{ad}(V_{max}) + F_g$ | Equilibrium point, $a = 0$ |
| 10 | Time to Accelerate | $t_2 - t_1 = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m}{F_t(V) - F_r - F_{ad}(V) - F_g}\, dV$ | Rearrange $a = dV/dt$ and integrate |
| 11 | Distance to Accelerate | $s_2 - s_1 = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m \cdot V}{F_t(V) - F_r - F_{ad}(V) - F_g}\, dV$ | Use $a = V \cdot dV/ds$ |
| 12 | N/V Ratio Trick | $\dfrac{N}{V} = \dfrac{i_g i_0}{r} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ | Engine rpm / vehicle speed in SI |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance Marketing:** Car manufacturers quote "0–100 km/h in X seconds" — this metric is the **integral of $1/a(V) \, dV$**, the very formula you will derive.
- **EV Powertrain Sizing:** Electric vehicles have flat torque curves, so the "force-limited" region extends much further than ICE vehicles — drastically improving low-end acceleration.
- **Heavy Truck Design:** Grade resistance dominates for trucks on mountain roads; engineers must size the engine so $F_t > F_r + F_{ad} + F_g$ at all inclines.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving controllers require instantaneous $a(V)$ lookups to compute safe following distances and merging gaps.
- **Fuel Economy Optimization:** Running the engine at its peak Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) point requires a specific $V$ window — acceleration calculations tell the engineer where this window lies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation: Force Balance → Acceleration → Time → Distance

This section presents the full KTU-board-style derivation that links tractive effort to performance metrics. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **No shortcuts.**

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Force Equilibrium

Consider a rear-wheel-drive vehicle of mass $m$ moving up a gradient of slope angle $\theta$ at speed $V$. Four forces act longitudinally:

$$F_t \;\; (\rightarrow) \quad \text{vs.} \quad F_r + F_{ad} + F_g \;\; (\leftarrow)$$

By Newton's Second Law:

$$F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g = m \cdot a$$

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Substitution of Constitutive Force Laws

Now substitute each force with its physical model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r} \quad \text{(torque-limited, low speed)} \\
F_t &= \frac{P}{V} \quad \text{(power-limited, high speed)} \\
F_r &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \\
F_{ad} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2 \\
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Insert these into the force balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r} - f_r \cdot m \cdot g - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2 - m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = m \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Solving Explicitly for Acceleration

Rearrange to isolate $a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a(V) = \frac{1}{m} \left[ \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r} - f_r \cdot m \cdot g - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2 - m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify by cancelling $m$ from the rolling and grade terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a(V) = \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{m \cdot r} - f_r \cdot g - \frac{\rho C_d A}{2m} V^2 - g \cdot \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental acceleration equation** you must know for KTU. It expresses $a$ as a **downward-opening parabola** in $V$ (constant terms minus a $V^2$ term).

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Maximum Acceleration and Maximum Velocity

**Maximum Acceleration** occurs at $V \to 0$ (standstill, drag is zero):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{max} = \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{m \cdot r} - f_r \cdot g - g \cdot \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum Velocity** occurs when $a = 0$. In the power-limited regime:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{P}{V_{max}} - f_r \cdot m \cdot g - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V_{max}^2 - m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
\frac{P}{V_{max}} &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V_{max}^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply both sides by $V_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \left( f_r \cdot m \cdot g + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \right) V_{max} + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V_{max}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **cubic equation** in $V_{max}$. For level road ($\theta = 0$) and ignoring rolling resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V_{max}^3 \\
V_{max} &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{2P}{\rho C_d A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This $V_{max} \propto P^{1/3}$ cubic-root relationship is why doubling engine power only increases top speed by ~26%, not 100%. A favourite KTU viva question!

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Time to Accelerate from $V_1$ to $V_2$

Starting from $a = \dfrac{dV}{dt}$, rearrange and integrate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dt &= \frac{dV}{a(V)} \\
t_2 - t_1 &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{1}{a(V)}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $a(V)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m}{F_t(V) - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2 - m g \sin\theta}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

In the **power-limited regime** ($F_t = P/V$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m \cdot V}{P - f_r m g V - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^3 - m g \sin\theta \cdot V}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

This integral generally requires **numerical evaluation** (Simpson's rule, trapezoidal rule). For KTU exams, problems are usually simplified so analytical integration is possible.

#### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Distance to Accelerate

Using $a = V \cdot \dfrac{dV}{ds}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ds &= \frac{V \, dV}{a(V)} \\
\Delta s &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{V}{a(V)}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

Or in expanded form (power-limited):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m V^2}{P - f_r m g V - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^3 - m g \sin\theta \cdot V}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Full Solution)

**Problem:** A passenger car has mass $m = 1500$ kg, rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.015$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.35$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², driveline efficiency $\eta = 0.9$, tyre radius $r = 0.32$ m, gear ratio (1st gear) $i_g \cdot i_0 = 12$, and engine torque $T_{eng} = 180$ Nm. Compute maximum acceleration on level road.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Tractive force in 1st gear} \quad F_t &= \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r} \\
F_t &= \frac{180 \times 12 \times 0.9}{0.32} \\
F_t &= \frac{1944}{0.32} \\
F_t &= 6075 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Rolling resistance (level road)} \quad F_r &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \\
F_r &= 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \\
F_r &= 220.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Drag at } V \approx 0 \text{ (initial acceleration)} \quad F_{ad} &\approx 0 \\
\text{Step 4: Grade resistance (level road)} \quad F_g &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Net force} \quad F_{net} &= F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g \\
F_{net} &= 6075 - 220.7 - 0 - 0 \\
F_{net} &= 5854.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6: Maximum acceleration} \quad a_{max} &= \frac{F_{net}}{m} \\
a_{max} &= \frac{5854.3}{1500} \\
a_{max} &= 3.90 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to familiar units: $a_{max} = 3.90 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.397\,g$. This is realistic for a low-gear launch of a 1500 kg passenger car.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Force-Limited vs. Power-Limited

| Aspect | Force-Limited Region | Power-Limited Region |
|---|---|---|
| Speed range | Low (typically 0–40 km/h) | Mid-to-high (40 km/h → $V_{max}$) |
| Controlling equation | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{r}$ | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{V}$ |
| Acceleration trend | **Decreasing** (due to rising drag) | **Decreasing** (due to falling $F_t$) |
| $a(V)$ shape | Roughly linear (drag dominates) | Hyperbolic / $1/V$ behaviour |
| Typical for | ICE launches, EV launches, trucks uphill | Highway overtaking, top speed |
| KTU exam tip | Often combined with tyre friction limit $\mu W$ | Often integrated for time/distance problems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vehicle Longitudinal Force Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block visualizes the force flow during acceleration — from engine to wheels, and the resistance feedback loop acting against motion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[ENGINE TORQUE Teng] --> nodeB[GEARBOX ig]
    nodeB --> nodeC[FINAL DRIVE i0]
    nodeC --> nodeD[DRIVELINE EFF eta]
    nodeD --> nodeE[TYRE RADIUS r]
    nodeE --> nodeF[TRACTIVE FORCE Ft]
    
    nodeF --> nodeG[FORCE BALANCE NODE]
    
    nodeH[ROLLING RESISTANCE Fr = fr mg] --> nodeG
    nodeI[AERO DRAG Fad = 0.5 rho Cd A V2] --> nodeG
    nodeJ[GRADE RESISTANCE Fg = mg sin theta] --> nodeG
    nodeK[INERTIA ma] --> nodeG
    
    nodeG --> nodeL[NET ACCELERATION a = Ft minus resistances OVER m]
    
    nodeL --> nodeM[INTEGRATE OVER V to GET TIME]
    nodeL --> nodeN[INTEGRATE V dV to GET DISTANCE]
    
    nodeM --> nodeO[0 to 100 km/h PERFORMANCE]
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Acceleration Calculation

The flowchart below shows the **step-by-step calculation methodology** that a vehicle dynamics engineer follows. This is the exact flow you must demonstrate in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[INPUT: Vehicle Parameters m, fr, Cd, A, eta, r] --> stepB[INPUT: Engine Map Teng or P]
    stepB --> stepC{DETERMINE GEAR RATIO ig x i0}
    
    stepC --> stepD[COMPUTE Ft FROM TORQUE SIDE]
    stepC --> stepE[COMPUTE Ft FROM POWER SIDE P OVER V]
    
    stepD --> stepF{SELECT APPLICABLE REGIME}
    stepE --> stepF
    
    stepF --> stepG[COMPUTE RESISTANCES Fr Fad Fg]
    stepG --> stepH[APPLY NEWTONS SECOND LAW]
    stepH --> stepI[CALCULATE a V FUNCTION]
    
    stepI --> stepJ{REQUIREMENT}
    stepJ --> stepK[INSTANTANEOUS ACCELERATION]
    stepJ --> stepL[TIME TO ACCELERATE]
    stepJ --> stepM[DISTANCE TO ACCELERATE]
    
    stepL --> stepN[INTEGRATE 1 OVER a V dV]
    stepM --> stepO[INTEGRATE V OVER a V dV]
    
    stepK --> stepP[OUTPUT a in m per s2]
    stepN --> stepP
    stepO --> stepP
```

### 4.3 Free Body Diagram (Schematic Text Representation)

```
         V (forward direction)
         ←———————————————————→
                                 
        [ VEHICLE BODY mass m ]
                                 
   Ft →                       ←  Fad (air drag)
        →                     ← Fr  (rolling res)
        →                     ← Fg  (grade res)
                                 
   Net Force along x = Ft - Fr - Fad - Fg = m·a
                                 
   ─────────────────────────────  Road Surface (slope θ)
```

### 4.4 Acceleration vs Velocity Characteristic Curve (Shape Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[START: a is maximum at V=0] --> B[Decreasing linearly due to drag growth]
    B --> C[Transition to power-limited]
    C --> D[Continues to decrease as 1 OVER V]
    D --> E[Reaches ZERO at Vmax]
    
    A1[V=0] --- A
    B1[V rising] --- B
    C1[Mid speed] --- C
    D1[High speed] --- D
    E1[Vmax] --- E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual interpretation:** The $a(V)$ curve is **NOT a horizontal line**. It is a monotonically decreasing curve that crosses zero at $V_{max}$. This shape is what the KTU examiner expects you to draw and label.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term 'tractive force' in the context of vehicle acceleration. How is it related to engine torque and engine power?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tractive force ($F_t$) is the total longitudinal force developed at the tyre–road contact patch that propels the vehicle forward. It can be expressed in two equivalent ways:

**(i) From the torque side:**
$$F_t = \frac{T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta}{r}$$

where $T_{eng}$ is engine torque, $i_g$ is gearbox ratio, $i_0$ is final drive ratio, $\eta$ is driveline efficiency, and $r$ is the effective tyre rolling radius.

**(ii) From the power side:**
$$F_t = \frac{P}{V}$$

where $P$ is the power delivered to the wheels and $V$ is the vehicle speed. Equating both gives the fundamental relationship:
$$P = F_t \cdot V = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$$
which is the mechanical power-flow equation. **(3 marks awarded for definition + both formulas + relationship.)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**List the four major forces that govern the longitudinal acceleration of a road vehicle and write their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four forces acting on a vehicle along its longitudinal axis are:

| # | Force | Expression | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{r} = \dfrac{P}{V}$ | Engine torque at wheel |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | Tyre hysteresis |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$ | Air displacement |
| 4 | Grade Resistance | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Gravitational component |

Newton's Second Law gives: $m \cdot a = F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g$. **(Full 3 marks for listing all four with formulas.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the fundamental equation for the longitudinal acceleration of a road vehicle moving up a gradient of slope $\theta$ at speed $V$. Clearly define every term and identify the force-limited vs. power-limited regimes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car has the following data: mass $m = 1200$ kg, $f_r = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.0$ m², $\eta = 0.88$, $r = 0.31$ m, $i_g \cdot i_0 = 11$ in 1st gear, engine torque $T_{eng} = 150$ Nm, rated power $P = 60$ kW. Compute (i) the maximum acceleration on level road, (ii) the maximum velocity on a 5% upgrade, and (iii) the time to accelerate from 20 km/h to 80 km/h in the power-limited regime. Use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Start with the free body diagram and Newton's Second Law:

$$F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g = m \cdot a \quad \text{(1 mark for writing the balance)}$$

Substitute the force laws:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{r} - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2 - m g \sin\theta = m a
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for substitution)**

Solving for $a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a(V) = \frac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{m r} - f_r g - \frac{\rho C_d A}{2m} V^2 - g \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for isolating $a$)**

Regime identification:

- **Force-limited regime:** $F_t = \dfrac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{r}$ (constant, low speed).
- **Power-limited regime:** $F_t = \dfrac{P}{V}$ (decreases with speed, high speed). **(2 marks for regimes)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Maximum acceleration on level road (V ≈ 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{150 \times 11 \times 0.88}{0.31} = \frac{1452}{0.31} = 4683.87 \text{ N} \\
F_r &= 0.012 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 141.26 \text{ N} \\
F_{ad} &\approx 0 \text{ (at } V \approx 0 \text{)} \\
F_g &= 0 \text{ (level road)} \\
a_{max} &= \frac{4683.87 - 141.26}{1200} = \frac{4542.61}{1200} = 3.79 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks for part (i))**

**(ii) Maximum velocity on 5% upgrade:**

5% grade means $\sin\theta \approx 0.05$ (for small angles).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 588.6 \text{ N} \\
F_r &= 141.26 \text{ N} \\
\frac{60000}{V_{max}} &= 141.26 + 588.6 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 \times V_{max}^2 \\
\frac{60000}{V_{max}} &= 729.86 + 0.3675 \, V_{max}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply by $V_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
60000 &= 729.86 \, V_{max} + 0.3675 \, V_{max}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving by trial: at $V_{max} = 40$ m/s (144 km/h):
RHS = $729.86 \times 40 + 0.3675 \times 64000 = 29194 + 23520 = 52714$ N·m/s — less than 60000.
At $V_{max} = 43$ m/s: RHS = $729.86 \times 43 + 0.3675 \times 79507 = 31384 + 29221 = 60605$ — slightly above.
Thus $V_{max} \approx 42.8$ m/s $\approx$ **154 km/h**. **(2 marks for part (ii))**

**(iii) Time to accelerate from 20 km/h to 80 km/h (power-limited):**

Convert: $V_1 = 20/3.6 = 5.56$ m/s, $V_2 = 80/3.6 = 22.22$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t = \int_{5.56}^{22.22} \frac{m V}{P - (f_r m g + m g \sin\theta)V - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^3}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

For level road ($\theta = 0$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t = \int_{5.56}^{22.22} \frac{1200 \, V}{60000 - 141.26 \, V - 0.3675 \, V^3}\, dV
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerical integration (Simpson's rule with $n = 4$ subintervals, $h = (22.22 - 5.56)/4 = 4.165$):

| $V$ (m/s) | Integrand $f(V) = \dfrac{1200 V}{60000 - 141.26 V - 0.3675 V^3}$ |
|---|---|
| 5.56 | 0.124 |
| 9.72 | 0.260 |
| 13.89 | 0.502 |
| 18.06 | 1.218 |
| 22.22 | 4.92 |

Simpson's rule: $\Delta t \approx \frac{h}{3}[f_0 + 4(f_1+f_3) + 2 f_2 + f_4]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t \approx \frac{4.165}{3}[0.124 + 4(0.260 + 1.218) + 2(0.502) + 4.92] \\
\Delta t \approx 1.388 \times [0.124 + 5.912 + 1.004 + 4.92] \\
\Delta t \approx 1.388 \times 11.96 \approx 16.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for part (iii) — 1 for integral setup + 1 for numerical answer)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** What is the 'power-limited' region of a vehicle's acceleration curve? Derive the relationship between maximum velocity $V_{max}$ and rated engine power $P$ for a level road, neglecting rolling resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A truck of mass 8000 kg is to maintain a constant speed of 60 km/h on a 4% upgrade. The driveline efficiency is 0.85, the engine rated power is 120 kW, the aerodynamic parameters are $C_d = 0.6$, $A = 6.0$ m², and $f_r = 0.008$. Compute the engine torque required at this steady condition. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $r = 0.5$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **power-limited region** is the speed range in which the engine can supply all its rated power $P$ to the wheels. In this regime, the tractive force is no longer limited by the engine torque × gear ratio but by the available power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t = \frac{P}{V} \quad \text{(3 marks for stating the regime)}
\end{aligned}
$$

On a level road, neglecting rolling resistance, the only resistance is aerodynamic drag:
$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$$

Setting $F_t = F_{ad}$ at maximum velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{V_{max}} &= \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V_{max}^2 \\
P &= \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V_{max}^3 \\
V_{max} &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{2P}{\rho C_d A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks for the derivation)**. The cubic-root dependence explains why top speed increases slowly with engine power.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

At **constant speed** on an upgrade, acceleration is zero. So the tractive force equals the sum of all resistances:

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_g$$

Convert speed: $V = 60/3.6 = 16.67$ m/s.

Compute each resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= 0.008 \times 8000 \times 9.81 = 627.84 \text{ N} \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 6.0 \times 16.67^2 \\
&= 1.225 \times 1.8 \times 277.89 = 612.7 \text{ N} \\
F_g &= 8000 \times 9.81 \times \sin(\arctan 0.04) \\
F_g &\approx 8000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 3139.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total tractive force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= 627.84 + 612.7 + 3139.2 = 4379.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Engine torque required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta}{r} \\
T_{eng} &= \frac{F_t \cdot r}{\eta \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming direct drive at top gear with $i_g \cdot i_0 = 4$ (typical for trucks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eng} &= \frac{4379.7 \times 0.5}{0.85 \times 4} \\
T_{eng} &= \frac{2189.85}{3.4} \\
T_{eng} &\approx 644 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks for steady-state equation, 2 marks for resistance calculation, 2 marks for $F_t$ summation, 1 mark for torque back-calculation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the driveline efficiency $\eta$:** Many students write $F_t = T i_g i_0 / r$ without $\eta$. KTU strictly awards zero partial credit here. Always include $\eta$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Using kg·m/s instead of km/h:** When the problem statement gives speeds in km/h, you MUST convert to m/s before applying the $P = F_t V$ formula. Mixed-unit errors cost 1–2 marks routinely.
> 3. **Treating drag as linear in $V$:** A classic blunder. Aerodynamic drag is quadratic in $V$ — $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A V^2$. Writing it linear is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Ignoring $F_g$ on upgrades:** On a level road $F_g = 0$, but on any non-zero gradient, it must be included. Examiners specifically test this transition.
> 5. **Assuming $a$ is constant:** Acceleration is a function of $V$. For time/distance integrals, you cannot use $t = \Delta V / a$ unless $a$ is given as constant. Always integrate.
> 6. **Confusing force-limited with power-limited:** Force-limited uses torque; power-limited uses $P/V$. At low speeds the engine is torque-limited; at high speeds the engine is power-limited. Wrong regime = 3 marks lost.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Core equation:** $a = \dfrac{F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g}{m}$ — write this FIRST in every problem.
- **Tractive force has two faces:** $F_t = T i_g i_0 \eta / r$ (torque side) **AND** $F_t = P/V$ (power side). The applicable face depends on speed.
- **Four resistances, four formulas:** rolling ($f_r m g$), aerodynamic ($0.5 \rho C_d A V^2$), grade ($m g \sin\theta$), and inertia ($m a$). Memorize all four.
- **$V_{max}$ relationship:** On a level road with no rolling resistance, $V_{max} = \sqrt[3]{2P / (\rho C_d A)}$ — cubic-root scaling.
- **$a(V)$ curve shape:** Monotonically decreasing parabola — maximum at $V = 0$, zero at $V_{max}$.
- **Time integral:** $\Delta t = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m \, dV}{F_t(V) - F_r - F_{ad}(V) - F_g}$ — requires numerical methods generally.
- **Distance integral:** $\Delta s = \displaystyle\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{m V \, dV}{F_t(V) - F_r - F_{ad}(V) - F_g}$ — derived from $a = V \, dV/ds$.
- **Force-limited vs Power-limited:** Force-limited at low speeds (constant $F_t$, drag grows), Power-limited at high speeds ($F_t \propto 1/V$).
- **Constant speed condition:** $a = 0$ means $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_g$ — useful for fuel-consumption and gradeability problems.
- **Air density:** Always use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Steep grade approximation:** For $\theta < 10°$, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx$ grade (in fraction, e.g., 5% = 0.05).
- **Tyre-road friction limit:** Tractive force cannot exceed $\mu W$ — this caps the maximum acceleration in low-speed launches.
- **Effective mass correction:** If rotating inertia matters, use $m_{eff} = m(1 + J_{eng} i_g^2 i_0^2 / (m r^2))$. Rarely needed for KTU but viva-favourite.
- **Conversion cheats:** 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s; 1 hp = 745.7 W; 1 g = 9.81 m/s².
- **Typical values to remember:** $C_d$ for sedan ≈ 0.30, SUV ≈ 0.38, truck ≈ 0.6; $f_r$ for car ≈ 0.013; frontal area for sedan ≈ 2.2 m².

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tractive Effort and Reactions for Different Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tractive Effort — The Engineering Definition

**Tractive Effort (TE)** is the net longitudinal force generated at the tire–road contact patch of the driven wheels of a vehicle, which is responsible for propelling the vehicle forward against all motion-resisting forces. In rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

> **Tractive Effort (TE) = Driving Force at Road Contact (F_d) − Rolling Resistance of Driven Wheels (T_r)**

It is the **"push"** or **"pull"** the vehicle exerts on the road to overcome the combined resistive forces of rolling friction, aerodynamic drag, gradient, and inertia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"Tractive effort is the net available longitudinal propulsive force at the driven wheel–road interface, which determines the vehicle's ability to initiate and sustain motion under given operating conditions."*

The governing dynamic equilibrium of a vehicle moving with acceleration *a* on a gradient *θ* is:

$$\text{Tractive Effort (TE)} = R_{\text{roll}} + R_{\text{grade}} + R_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{inertia}}$$

Where each term represents a distinct resistive force that the tractive effort must overcome.

## 1.2 Reaction Forces — The "Ground Push-Back"

**Normal Reaction (R)** on each wheel is the vertical force exerted by the road surface upward on the wheel. It is governed by **static weight distribution** plus **dynamic weight transfer** during acceleration, braking, or cornering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Friends-Pulling-a-Sled" Picture:**
>
> Imagine two friends, *Front Friend* and *Rear Friend*, pulling a loaded sled up a slope.
> - The sled's **weight** sits mostly on the rear friend (because most of the load is behind). This is **static reaction**.
> - When they **start running (acceleration)**, the load shifts backward — the rear friend feels heavier, and the front friend feels lighter. This is **dynamic weight transfer**.
> - Only the friend who is actually **pushing against the ground with friction** can pull the sled. If the rear friend is on ice (low friction), no matter how hard he pulls, the sled won't move.
>
> In a car, the **driven wheels are the "friends with grip."** The maximum tractive effort is limited by **friction × normal reaction on the driven wheels.** This is why FWD cars behave differently on slippery inclines compared to RWD cars.

## 1.3 The Four Classic Drive Configurations

| Drive Type | Drivetrain Layout | Driven Wheels | CG Placement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FR** — Front Engine, Rear Drive | Longitudinal engine, propshaft to rear axle | Rear | Front-biased |
| **FF** — Front Engine, Front Drive | Transverse engine, no propshaft | Front | Front-biased |
| **RR** — Rear Engine, Rear Drive | Rear-mounted engine | Rear | Rear-biased |
| **4WD / AWD** | Engine + transfer case | All four | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The placement of the driven wheels relative to the CG completely changes the **maximum tractive effort equation**, because weight transfer during acceleration shifts load **toward the rear**, helping RWD but **hurting** FWD during hard launches.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** ***g* = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Coefficient of road adhesion (μ):** dry asphalt ≈ 0.8–1.0, wet asphalt ≈ 0.4–0.6, snow ≈ 0.2–0.3, ice ≈ 0.1
- **Wheelbase symbol convention (KTU standard):** ***L*** or ***b*** — total distance between front and rear axles
- **CG height:** ***h*** — vertical distance from ground to mass center

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free Body of a Vehicle — The Five-Force Model

A vehicle in motion experiences **five primary forces**, all acting along or about the longitudinal axis:

1. **Weight (W = mg)** — acts downward through CG
2. **Aerodynamic Drag (R_aero = ½ ρ C_d A V²)** — acts rearward at CG height
3. **Rolling Resistance (R_roll = μ_r W)** — acts rearward at tire patch
4. **Grade Resistance (R_grade = W sin θ)** — acts along slope
5. **Inertia Force (F_i = (W/g) × a)** — acts rearward during acceleration

The **Tractive Effort must equal or exceed the sum of (2), (3), (4), and (5)** to maintain motion.

## 2.2 Static Weight Distribution

For a stationary vehicle on level ground:

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} \quad \text{(weight on front axle)}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} \quad \text{(weight on rear axle)}$$

Where:
- ***a*** = longitudinal distance from **front axle** to CG
- ***b'*** = longitudinal distance from **rear axle** to CG  
- ***L*** = wheelbase = *a* + *b'*

## 2.3 Dynamic Weight Transfer During Acceleration

When the vehicle accelerates forward with acceleration *a*, the inertia force (W/g)·a acts at CG height *h* — creating a **pitching moment** that transfers load rearward.

**Derivation of weight transfer ΔW:**

Taking moments about the front contact patch:

$$R_r \cdot L = W \cdot a - \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}} \cdot h}{g}$$

$$R_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot h}{g \cdot L} \cdot a_{\text{lin}}$$

Therefore the **net change in rear axle load** is:

$$\Delta W = \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The product *h × a_lin / g × L* represents the fraction of weight shifted from front to rear during longitudinal acceleration. **Higher CG → more transfer. Higher acceleration → more transfer.**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Effort | TE = F_d − T_r | N | General |
| 2 | Total Resistance | R_total = R_roll + R_grade + R_aero + F_i | N | Dynamic equilibrium |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance | R_roll = f_r · W | N | f_r = rolling resistance coeff |
| 4 | Grade Resistance | R_grade = W · sin θ ≈ W · θ (small angles) | N | θ in radians |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Drag | R_aero = ½ · ρ · C_d · A_f · V² | N | ρ = 1.2 kg/m³ (air) |
| 6 | Inertia Force | F_i = (W/g) · a_lin | N | Linear acceleration |
| 7 | Max TE (no slip) | TE_max = μ · R_driven | N | Driven wheel limit |
| 8 | Weight Transfer | ΔW = (W · h · a_lin)/(g · L) | N | During acceleration |
| 9 | Static Front Reaction | R_f0 = W · b'/L | N | Stationary |
| 10 | Static Rear Reaction | R_r0 = W · a/L | N | Stationary |
| 11 | Dynamic Rear Reaction | R_r = W_r + ΔW | N | RWD/FWD analysis |
| 12 | Dynamic Front Reaction | R_f = W_f − ΔW | N | RWD/FWD analysis |
| 13 | Max Grade (RWD) | sin θ_max = (μ(W_r+ΔW) − f_r W − R_aero)/W | — | Tractive limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The single most-tested relation is **TE_max = μ × R_driven**. Always identify the **driven axle** first, compute its dynamic normal reaction, then apply the friction limit.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle launch control:** Modern ECUs limit throttle opening to keep *TE ≤ μ·R_driven*, preventing wheelspin.
- **AWD systems:** Torque vectoring redistributes tractive effort to wheels with highest μ·R product.
- **Hill-start assist:** Holds brake until clutch engagement, since rearward weight transfer hasn't occurred yet on an incline.
- **Truck design:** RR (rear-engine) drive is favored for heavy haulage because rear CG maximizes W_r, hence larger μ·R_r.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation — Tractive Effort Equation from Newton's Second Law

Starting from a free-body of the entire vehicle moving on an inclined plane:

### Step 1: Identify forces acting on vehicle CG

Forces along the longitudinal axis (positive = forward):

- Tractive Effort at driven wheel contact patch: **TE**
- Rolling resistance: **−R_roll** (acts rearward)
- Aerodynamic drag: **−R_aero** (acts rearward)
- Grade component: **−W sin θ** (acts rearward on incline)
- Inertia: **−(W/g) · a_lin** (pseudo-force on accelerating vehicle)

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a_{\text{lin}}$$

$$\text{TE} - R_{\text{roll}} - R_{\text{aero}} - W\sin\theta - \frac{W}{g}a_{\text{lin}} = \frac{W}{g} \cdot a_{\text{lin}}$$

Wait — the inertia force **is** the ma term. Combining them:

$$\text{TE} - R_{\text{roll}} - R_{\text{aero}} - W\sin\theta = \frac{W}{g} \cdot a_{\text{lin}}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for TE

$$\boxed{\text{TE} = R_{\text{roll}} + R_{\text{aero}} + W\sin\theta + \frac{W}{g} a_{\text{lin}}}$$

### Step 4: Express in expanded form

$$\text{TE} = f_r W + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 + W\sin\theta + \frac{W a_{\text{lin}}}{g}$$

**Conversion logic:** The left side (TE) is the available propulsive force; the right side sums every resistive force plus the inertia term *ma*. For **constant cruising speed** (a_lin = 0), tractive effort equals total resistance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Dynamic Reaction on Driven Wheels — Case A: Rear Wheel Drive (RWD)

### Free-Body Setup

Vehicle with:
- Total weight **W** acting at CG located at height *h*
- CG at distance *a* ahead of rear axle, *b'* ahead of front axle  
- Wheelbase *L = a + b'*
- Accelerating at rate *a_lin*

### Step 1: Sum vertical forces

$$R_f + R_r = W$$

### Step 2: Take moments about front contact patch (positive CCW)

$$R_r \cdot L - W \cdot b' - \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g} \cdot h = 0$$

> **Logic:** The weight W acts downward at horizontal distance *b'* behind the front patch — creates clockwise moment. The inertia pseudo-force *(W/g)·a_lin* acts at height *h*, in the *rearward* direction — creates counter-clockwise moment (resisting rear lift). Wait — correct the sign.

### Step 3 (Corrected): Inertia force points rearward at CG height

In a non-inertial frame accelerating forward, the inertial pseudo-force on the vehicle mass acts **rearward** at height *h*. Taking moments about front contact patch:

- Weight W (downward) at distance *b'* ahead of rear axle = distance *b'* **behind** front patch → creates **clockwise** moment
- Inertial force *(W/g)·a_lin* (rearward) at height *h* → creates **counter-clockwise** moment (rearward force above ground = nose-down = increases rear load)

$$R_r \cdot L = W \cdot b' + \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}} \cdot h}{g}$$

$$R_r = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} + \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

### Step 4: Identify the components

$$\boxed{R_r^{\text{RWD}} = \underbrace{\frac{W \cdot b'}{L}}_{\text{Static rear load } W_r} + \underbrace{\frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}}_{\text{Dynamic transfer } \Delta W}}$$

**Conclusion:** Acceleration **adds** to the rear axle load in RWD — a **performance advantage**.

### Step 5: Maximum tractive effort for RWD

$$\boxed{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu \cdot \left[ \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} + \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L} \right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Reaction — Case B: Front Wheel Drive (FWD)

### Step 1: Same free body, but driven wheels are the front pair

$$R_f + R_r = W$$

### Step 2: Take moments about rear contact patch (positive CCW)

- Weight W (downward) at distance *a* ahead of rear patch → creates **counter-clockwise** moment
- Inertia force (rearward) at height *h* → creates **clockwise** moment (rearward force above ground = nose-up = decreases front load)

$$R_f \cdot L = W \cdot a - \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}} \cdot h}{g}$$

### Step 3: Solve for R_f

$$R_f = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

$$\boxed{R_f^{\text{FWD}} = \underbrace{\frac{W \cdot a}{L}}_{\text{Static front load } W_f} - \underbrace{\frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}}_{\text{Dynamic transfer } \Delta W}}$$

**Conclusion:** Acceleration **subtracts** from the front axle load in FWD — a **performance penalty** during hard launches.

### Step 4: Maximum tractive effort for FWD

$$\boxed{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}} = \mu \cdot \left[ \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L} \right]}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Dynamic Reaction — Case C: Four Wheel Drive (4WD/AWD)

### Step 1: All wheels contribute traction

Total vertical load remains *W* (no net change in weight), and assuming equal distribution of tractive effort among axles:

$$R_f^{\text{dyn}} = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}, \quad R_r^{\text{dyn}} = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L}$$

> **Wait — correction:** Weight transfer *does* still occur in 4WD during acceleration. The dynamic equations are identical to FWD/RWD for each axle, but **both axles deliver tractive effort simultaneously**.

### Step 2: Combined maximum tractive effort

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{4WD}} = \mu \cdot R_f^{\text{dyn}} + \mu \cdot R_r^{\text{dyn}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{4WD}} = \mu \cdot W}$$

**Conclusion:** 4WD achieves the **theoretical maximum** tractive effort limited only by total vehicle weight and adhesion coefficient.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | RWD | FWD | 4WD | RR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Driven axle | Rear | Front | Both | Rear |
| W_static (driven) | W · b'/L | W · a/L | W (total) | W · a/L (high) |
| Effect of acceleration | **+** ΔW to rear | **−** ΔW from front | Distributes | **+** ΔW to rear |
| Best at launch | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ |
| Hill-start behavior | Good | Poor (load unloads) | Excellent | Excellent |
| TE_max formula | μ(W·b'/L + ΔW) | μ(W·a/L − ΔW) | μ·W | μ(W·b'/L + ΔW) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle weighs **W = 15000 N**, has wheelbase **L = 2.8 m**, CG located at **a = 1.2 m** from front axle and **b' = 1.6 m** from rear axle, CG height **h = 0.6 m**. The vehicle accelerates at **a_lin = 2 m/s²** on level ground. Take **μ = 0.7** and **g = 9.81 m/s²**. Find the maximum tractive effort if the vehicle is (i) RWD, (ii) FWD, (iii) 4WD.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute static reactions**

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} = \frac{15000 \times 1.6}{2.8} = 8571.43 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{15000 \times 1.2}{2.8} = 6428.57 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute dynamic weight transfer**

$$\Delta W = \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L} = \frac{15000 \times 0.6 \times 2}{9.81 \times 2.8}$$

$$\Delta W = \frac{18000}{27.468} = 655.27 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Case (i) — RWD**

$$R_r^{\text{dyn}} = W_r + \Delta W = 6428.57 + 655.27 = 7083.84 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu \cdot R_r^{\text{dyn}} = 0.7 \times 7083.84 = 4958.69 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Case (ii) — FWD**

$$R_f^{\text{dyn}} = W_f - \Delta W = 8571.43 - 655.27 = 7916.16 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}} = \mu \cdot R_f^{\text{dyn}} = 0.7 \times 7916.16 = 5541.31 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Case (iii) — 4WD**

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{4WD}} = \mu \cdot W = 0.7 \times 15000 = 10500 \text{ N}$$

### Insight

$$\text{Ratio: } \frac{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}}}{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}}} = \frac{5541.31}{4958.69} \approx 1.117$$

At *this* acceleration, FWD still produces more tractive effort than RWD because the **static front load (W_f)** is initially higher than the static rear load (W_r). Only at **higher accelerations** does RWD overtake FWD.

**Critical acceleration** where RWD = FWD:

$$\mu\left(W_r + \Delta W\right) = \mu\left(W_f - \Delta W\right)$$

$$W_r + \Delta W = W_f - \Delta W \implies \Delta W = \frac{W_f - W_r}{2}$$

$$\frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{crit}}}{g \cdot L} = \frac{W_f - W_r}{2}$$

$$a_{\text{crit}} = \frac{g \cdot L \cdot (W_f - W_r)}{2 W h} = \frac{9.81 \times 2.8 \times 2142.86}{2 \times 15000 \times 0.6}$$

$$a_{\text{crit}} = \frac{58845.6}{18000} \approx 3.27 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Conclusion:** Above **3.27 m/s²** acceleration, RWD produces more tractive effort than FWD in this configuration.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Tractive Effort. Why is it limited by the coefficient of road adhesion?**

**Model Answer:**

Tractive effort is the net longitudinal propulsive force developed at the contact patch between a driven wheel and the road surface. It represents the usable thrust after overcoming the wheel's own rolling resistance.

It is limited by the road adhesion coefficient **μ** because the maximum force that can be transmitted across the tire-road interface is given by the friction limit:

$$\text{TE}_{\max} = \mu \cdot R_{\text{driven}}$$

Beyond this value, the tire slips or spins, converting kinetic energy into heat rather than vehicle motion.

**(3 marks)** — *Definition (1), Formula (1), Physical explanation (1)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the difference between RWD and FWD in terms of weight transfer during acceleration.**

**Model Answer:**

In **RWD**, the rear wheels are driven. During forward acceleration, the inertia force acts rearward at CG height, creating a pitching moment that **increases** the load on the rear wheels by an amount:

$$\Delta W = \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

This **increases** maximum tractive effort: $\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu(W_r + \Delta W)$.

In **FWD**, the front wheels are driven. The same inertial pitching moment **decreases** the front axle load:

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}} = \mu(W_f - \Delta W)$$

Hence RWD has a launch advantage; FWD suffers load reduction on driven wheels during hard acceleration.

**(3 marks)** — *Identification (1), RWD expression (1), FWD expression (1)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for maximum tractive effort of a Rear Wheel Drive (RWD) vehicle on level ground, including the effect of weight transfer during acceleration. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeled passenger car weighs **18 kN**, has a wheelbase of **2.6 m**, CG located **1.1 m** from the front axle and **1.5 m** from the rear axle. CG height is **0.55 m**. The car accelerates at **3 m/s²** on level ground. If the coefficient of road adhesion is **0.75**, calculate:
   - (i) Static reactions on each axle
   - (ii) Dynamic reactions during acceleration
   - (iii) Maximum tractive effort assuming the car is RWD

**Take g = 9.81 m/s².** **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a free body diagram, derive expressions for the normal reactions on the front and rear wheels of a vehicle during acceleration. Compare the case of Front Wheel Drive and Rear Wheel Drive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1800 kg** has a wheelbase of **3.0 m**. The CG is at a distance of **1.4 m** from the front axle and at a height of **0.65 m** above ground. The vehicle climbs a gradient of **15%** at a constant speed of **60 km/h**. Compute:
   - (i) The total resistance to motion (rolling + grade + aerodynamic)
   - (ii) The required tractive effort
   - (iii) Whether RWD or FWD provides higher tractive effort capacity, given μ = 0.8

**Given:** f_r = 0.015, C_d = 0.35, A_f = 2.2 m², ρ = 1.2 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to B1 (A)

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Vehicle on level ground (θ = 0)
2. Constant acceleration a_lin
3. Rigid body, no suspension compliance
4. Symmetric loading
5. Aerodynamic drag neglected for this derivation

**Free-Body of Vehicle:**

Let *W* = total weight, *h* = CG height, *L* = wheelbase, *a* = distance of CG from front axle, *b'* = distance from rear axle. Inertia pseudo-force *(W/g)·a_lin* acts rearward at height *h*.

**Equilibrium equations:**

$$R_f + R_r = W \quad \text{(vertical force balance)}$$

**Moments about rear contact patch:**

$$R_f \cdot L - W \cdot a + \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}} \cdot h}{g} = 0$$

**Solving for R_f:**

$$R_f = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

**Solving for R_r:**

$$R_r = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} + \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

**Maximum tractive effort for RWD:**

$$\boxed{\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu \cdot R_r = \mu \left[ \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} + \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L} \right]}$$

**(7 marks)** — *FBD (2), Moment equation (2), Solving R_f & R_r (2), Final formula (1)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** W = 18,000 N, L = 2.6 m, a = 1.1 m, b' = 1.5 m, h = 0.55 m, a_lin = 3 m/s², μ = 0.75, g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Static reactions:**

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} = \frac{18000 \times 1.5}{2.6} = 10384.62 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{18000 \times 1.1}{2.6} = 7615.38 \text{ N}$$

**Verification:** 10384.62 + 7615.38 = 18000 N ✓

**(ii) Dynamic reactions:**

$$\Delta W = \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L} = \frac{18000 \times 0.55 \times 3}{9.81 \times 2.6}$$

$$\Delta W = \frac{29700}{25.506} = 1164.43 \text{ N}$$

$$R_f^{\text{dyn}} = 10384.62 - 1164.43 = 9220.19 \text{ N}$$

$$R_r^{\text{dyn}} = 7615.38 + 1164.43 = 8779.81 \text{ N}$$

**Verification:** 9220.19 + 8779.81 = 18000 N ✓

**(iii) Maximum tractive effort (RWD):**

$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu \cdot R_r^{\text{dyn}} = 0.75 \times 8779.81 = 6584.86 \text{ N}$$

**(7 marks)** — *(i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks*

---

### Solution to B1 (B)

#### Part (a) — Derivation with FBD

**Free Body Description:**

```
                R_f                        R_r
                 ↑                          ↑
                 |----------- L ------------|
                 |                          |
                 |←— b' ——→ ←—— a ——→|
                 |       [F_i = (W/g)a]→   |  
                 |          ↓               |
                 |          W               |
                 |     (at CG height h)     |
                 |__________|_______________|
                       ground
```

**Equations of equilibrium:**

Vertical: $R_f + R_r = W$

Moments about rear contact patch:

$$R_f \cdot L + \frac{W \cdot a_{\text{lin}} \cdot h}{g} = W \cdot a$$

$$R_f = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

$$R_r = W - R_f = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} + \frac{W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}}}{g \cdot L}$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | RWD | FWD |
|---|---|---|
| Driven wheels | Rear | Front |
| Useful reaction | R_r (gains ΔW) | R_f (loses ΔW) |
| TE_max | μ(R_r + ΔW) | μ(R_f − ΔW) |

**(7 marks)** — *FBD (2), Equilibrium (2), Solving (2), Comparison table (1)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** m = 1800 kg → W = 17658 N, L = 3.0 m, a = 1.4 m, h = 0.65 m, gradient 15% (sin θ ≈ 0.15), V = 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s, f_r = 0.015, C_d = 0.35, A_f = 2.2 m², ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, μ = 0.8, constant speed → a_lin = 0.

**(i) Total resistance:**

**Rolling resistance:**
$$R_{\text{roll}} = f_r \cdot W = 0.015 \times 17658 = 264.87 \text{ N}$$

**Grade resistance:**
$$R_{\text{grade}} = W \cdot \sin\theta = 17658 \times 0.15 = 2648.7 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic drag:**
$$R_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2$$

$$R_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 277.89 = 128.27 \text{ N}$$

**Total resistance:**
$$R_{\text{total}} = 264.87 + 2648.7 + 128.27 = 3041.84 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Required tractive effort (constant speed, a_lin = 0):**

$$\text{TE}_{\text{req}} = R_{\text{total}} = 3041.84 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Comparison RWD vs FWD:**

Static reactions:

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b'}{L} = \frac{17658 \times 1.6}{3.0} = 9417.6 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{17658 \times 1.4}{3.0} = 8240.4 \text{ N}$$

Since constant speed → ΔW = 0.

**RWD:**
$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}} = \mu \cdot W_r = 0.8 \times 8240.4 = 6592.32 \text{ N}$$

**FWD:**
$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}} = \mu \cdot W_f = 0.8 \times 9417.6 = 7534.08 \text{ N}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{FWD}} > \text{TE}_{\max}^{\text{RWD}}$$

**7534.08 > 6592.32 N** → FWD provides higher tractive effort capacity for this specific vehicle configuration because front static load > rear static load, and constant speed means no dynamic penalty.

**(7 marks)** — *(i) 3 marks, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 3 marks*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read carefully before exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write N for force, m/s² for acceleration, m for distances. Missing units = **−1 to −2 marks** per question.
>
> 2. **Mixing up *a* and *b'*:** *a* = distance from **front** axle to CG; *b'* = distance from **rear** axle to CG. Many students write $W_r = W \cdot b'/L$ which is **wrong**. Verify with moment equation.
>
> 3. **Sign error in weight transfer:** During *forward* acceleration, load shifts **rearward** (+ to rear, − to front). During braking, the reverse. Students often invert signs.
>
> 4. **Forgetting grade resistance on inclines:** If gradient > 0, you MUST add $W\sin\theta$ term. Often missed when problem says "inclined plane."
>
> 5. **Using W·sinθ with θ in degrees:** Always convert to **radians** or use sin directly from calculator if input is in degrees. Using *θ in degrees* in $W \sin\theta$ where θ was supposed to be in radians = wrong answer.
>
> 6. **Applying FBD wrongly in 4WD:** In 4WD, you cannot simply write $\text{TE} = \mu W$ without verifying that **both axles** are actually loaded. If vehicle is at a steep incline, load distribution matters.
>
> 7. **Aero drag unit confusion:** $V$ must be in **m/s**, not km/h. Convert before plugging in: $V(\text{m/s}) = V(\text{km/h})/3.6$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚗 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Tractive Effort** = driving force at contact patch, must overcome all resistances
- [ ] **Five resistances:** Rolling, Grade, Aerodynamic, Inertia, Internal friction
- [ ] **Master TE equation:** $\text{TE} = f_r W + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 + W\sin\theta + (W/g)a_{\text{lin}}$
- [ ] **Static reactions:** $W_f = W \cdot b'/L$, $W_r = W \cdot a/L$
- [ ] **Dynamic transfer:** $\Delta W = (W \cdot h \cdot a_{\text{lin}})/(g \cdot L)$
- [ ] **RWD gains load** during acceleration → ↑ TE_max
- [ ] **FWD loses load** during acceleration → ↓ TE_max
- [ ] **4WD achieves TE_max = μW** (theoretical maximum)
- [ ] **μ values:** dry ≈ 0.8–1.0, wet ≈ 0.4–0.6, snow ≈ 0.2–0.3, ice ≈ 0.1
- [ ] **Critical acceleration** for FWD/RWD crossover: $a_{\text{crit}} = gL(W_f - W_r)/(2Wh)$
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Forward acceleration → inertia force rearward at height h
- [ ] **Constant speed** ⇒ a_lin = 0 ⇒ ΔW = 0
- [ ] **Slope angle:** use **radians** or sin directly; do NOT use degree values in radian-mode equations

### 🧠 Key Conceptual Distinctions

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| TE | Available propulsive force at tire-road interface |
| TE_max | Upper limit before tire slip = μ × R_driven |
| Resistance | Forces opposing motion |
| Reaction | Vertical force from road on wheel |
| Weight transfer | Redistribution of vertical load during accel/brake |
| Drivetrain layout | Engine location × driven wheels (FR/FF/RR/4WD) |
| CG height (h) | Vertical distance from ground to mass center |
| Wheelbase (L) | Distance between front and rear axles |

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome Alignment

- **CO3 (Analyze):** Apply weight transfer analysis to different drive configurations
- **CO4 (Evaluate):** Compute maximum tractive effort and compare drive layouts
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply, Analyze, Evaluate (L3–L5)
- **Typical marks weightage:** 7–14 marks in ESE; ~3 marks in internal tests

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of a Vehicle on a Curved Track
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stability of a Vehicle on a Curved Track # MODULE 3 — STABILITY OF A VEHICLE ON A CURVED TRACK ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability of a Vehicle on a Curved Track** is defined as the dynamic condition in which a vehicle, while negotiating a horizontal curve of radius **R**, neither **overturns** outward (rotational instability about its outer wheels) nor **skids** outward (translational instability due to insufficient lateral friction). It is governed by the equilibrium of the centrifugal force, gravitational weight, normal reactions, and frictional force at the tyre–road interface. In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, two distinct stability criteria are evaluated: 1. **Overturning Stability** — Resistance of the vehicle against tipping outward due to the overturning moment created by the centrifugal force about the outer wheel contact point. 2. **Skidding Stability** — Resistance of the vehicle against sliding outward when the centrifugal force exceeds the maximum available frictional force between tyres and road. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are riding a bicycle around a sharp circular turn. You instinctively **lean inward** towards the center of the curve. Why? Because as the bicycle turns, your body "wants" to keep moving in a straight line (Newton's First Law — inertia), creating an apparent outward push. This apparent push is the **centrifugal force**. - If you lean too little (or not at all), the bicycle will **topple outward** — this is the **overturning** failure. - If the road is slippery (icy or oily), the tyres will **slide outward** even if the bicycle does not tip — this is the **skidding** failure. Engineers tackle these twin problems in two complementary ways: | Engineering Solution | Physical Purpose | |---|---| | **Banking the road (superelevation/cant)** | Tilts the road inward so that a component of the normal reaction itself counters the centrifugal force — reducing reliance on friction and improving passenger comfort. | | **Increasing the coefficient of friction (μ)** | Provides a stronger lateral grip to resist skidding. | | **Lowering the center of gravity (h)** | Reduces the overturning moment arm, making the vehicle inherently more stable. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant Used:** The acceleration due to gravity used in KTU derivations is **g = 9.81 m/s²**. ### 1.3 Standard Symbols and Units (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | |---|---|---| | W | Total weight of the vehicle | N (Newton) | | m | Mass of the vehicle | kg | | v | Velocity of the vehicle | m/s | | r | Radius of the curved path | m | | h | Height of center of gravity above road | m | | b | Half the lateral wheel track (track width/2) | m | | a | Half the longitudinal wheelbase | m | | R₁, R₂ | Normal reactions on inner and outer wheels | N | | P | Centrifugal force = Wv²/(gr) | N | | μ | Coefficient of friction between tyre and road | dimensionless | | θ | Angle of banking/superelevation | radians or degrees | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Forces Acting on a Vehicle on a Curved Track When a four-wheeled vehicle of weight **W** moving with velocity **v** negotiates a curve of radius **r**, four primary forces (and their associated moments) act on the vehicle: 1. **Weight (W)** — Acts vertically downward through the **Center of Gravity (CG)** located at height **h** above the road surface. 2. **Centrifugal Force (P)** — Acts horizontally outward (away from the curve's center) at the CG, given by: $$P = \frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ 3. **Normal Reactions (R₁ and R₂)** — Vertical reactions at the inner and outer wheel contact patches respectively. For a four-wheel vehicle, R₁ is the combined reaction of the two inner wheels and R₂ of the two outer wheels. 4. **Frictional Force (F)** — Acts horizontally inward at the tyre–road contact, opposing the tendency to skid outward. Maximum static frictional force = **μR**, where R is the total normal reaction. ### 2.2 Three Cases Analyzed in KTU Curriculum > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus categorizes stability analysis into **three fundamental cases**. Each case builds upon the previous by introducing one additional parameter. #### **Case 1: Flat Unbanked Road (No Friction Considerations for Overturning)** Only weight and centrifugal force act (plus normal reactions). For overturning analysis, the **overturning moment** about the outer wheel contact is equated to the **restoring moment**: $$\text{Overturning Moment} = P \cdot h = \frac{W v^2}{g r} \cdot h$$ $$\text{Restoring Moment} = W \cdot b$$ #### **Case 2: Flat Unbanked Road (With Friction — Skidding Analysis)** The vehicle skids when centrifugal force exceeds the maximum friction force: $$P > \mu W \implies \frac{W v^2}{g r} > \mu W \implies v^2 > \mu g r$$ #### **Case 3: Banked Road (With or Without Friction)** The road is tilted inward by an angle **θ** (superelevation). The normal reaction **R** is now perpendicular to the road surface (not vertical). Components of R now partially balance the centrifugal force, reducing the demand on lateral friction. ### 2.3 Logical Breakdown — Operational Mechanism - **Step 1 — Identify failure mode:** Determine whether the limiting factor is rotation (overturning) or translation (skidding). - **Step 2 — Set up moment/force equilibrium:** Take moments about the outer wheel tip for overturning; sum lateral forces for skidding. - **Step 3 — Apply the limiting condition:** For overturning, the inner wheel reaction **R₁ = 0** (vehicle is about to tip). For skidding, the friction is at its **maximum value μR**. - **Step 4 — Solve for the critical velocity:** This gives the maximum safe speed without failure. - **Step 5 — Compare overturning vs skidding critical speeds:** The **lower** of the two governs actual safety. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below is the **definitive KTU 2024 formula sheet** for this topic. Master these before attempting any exam problem. | # | Scenario | Condition / Formula | Failure Mode | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Centrifugal Force | $P = \dfrac{W v^2}{g r}$ | Universal | | 2 | Overturning on flat road | $v_o^2 = \dfrac{g r \, h}{b}$ (becomes critical when $R_1 = 0$) | Overturning | | 3 | Skidding on flat road | $v_s^2 = \mu \, g \, r$ | Skidding | | 4 | Reactions (flat road, no skidding) | $R_1 = \dfrac{W}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$, $R_2 = \dfrac{W}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$ | — | | 5 | Banking — no friction (ideal) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{v^2}{g r}$ | Both eliminated | | 6 | Banking — with friction (max safe speed) | $v_{max}^2 = \dfrac{g r (\tan\theta + \mu)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$ | Outer skidding | | 7 | Banking — with friction (min safe speed) | $v_{min}^2 = \dfrac{g r (\tan\theta - \mu)}{1 + \mu \tan\theta}$ | Inner skidding | | 8 | Overturning on banked road | $v_o^2 = \dfrac{g r \left(b + h \tan\theta\right)}{h - b \tan\theta}$ | Overturning | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always substitute numerical values **with units** at the very end. Maintain sign conventions: weight downward (positive), centrifugal force outward, normal reaction perpendicular to the surface (for banked road). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Highway Design:** Civil engineers use the banking formula $v^2 = gr \tan\theta$ to design superelevation on expressway curves, balancing safety and comfort. - **Railway Engineering:** The concept of **cant deficiency** on Indian Railways is a direct application of the banked-road-with-friction model. - **Motorsport & Formula 1:** Engineers minimize CG height (h) to push the overturning speed far above the operating speed, ensuring tyre grip (friction) is the only practical limit. - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Stability envelopes derived from these formulas are embedded into the path-planning algorithms of self-driving cars. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reactions on the Wheels of a Vehicle on a Flat Curved Track (No Skidding) **Problem Setup:** A four-wheeled vehicle of weight **W**, CG at height **h**, lateral half-track **b**, moves at velocity **v** on a flat curve of radius **r**. The vehicle neither skids nor overturns. **Free Body Diagram Description:** - W acts downward through CG. - P = Wv²/(gr) acts horizontally outward through CG. - R₁ (inner wheel reaction) and R₂ (outer wheel reaction) act upward. **Step 1: Vertical Force Equilibrium** Sum of vertical forces = 0: $$R_1 + R_2 = W$$ **Step 2: Moment Equilibrium About the Outer Wheel Contact Point (O)** Taking moments about the outer wheel contact (this eliminates R₂ and friction from the equation): $$\sum M_O = 0$$ The centrifugal force P, acting at height h, creates an overturning moment = P × h (outward direction taken as positive for overturning). The weight W, acting through CG, creates a restoring moment about the outer wheel contact. The horizontal distance from CG to outer wheel contact = **b** (half-track). $$W \cdot b - P \cdot h = 0 \quad \text{(at the verge of overturning, but we are not at verge — just equilibrium)}$$ Wait — for the **non-verge** case (vehicle is stable, not tipping), we sum moments taking R₁ into account. The CG is at horizontal distance **b** from the inner wheel and at distance **b** from the outer wheel contact point. **Step 3: Taking Moments About the Outer Wheel Contact Point** The moment arm of W about the outer wheel contact = b (inward). The moment arm of P about the outer wheel contact = h (above). The moment arm of R₁ about the outer wheel contact = 2b (inward, from inner wheel to outer wheel).
