Setting the sum of moments to zero (clockwise = counter-clockwise): $$R_1 \cdot (2b) + P \cdot h = W \cdot b$$ **Step 4: Substitute P = Wv²/(gr)** $$2b \, R_1 + \frac{W v^2}{g r} \cdot h = W b$$ **Step 5: Solve for R₁** $$2b \, R_1 = W b - \frac{W v^2 h}{g r}$$ $$2b \, R_1 = W\left(b - \frac{v^2 h}{g r}\right)$$ $$R_1 = \frac{W}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$$ **Step 6: Solve for R₂ using R₁ + R₂ = W** $$R_2 = W - R_1 = W - \frac{W}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$$ $$R_2 = \frac{W}{2}\left(1 + \frac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** As **v** increases, R₁ decreases and R₂ increases. When R₁ = 0, all weight transfers to the outer wheels — the vehicle is at the verge of overturning. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Overturning on a Flat Curved Track **At the verge of overturning**, the inner wheels just lose contact with the ground, so **R₁ = 0**. **Step 1:** Substitute R₁ = 0 into the R₁ formula: $$0 = \frac{W}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v_o^2 \, h}{g r b}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Simplify the bracket: $$1 - \frac{v_o^2 h}{g r b} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Solve for v₀²: $$v_o^2 = \frac{g r b}{h}$$ $$\boxed{v_o = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \, r \, b}{h}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **overturning critical velocity**. If the vehicle exceeds this speed on a flat curve, it will tip outward. Note that **lowering h** (low CG) and **widening b** (wide track) both increase overturning safety. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition for Skidding on a Flat Curved Track **Step 1:** Skidding occurs when centrifugal force = maximum friction force: $$P = \mu W$$ **Step 2:** Substitute P = Wv²/(gr): $$\frac{W v_s^2}{g r} = \mu W$$ **Step 3:** Cancel W from both sides: $$\frac{v_s^2}{g r} = \mu$$ **Step 4:** Solve for vₛ²: $$\boxed{v_s = \sqrt{\mu \, g \, r}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Skidding speed depends **only on μ, g, r** — it is **independent of vehicle dimensions** (h, b). Overturning speed, in contrast, depends entirely on the vehicle's geometry. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability on a Banked Road WITHOUT Friction **Setup:** The road is banked at angle **θ** inward. The vehicle moves at the "ideal" speed where no lateral friction is needed. **Free Body Diagram Description:** - W downward through CG. - P = Wv²/(gr) horizontally outward through CG. - R, the normal reaction, acts perpendicular to the banked surface (tilted inward by angle θ from the vertical). **Step 1:** Resolve R into components: - Vertical component: $R \cos\theta$ - Horizontal component (inward, toward curve center): $R \sin\theta$ **Step 2: Vertical Force Equilibrium** $$R \cos\theta = W$$ **Step 3: Horizontal Force Equilibrium (No Friction)** $$R \sin\theta = P = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ **Step 4: Divide Equation (Step 3) by Equation (Step 2)** $$\frac{R \sin\theta}{R \cos\theta} = \frac{W v^2 / (g r)}{W}$$ $$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{g r}$$ **Step 5: Solve for v** $$\boxed{v = \sqrt{g \, r \tan\theta}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At this speed, the vehicle requires **zero lateral friction**. This is called the **"natural" or "equilibrium" speed** of the banked curve — passengers experience no sideways push. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Maximum and Minimum Speeds on a Banked Road WITH Friction This is the **most important KTU 14-mark derivation**. Setup: Banked road (angle θ), coefficient of friction μ, vehicle may travel faster or slower than the natural speed. **Case A: Maximum Safe Speed (v_max) — Vehicle Tends to Skid Outward** Friction acts **down the slope** (inward, toward curve center), assisting the banking. Both R sin θ and μR cos θ together balance P. **Vertical equilibrium:** $$R \cos\theta = W + \mu R \sin\theta$$ Wait — let me reconsider the sign convention carefully. Let me re-derive cleanly: **Free Body Setup for v_max:** - W downward through CG. - P outward through CG. - R perpendicular to banked surface. - Friction F = μR acts **down the incline** (toward the curve center, inward) — opposing the outward tendency to skid. **Vertical equilibrium:** $$R \cos\theta = W + \mu R \sin\theta$$ Wait — friction acts parallel to the road surface. If friction is "down the slope" (toward the center), and the slope inclines inward toward the center, then friction has: - Horizontal component (inward): μR cos θ - Vertical component (downward): μR sin θ So the vertical equation becomes: $$R \cos\theta = W + \mu R \sin\theta$$ **Horizontal equilibrium:** $$R \sin\theta + \mu R \cos\theta = P = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ **Solving the two equations:** From vertical: $$R(\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta) = W$$ $$R = \frac{W}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta}$$ Substitute into horizontal: $$\frac{W \sin\theta}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta} + \frac{\mu W \cos\theta}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta} = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ $$\frac{W(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta} = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ $$\tan\theta + \mu = \frac{v^2}{g r}(1 - \mu \tan\theta)$$ $$\boxed{v_{max}^2 = \dfrac{g r (\tan\theta + \mu)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}}$$ **Case B: Minimum Safe Speed (v_min) — Vehicle Tends to Skid Inward (Down the Slope)** Friction acts **up the slope** (outward, away from curve center) — preventing the vehicle from sliding down the bank toward the center. **Vertical equilibrium:** $$R \cos\theta + \mu R \sin\theta = W$$ **Horizontal equilibrium:** $$R \sin\theta - \mu R \cos\theta = P = \frac{W v^2}{g r}$$ By the same algebraic procedure: $$\boxed{v_{min}^2 = \dfrac{g r (\tan\theta - \mu)}{1 + \mu \tan\theta}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The minus sign in $1 - \mu \tan\theta$ means **μ tan θ must be less than 1** for the maximum speed formula to give a real (finite) answer. Physically, if μ is too high, the vehicle cannot skid outward at all — it would have to overturn first. --- ### 3.6 Comparative Stability Table | Failure Mode | Speed Limit | Depends On | |---|---|---| | Overturning | $v_o = \sqrt{g r b / h}$ | Vehicle geometry (b, h) and curve (r) | | Skidding outward (flat) | $v_s = \sqrt{\mu g r}$ | Friction (μ), curve (r) | | Skidding outward (banked) | $v_{max} = \sqrt{gr(\tan\theta + \mu)/(1 - \mu\tan\theta)}$ | μ, θ, r | | Skidding inward (banked) | $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr(\tan\theta - \mu)/(1 + \mu\tan\theta)}$ | μ, θ, r | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Vehicle on a Flat Curved Track The following Mermaid block renders the functional free-body architecture of a vehicle negotiating a flat curve, mapping all force vectors, moment arms, and reactions. ```mermaid flowchart LR CG["CG: Center of Gravity
Height h above road"] W["W: Weight
Acts downward through CG"] P["P = Wv²/gr
Centrifugal force
Acts horizontally outward at CG"] R1["R1: Inner Wheel Reaction
Vertical upward at inner wheels"] R2["R2: Outer Wheel Reaction
Vertical upward at outer wheels"] F["Friction Force F
Acts inward at tyre contact"] innerwheel["Inner Wheels
Distance b inward from CG"] outerwheel["Outer Wheels
Distance b outward from CG"] momentOT["Overturning Moment
= P × h"] momentRS["Restoring Moment
= W × b"] CG --> W CG --> P innerwheel --> R1 outerwheel --> R2 outerwheel --> F P --> momentOT W --> momentRS momentOT --> resultA{"Compare Moments"} momentRS --> resultA resultA -->|Overturning when P*h > W*b| tip["Vehicle Tips Outward"] resultA -->|Stable when P*h less than W*b| safe["Vehicle Stable"] ``` ### 4.2 Stability Analysis Flowchart — Decision Logic for KTU Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Start: Vehicle on Curved Track"]) q1{"Road Banked?"} flat["Flat Road Path"] banked["Banked Road Path"] q2{"Check Failure Mode"} overturn["Overturning Analysis:
Set R1 = 0"] skid["Skidding Analysis:
Set P = mu W"] q3{"Friction Considered?"} nofric["Ideal Banking:
tan theta = v² / gr"] withfric["With Friction:
Use v_max and v_min formulas"] comp["Compare v_o vs v_s
or v_min less than v less than v_max"] decision{"Failure?"} yes["Vehicle Fails Stability"] no["Vehicle Safe"] start --> q1 q1 -->|No| flat q1 -->|Yes| banked flat --> q2 q2 --> overturn q2 --> skid banked --> q3 q3 -->|No| nofric q3 -->|Yes| withfric overturn --> comp skid --> comp nofric --> comp withfric --> comp comp --> decision decision -->|Yes| yes decision -->|No| no ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Banking System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"] v["Vehicle Velocity v"] r["Curve Radius r"] mu["Friction Coefficient mu"] theta["Banking Angle theta"] h["CG Height h"] b["Half Track Width b"] end subgraph COMPUTE["Computation Engine"] natSpeed["Natural Speed:
v_nat = sqrt gr tan theta"] vmax["Maximum Speed:
v_max formula"] vmin["Minimum Speed:
v_min formula"] vo["Overturning Speed:
v_o = sqrt grb / h"] end subgraph DECISION["Stability Verdict"] verdict{"Operating Speed v
Within Safe Envelope?"} safeOutput["STABLE: v_min less than or equal to v less than or equal to v_max"] unsafeOutput["UNSTABLE: Failure Likely"] end v --> natSpeed r --> natSpeed theta --> natSpeed mu --> vmax mu --> vmin vmax --> verdict vmin --> verdict v --> verdict verdict -->|Yes| safeOutput verdict -->|No| unsafeOutput ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the FBD (4.1), observe how the centrifugal force P and weight W both act at the **same point (CG)** but in **orthogonal directions**. The two moment equations (overturning vs restoring) compete about the outer wheel contact, which is the pivot point for the rotational stability analysis. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following terms with respect to vehicle stability on a curved track:** **(a)** Overturning **(b)** Skidding **(c)** Superelevation **Model Answer:** **(a) Overturning:** Overturning is the rotational failure of a vehicle in which it tips outward about the outer wheels due to the overturning moment created by the centrifugal force exceeding the restoring moment due to the vehicle's weight. The condition for the verge of overturning is when the inner wheel reaction $R_1 = 0$. **(b) Skidding:** Skidding is the translational failure of a vehicle in which the wheels slide outward (or inward on a banked road) when the centrifugal force exceeds the maximum available frictional force at the tyre–road contact. The condition is $\frac{Wv^2}{gr} > \mu W$, i.e., $v^2 > \mu g r$. **(c) Superelevation (Banking):** Superelevation is the inward tilting of the road surface at a curve by an angle **θ** such that a component of the normal reaction itself counters the centrifugal force, reducing reliance on friction. The ideal banking condition is $\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{gr}$. --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A vehicle of weight 20 kN is moving on a curve of radius 100 m. If the height of CG is 0.8 m and the track width is 1.6 m, find the maximum speed to avoid overturning. Take g = 9.81 m/s².** **Model Answer:** Given: W = 20,000 N, r = 100 m, h = 0.8 m, b = 1.6/2 = 0.8 m, g = 9.81 m/s². For overturning on a flat road: $$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{g r b}{h}}$$ $$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 100 \times 0.8}{0.8}}$$ $$v_o = \sqrt{9.81 \times 100} = \sqrt{981}$$ $$\boxed{v_o \approx 31.32 \text{ m/s} \approx 112.76 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Two fully solved alternatives are provided below. --- #### **Question B — OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** A four-wheeled vehicle weighing 25 kN has a wheel track of 1.5 m. Its center of gravity is 0.6 m above the ground and lies 1.2 m ahead of the rear axle and 1.2 m behind the front axle. The vehicle is going around a curve of radius 50 m. Determine: **(i)** The critical speed at which the vehicle would skid, assuming the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road is 0.4. **[7 Marks]** **(ii)** The critical speed at which the vehicle would overturn. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Find the reactions on each of the four wheels when the vehicle is travelling at 30 km/h on the curve. **[Bonus — Conceptual]** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - W = 25,000 N - Track width = 1.5 m, so b = 0.75 m - CG height h = 0.6 m - Curve radius r = 50 m - Coefficient of friction μ = 0.4 - g = 9.81 m/s² --- **Part (a)(i): Critical Skidding Speed** For skidding on a flat road: $$v_s = \sqrt{\mu \, g \, r}$$ Substituting: $$v_s = \sqrt{0.4 \times 9.81 \times 50}$$ $$v_s = \sqrt{0.4 \times 490.5} = \sqrt{196.2}$$ $$\boxed{v_s \approx 14.01 \text{ m/s} \approx 50.43 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- **Part (a)(ii): Critical Overturning Speed** For overturning: $$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{g \, r \, b}{h}}$$ Substituting: $$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 50 \times 0.75}{0.6}}$$ $$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 50 \times 0.75}{0.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{367.875}{0.6}} = \sqrt{613.125}$$ $$\boxed{v_o \approx 24.76 \text{ m/s} \approx 89.13 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- **Comparison & Conclusion:** Since $v_s$ (14.01 m/s) < $v_o$ (24.76 m/s), the vehicle will **skid first** before it can overturn. Therefore, the **practical safe speed** is governed by the skidding limit. --- #### **Question B — OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** A highway curve of radius 200 m is banked at an angle of 15°. Determine the speed at which a vehicle should travel so that it experiences no tendency to skid. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **[7 Marks]** **(b)** If the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road is 0.3, determine the **maximum** speed at which the vehicle can negotiate the curve without skidding outward. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - r = 200 m - θ = 15°, so tan 15° ≈ 0.2679 - g = 9.81 m/s² - μ = 0.3 --- **Part (a): Speed for Zero Skidding Tendency (No Friction)** For an ideally banked road with no friction: $$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{g r}$$ $$v^2 = g r \tan\theta$$ $$v^2 = 9.81 \times 200 \times \tan 15°$$ $$v^2 = 9.81 \times 200 \times 0.2679 = 525.66 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$\boxed{v \approx 22.93 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.5 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- **Part (b): Maximum Speed with Friction** For the maximum safe speed on a banked road: $$v_{max}^2 = \frac{g r (\tan\theta + \mu)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$$ Compute each term: - $\tan\theta + \mu = 0.2679 + 0.3 = 0.5679$ - $1 - \mu \tan\theta = 1 - (0.3)(0.2679) = 1 - 0.0804 = 0.9196$ $$v_{max}^2 = \frac{9.81 \times 200 \times 0.5679}{0.9196}$$ $$v_{max}^2 = \frac{1114.36}{0.9196} = 1211.78 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 34.81 \text{ m/s} \approx 125.3 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- **Conclusion:** The vehicle can safely travel anywhere from the minimum speed (below which it skids inward) up to **125.3 km/h** (above which it skids outward). The ideal comfort speed is **82.5 km/h**. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing b with full track width:** Half-track **b** = (track width)/2 must be used in overturning formulas. Using the full track width doubles the overturning speed — a 2 to 4 mark error. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s:** Velocity in formulas must be in **m/s**. Conversion: $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} \times \frac{5}{18}$. > > 3. **Sign errors in banked-road friction formulas:** The maximum speed formula has $(1 - \mu \tan\theta)$ in the denominator. Forgetting the negative sign gives the wrong (and physically meaningless) answer. > > 4. **Not comparing $v_o$ and $v_s$:** KTU examiners specifically award marks for the final comparison ("vehicle skids first because $v_s < v_o$"). Skipping this step costs 2 marks. > > 5. **Mixing up R₁ and R₂:** R₁ is the **inner** wheel reaction (decreases with speed); R₂ is the **outer** wheel reaction (increases with speed). Reversing them is a common slip. > > 6. **Wrong value of g:** Always use **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless specified. Using 10 m/s² introduces ~2% error. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **centrifugal force** $P = Wv^2/(gr)$ acts **horizontally outward** through the **center of gravity**. - ✅ **Overturning critical speed** on flat road: $v_o = \sqrt{grb/h}$. Depends on **vehicle geometry**. - ✅ **Skidding critical speed** on flat road: $v_s = \sqrt{\mu g r}$. Depends on **friction and curve only**. - ✅ The vehicle **fails by skidding** if $v_s < v_o$, and **fails by overturning** if $v_o < v_s$. - ✅ **Inner wheel reaction**: $R_1 = \frac{W}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$ — decreases with speed. - ✅ **Outer wheel reaction**: $R_2 = \frac{W}{2}\left(1 + \frac{v^2 h}{g r b}\right)$ — increases with speed. - ✅ At the verge of overturning: **R₁ = 0** (inner wheels lift off). - ✅ **Ideal banking** (no friction): $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$. - ✅ **Maximum speed on banked road (with friction)**: $v_{max} = \sqrt{gr(\tan\theta + \mu)/(1 - \mu\tan\theta)}$. - ✅ **Minimum speed on banked road (with friction)**: $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr(\tan\theta - \mu)/(1 + \mu\tan\theta)}$. - ✅ Banking reduces dependence on friction, improves comfort, and allows higher safe speeds. - ✅ Lowering **h** (CG height) and increasing **b** (track width) improve **overturning stability** but do **not** affect skidding stability. - ✅ Higher **μ** (friction) improves **skidding stability** but does **not** affect overturning stability. - ✅ For all numerical problems: convert units to SI (m, s, N) **before** substituting into formulas. ---

topics.title: Slope and a Banked Road, Gyroscopic Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slope and a Banked Road, Gyroscopic Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Slope (Gradient Resistance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Slope* refers to the inclination of a road surface with respect to the horizontal plane. When a vehicle ascends or descends a gradient, the weight component along the direction of motion acts as an additional resistance (ascending) or as an assistive force (descending). This is termed **Gradient Resistance** and is one of the primary forces a vehicle's tractive effort must overcome, alongside rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing a heavy trolley up a ramp versus pushing it on flat ground. On the ramp, even before you start, gravity is pulling the trolley *backwards* along the slope. That extra "backward pull" is gradient resistance. The steeper the ramp (higher slope angle), the more effort you need — not because the trolley got heavier, but because more of its weight is now acting in the direction *opposite* to your push. Conversely, if you let it roll down, gravity *helps* you.

**Key Physical Constant:**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

### 1.2 Banked Road

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *banked road* is a road that has been deliberately raised on its outer edge along a curved path to form an angle with the horizontal. Banking introduces a component of the normal reaction that counteracts the **centrifugal force** developed during cornering, thereby improving vehicle stability, reducing tyre wear, and allowing higher safe cornering speeds without relying solely on tyre friction.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a racing velodrome (the steep circular track used in track cycling). Cyclists lean into the curve, but the *track itself* is tilted upward toward the outside. Why? Because if you tilt the floor inward slightly, the floor pushes back on you with a force that has a small "sideways" component — that component replaces the friction force needed to keep you from sliding outward. Banking is the road's way of "leaning into the curve" on behalf of the vehicle.

---

### 1.3 Gyroscopic Effects

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The *gyroscopic effect* is the phenomenon where a spinning body (such as a vehicle's engine flywheel or wheels) resists any change in the orientation of its spin axis. When the vehicle undergoes **pitching** (longitudinal rotation), **rolling** (lateral rotation), or **yawing** (vertical-axis rotation), the spinning components generate a reactive **gyroscopic couple** that either assists or opposes the vehicle's maneuver, significantly affecting stability.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Hold a spinning bicycle wheel by both ends of its axle and try to twist it sideways. You'll feel a strong, unexpected resistance — and the wheel will try to turn *perpendicular* to your push rather than where you intended. This is gyroscopic precession. Now imagine this wheel is mounted inside a car: every time you steer or brake, the spinning wheel "fights back" with a couple. On a two-wheeler, this same effect is what keeps the bike upright once moving — but it also makes it harder to turn at low speeds.

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Topics Are Grouped Together in Module 3

All three topics deal with **secondary forces** acting on a vehicle beyond straight-line driving:
- **Slope** modifies the effective resistance in the longitudinal direction.
- **Banking** modifies the lateral force balance during cornering.
- **Gyroscopic effects** are reactive couples generated by rotating masses during vehicle maneuvering.

Together they form the **stability triad** of a moving vehicle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Slope — Theoretical Breakdown

When a vehicle of weight $W = mg$ travels up an incline of angle $\alpha$:

1. **Weight resolution** — The weight vector $W$ is resolved into two components:
   - Normal to slope: $W \cos\alpha$ (acts into the road surface).
   - Parallel to slope: $W \sin\alpha$ (acts **opposite** to motion when ascending).
2. **Gradient Resistance Force:**
$$F_g = W \sin\alpha = mg \sin\alpha$$
3. **Grade Definition (Engineering):** Grade (%) is defined as rise per 100 units of horizontal run.
$$\text{Grade (\%)} = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} \times 100 = \tan\alpha \times 100$$
4. **For small angles** ($\alpha < 10°$): $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$ (radians), so engineers often use grade percentage directly.
5. **Total Resistance on a Slope:**
$$F_{\text{total}} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g$$
where $F_{rr}$ = rolling resistance, $F_{ad}$ = aerodynamic drag.

---

### 2.2 Banked Road — Theoretical Breakdown

A vehicle of weight $W$ negotiates a curve of radius $r$ at speed $v$ on a road banked at angle $\theta$.

1. **Three forces act on the vehicle:**
   - Weight $W$ vertically downward.
   - Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the banked surface.
   - Frictional force $F_f = \mu N$ along the banked surface (direction depends on speed).

2. **Centrifugal Force** (in the rotating frame): $F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}$

3. **Force resolution** along the banked surface gives two equilibrium equations:
   - Horizontal: $N \sin\theta \pm \mu N \cos\theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$
   - Vertical: $N \cos\theta \mp \mu N \sin\theta = mg$

4. **Three classical cases** arise:

   - **Case A — No friction, no slipping at a single design speed:**
$$v_0 = \sqrt{rg \tan\theta}$$

   - **Case B — Maximum safe speed (vehicle tends to skid outward, friction acts inward):**
$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}}$$

   - **Case C — Minimum safe speed (vehicle tends to skid inward, friction acts outward):**
$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu \tan\theta}}$$

5. **Physical limit:** For $v_{\min}$ to be real, $\tan\theta > \mu$. This is why very steep banking on icy roads can be *dangerous* at low speeds.

---

### 2.3 Gyroscopic Effects — Theoretical Breakdown

A spinning mass with polar mass moment of inertia $I_e$ (about spin axis) and spin angular velocity $\omega$ experiences a gyroscopic couple when its spin axis is forced to rotate (precess) at angular velocity $\omega_p$.

1. **Fundamental Gyroscopic Couple Equation:**
$$C = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$$
2. **Direction (Right-Hand Rule):** The reactive couple acts about an axis mutually perpendicular to both $\omega$ (spin) and $\omega_p$ (precession).
3. **Three Vehicle Scenarios:**

   - **Pitching** (vehicle nose-dives on braking or lifts on acceleration):
$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{\text{pitch}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{R}$$
   where $R$ is the instantaneous radius of the pitching motion.

   - **Rolling** (vehicle leans during cornering — affects two-wheelers mainly):
$$C_{\text{roll}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{r \cos\phi}$$
   where $\phi$ is the lean/camber angle.

   - **Yawing** (vehicle turning about a vertical axis at angular velocity $\omega_y = v/r$):
$$C_{\text{yaw}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{r}$$
   where $r$ is the turning radius.

4. **Stability Interpretation:**
   - On **two-wheelers**, gyroscopic effect **aids stability** at speed (resists tipping).
   - On **four-wheelers**, the gyroscopic couple from the engine flywheel **resists steering inputs**, making low-speed turning sluggish.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary Table for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Special Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Gradient Resistance | $F_g = W\sin\alpha$ | $F_g$ [N], $W$ [N], $\alpha$ [rad or °] | Up-slope motion |
| 2 | Grade Percentage | $\text{Grade\%} = \tan\alpha \times 100$ | Dimensionless | Engineering convention |
| 3 | Approx. for small slope | $F_g \approx W \cdot (\text{Grade}/100)$ | N | Valid for $\alpha < 10°$ |
| 4 | Total Tractive Effort on Slope | $F_{TE} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g$ | N | Steady-state climbing |
| 5 | Banking — Design Speed | $v_0 = \sqrt{rg\tan\theta}$ | $v_0$ [m/s], $r$ [m], $g$ [m/s²], $\theta$ [rad] | No friction, equilibrium |
| 6 | Banking — Max Speed (friction inward) | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg\dfrac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu\tan\theta}}$ | Same as above + $\mu$ | Vehicle tends to slide outward |
| 7 | Banking — Min Speed (friction outward) | $v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg\dfrac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu\tan\theta}}$ | Same as above | Vehicle tends to slide inward; requires $\tan\theta > \mu$ |
| 8 | Gyroscopic Couple (general) | $C = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | $C$ [N·m], $I_e$ [kg·m²], $\omega$ [rad/s], $\omega_p$ [rad/s] | Active precession required |
| 9 | Gyroscopic Couple — Pitch | $C_{\text{pitch}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot (v/R)$ | $R$ [m] radius of pitch arc | Braking/acceleration |
| 10 | Gyroscopic Couple — Yaw | $C_{\text{yaw}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot (v/r)$ | $r$ [m] turning radius | Cornering maneuver |
| 11 | Gyroscopic Couple — Roll | $C_{\text{roll}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot (v/(r\cos\phi))$ | $\phi$ lean angle [rad] | Two-wheeled vehicle |
| 12 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c = mv^2/r$ | $F_c$ [N], $m$ [kg] | Used in banking derivation |
| 13 | Reaction Angle for Equilibrium | $\tan\theta = v^2/(rg)$ | — | Rearranged Case A formula |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Slope Analysis:** Used in commercial vehicle design (trucks, buses) for **grade-ability** specifications — e.g., a truck rated for "30% grade" can climb a slope whose rise is 30 m per 100 m of horizontal run.
- **Banking Design:** Civil engineers use the banking formulas to design highway curves, race tracks (Nürburgring, Brooklands), and railway transitions. F1 circuits bank some corners at over 15°.
- **Gyroscopic Effects:** Critical in **motorcycle dynamics** (why training wheels are needed at low speeds), **ship stabilizers**, and **aircraft inertial navigation systems**. Modern car designers also minimize flywheel gyroscopic effects for sharper handling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Resistance on a Slope

**Setup:** Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ on an incline of angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The vehicle ascends the slope at constant velocity.

**Free Body Forces:**
- Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the inclined surface.
- Tractive force $F$ acting up the slope.
- Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ and aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$ acting down the slope.

**Step 1 — Resolve weight along and perpendicular to the slope:**

Resolving $W$ vertically downward into components parallel and normal to the incline:

$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\alpha \quad (\text{along slope, downward direction})$$

$$W_{\perp} = W \cos\alpha \quad (\text{normal to slope, into surface})$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium along the slope:**

For steady ascent at constant velocity, sum of forces along slope = 0:

$$F = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + W \sin\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Identify the gradient resistance term:**

The component that exists *only because of the slope* is:

$$F_g = W \sin\alpha = mg \sin\alpha$$

This is the **gradient resistance** — additional force the engine must overcome.

**Step 4 — Small-angle approximation (engineering utility):**

For $\alpha$ in radians, when $\alpha$ is small:

$$\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$$

Grade is defined as:

$$\text{Grade} = \tan\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_g \approx W \cdot \text{Grade}$$

In percentage:

$$F_g \approx \frac{W \cdot G}{100}$$

where $G$ is grade in percent.

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration:**

Let $m = 1500$ kg, $\alpha = 10°$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$F_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10°) = 14715 \times 0.1736 \approx 2555 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Grade\%} = \tan(10°) \times 100 = 17.63\%$$

$$F_g \approx \frac{14715 \times 17.63}{100} \approx 2595 \text{ N} \quad (\text{matches within 1.6\%})$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Banking Angle for Zero Friction (Design Speed)

**Setup:** Vehicle of mass $m$ moves at constant speed $v$ along a circular path of radius $r$ on a road banked at angle $\theta$. Friction is neglected.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram in the rotating frame:**

Forces acting on vehicle:
- Weight $W = mg$ vertically downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to banked surface (tilted $\theta$ from vertical).

**Step 2 — Resolve normal reaction $N$ into horizontal and vertical components:**

$$N_x = N \sin\theta \quad (\text{horizontal, pointing toward curve center})$$

$$N_y = N \cos\theta \quad (\text{vertical, upward})$$

**Step 3 — Apply vertical equilibrium:**

Sum of vertical forces = 0:

$$N \cos\theta = mg$$

$$\therefore N = \frac{mg}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply horizontal equilibrium (centripetal):**

The horizontal component of $N$ provides the centripetal force:

$$N \sin\theta = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Divide the two equilibrium equations:**

$$\frac{N \sin\theta}{N \cos\theta} = \frac{mv^2/r}{mg}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{rg}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for design speed or banking angle:**

$$v_0 = \sqrt{rg \tan\theta} \quad \text{or} \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{rg}\right)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum and Minimum Speed on Banked Road with Friction

**Setup:** Same as above, but friction coefficient $\mu$ between tyres and road is now considered.

**Case B — Maximum Speed (vehicle tends to slide outward, friction acts inward):**

**Step 1 — Forces:**
- Weight $W = mg$ downward.
- Normal $N$ perpendicular to banked surface.
- Friction $f = \mu N$ acting **down the slope** (since vehicle tends to slide outward/up the slope, friction opposes motion → down the slope).

**Step 2 — Resolved force equations:**

Horizontal (toward center):
$$N \sin\theta + \mu N \cos\theta = \frac{mv_{\max}^2}{r}$$

Vertical (upward):
$$N \cos\theta - \mu N \sin\theta = mg$$

**Step 3 — Divide horizontal by vertical:**

$$\frac{N(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)}{N(\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta)} = \frac{v_{\max}^2/r}{g}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $\sin/\cos$ division:**

$$\frac{\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta}{\cos\theta - \mu \sin\theta} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{rg}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\theta$:

$$\frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{rg}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}}$$

**Case C — Minimum Speed (vehicle tends to slide inward, friction acts outward):**

Following identical logic but with friction acting **up the slope**:

$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu \tan\theta}}$$

**Constraint:** $v_{\min}$ is real only if $\tan\theta \geq \mu$ — otherwise the road is too slippery for safe low-speed travel on the bank.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Gyroscopic Couple on a Turning Vehicle (Yaw)

**Setup:** A vehicle fitted with a flywheel of polar moment of inertia $I_e$ spins at $\omega$ rad/s about a longitudinal (forward) axis. The vehicle turns about a vertical axis at yaw rate $\dot{\psi} = v/r$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Identify angular velocities:**
- Spin: $\vec{\omega}_s = \omega \hat{i}$ (along vehicle's longitudinal axis).
- Precession (yaw): $\vec{\omega}_p = (v/r) \hat{k}$ (along vertical axis).

**Step 2 — Apply the gyroscopic couple formula:**

$$\vec{C} = I_e \vec{\omega}_s \times \vec{\omega}_p$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitude using $|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = ab\sin(90°)$ (since axes are perpendicular):**

$$C = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Identify direction using right-hand rule:**

$\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$, meaning the gyroscopic couple acts along the **lateral** axis of the vehicle.

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

This lateral couple either **assists or opposes** the steering input depending on whether the engine is in front or rear of the vehicle (and on the spin direction). It is felt by the driver as an extra "resistance" to quick steering changes — more pronounced in heavy commercial vehicles.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A vehicle of mass 1200 kg travels at 72 km/h on a circular road of radius 80 m. The road is banked at 15°. Find:
> (a) The design speed for the given banking.
> (b) The maximum and minimum safe speeds if $\mu = 0.25$.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 1200$ kg, $v = 72$ km/h $= 20$ m/s, $r = 80$ m, $\theta = 15°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Part (a) — Design Speed:**

$$v_0 = \sqrt{rg\tan\theta} = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times \tan(15°)}$$

$$\tan(15°) = 0.2679$$

$$v_0 = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times 0.2679} = \sqrt{210.27} \approx 14.5 \text{ m/s} \approx 52.2 \text{ km/h}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Speed:**

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu\tan\theta}}$$

$$\text{Numerator term} = 0.2679 + 0.25 = 0.5179$$

$$\text{Denominator term} = 1 - 0.25 \times 0.2679 = 1 - 0.0670 = 0.9330$$

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times \frac{0.5179}{0.9330}} = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times 0.5551}$$

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{435.66} \approx 20.87 \text{ m/s} \approx 75.1 \text{ km/h}$$

**Minimum Speed:**

$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu\tan\theta}}$$

$$\text{Numerator term} = 0.2679 - 0.25 = 0.0179$$

$$\text{Denominator term} = 1 + 0.25 \times 0.2679 = 1 + 0.0670 = 1.0670$$

$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times \frac{0.0179}{1.0670}} = \sqrt{80 \times 9.81 \times 0.01678}$$

$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{13.17} \approx 3.63 \text{ m/s} \approx 13.07 \text{ km/h}$$

**Result Summary:**
- $v_0 \approx 52.2$ km/h (no-friction design speed)
- $v_{\max} \approx 75.1$ km/h (safe upper limit)
- $v_{\min} \approx 13.1$ km/h (safe lower limit)
- Since the actual speed (72 km/h) is below $v_{\max}$, the vehicle is safe on this banked road.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Slope Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle of Mass m on Incline alpha] --> B[Weight W = mg acts vertically downward]
    B --> C1[Resolve along slope]
    B --> C2[Resolve perpendicular to slope]
    C1 --> D1[W sin alpha = Gradient Resistance Fg]
    C2 --> D2[W cos alpha = Normal Load component]
    D1 --> E1[Tractive Effort must overcome Fg + F_rr + F_ad]
    D2 --> E2[Normal Reaction N = W cos alpha]
    E1 --> F1[Up-slope motion: Fg opposes motion]
    E1 --> F2[Down-slope motion: Fg assists motion]
    D1 --> G[Grade percent = tan alpha times 100]
    G --> H[Fg approx W times Grade percent divided by 100]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Banked Road Force Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle on Banked Curve radius r, speed v] --> B[Banking Angle theta]
    A --> C[Friction Coefficient mu]
    B --> D1[Normal Reaction N perpendicular to bank]
    C --> D2[Frictional Force mu N along bank]
    D1 --> E1[Resolve N into N sin theta horizontal and N cos theta vertical]
    D2 --> F1[Three cases based on tendency of skid]
    F1 --> G1[Case A: No friction - v0 equals sqrt r g tan theta]
    F1 --> G2[Case B: Tendency slide outward - v_max with mu inward]
    F1 --> G3[Case C: Tendency slide inward - v_min with mu outward]
    E1 --> H1[Horizontal equilibrium: centripetal force balance]
    E1 --> H2[Vertical equilibrium: weight balance]
    H1 --> I1[Derive tan theta equals v squared divided by r g]
    H2 --> I1
    G2 --> J1[v_max = sqrt of rg times tan theta plus mu over 1 minus mu tan theta]
    G3 --> J2[v_min = sqrt of rg times tan theta minus mu over 1 plus mu tan theta]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Gyroscopic Effect on Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spinning Mass with Ie and spin omega] --> B[Forced Precession at omega_p]
    B --> C[Gyroscopic Couple C = Ie times omega times omega_p]
    C --> D1[Pitching scenario - vehicle brakes or accelerates]
    C --> D2[Yawing scenario - vehicle turns about vertical axis]
    C --> D3[Rolling scenario - vehicle leans during cornering]
    D1 --> E1[omega_p = v over R - pitch radius]
    D1 --> F1[C_pitch = Ie times omega times v over R]
    D2 --> E2[omega_p = v over r - turn radius]
    D2 --> F2[C_yaw = Ie times omega times v over r]
    D3 --> E3[omega_p = v over r cos phi - with lean angle phi]
    D3 --> F3[C_roll = Ie times omega times v over r cos phi]
    F1 --> G1[Affects load transfer front to rear]
    F2 --> G2[Affects steering resistance and stability]
    F3 --> G3[Affects two-wheeler stability significantly]
    G1 --> H1[Two-wheeler: stability aided at high speed]
    G2 --> H2[Four-wheeler: increased steering effort at low speed]
    G3 --> H3[Two-wheeler: tendency to capsize at very low speed]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparison Table — Stability Forces at a Glance

| Direction | Cause | Force/Couple | Stability Role |
|-----------|-------|--------------|----------------|
| Longitudinal | Slope | $W \sin\alpha$ | Resists ascent / aids descent |
| Lateral | Banked curve centripetal demand | $N \sin\theta$ component | Counteracts centrifugal slip |
| Lateral | Tyre friction | $\mu N$ | Supplementary lateral grip |
| Reactive | Engine/wheel gyroscopic | $I_e \omega \omega_p$ | Resists directional change |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define gradient resistance of a vehicle. State the expression for it and explain why it is important in commercial vehicle design.**

**Model Answer:**
Gradient resistance is the component of a vehicle's weight acting along the inclined surface of a slope, opposing its motion during ascent. It is given by:

$$F_g = W \sin\alpha = mg \sin\alpha$$

where $W$ is the vehicle weight, $m$ its mass, $g$ the gravitational acceleration, and $\alpha$ the slope angle. In commercial vehicle design, gradient resistance determines the **gradeability** — the maximum slope a vehicle can climb at a given speed. Trucks and buses are rated for specific grades (e.g., 25%, 30%) because failure to overcome gradient resistance at low speeds can stall the engine or cause overheating.

---

**Q2. What is meant by "angle of banking" of a road? Why are curved roads on highways banked?**

**Model Answer:**
The angle of banking $\theta$ is the angle made by the surface of a curved road with the horizontal, such that the outer edge is raised relative to the inner edge. Curved highways are banked to:
1. Counteract the **centrifugal force** developed when vehicles negotiate the curve, by introducing an inward horizontal component of the normal reaction.
2. Allow vehicles to negotiate curves at higher **safe speeds** without depending entirely on tyre–road friction.
3. Reduce **lateral tyre wear** and improve passenger comfort by minimizing the sideways force felt by occupants.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A vehicle of mass 1000 kg is travelling up a slope of 1 in 25 (i.e., 1 m rise per 25 m run) at a constant speed of 60 km/h. The rolling resistance coefficient is 0.015 and the aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d A = 0.7$ m². Air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Calculate:
- (i) Gradient resistance.
- (ii) Rolling resistance.
- (iii) Aerodynamic drag.
- (iv) Total tractive effort required.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the angle of banking of a curved road for the design speed at which no friction is required to prevent skidding. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1000$ kg, $v = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s.
- Slope: 1 in 25 $\Rightarrow \tan\alpha = 1/25 = 0.04 \Rightarrow \alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.04) = 2.291°$.
- $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d A = 0.7$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Gradient Resistance:**

$$F_g = mg \sin\alpha$$

$$\sin(2.291°) = 0.04 \quad (\text{since } \alpha \text{ small})$$

$$F_g = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 392.4 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot mg = 0.015 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.7 \times (16.67)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.42 \times 277.89 = 116.71 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Total Tractive Effort:**

$$F_{TE} = F_g + F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 392.4 + 147.15 + 116.71 = 656.26 \text{ N}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The road is circular with constant radius $r$.
2. Vehicle moves at constant speed $v$ (no tangential acceleration).
3. Road surface is smooth — friction is neglected.
4. Vehicle treated as a point mass.
5. Banking angle is uniform throughout the curve.

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.2 above for full working)

From vertical and horizontal equilibrium of forces in the rotating frame:

$$N \cos\theta = mg, \quad N \sin\theta = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Dividing:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{rg}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{rg}\right)}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** A curved highway of radius 120 m is banked at 12°. If the coefficient of friction between tyres and road is 0.3, determine the maximum and minimum safe speeds of vehicles on this curve. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **gyroscopic effect** on a four-wheeled vehicle. Derive the expression for the gyroscopic couple during:
- (i) Pitching
- (ii) Yawing

State how each affects vehicle handling. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** $r = 120$ m, $\theta = 12°$, $\mu = 0.3$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$\tan(12°) = 0.2126$

**Maximum Speed:**

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}}$$

$$\text{Numerator term} = 0.2126 + 0.3 = 0.5126$$

$$\text{Denominator term} = 1 - 0.3 \times 0.2126 = 1 - 0.0638 = 0.9362$$

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{0.5126}{0.9362} = 0.5476$$

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{120 \times 9.81 \times 0.5476} = \sqrt{644.65} \approx 25.39 \text{ m/s} \approx 91.4 \text{ km/h}$$

**Minimum Speed:**

$$v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \frac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu \tan\theta}}$$

$$\text{Numerator term} = 0.2126 - 0.3 = -0.0874$$

Since this is **negative**, $v_{\min}$ is **imaginary**. This means that even when the vehicle is stationary, friction alone is sufficient to prevent it from sliding down the bank. In other words, **no minimum speed constraint exists** — vehicles can stop safely on this curve.

---

**Part (b):**

**Gyroscopic Effect — Concept:**
A four-wheeled vehicle contains rotating masses (engine flywheel, wheels, driveshaft) that spin at high angular velocity. When the vehicle undergoes a directional change (pitch, yaw, or roll), the spin axis of these components is forced to rotate. By Newton's laws, the rotating masses generate a **reactive gyroscopic couple** that resists the imposed motion.

**(i) Pitching:**

When the vehicle brakes or accelerates, it rotates about a lateral axis with precessional angular velocity:

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R}$$

where $R$ is the instantaneous radius of the pitch arc.

$$\boxed{C_{\text{pitch}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{R}}$$

This couple acts about a vertical axis, causing a **yaw disturbance** during heavy braking or acceleration — felt as a slight swerve in a rear-wheel-drive car under hard acceleration.

**(ii) Yawing:**

When the vehicle turns at yaw rate $\dot{\psi} = v/r$:

$$\boxed{C_{\text{yaw}} = I_e \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{v}{r}}$$

This couple acts about a **lateral axis**, causing the vehicle to resist steering changes. The effect is most noticeable in heavy vehicles (trucks, buses) at low speeds — the driver feels a "heaviness" in steering.

**Effect on Handling:**
- **At high speeds:** gyroscopic couple reduces, steering is light and responsive.
- **At low speeds with heavy flywheel:** gyroscopic couple is significant, increasing steering effort.
- This is why modern cars use **smaller, lighter flywheels** and why some high-performance vehicles mount the engine low and rearward to minimize the polar moment of inertia about the yaw axis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\sin\alpha$ with $\tan\alpha$ in slope problems:** For small angles they are nearly equal, but for steeper grades (e.g., 30°), $\sin 30° = 0.5$ while $\tan 30° = 0.577$ — a 15% error. Always check the angle first.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in banking formulas:** The $\pm\mu$ signs flip between $v_{\max}$ and $v_{\min}$. Students often copy the formula without checking the direction of friction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s:** The banking formulas require SI units. Failure to convert is the **#1 reason** students lose 2–3 marks per problem.
>
> 4. **Mis-identifying the precession axis in gyroscopic problems:** Pitch → vertical reaction couple. Yaw → lateral reaction couple. Mixing these up costs full marks on derivations.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the constraint $\tan\theta > \mu$ for $v_{\min}$:** Always comment on whether $v_{\min}$ is physically meaningful in your answer.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations: KTU examiners specifically award marks for listing assumptions (no slip, constant speed, smooth surface, point mass, etc.).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slope, Banking & Gyroscopic Effects**

- [x] Gradient resistance force: $F_g = W\sin\alpha = mg\sin\alpha$.
- [x] Grade percentage is defined as $\tan\alpha \times 100$, not $\sin\alpha \times 100$.
- [x] For small slopes, $F_g \approx W \times (\text{Grade\%}) / 100$ — engineering approximation.
- [x] Banking design speed (no friction): $v_0 = \sqrt{rg\tan\theta}$.
- [x] Maximum safe speed with friction: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \dfrac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu\tan\theta}}$.
- [x] Minimum safe speed with friction: $v_{\min} = \sqrt{rg \cdot \dfrac{\tan\theta - \mu}{1 + \mu\tan\theta}}$.
- [x] $v_{\min}$ is real **only if** $\tan\theta \geq \mu$.
- [x] Gyroscopic couple magnitude: $C = I_e \omega \omega_p$ — always three quantities multiplied.
- [x] Pitching couple: $C_p = I_e \omega (v/R)$ — direction is along vertical axis (yaw disturbance).
- [x] Yawing couple: $C_y = I_e \omega (v/r)$ — direction is along lateral axis (steering resistance).
- [x] Gyroscopic effect **aids two-wheeler stability at speed** but **resists four-wheeler steering at low speed**.
- [x] Always convert **km/h to m/s** by multiplying by $5/18$ before substituting in formulas.
- [x] Always state **assumptions** explicitly in derivation-based questions.
- [x] Remember the **right-hand rule** for gyroscopic couple direction.
- [x] For $v_{\max}$ denominator: $1 - \mu\tan\theta$ must be **positive** — else vehicle cannot corner safely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Transfer During Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Weight Transfer During Acceleration # Weight Transfer During Acceleration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > **Longitudinal Weight Transfer** (also called **Load Transfer**) is the redistribution of the vehicle's normal reaction forces between the front and rear axles caused by inertial forces acting at the **Centre of Gravity (CG)** when the vehicle is subjected to longitudinal acceleration (driving) or deceleration (braking). It is governed by Newton's Second Law of Motion and is a critical factor in determining tire grip limits, brake balance, and overall vehicle stability. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on a bus that suddenly accelerates forward. Your body instinctively leans backward, and you feel heavier on your heels. Your brain is registering the **inertia of your mass** resisting the forward motion. A car experiences the exact same physical phenomenon, but instead of a human body, it is the entire vehicle mass (concentrated at its **Centre of Gravity**) that "leans" backward. As the car accelerates forward, the rear suspension compresses (becomes loaded), while the front suspension extends (becomes unloaded). The opposite happens during hard braking — the front axle becomes "pushed into the ground," which is why nose-dive occurs, and why front brakes do most of the work. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Involved:** > - **Wheelbase (L):** Total distance between the front and rear axle contact patches (measured in **metres**). > - **Track Width (t):** Distance between the left and right wheel centres (measured in **metres**). > - **CG Height (h):** Vertical distance of the Centre of Gravity from the ground (measured in **metres**). > - **Longitudinal Acceleration (aₓ):** Measured in **m/s²** (positive during traction, negative during braking). > - **Vehicle Mass (m):** Measured in **kg**. > - **Standard Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Free Body Analysis During Acceleration When a vehicle accelerates forward with longitudinal acceleration **aₓ**, an inertial force **F = m·aₓ** acts at the CG in the opposite direction (backward) due to **D'Alembert's Principle**. This creates a moment about the contact patches that redistributes the vertical normal reactions. ### Key Logical Steps: 1. **Static Equilibrium (No Acceleration):** The front axle carries $W_f = W \cdot \frac{b}{L}$ and the rear axle carries $W_r = W \cdot \frac{a}{L}$, where **a** is the distance from the **front axle to CG** and **b** is the distance from the **CG to rear axle**. 2. **Dynamic Condition (Acceleration Applied):** The inertial force at CG creates an **overturning moment** $M = m \cdot a_x \cdot h$ about the contact patches. 3. **Redistribution:** This moment is reacted by the vertical tire forces, increasing load on one axle and decreasing it on the other. 4. **Direction of Transfer:** - **Forward Acceleration (Traction):** Weight transfers from **Front → Rear**. - **Braking (Deceleration):** Weight transfers from **Rear → Front**. 5. **Effect on Vehicle Dynamics:** - For **Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD):** Acceleration increases rear grip → improves traction. - For **Front-Wheel Drive (FWD):** Acceleration decreases front grip → potential traction limit. - **Braking:** Front grip increases → shorter stopping distances, but risk of rear-wheel lock-up and spin. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Symbol Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Longitudinal Weight Transfer (ΔWₓ) | $\Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ | $m$ = mass, $a_x$ = longitudinal accel, $h$ = CG height, $L$ = wheelbase | N (Newtons) | | Front Axle Load (Static + Dynamic) | $W_{f,dyn} = W \cdot \frac{b}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ | $b$ = distance CG to rear axle | N | | Rear Axle Load (Static + Dynamic) | $W_{r,dyn} = W \cdot \frac{a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ | $a$ = distance front axle to CG | N | | Static Front Load | $W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}$ | $W = m \cdot g$ | N | | Static Rear Load | $W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}$ | $L = a + b$ | N | | Percentage Weight Transfer | $\%\Delta W_x = \frac{a_x \cdot h}{g \cdot L} \times 100$ | expressed as percentage of total weight | % | | Acceleration Limit (No Wheel Lift) | $a_{x,max} = \frac{L \cdot g}{h}$ | theoretical wheel lift-off threshold | m/s² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > - In **Racing/F1**: Designers minimize CG height (**h**) to reduce transfer and maintain balanced handling. > - In **EV Design**: Heavy battery packs lower **h**, dramatically improving acceleration stability. > - In **Brake System Design**: Engineers use this formula to size front brake calipers (typically handle 65–75% of total braking force). > - In **Active Suspension Systems**: Real-time weight transfer calculations are used for active anti-roll control. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Longitudinal Weight Transfer Formula ### Setup and Assumptions Consider a two-axle vehicle of mass **m** with: - Front axle at origin (0, 0) - Rear axle at (L, 0) - CG located at (a, h) where **a** is the horizontal distance from the front axle and **h** is the CG height - Vehicle accelerating in positive x-direction with acceleration $a_x$ ### Step 1: Apply D'Alembert's Principle A pseudo-force equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the accelerating force acts at the CG. $$ F_{inertia} = -m \cdot a_x $$ This inertial force acts **horizontally backward** at height **h** above the ground. ### Step 2: Sum of Forces in Vertical Direction The vertical equilibrium gives us: $$ W_{f,dyn} + W_{r,dyn} = m \cdot g $$ where $W_{f,dyn}$ and $W_{r,dyn}$ are the dynamic normal reactions at front and rear axles. ### Step 3: Take Moments About the Front Axle Contact Patch To eliminate the unknown front reaction, we sum moments about the front contact point. The CG is at horizontal distance **a** from the front axle. $$ \begin{aligned} \sum M_{front} &= 0 \\ W_{r,dyn} \cdot L - (m \cdot g) \cdot a - (m \cdot a_x) \cdot h &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation of each term:** - $W_{r,dyn} \cdot L$: Moment arm of the rear vertical reaction about the front contact patch. - $(m \cdot g) \cdot a$: Moment of the gravitational weight acting downward at the CG, with horizontal lever arm **a**. - $(m \cdot a_x) \cdot h$: Moment of the inertial force acting at CG height **h**, tending to overturn the vehicle backward (toward the rear during forward acceleration). ### Step 4: Solve for Rear Dynamic Load $$ \begin{aligned} W_{r,dyn} \cdot L &= m \cdot g \cdot a + m \cdot a_x \cdot h \\ W_{r,dyn} &= \frac{m \cdot g \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \end{aligned} $$ We recognize the first term as the **static rear load** $W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}$, and the second term as the **dynamic weight transfer** $\Delta W_x$. $$ \boxed{W_{r,dyn} = W \cdot \frac{a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}} $$ ### Step 5: Solve for Front Dynamic Load Using Vertical Equilibrium $$ \begin{aligned} W_{f,dyn} &= m \cdot g - W_{r,dyn} \\ W_{f,dyn} &= m \cdot g - \frac{m \cdot g \cdot a}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \\ W_{f,dyn} &= \frac{m \cdot g \cdot (L - a)}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \end{aligned} $$ Since $L - a = b$: $$ \boxed{W_{f,dyn} = W \cdot \frac{b}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}} $$ ### Step 6: Isolate the Weight Transfer Term The change in axle load compared to the static case is: $$ \Delta W_x = W_{r,dyn} - W_r = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} $$ This confirms the **fundamental longitudinal weight transfer equation**: $$ \boxed{\Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}} $$ ### Step 7: Derive the Wheel Lift-Off Limit For the front wheels to just lose contact with the ground ($W_{f,dyn} = 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} W \cdot \frac{b}{L} &= \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \\ m \cdot g \cdot \frac{b}{L} &= \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \\ a_{x,max} &= \frac{b \cdot g}{h} \end{aligned} $$ For a symmetrically loaded vehicle where $a = b = L/2$: $$ \boxed{a_{x,max} = \frac{L \cdot g}{2h}} $$ ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example **Problem Statement:** A passenger car has the following parameters: - Total Mass: $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ - Wheelbase: $L = 2.6 \text{ m}$ - CG Height: $h = 0.55 \text{ m}$ - Distance from front axle to CG: $a = 1.3 \text{ m}$ - Distance from CG to rear axle: $b = 1.3 \text{ m}$ - Acceleration: $a_x = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$ (forward traction) **Find:** (i) Static load on each axle, (ii) Dynamic weight transfer, (iii) Dynamic load on each axle. ### Solution **Step 1: Calculate Total Weight** $$ W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Static Loads** $$ W_f = W \cdot \frac{b}{L} = 14715 \times \frac{1.3}{2.6} = 7357.5 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_r = W \cdot \frac{a}{L} = 14715 \times \frac{1.3}{2.6} = 7357.5 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Dynamic Weight Transfer** $$ \Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} = \frac{1500 \times 3 \times 0.55}{2.6} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta W_x = 952.27 \text{ N}} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Dynamic Axle Loads** $$ W_{f,dyn} = 7357.5 - 952.27 = 6405.23 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_{r,dyn} = 7357.5 + 952.27 = 8309.77 \text{ N} $$ **Observation:** A modest acceleration of just **3 m/s²** (≈ 0.3 g) causes a weight transfer of nearly **1000 N** — about **6.5%** of the total vehicle weight. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Force Diagram During Acceleration (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Vehicle["VEHICLE BODY"] direction TB CG["CENTRE OF GRAVITY
Mass m at height h
Inertial Force m*a acts horizontally backward"] MassArrow["Inertial Force
F_inertia = m * a_x
Direction: BACKWARD"] end subgraph FrontAxle["FRONT AXLE REGION"] direction TB F1["Normal Reaction Wf
DECREASES during accel
Front unloads"] F1Vec["Reduced Vertical Load
Wf_dyn = Wf - deltaW"] end subgraph RearAxle["REAR AXLE REGION"] direction TB F2["Normal Reaction Wr
INCREASES during accel
Rear loads up"] F2Vec["Increased Vertical Load
Wr_dyn = Wr + deltaW"] end subgraph Ground["GROUND CONTACT PATCHES"] direction TB PatchF["Front Tire Contact
Wheelbase L"] PatchR["Rear Tire Contact
Wheelbase L"] end CG -- "Height h
Moment arm" --> MassArrow MassArrow --> F1Vec MassArrow --> F2Vec F1Vec --> PatchF F2Vec --> PatchR F1 --> F1Vec F2 --> F2Vec style Vehicle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px style FrontAxle fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px style RearAxle fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style Ground fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px style CG fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style MassArrow fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Load Redistribution Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STATIC CONDITION
Vehicle at Rest
Wf = W x b divided by L
Wr = W x a divided by L"] --> B{"Acceleration
Applied?"} B -- "Forward Acceleration
a_x positive" --> C["Longitudinal Force
F = m x a_x at CG"] B -- "Braking Deceleration
a_x negative" --> D["Deceleration Force
F = m x a_x at CG"] C --> E["Inertial Force
Acts BACKWARD
at height h"] D --> F["Inertial Force
Acts FORWARD
at height h"] E --> G["Moment = m x a_x x h
about contact patches"] F --> G G --> H["Weight Transfer
deltaW = m x a_x x h divided by L"] H --> I["REAR LOAD INCREASES
during acceleration"] H --> J["FRONT LOAD INCREASES
during braking"] I --> K["Rear tires gain grip
Better traction"] J --> L["Front tires gain grip
Shorter stopping distance"] style A fill:#e3f2fd style B fill:#fff9c4 style C fill:#c8e6c9 style D fill:#ffcdd2 style G fill:#ffe0b2 style H fill:#f8bbd0 style I fill:#a5d6a7 style J fill:#ef9a9a ``` ## 4.3 Free Body Diagram (Vehicle Side View Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VehicleFrame["FREE BODY DIAGRAM - SIDE VIEW"] direction LR subgraph FrontAxlePoint[" "] FContact["FRONT CONTACT
PATCH"] end subgraph CGLocation[" "] CGMass["CG
Location
a from front
h above ground"] WeightVec["Weight W = mg
Acts DOWNWARD"] InertiaVec["Inertia m*a_x
Acts HORIZONTAL"] end subgraph RearAxlePoint[" "] RContact["REAR CONTACT
PATCH"] end FContact ---|"Distance L"| RContact FContact ---|"Distance a"| CGMass CGMass ---|"Distance b"| RContact CGMass -.-> WeightVec CGMass -.-> InertiaVec end style VehicleFrame fill:#fafafa,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:2px style FrontAxlePoint fill:#ffebee style RearAxlePoint fill:#e8f5e9 style CGLocation fill:#fff8e1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Conceptual Definition [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** Define **longitudinal weight transfer** in a vehicle. State the formula for weight transfer during acceleration and explain the role of Centre of Gravity height. **Model Answer:** Longitudinal weight transfer refers to the redistribution of vertical normal reaction forces between the front and rear axles when a vehicle undergoes longitudinal acceleration or deceleration. It occurs due to the inertia of the vehicle mass concentrated at the Centre of Gravity (CG). The formula is: $$ \Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} $$ where **m** is the vehicle mass, **aₓ** is the longitudinal acceleration, **h** is the CG height, and **L** is the wheelbase. **Role of CG Height:** CG height **h** appears directly in the numerator. A higher CG results in **greater weight transfer** for the same acceleration, leading to compromised handling. Lowering CG (as in sports cars or EVs with floor-mounted batteries) reduces weight transfer and improves stability. --- ### Question 2: Conceptual Application [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q:** Why does the front axle of a braking car get loaded heavily compared to the rear axle? Explain with reference to weight transfer. **Model Answer:** During braking, the vehicle decelerates. By D'Alembert's principle, an inertial force equal to $m \cdot a_x$ acts **forward** at the CG height **h**. This creates an overturning moment $m \cdot a_x \cdot h$ about the contact patches. This moment causes additional vertical load to transfer to the **front axle**, increasing the front normal reaction. Consequently, the front tires gain increased grip (proportional to vertical load), allowing them to generate larger braking forces without locking. This is why **front brakes are designed larger** and handle approximately 65–75% of total braking effort in modern passenger cars. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to **square or multiply** by CG height **h** — they write $\Delta W = m \cdot a / L$ without the **h** factor. This loses **3 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Sign convention is critical. Mark weight transfer **positive** when moving from front to rear (during acceleration) and **negative** during braking. Unclear signs cost **2 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Many students confuse **static** and **dynamic** loads. Always state both clearly in your answer. > - **Common Mistake 4:** In derivations, forgetting to mention **D'Alembert's Principle** or the **moment about contact patches** loses critical **process marks**. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** A passenger car has the following specifications: - Total mass $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$ - Wheelbase $L = 2.7 \text{ m}$ - CG height $h = 0.6 \text{ m}$ - Distance from front axle to CG = $1.4 \text{ m}$ - Distance from CG to rear axle = $1.3 \text{ m}$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for longitudinal weight transfer during acceleration. Calculate the static and dynamic loads on each axle when the vehicle accelerates at $4 \text{ m/s}^2$. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the vehicle is rear-wheel drive and the maximum coefficient of friction between tire and road is $\mu = 0.85$, determine the maximum tractive acceleration possible without wheel spin. Also comment on whether the rear wheels lift off. ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) Solution: **Step 1: State the formula derivation approach** Apply D'Alembert's principle. During acceleration $a_x$, an inertial force $m \cdot a_x$ acts backward at CG height $h$. **Step 2: Take moments about the rear contact patch to find $W_{f,dyn}$** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum M_{rear} &= 0 \\ W_{f,dyn} \cdot L - W \cdot b + m \cdot a_x \cdot h &= 0 \\ W_{f,dyn} &= \frac{W \cdot b}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Take moments about the front contact patch to find $W_{r,dyn}$** $$ W_{r,dyn} = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} $$ **Step 4: Isolate the weight transfer term** $$ \Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} $$ **Step 5: Substitute numerical values** $$ W = 1800 \times 9.81 = 17658 \text{ N} $$ Static loads: $$ W_f = 17658 \times \frac{1.3}{2.7} = 8502.67 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_r = 17658 \times \frac{1.4}{2.7} = 9155.33 \text{ N} $$ Dynamic weight transfer: $$ \Delta W_x = \frac{1800 \times 4 \times 0.6}{2.7} = 1600 \text{ N} $$ Dynamic loads: $$ \boxed{W_{f,dyn} = 8502.67 - 1600 = 6902.67 \text{ N}} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{r,dyn} = 9155.33 + 1600 = 10755.33 \text{ N}} $$ #### Part (b) Solution: **Step 1: Calculate maximum tractive force available at rear wheels** Maximum traction force occurs when rear tires operate at the friction limit: $$ F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot W_{r,dyn} = \mu \cdot \left( W \cdot \frac{a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} \right) $$ **Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law for acceleration limit** The traction force equals $m \cdot a_x$: $$ m \cdot a_{x,max} = \mu \cdot \left( W \cdot \frac{a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_{x,max} \cdot h}{L} \right) $$ **Step 3: Solve for $a_{x,max}$** $$ \begin{aligned} m \cdot a_{x,max} &= \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{a}{L} + \frac{\mu \cdot m \cdot a_{x,max} \cdot h}{L} \\ m \cdot a_{x,max} - \frac{\mu \cdot m \cdot a_{x,max} \cdot h}{L} &= \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{a}{L} \\ m \cdot a_{x,max} \left(1 - \frac{\mu \cdot h}{L}\right) &= \frac{\mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot a}{L} \\ a_{x,max} &= \frac{\mu \cdot g \cdot a}{L - \mu \cdot h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Substitute values** $$ a_{x,max} = \frac{0.85 \times 9.81 \times 1.4}{2.7 - 0.85 \times 0.6} $$ $$ a_{x,max} = \frac{11.6739}{2.7 - 0.51} = \frac{11.6739}{2.19} $$ $$ \boxed{a_{x,max} = 5.33 \text{ m/s}^2} $$ **Step 5: Check wheel lift-off condition** For front wheels to lose contact: $$ a_{x,lift} = \frac{b \cdot g}{h} = \frac{1.3 \times 9.81}{0.6} = 21.255 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ Since $a_{x,max} = 5.33 \text{ m/s}^2 \ll 21.255 \text{ m/s}^2$, **no wheel lift-off occurs**. The vehicle is traction-limited, not stability-limited. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q:** A racing car is tested on a straight track. The vehicle data is: - Mass $m = 1200 \text{ kg}$ - Wheelbase $L = 2.4 \text{ m}$ - CG height $h = 0.35 \text{ m}$ - CG location: $a = 1.1 \text{ m}$ from front, $b = 1.3 \text{ m}$ from rear - Tire-road friction coefficient $\mu = 1.1$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the longitudinal weight transfer when the car decelerates at $8 \text{ m/s}^2$. Find the dynamic front and rear axle loads. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss how lowering the CG by 0.1 m (using a redesigned chassis) affects the weight transfer and maximum deceleration. Compute the new values. ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) Solution: **Step 1: Identify the deceleration scenario** When decelerating at $8 \text{ m/s}^2$, treat $a_x = -8 \text{ m/s}^2$. The magnitude of weight transfer is: $$ \Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L} = \frac{1200 \times 8 \times 0.35}{2.4} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta W_x = 1400 \text{ N}} $$ **Step 2: Calculate static loads** $$ W = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_f = 11772 \times \frac{1.3}{2.4} = 6376.5 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_r = 11772 \times \frac{1.1}{2.4} = 5395.5 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3: Calculate dynamic loads during deceleration** During braking, weight transfers from rear to front: $$ W_{f,dyn} = W_f + \Delta W_x = 6376.5 + 1400 = 7776.5 \text{ N} $$ $$ W_{r,dyn} = W_r - \Delta W_x = 5395.5 - 1400 = 3995.5 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{f,dyn} = 7776.5 \text{ N}, \quad W_{r,dyn} = 3995.5 \text{ N}} $$ #### Part (b) Solution: **Step 1: New CG Height** $$ h_{new} = 0.35 - 0.1 = 0.25 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: New weight transfer at same deceleration** $$ \Delta W_{x,new} = \frac{1200 \times 8 \times 0.25}{2.4} = 1000 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3: Reduction in transfer** $$ \text{Reduction} = 1400 - 1000 = 400 \text{ N} \quad (\approx 28.6\% \text{ decrease}) $$ **Step 4: Maximum deceleration limit** Maximum deceleration is limited by tire friction. The maximum achievable deceleration before lock-up occurs when both axles are saturated: $$ a_{x,max} = \mu \cdot g = 1.1 \times 9.81 = 10.79 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ However, with proper brake bias (more force on front), the achievable deceleration in practice is: $$ a_{x,max} \approx 0.85 \cdot \mu \cdot g = 0.85 \times 10.79 = 9.17 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ **Step 5: Practical observation** Lowering CG by **0.1 m** reduces weight transfer by **400 N** (a **28.6% improvement**). This results in: - More balanced brake utilization between front and rear axles. - Reduced risk of **rear wheel lock-up** and skid. - Improved handling consistency during threshold braking. - A typical reason why **Formula 1 cars** maintain extremely low CG heights (around **200–300 mm**). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - **Weight Transfer** = Redistribution of vertical tire loads between axles during longitudinal acceleration. - Caused by **inertia forces** acting at the **Centre of Gravity (CG)**. - **Direction:** Forward acceleration = transfer to rear; Braking = transfer to front. ### 📐 Must-Know Formulas - **Weight Transfer:** $\Delta W_x = \dfrac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ - **Rear Dynamic Load:** $W_{r,dyn} = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{L} + \dfrac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ - **Front Dynamic Load:** $W_{f,dyn} = \dfrac{W \cdot b}{L} - \dfrac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$ - **Wheel Lift-Off Limit:** $a_{x,max} = \dfrac{L \cdot g}{2h}$ (for symmetric CG) ### 🎯 High-Yield Points for KTU Exams 1. Always apply **D'Alembert's Principle** to introduce the inertial force at CG. 2. **Sign convention:** Forward acceleration is positive; braking is negative. 3. **Wheelbase (L)** is in the **denominator** — longer wheelbase = less transfer. 4. **CG height (h)** is in the **numerator** — lower CG = better stability. 5. For **RWD vehicles**, acceleration improves rear grip (self-loading). 6. For **FWD vehicles**, acceleration reduces front grip (traction-limited). 7. **EVs** have naturally low CG due to floor-mounted battery packs, giving them exceptional acceleration stability. ### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls - Forgetting to include **h** in the formula. - Mixing up **static vs dynamic** loads. - Not specifying units (N vs kg). - Confusing **a** (distance) with **a** (acceleration) — use different notation in derivations. - Forgetting that $W = m \cdot g$, not just $m$. ### 🏎️ Real-World Applications - **Racing:** Lowest possible CG, optimal brake bias. - **EV Design:** Floor-mounted battery pack for stability. - **Anti-squat/Dive Geometry:** Suspension design to counteract transfer. - **Active Suspension:** Real-time load transfer compensation.

topics.title: Cross Wind Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross Wind Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cross Wind Handling** is the study and analysis of a vehicle's dynamic response (both transient and steady-state) when subjected to a lateral aerodynamic disturbance — a wind component blowing perpendicular to the direction of vehicle travel — together with the steering strategies and design features required to maintain directional stability, course-keeping accuracy, and driver comfort.

The aerodynamic disturbance produces two primary external actions on the vehicle:

1. An **aerodynamic side force** ($F_{sa}$) acting laterally through the **centre of pressure (COP)** of the body.
2. An **aerodynamic yawing moment** ($N_a$) about the vehicle's vertical axis, since the COP generally does not coincide with the centre of gravity (CG).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Cross wind handling* refers to the directional and yawing response of a road vehicle to lateral wind components, quantified by the **crosswind sensitivity coefficient**, side-slip deviation, and the corrective steering input required to maintain the desired path.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine cycling on a calm day versus cycling during a strong side-breeze. The bicycle is pulled sideways and tends to "weave" off your intended straight line. To stay on course, you instinctively turn the handlebars slightly **into** the wind. A car experiences exactly the same physics — except the magnitudes scale with the **square of velocity**, so at highway speeds even a mild crosswind can produce a significant lateral push.

Two everyday scenarios illustrate the engineering relevance:

- **High-speed trucks and buses** with large, flat side panels behave like "sails" and are notoriously unstable in crosswind.
- **Lightweight trailers and motorhomes** have a high **crosswind sensitivity** because of their small restoring lateral stiffness and rearward centre of pressure.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following quantities form the foundation of cross wind analysis. They are rendered in **bold** because they are high-yield KTU items:

- **Air density:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ (ISA, sea level)
- **Frontal projected area of the vehicle:** $A \; [\mathbf{m^2}]$
- **Vehicle forward speed:** $V \; [\mathbf{m/s}]$
- **Crosswind speed (lateral component):** $V_w \; [\mathbf{m/s}]$
- **Resultant (relative) wind speed:** $V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_w^2} \; [\mathbf{m/s}]$
- **Aerodynamic side-force coefficient:** $C_{sa}$ (dimensionless, typically $C_{sa} \approx 0.5$ to $1.5$ for passenger cars)
- **Aerodynamic yawing-moment coefficient:** $C_{na}$ (dimensionless, sign convention positive nose-left)

> [!TIP]
> **Why does $V_r$ use a square root?** Because vehicle velocity ($\vec{V}$) and crosswind velocity ($\vec{V}_w$) are perpendicular vectors. The Pythagorean theorem gives the magnitude of their sum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Problem

A cross wind handling analysis is conventionally split into two layers:

1. **Aerodynamic layer** — computes the resultant side force $F_{sa}$ and yawing moment $N_a$ from wind-body interaction.
2. **Vehicle dynamics layer** — feeds these aerodynamic inputs into the **bicycle model** (2-DOF yaw–sideslip model) to obtain the vehicle's response $\beta(t)$, $r(t)$, and the required corrective steering $\delta(t)$.

### 2.2 Logical Steps (Bullet Flow)

**Step 1 — Vector composition of velocities**

The relative wind velocity seen by the moving vehicle is the vector sum of the vehicle velocity and the (opposite) wind velocity:

$$\vec{V}_r = \vec{V} + \vec{V}_w$$

Choosing the vehicle-fixed $x$-axis forward and $y$-axis leftward:
- $V_{rx} = V$
- $V_{ry} = V_w$
- $V_r = \sqrt{V^2 + V_w^2}$
- $\psi = \arctan\!\left(V_w \,/\, V\right)$ — *wind direction angle* relative to the vehicle longitudinal axis.

**Step 2 — Side force from crosswind**

$$F_{sa} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{sa}\, A\, V_r^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Yawing moment from crosswind**

$$N_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{na}\, A\, l\, V_r^{2}$$

where $l$ is the wheelbase (or any chosen reference length used during wind-tunnel testing).

**Step 4 — Bicycle-model equations of motion under crosswind**

Let $\beta$ = sideslip angle at CG, $r$ = yaw rate, $\delta$ = front-wheel steering angle, $a, b$ = CG-to-front-axle and CG-to-rear-axle distances, $m$ = mass, $I_z$ = yaw inertia.

$$m V (\dot{\beta} + r) = Y_{\beta}\,\beta + Y_r\,r + Y_\delta\,\delta + F_{sa}$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = N_\beta\,\beta + N_r\,r + N_\delta\,\delta + N_a$$

The derivatives $Y_\beta, Y_r, Y_\delta, N_\beta, N_r, N_\delta$ are the standard **stability derivatives** of the linearised bicycle model.

**Step 5 — Steady-state (path-holding) response**

If the driver corrects steering to hold a constant heading ($\beta = $ const, $r = 0$, $\dot{r} = 0$), the corrective steering angle is found by setting $N_\delta\,\delta_{ss} + N_a = 0$:

$$\delta_{ss} = -\,\frac{N_a}{N_\delta} = -\,\frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{na}\, A\, l\, V_r^{2}}{N_\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Quasi-static sideslip deviation**

If no steering correction is applied, the steady-state sideslip angle becomes:

$$\beta_{ss} = -\,\frac{F_{sa}}{Y_\beta + \frac{N_\beta}{V}} \quad \text{(quasi-static form)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative wind speed | $V_r = \sqrt{V^{2} + V_w^{2}}$ | m/s | Pythagoras (vectors are perpendicular) |
| 2 | Wind direction angle | $\psi = \arctan(V_w/V)$ | rad | Angle of $\vec{V}_r$ w.r.t. vehicle $x$-axis |
| 3 | Aerodynamic side force | $F_{sa} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{sa} A V_r^{2}$ | N | Pushes vehicle laterally |
| 4 | Aerodynamic yawing moment | $N_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{na} A l V_r^{2}$ | N·m | Tends to rotate vehicle |
| 5 | Steady-state corrective steer | $\delta_{ss} = -N_a / N_\delta$ | rad | Steering into wind to balance moment |
| 6 | Quasi-static sideslip | $\beta_{ss} = -F_{sa} / Y_\beta$ | rad | For $V$ fixed, small angles |
| 7 | Crosswind sensitivity | $S = \partial \beta / \partial V_w \approx \dfrac{\rho C_{sa} A V}{Y_\beta}$ | rad·s/m | Higher → worse handling |
| 8 | Critical crosswind speed | $V_{w,crit}$ from $V_r = V_{max}$ | m/s | Design limit for high-speed vehicles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (Real-World Utility)**
> Cross wind handling analysis is mandatory in the homologation of buses, trucks, and high-speed trains in Europe (ECE R107, EN 14067). It determines the maximum permissible operating speed on exposed routes and informs the design of **spoilers, side-skirts, vortex generators, and yaw-damping control systems**.

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter
