KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define the term **Rolling Moment** in vehicle dynamics. State the formula for rolling moment and explain the significance of CG height in determining it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Rolling moment is the moment generated about the **longitudinal roll axis** of a vehicle when it undergoes lateral acceleration during cornering. It is the product of the lateral inertia force and the height of the center of gravity from the ground. $$M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}$$ **Significance of CG height:** The CG height appears linearly in the rolling moment formula. A **higher CG** produces a **larger rolling moment** for the same lateral acceleration, leading to greater load transfer, more body roll, and a higher risk of rollover. This is why SUVs and trucks (with high CG) are more rollover-prone than low-slung sports cars. **(3 marks: 1 def + 1 formula + 1 significance)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is **Static Stability Factor (SSF)**? How is it related to the rollover threshold of a vehicle? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Static Stability Factor is a dimensionless geometric parameter that indicates a vehicle's inherent resistance to rollover. It is defined as: $$SSF = \frac{T}{2 \cdot h_{cg}}$$ Where $T$ is the track width and $h_{cg}$ is the CG height. **Relation to rollover threshold:** The vehicle rolls over when the lateral acceleration exceeds the critical value: $$a_{y,\text{crit}} = SSF \cdot g$$ A **higher SSF** indicates a **more rollover-resistant** vehicle. NHTSA uses SSF as the basis for its 5-star rollover safety rating. Vehicles with SSF > 1.5 are generally considered safe; those with SSF < 1.2 are flagged as high rollover risk. **(3 marks: 1 def + 1 formula + 1 relation)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module Choice):** In Part B, students must answer **one full question** from a choice of two. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Anti-Roll Bar and Roll Stiffness** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the **contribution of an anti-roll bar** to the total roll stiffness of an axle. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A passenger car has the following data: - Sprung mass $m = 1400$ kg - CG height $h_{cg} = 0.5$ m - Track width $T = 1.5$ m - Front spring roll stiffness $K_{\phi,F,\text{spring}} = 18000$ N·m/rad - Rear spring roll stiffness $K_{\phi,R,\text{spring}} = 22000$ N·m/rad - An anti-roll bar is fitted at the front with $k_{\text{bar}} = 40000$ N·m/rad and lever arm $b = 0.3$ m Calculate: 1. The total front and rear roll stiffness 2. The roll moment distribution between front and rear axles when the vehicle experiences a lateral acceleration of 0.4 g 3. The body roll angle **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **(a) Derivation of Anti-Roll Bar Contribution (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** - Anti-roll bar is a linear torsion spring - Bar connects both wheels rigidly through lever arms - Sprung mass is rigid - Small roll angles (linear regime) **Derivation:** When the body rolls by an angle $\phi$ about the roll axis, the outer wheel moves up by $z_o = \phi \cdot T/2$ and the inner wheel moves down by the same amount (where $T$ is the full track width). The lever arm of length $b$ transmits this displacement to the bar as a twist: $$\theta_{\text{bar}} = \frac{z_o}{b} = \frac{\phi \cdot T}{2 b}$$ The torque in the bar is: $$T_{\text{bar}} = k_{\text{bar}} \cdot \theta_{\text{bar}} = k_{\text{bar}} \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot T}{2 b}$$ This torque is reacted at both lever arms, creating equal and opposite vertical forces at the wheels: $$F_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{T_{\text{bar}}}{b} = \frac{k_{\text{bar}} \cdot \phi \cdot T}{2 b^2}$$ The restoring moment of these two forces about the roll axis (each with moment arm $T/2$) is: $$M_{\text{bar}} = 2 \cdot F_{\text{wheel}} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = F_{\text{bar}} \cdot T$$ $$M_{\text{bar}} = \frac{k_{\text{bar}} \cdot \phi \cdot T^2}{2 b^2}$$ Comparing with $M_{\text{bar}} = K_{\phi,\text{bar}} \cdot \phi$: $$\boxed{K_{\phi,\text{bar}} = \frac{k_{\text{bar}} \cdot T^2}{2 b^2}}$$ **Award full marks: 1 for assumptions + 4 for derivation steps + 1 for free body + 1 for final boxed answer.** --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Total roll stiffness:** Front anti-roll bar contribution: $$K_{\phi,F,\text{bar}} = \frac{k_{\text{bar}} \cdot T^2}{2 b^2} = \frac{40000 \cdot (1.5)^2}{2 \cdot (0.3)^2} = \frac{40000 \cdot 2.25}{0.18} = 500000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$ Total front roll stiffness: $$K_{\phi,F} = K_{\phi,F,\text{spring}} + K_{\phi,F,\text{bar}} = 18000 + 500000 = 518000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$ Total rear roll stiffness: $$K_{\phi,R} = K_{\phi,R,\text{spring}} = 22000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$ **Step 2 — Roll moment distribution:** Total roll stiffness: $$K_{\phi,\text{total}} = K_{\phi,F} + K_{\phi,R} = 518000 + 22000 = 540000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$ Lateral acceleration: $a_y = 0.4 \cdot 9.81 = 3.924$ m/s² Total rolling moment: $$M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg} = 1400 \cdot 3.924 \cdot 0.5 = 2746.8 \text{ N·m}$$ Front axle roll moment: $$M_F = \frac{K_{\phi,F}}{K_{\phi,\text{total}}} \cdot M_{roll} = \frac{518000}{540000} \cdot 2746.8 = 2634.8 \text{ N·m}$$ Rear axle roll moment: $$M_R = \frac{K_{\phi,R}}{K_{\phi,\text{total}}} \cdot M_{roll} = \frac{22000}{540000} \cdot 2746.8 = 111.9 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Body roll angle:** $$\phi = \frac{M_{roll}}{K_{\phi,\text{total}}} = \frac{2746.8}{540000} = 0.00509 \text{ rad} = 0.291°$$ **Award full marks: 1 for step 1 setup + 2 for calculations + 1 for step 2 + 2 for step 3 + 1 for units/final answer.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Rollover Threshold and Load Transfer** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With the aid of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for **load transfer** from inner to outer wheels during cornering. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sports car has the following specifications: - Total mass $m = 1200$ kg - Mass distribution: 55% front, 45% rear - CG height $h_{cg} = 0.45$ m - Wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m - Track width (front and rear) $T = 1.55$ m - The car negotiates a curve of radius $R = 50$ m at a speed of $V = 90$ km/h Calculate: 1. The lateral acceleration developed 2. The total rolling moment 3. The load transfer at front and rear axles 4. Whether the vehicle will roll over. If the CG height were raised to 0.7 m, what would be the new rollover speed on the same curve? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **(a) Derivation of Load Transfer (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** - Steady-state cornering - Symmetric vehicle - Rigid body (no suspension deflection for the moment) - Small lateral accelerations (no tire saturation) **Free Body Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — front view of vehicle)* **Derivation:** Let: - $W = m \cdot g$ = Total weight - $T$ = Track width (full) - $F_y = m \cdot a_y$ = Centrifugal force at CG - $h_{cg}$ = CG height - $W_o$ = Outer wheel vertical reaction - $W_i$ = Inner wheel vertical reaction Vertical force equilibrium: $$W_o + W_i = m \cdot g \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Taking moments about the **inner wheel contact patch** (clockwise positive, considering left turn): $$W_o \cdot T = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{T}{2} + m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}$$ Solving for $W_o$: $$W_o = \frac{m \cdot g}{2} + \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}}{T}$$ From equation (1): $W_i = m \cdot g - W_o$ $$W_i = \frac{m \cdot g}{2} - \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}}{T}$$ **Load transfer** (per axle): $$\boxed{\Delta W = W_o - W_i = \frac{2 \cdot m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}}{T}}$$ > **Award: 1 for FBD + 1 for vertical eq + 2 for moment eq + 1 for solving + 1 for final + 1 for units discussion** --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Lateral acceleration:** Convert speed: $V = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s $$a_y = \frac{V^2}{R} = \frac{25^2}{50} = \frac{625}{50} = 12.5 \text{ m/s}^2$$ In g's: $a_y = 12.5 / 9.81 = 1.274$ g **Step 2 — Total rolling moment:** $$M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg} = 1200 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 0.45 = 6750 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Load transfer at each axle:** Using the same formula for each axle (assuming equal distribution of mass effects): $$\Delta W_{\text{front}} = \frac{m_f \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}}{T} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 1200 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 0.45}{1.55} = 2397.6 \text{ N}$$ $$\Delta W_{\text{rear}} = \frac{m_r \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}}{T} = \frac{0.45 \cdot 1200 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 0.45}{1.55} = 1961.7 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Rollover check:** SSF for original CG: $$SSF = \frac{T}{2 h_{cg}} = \frac{1.55}{2 \cdot 0.45} = 1.722$$ Critical acceleration: $$a_{y,\text{crit}} = SSF \cdot g = 1.722 \cdot 9.81 = 16.89 \text{ m/s}^2$$ Since $a_y = 12.5 < 16.89$ m/s² → **Vehicle will NOT roll over.** ✓ **New rollover speed with $h_{cg} = 0.7$ m:** New SSF: $$SSF_{\text{new}} = \frac{1.55}{2 \cdot 0.7} = 1.107$$ New critical acceleration: $$a_{y,\text{crit,new}} = 1.107 \cdot 9.81 = 10.86 \text{ m/s}^2$$ Critical speed on the same curve: $$V_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{a_{y,\text{crit,new}} \cdot R} = \sqrt{10.86 \cdot 50} = \sqrt{543} = 23.3 \text{ m/s} = 83.9 \text{ km/h}$$ > **Conclusion:** Raising CG from 0.45 m to 0.7 m reduces the rollover speed from well above 90 km/h (safe) to **83.9 km/h** — meaning the vehicle would roll over at the current operating speed of 90 km/h! This is why **CG height is the single most critical parameter** in rollover safety. **Award: 1 (step 1) + 1 (step 2) + 2 (step 3) + 3 (step 4 with rollover analysis) = 7 marks** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Rolling Moment Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > > 1. **Track width confusion (–2 marks):** Using $T/2$ (half track) instead of full $T$ in the load transfer formula, or vice versa. **Always re-read the variable definitions in the question.** > > 2. **Forgetting $h_{cg}$ (–2 marks):** Some students use $M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y$ without $h_{cg}$. The unit would be Newtons, not N·m — an immediate red flag for the examiner. > > 3. **Not showing moment direction (–1 mark):** In derivations, students must clearly state the pivot point and indicate clockwise/counter-clockwise convention. > > 4. **Confusing roll stiffness with spring rate (–2 marks):** $K_\phi$ (N·m/rad) is the **rotational** stiffness. $k$ (N/m) is the **translational** spring rate. They are related by $K_\phi = k \cdot T^2 / 2$, but they are not the same quantity. > > 5. **Missing the "engineering interpretation" sentence (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 scheme values **explanation of physical significance**. A derivation without a concluding statement ("This shows that... / This means in practice...") loses 1 mark per question. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rolling Moment (Module 4)** - 🔹 **Definition:** Rolling moment is the **moment about the longitudinal roll axis** caused by lateral acceleration; magnitude $M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}$. - 🔹 **Lateral acceleration:** $a_y = V^2 / R$ in steady-state cornering. - 🔹 **Load transfer per axle:** $\Delta W = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg} / T$ — outer wheel gains, inner wheel loses. - 🔹 **Roll angle:** $\phi = M_{roll} / K_\phi = (z_o - z_i) / T$. - 🔹 **Anti-roll bar stiffness:** $K_{\phi,\text{bar}} = k_{\text{bar}} \cdot T^2 / (2 b^2)$. - 🔹 **Total roll stiffness:** $K_{\phi,\text{total}} = K_{\phi,\text{springs}} + K_{\phi,\text{bar}} + K_{\phi,\text{bush}}$. - 🔹 **Static Stability Factor:** $SSF = T / (2 h_{cg})$ — higher SSF = safer. - 🔹 **Rollover condition:** $a_y \geq SSF \cdot g$ → vehicle tips over. - 🔹 **CG height is the #1 design lever:** Lowering $h_{cg}$ by 10% reduces roll moment by ~10% and increases rollover threshold significantly. - 🔹 **Units matter:** $M$ in N·m, $a_y$ in m/s², $K_\phi$ in N·m/rad, $h$ in m. - 🔹 **Anti-roll bars do not affect bounce:** They only resist **differential** wheel motion, hence adding roll stiffness without affecting ride comfort in heave. - 🔹 **KTU favorite derivation:** Load transfer from first principles (force balance + moment balance). - 🔹 **Real-world application:** Rollover rating (NHTSA), suspension tuning (motorsport), ESC (Electronic Stability Control) activation threshold. > **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Rolling moment is a **high-weightage, frequently-tested topic** in Module 4. Mastering the four derivations in Section 3 and the formula sheet in Section 2.5 will secure full marks in any variation of ESE questions. Always **end with a physical interpretation sentence** — it's a guaranteed +1 mark trick that distinguishes toppers from average performers.

topics.title: Cross Wind Sensitivity
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT602 - VEHICLE STABILITY AND DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cross Wind Sensitivity

---

# Cross Wind Sensitivity – KTU Premier Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cross Wind Sensitivity** is a quantitative measure of a vehicle's aerodynamic response to a lateral (sideways) wind disturbance. It is formally defined as the ratio of the vehicle's lateral deviation or side-slip angle response to the magnitude of the crosswind velocity component acting perpendicular to the vehicle's direction of travel.

Mathematically, it is characterized by two primary non-dimensional aerodynamic coefficients measured in a wind tunnel:

- **Aerodynamic Side Force Coefficient (C_S or C_Y)** – the ratio of lateral aerodynamic force to the product of dynamic pressure and frontal reference area.
- **Aerodynamic Yawing Moment Coefficient (C_N)** – the ratio of the yawing moment about the vertical axis to dynamic pressure, frontal area, and a characteristic length (usually wheelbase).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cross Wind Sensitivity is a critical module-4 topic that links vehicle aerodynamics directly to directional stability. Examiners frequently test the definitions of C_S and C_N and the formula for critical crosswind velocity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a calm road, and suddenly a strong gust of wind blows from your left side. Three things happen almost instantly:

1. You feel a push on your left shoulder – that is the **side force** trying to slide you sideways.
2. The wind catches the front of your bicycle and tries to twist the handlebar – that is the **yawing moment**.
3. If the wind is strong enough, you wobble or veer into the other lane – that is the **path deviation** which defines sensitivity.

A tall box-truck behaves like a tall, flat billboard; even a moderate wind can tip it or push it off course. A streamlined sports car behaves like a low, aerodynamic wedge; the same wind barely nudges it. This difference in response is precisely what **Cross Wind Sensitivity** quantifies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Real-World Metrics (bolded as required):**
> - **C_Y (Side Force Coefficient):** typically **0.1 to 0.6** for passenger cars
> - **C_N (Yawing Moment Coefficient):** typically **0.05 to 0.25** for passenger cars
> - **Critical Crosswind Velocity (V_cr):** typically **15 m/s to 25 m/s** for trucks, higher for cars
> - **Air Density (ρ):** standard value **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, ISA conditions

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Crosswind Action on a Vehicle

When a vehicle travels at a forward velocity **V** through still air, it experiences a relative wind equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to its motion. When an actual crosswind **V_w** blows perpendicular to the road, the resultant relative wind seen by the vehicle is the **vector sum** of the vehicle's velocity and the crosswind velocity.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown of how crosswind affects a vehicle:**

1. **Relative Wind Formation:** The vehicle moving at velocity V and the crosswind V_w combine vectorially. The magnitude and direction of the resultant relative wind W are computed first.
2. **Aerodynamic Force Generation:** This relative wind generates pressure distributions on the vehicle body, producing a side force F_S and a yawing moment M_Z about the center of gravity.
3. **Force and Moment Coefficients:** These are non-dimensionalized using dynamic pressure (q = 0.5·ρ·W²), frontal area A, and wheelbase L.
4. **Static Response:** The vehicle experiences a steady-state side-slip angle (β) and yaw rate (r) governed by the linear bicycle model.
5. **Dynamic Response:** Under gusty conditions (sinusoidal or step gust), the vehicle undergoes transient oscillations before settling.
6. **Driver Correction:** The driver applies steering corrections; sensitivity determines how much correction is needed.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Coefficient

- **Why C_Y matters:** It determines the steady lateral drift of the vehicle. A high C_Y means the wind pushes the vehicle's mass center sideways quickly.
- **Why C_N matters:** It determines the rotational tendency. If the aerodynamic center of pressure is behind the CG (positive C_N in KTU sign convention), the wind tends to align the vehicle with the wind direction, which is destabilizing in yaw.
- **Why both matter:** A vehicle can have low C_Y but high C_N (and vice versa). Stability requires both to be controlled through body shape, rear-end styling, and add-on devices (spoilers, vortex generators).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}$ | Pressure [Pa]; ρ [kg/m³]; W [m/s] |
| 2 | Side Force | $F_{S} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,C_{Y}$ | F_S in Newtons |
| 3 | Yawing Moment | $M_{Z} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,L\,C_{N}$ | M_Z in N·m |
| 4 | Side Force Coefficient | $C_{Y} = \dfrac{F_{S}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^{2} A}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Yawing Moment Coefficient | $C_{N} = \dfrac{M_{Z}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^{2} A L}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Resultant Wind Speed | $W = \sqrt{V^{2} + V_{w}^{2}}$ | m/s |
| 7 | Yaw Angle of Resultant Wind | $\psi_{a} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{w}}{V}\right)$ | radians |
| 8 | Critical Crosswind Velocity | $V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m\,g}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,C_{Y}}}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Sensitivity Index (S) | $S = \dfrac{\beta}{V_{w}/V}$ | rad/(rad) |
| 10 | Steady-State Side-slip | $\beta_{ss} = \dfrac{F_{S}\,L}{C_{\alpha r}\,V^{2}}$ | radians |
| 11 | Gust Energy Spectrum | $S_{u}(f) = \dfrac{4\,\sigma_{u}^{2}\,L_{u}/U}{[1+4(fL_{u}/U)^{2}]}$ | von Kármán spectrum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Note:** All table entries above use `\vert`/division rather than literal `|` characters to keep markdown tables intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Cross wind sensitivity analysis is essential for:

- **Passenger Cars:** Designing notchback vs. fastback shapes to minimize C_N.
- **Trucks & Buses:** Mandatory analysis under ECE R111 (anti-crosswind behavior) and ISO 12021-1 standards.
- **Racing (Formula 1):** Managing yaw sensitivity under high-speed cornering in turbulent air.
- **High-Speed Trains:** TGV and Shinkansen designs include nose-shape optimization to control crosswind buffeting.
- **Motorcycles:** Critical at speeds above 25 m/s; riders lean into crosswinds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Resultant Wind Velocity Vector

**Given:**
- Vehicle forward speed: $V$ along the x-axis
- Crosswind speed: $V_w$ along the y-axis (positive to the left)

**Step 1:** Write the velocity components in the vehicle's body-fixed coordinate frame.

$$
V_x = V, \quad V_y = V_w
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude of the resultant relative wind speed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sqrt{V_x^{2} + V_y^{2}} \\
  &= \sqrt{V^{2} + V_{w}^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the yaw angle of the resultant wind measured from the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_a &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_y}{V_x}\right) \\
       &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_w}{V}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** For small crosswinds ($V_w \ll V$), $\psi_a \approx V_w/V$. This linearization is widely used in linear bicycle models.

### 3.2 Derivation of Side Force and Yawing Moment

**Given:** Wind tunnel test data providing F_S and M_Z at a given wind speed W.

**Step 1:** Compute the dynamic pressure for the test condition.

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the side force coefficient by non-dimensionalizing the measured force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_Y &= \dfrac{F_S}{q \cdot A} \\
    &= \dfrac{F_S}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Define the yawing moment coefficient using the wheelbase L as reference length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_N &= \dfrac{M_Z}{q \cdot A \cdot L} \\
    &= \dfrac{M_Z}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to recover the dimensional quantities in a real driving scenario where wind speed is $V_w$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_S^{\text{real}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(V^{2}+V_w^{2})\,A\,C_Y \\
M_Z^{\text{real}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(V^{2}+V_w^{2})\,A\,L\,C_N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (Small-angle simplification):** For typical KTU exam problems, $V_w \ll V$, so $W^{2} \approx V^{2}$.

$$
F_S \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,C_Y, \quad M_Z \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,L\,C_N
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Critical Crosswind Velocity

**Concept:** The critical crosswind velocity is the wind speed at which the aerodynamic side force equals the vehicle's weight, i.e., the vehicle is on the verge of being lifted or laterally displaced beyond control.

**Step 1:** Set the aerodynamic side force equal to the gravitational restoring weight.

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{cr}^{2}\,A\,C_Y = m\,g
$$

**Step 2:** Solve algebraically for $V_{cr}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cr}^{2} &= \dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y} \\
V_{cr}    &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Insert numerical values for verification.

For a car with $m = 1500$ kg, $A = 2.2$ m², $C_Y = 0.3$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cr} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 2.2 \times 0.3}} \\
       &= \sqrt{\dfrac{29430}{0.8085}} \\
       &= \sqrt{36400.7} \\
       &\approx 190.8 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is unrealistically high for normal cars, confirming they are not "lifted" by crosswind. For trucks with $C_Y = 0.6$, the value drops, illustrating why trucks are far more vulnerable.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Static Sensitivity Index

**Linear bicycle model equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\,V\,\dot{\beta} + m\,V\,r &= F_{Sf} + F_{Sr} \\
I_{zz}\,\dot{r} &= L\,F_{Sf} - L_{r}\,F_{Sr} + M_Z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** At steady state, $\dot{\beta} = \dot{r} = 0$, and rear cornering force dominates.

$$
\beta_{ss} = \dfrac{F_S \, L}{C_{\alpha r}\,V^{2}} \quad \text{(approximate)}
$$

**Step 2:** Define sensitivity as the ratio of side-slip response to wind input.

$$
S = \dfrac{\beta_{ss}}{V_w / V}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify.

$$
S = \dfrac{F_S \, L}{C_{\alpha r}\,V\,V_w}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This shows that sensitivity increases linearly with side force and decreases with rear cornering stiffness. Adding a rear spoiler reduces C_Y, thus reducing S.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Crosswind Sensitivity Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Geometry and Mass] --> B[Wind Tunnel Test or CFD Simulation]
    B --> C[Measure Side Force Fs]
    B --> D[Measure Yawing Moment Mz]
    C --> E[Compute Side Force Coefficient CY]
    D --> F[Compute Yawing Moment Coefficient CN]
    E --> G[Compute Critical Wind Velocity Vcr]
    F --> H[Compute Steady State Sideslip Beta ss]
    G --> I[Compare with ECE R111 Limits]
    H --> J[Driver In the Loop Correction Torque]
    I --> K[Stability Pass or Fail]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Design Iteration on Body Shape]
```

### 4.2 Free Body Diagram of Forces and Moments (Functional Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vehicle CG at origin] --> B1[Side Force Fs acts at Aerodynamic Center]
    A1 --> C1[Yawing Moment Mz about CG]
    A1 --> D1[Front Tire Lateral Force Fyf]
    A1 --> E1[Rear Tire Lateral Force Fyr]
    B1 --> F1[Net Lateral Acceleration ay]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Sideslip Angle Beta]
    F1 --> H1[Yaw Rate r]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Vehicle Body Shape vs. Crosswind Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Body Style] --> B[Notchback Sedan]
    A --> C[Fastback Coupe]
    A --> D[Squareback Wagon]
    A --> E[SUV or Van]
    B --> B1[High CN due to rear wake]
    C --> C1[Low CN due to attached flow]
    D --> D1[High CY and CN]
    E --> E1[Very high CY due to flat sides]
    B1 --> F[Highest Crosswind Sensitivity]
    C1 --> G[Lowest Crosswind Sensitivity]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

### 4.4 Crosswind Sensitivity Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Gust Onset] --> P2[Rising Side Force]
    P2 --> P3[Sideslip Build Up]
    P3 --> P4[Driver Steering Input]
    P4 --> P5[Corrected Yaw]
    P5 --> P6[Gust End]
    P6 --> P7[Decay to Zero Sideslip]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the aerodynamic side force coefficient (C_Y) and yawing moment coefficient (C_N) for a road vehicle. State their typical ranges for a modern passenger car.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Side Force Coefficient C_Y:** Defined as the ratio of the lateral aerodynamic force acting on the vehicle to the product of dynamic pressure and the frontal reference area.

$$
C_Y = \dfrac{F_S}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A}
$$

- **Yawing Moment Coefficient C_N:** Defined as the ratio of the aerodynamic yawing moment about the vertical axis to the product of dynamic pressure, frontal area, and wheelbase.

$$
C_N = \dfrac{M_Z}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,L}
$$

- **Typical Ranges:** For modern passenger cars, $C_Y \approx 0.1$ to $0.6$ and $C_N \approx 0.05$ to $0.25$, measured at 0° yaw angle of the relative wind and varying with the actual yaw angle.

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark each definition + 1 mark for ranges)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meant by the term "Critical Crosswind Velocity" for a vehicle? Write the formula and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Critical Crosswind Velocity (V_cr)** is the wind speed at which the aerodynamic side force generated on the vehicle equals the vehicle's weight. At this condition, the lateral aerodynamic force balances gravity, meaning the vehicle is on the verge of being laterally displaced or lifted beyond safe control limits.

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y}}
$$

**Physical Significance:** It provides a single-number benchmark of how vulnerable a vehicle is to being pushed off course by wind. Trucks and vans have low V_cr (hence high sensitivity), while sports cars have high V_cr.

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

## Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.A (a)** A passenger car of mass 1500 kg and frontal area 2.2 m² travels at a speed of 25 m/s. It is subjected to a side wind of 8 m/s. If the aerodynamic side force coefficient is 0.35 and the yawing moment coefficient is 0.12, determine:
1. The resultant relative wind speed
2. The aerodynamic side force on the vehicle
3. The yawing moment about the CG (wheelbase L = 2.6 m)
4. The critical crosswind velocity

Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Resultant wind speed**

$$
W = \sqrt{V^{2} + V_{w}^{2}} = \sqrt{25^{2} + 8^{2}} = \sqrt{625 + 64} = \sqrt{689} \approx 26.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Aerodynamic side force**

$$
F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,C_Y = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 689 \times 2.2 \times 0.35
$$

$$
F_S \approx 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 689 \times 0.77 \approx 325.1 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Yawing moment**

$$
M_Z = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,W^{2}\,A\,L\,C_N = 325.1 \times 2.6 \times \dfrac{0.12}{0.35}
$$

$$
M_Z = 845.3 \times 0.3429 \approx 289.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

Alternative direct calculation:

$$
M_Z = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 689 \times 2.2 \times 2.6 \times 0.12 \approx 289.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Critical crosswind velocity**

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 2.2 \times 0.35}}
$$

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{29430}{0.94325}} = \sqrt{31200.5} \approx 176.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Results Summary:** $W \approx 26.25$ m/s, $F_S \approx 325$ N, $M_Z \approx 290$ N·m, $V_{cr} \approx 176.6$ m/s.

---

**Q.A (b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how the crosswind sensitivity of a passenger car can be reduced by (i) modifying the rear-end body shape and (ii) adding a rear spoiler. Discuss why a fastback design is generally better than a notchback design from a crosswind sensitivity viewpoint.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Rear-End Body Shape:** A notchback (with a vertical rear glass) creates a large separated wake region with strong vortices. This wake shifts the aerodynamic center of pressure behind the CG, increasing the yawing moment coefficient $C_N$ and making the vehicle yaw-sensitive. A fastback (sloping rear) allows smoother flow attachment, shifting the pressure center forward and reducing $C_N$.

**(ii) Rear Spoiler:** A spoiler separates the flow deliberately at a controlled location, reducing the strength of the unsteady wake and the side force coefficient $C_Y$. It also acts as a stabilizer for the pressure distribution in yaw, reducing both $C_Y$ and $C_N$ typically by 10–20%.

**Fastback vs. Notchback:** A fastback car has a streamlined rear roofline that promotes attached flow. This reduces the intensity of the wake, lowers both $C_Y$ and $C_N$, and thus reduces crosswind sensitivity. A notchback has an abrupt separation at the rear glass, producing strong wake unsteadiness that translates into higher $C_N$ and lower critical crosswind velocity.

*(Examiner's allocation: 3 marks body shape, 2 marks spoiler, 2 marks fastback vs notchback comparison)*

---

## Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q.B (a)** Derive the expression for critical crosswind velocity of a road vehicle. A truck of mass 8000 kg, frontal area 6 m², and side force coefficient 0.55 travels on a highway. Find the crosswind speed at which the side force equals the weight. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**

Equating aerodynamic side force to weight:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{cr}^{2}\,A\,C_Y = m\,g
$$

Solving:

$$
V_{cr}^{2} = \dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y}
$$

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,m\,g}{\rho\,A\,C_Y}}
$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8000 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 6 \times 0.55}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{156960}{4.04125}}
$$

$$
V_{cr} = \sqrt{38841} \approx 197.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is far above any natural wind speed, indicating that for trucks the danger is not lifting but rather side-slip and yawing before reaching V_cr. Truck safety regulations focus on yaw moment, not lift.

---

**Q.B (b)** A vehicle is subjected to a sinusoidal side gust $V_w(t) = V_{w0}\sin(\omega t)$. Using a quasi-static linear bicycle model, derive the steady-state sideslip angle response $\beta_{ss}(t)$ and discuss the role of rear cornering stiffness $C_{\alpha r}$ in reducing crosswind sensitivity.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Linear Bicycle Model Equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\,V\,\dot{\beta} + m\,V\,r &= C_{\alpha f}(\delta_f - \beta - \tfrac{L_f r}{V}) + C_{\alpha r}(-\beta + \tfrac{L_r r}{V}) + F_S(t) \\
I_{zz}\,\dot{r} &= L_f C_{\alpha f}(\delta_f - \beta - \tfrac{L_f r}{V}) - L_r C_{\alpha r}(-\beta + \tfrac{L_r r}{V}) + M_Z(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Quasi-static assumption** ($r \to 0$, $\dot{\beta} \to 0$ for low-frequency gusts).

$$
F_S(t) = C_{\alpha f}(\delta_f - \beta) + C_{\alpha r}(-\beta)
$$

For zero steering input $\delta_f = 0$:

$$
F_S(t) = -(C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r})\beta_{ss}(t)
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\beta_{ss}(t)$.**

$$
\beta_{ss}(t) = -\dfrac{F_S(t)}{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}
$$

With $F_S(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,C_Y \cdot \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\beta_{ss}(t) = -\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,C_Y}{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}} \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4: Role of $C_{\alpha r}$.** Increasing $C_{\alpha r}$ (e.g., by stiffer rear tires or adding a rear anti-roll bar) increases the denominator, thereby reducing $\beta_{ss}$. A stiffer rear axle resists the lateral wind-induced slip, keeping the vehicle more aligned with its intended path. However, excessive $C_{\alpha r}$ reduces understeer gradient and may cause oversteer; trade-off analysis is essential.

*(Examiner's allocation: 3 marks derivation, 2 marks final expression, 2 marks role of $C_{\alpha r}$)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cross Wind Sensitivity Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing C_Y and C_N:** Many students interchange these or use A instead of A·L in the yawing moment formula. Always remember C_N uses wheelbase as reference length.
> 2. **Forgetting the resultant wind speed W:** When crosswind is comparable to vehicle speed, using V instead of W introduces significant error. KTU 2024 questions explicitly test this with non-negligible V_w.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** KTU follows SAE sign convention (positive y to the left, positive z down). Mixing ISO and SAE conventions leads to wrong signs in M_Z and F_S.
> 4. **Using V_cr uncritically:** Critical crosswind velocity is a design metric, not a real-world failure speed. Examiners penalize answers that treat it as "the speed at which the car tips over."
> 5. **Skipping unit consistency:** Mixing N with kgf or km/h with m/s in intermediate steps loses 1–2 marks per part.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cross Wind Sensitivity** quantifies how much a vehicle responds to lateral wind; high sensitivity means unstable, low means stable.
- **C_Y** is the side force coefficient; **C_N** is the yawing moment coefficient. Both are measured in wind tunnels.
- **Resultant Wind Speed:** $W = \sqrt{V^2 + V_w^2}$ — always compute this before calculating F_S and M_Z.
- **Critical Crosswind Velocity:** $V_{cr} = \sqrt{2mg/(\rho A C_Y)}$ — benchmark metric.
- **Sensitivity Index:** $S = \beta/(V_w/V)$ — non-dimensional response metric.
- **Body Shape Effects:** Fastback > Notchback in crosswind stability due to better rear flow attachment.
- **Spoilers** reduce C_Y and C_N by managing wake separation.
- **Rear Cornering Stiffness $C_{\alpha r}$** directly reduces steady-state side-slip response to wind.
- **ECE R111** is the European standard that mandates crosswind behavior testing for heavy vehicles.
- **Typical Passenger Car Values:** $C_Y \in [0.1, 0.6]$, $C_N \in [0.05, 0.25]$.
- **Trucks** have high $C_Y$ due to flat sides, making them highly crosswind-sensitive despite high mass.
- **Sign Convention:** Always declare and stick to SAE or ISO before solving.
- **Quasi-static model** is valid for low-frequency gusts ($f < 0.5$ Hz); high-frequency gusts require full dynamic modeling.
- **Driver-in-the-loop** correction torque is the practical mitigation; sensitivity index helps design driver assist features.
